problems.promptEn: Practice 7.11. Companies A and B face the following interest rates (adjusted for the differential impact of taxes): Company A Company B U.S. dollars Floating + 0.5% Floating + 1.0% Canadian dollars 5.0% 6.5% Assume that A wants to borrow U.S. dollars at a floating rate of interest and B wants to borrow Canadian dollars at a fixed rate of interest. A financial institution is planning to arrange a swap and requires a 50-basis-point spread. If the swap is equally attractive to A and B, what rates of interest will A and B end up paying? Swaps
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 7.11. A е…¬еҸёе’Ң B е…¬еҸёйқўдёҙд»ҘдёӢеҲ©зҺҮпјҲж №жҚ®зЁҺж”¶зҡ„е·®ејӮеҪұе“ҚиҝӣиЎҢи°ғж•ҙпјүпјҡ A е…¬еҸё B е…¬еҸё зҫҺе…ғ жө®еҠЁ + 0.5% жө®еҠЁ + 1.0% еҠ е…ғ 5.0% 6.5% еҒҮи®ҫAжғід»Ҙжө®еҠЁеҲ©зҺҮеҖҹе…ҘзҫҺе…ғпјҢBжғід»Ҙеӣәе®ҡеҲ©зҺҮеҖҹе…ҘеҠ е…ғгҖӮдёҖе®¶йҮ‘иһҚжңәжһ„жӯЈи®ЎеҲ’е®үжҺ’жҺүжңҹпјҢе№¶иҰҒжұӮ 50 дёӘеҹәзӮ№зҡ„еҲ©е·®гҖӮеҰӮжһңжҺүжңҹеҜ№ A е’Ң B е…·жңүеҗҢзӯүеҗёеј•еҠӣпјҢйӮЈд№Ҳ A е’Ң B жңҖз»Ҳе°Ҷж”Ҝд»ҳеӨҡе°‘еҲ©зҺҮпјҹ жҺүжңҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
7.11
Company A has a comparative advantage in the Canadian dollar fixed-rate market. Company
B has a comparative advantage in the U.S. dollar floating-rate market. (This may be because
of their tax positions.) However, company A wants to borrow in the U.S. dollar floating-rate
market and company B wants to borrow in the Canadian dollar fixed-rate market. This gives
rise to the swap opportunity.
The differential between the U.S. dollar floating rates is 0.5% per annum, and the differential
between the Canadian dollar fixed rates is 1.5% per annum. The difference between the
differentials is 1% per annum. The total potential gain to all parties from the swap is therefore
1% per annum, or 100 basis points. If the financial intermediary requires 50 basis points, each
of A and B can be made 25 basis points better off. Thus a swap can be designed so that it
provides A with U.S. dollars at Floating + 0.25% per annum, and B with Canadian dollars at
6.25% per annum. The swap is shown in Figure S7.4.

US$ Floating+0.25%
US$ Floating+1%
C$ 6.25%
C% 5%

Figure S7.4 Swap for Problem 7.11

Principal payments flow in the opposite direction to the arrows at the start of the life of the
swap and in the same direction as the arrows at the end of the life of the swap. The financial
institution would be exposed to some foreign exchange risk which could be hedged using
forward contracts.

problems.promptEn: Practice 7.12. After it hedges its foreign exchange risk using forward contracts, is the financial institutionвҖҷs average spread in Figure 7.11 likely to be greater than or less than 20 basis points? Explain your answer.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 7.12. еңЁдҪҝз”ЁиҝңжңҹеҗҲзәҰеҜ№еҶІеӨ–жұҮйЈҺйҷ©еҗҺпјҢеӣҫ7.11дёӯйҮ‘иһҚжңәжһ„зҡ„е№іеқҮеҲ©е·®еҸҜиғҪеӨ§дәҺиҝҳжҳҜе°ҸдәҺ20дёӘеҹәзӮ№пјҹи§ЈйҮҠдҪ зҡ„зӯ”жЎҲгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
7.12
The financial institution will have to buy 1.1% of the AUD principal in the forward market
for each year of the life of the swap. Since AUD interest rates are higher than dollar interest
rates, AUD is at a discount in forward markets. This means that the AUD purchased for year
2 is less expensive than that purchased for year 1; the AUD purchased for year 3 is less
expensive than that purchased for year 2; and so on. This works in favor of the financial
institution and means that its spread increases with time. The spread is always above 20 basis
points.

problems.promptEn: Practice 7.13. вҖңNonfinancial companies with high credit risks are the ones that cannot access fixed-rate markets directly. They are the companies that are most likely to be paying fixed and receiving floating in an interest rate swap.вҖқ Assume that this statement is true. Do you think it increases or decreases the risk of a financial institutionвҖҷs swap portfolio? Assume that companies are most likely to default when interest rates are high.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 7.13. вҖңдҝЎз”ЁйЈҺйҷ©иҫғй«ҳзҡ„йқһйҮ‘иһҚе…¬еҸёж— жі•зӣҙжҺҘиҝӣе…Ҙеӣәе®ҡеҲ©зҺҮеёӮеңәгҖӮе®ғд»¬жҳҜжңҖжңүеҸҜиғҪеңЁеҲ©зҺҮжҺүжңҹдёӯж”Ҝд»ҳеӣәе®ҡеҲ©зҺҮе№¶жҺҘж”¶жө®еҠЁеҲ©зҺҮзҡ„е…¬еҸёгҖӮвҖқеҒҮи®ҫиҝҷдёӘйҷҲиҝ°жҳҜжӯЈзЎ®зҡ„гҖӮжӮЁи®ӨдёәиҝҷдјҡеўһеҠ иҝҳжҳҜеҮҸе°‘йҮ‘иһҚжңәжһ„жҺүжңҹжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲзҡ„йЈҺйҷ©пјҹеҒҮи®ҫеҪ“еҲ©зҺҮй«ҳж—¶пјҢе…¬еҸёжңҖжңүеҸҜиғҪиҝқзәҰгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
7.13
Consider two offsetting plain vanilla interest rate swaps that a financial institution enters into
with companies X and Y. We suppose that X is paying fixed and receiving floating while Y is
paying floating and receiving fixed.
The quote suggests that company X will usually be less creditworthy than company Y.
(Company X might be a BBB-rated company that has difficulty in accessing fixed-rate
markets directly; company Y might be a AAA-rated company that has no difficulty accessing
fixed or floating rate markets.) Presumably company X wants fixed-rate funds and company
Y wants floating-rate funds.
The financial institution will realize a loss if company Y defaults when rates are high or if
company X defaults when rates are low. These events are relatively unlikely since (a) Y is
unlikely to default in any circumstances and (b) defaults are less likely to happen when rates
are low. For the purposes of illustration, suppose that the probabilities of various events are
as follows:

Default by Y:

0.001
Default by X:

0.010
Rates high when default occurs:
0.7
Rates low when default occurs:
0.3

The probability of a loss is

0 001 0 7
0 010 0 3
0 0037
.
x.+.
x.=.

If the roles of X and Y in the swap had been reversed the probability of a loss would be

0 001 0 3
0 010 0 7
0 0073
.
x.+.
x.=.

Assuming companies are more likely to default when interest rates are high, the above
argument shows that the observation in quotes has the effect of decreasing the risk of a
financial institutionвҖҷs swap portfolio. It is worth noting that the assumption that defaults are

more likely when interest rates are high is open to question. The assumption is motivated by
the thought that high interest rates often lead to financial difficulties for corporations.
However, the empirical evidence on whether defaults are more likely when interest rates are
high is mixed.

еҰӮжһңеҲ©зҺҮй«ҳж—¶е…¬еҸё Y иҝқзәҰпјҢжҲ–иҖ…еҲ©зҺҮдҪҺж—¶е…¬еҸё X иҝқзәҰпјҢйҮ‘иһҚжңәжһ„е°ҶйҒӯеҸ—жҚҹеӨұгҖӮиҝҷдәӣдәӢд»¶иҫғдёҚеҸҜиғҪеҸ‘з”ҹпјҢеӣ дёә (a) е…¬еҸё Y еңЁд»»дҪ•зҠ¶еҶөдёӢиҝқзәҰзҡ„еҸҜиғҪжҖ§йғҪиҫғдҪҺпјҢдё” (b) еҲ©зҺҮдҪҺж—¶иҝқзәҰжҰӮзҺҮиҫғе°ҸгҖӮдёәдәҶиҜҙжҳҺпјҢеҒҮи®ҫеҗ„з§ҚдәӢд»¶жҰӮзҺҮеҰӮдёӢпјҡ

е…¬еҸё Y иҝқзәҰжҰӮзҺҮпјҡ0.001
е…¬еҸё X иҝқзәҰжҰӮзҺҮпјҡ0.010
иҝқзәҰж—¶еҲ©зҺҮй«ҳжҰӮзҺҮпјҡ0.7
иҝқзәҰж—¶еҲ©зҺҮдҪҺжҰӮзҺҮпјҡ0.3

еҲҷжҚҹеӨұзҡ„жҰӮзҺҮдёә

еҒҮи®ҫе…¬еҸёеңЁеҲ©зҺҮй«ҳж—¶жӣҙеҸҜиғҪиҝқзәҰпјҢдёҠиҝ°и®әиҜҒиЎЁжҳҺеј•з”ЁдёӯйҷҲиҝ°зҡ„жғ…еҶөдјҡеҮҸе°‘йҮ‘иһҚжңәжһ„жҺүжңҹжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲзҡ„йЈҺйҷ©гҖӮеҖјеҫ—жіЁж„Ҹзҡ„жҳҜпјҢеҲ©зҺҮй«ҳж—¶иҝқзәҰзҺҮдёҠеҚҮзҡ„еҒҮи®ҫе°ҡжңүдәүи®®гҖӮиҜҘеҒҮи®ҫзјҳдәҺй«ҳеҲ©зҺҮйҖҡеёёеҜјиҮҙе…¬еҸёиҙўеҠЎеӣ°йҡҫзҡ„жғіжі•пјҢдҪҶе®һиҜҒж•°жҚ®жҳҫзӨәиҝқзәҰжҳҜеҗҰжӣҙеҸҜиғҪеңЁй«ҳеҲ©зҺҮж—¶еҸ‘з”ҹе°ҡж— е®ҡи®әгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 7.14. Why is the expected loss to a bank from a default on a swap with a counterparty less than the expected loss from the default on a loan to the counterparty when the loan and swap have the same principal? Assume that there are no other derivatives transactions between the bank and the counterparty, that the swap is cleared bilaterally, and that no collateral is provided by the counterparty in the case of either the swap or the loan.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 7.14. еҪ“иҙ·ж¬ҫе’ҢжҺүжңҹзҡ„жң¬йҮ‘зӣёеҗҢж—¶пјҢдёәд»Җд№Ҳй“¶иЎҢеӣ дёҺдәӨжҳ“еҜ№жүӢзҡ„жҺүжңҹиҝқзәҰиҖҢйҖ жҲҗзҡ„йў„жңҹжҚҹеӨұе°ҸдәҺеӣ еҗ‘дәӨжҳ“еҜ№жүӢжҸҗдҫӣзҡ„иҙ·ж¬ҫиҝқзәҰиҖҢйҖ жҲҗзҡ„йў„жңҹжҚҹеӨұпјҹеҒҮи®ҫй“¶иЎҢдёҺдәӨжҳ“еҜ№жүӢд№Ӣй—ҙжІЎжңүе…¶д»–иЎҚз”ҹе“ҒдәӨжҳ“пјҢжҺүжңҹжҳҜеҸҢиҫ№жё…з®—зҡ„пјҢе№¶дё”ж— и®әжҳҜжҺүжңҹиҝҳжҳҜиҙ·ж¬ҫпјҢдәӨжҳ“еҜ№жүӢйғҪжІЎжңүжҸҗдҫӣжҠөжҠје“ҒгҖӮ

problems.explanation:
7.14
In an interest-rate swap a financial institutionвҖҷs exposure depends on the difference between a
fixed-rate of interest and a floating-rate of interest. It has no exposure to the notional
principal. In a loan the whole principal can be lost.

problems.promptEn: Practice 7.15. A bank finds that its assets are not matched with its liabilities. It is taking floating-rate deposits and making fixed-rate loans. How can swaps be used to offset the risk?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 7.15. дёҖе®¶й“¶иЎҢеҸ‘зҺ°е…¶иө„дә§дёҺиҙҹеҖәдёҚеҢ№й…ҚгҖӮе®ғжҺҘеҸ—жө®еҠЁеҲ©зҺҮеӯҳж¬ҫе№¶еҸ‘ж”ҫеӣәе®ҡеҲ©зҺҮиҙ·ж¬ҫгҖӮеҰӮдҪ•еҲ©з”ЁжҺүжңҹжқҘжҠөж¶ҲйЈҺйҷ©пјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
7.15
The bank is paying a floating-rate on the deposits and receiving a fixed-rate on the loans. It
can offset its risk by entering into interest rate swaps (with other financial institutions or
corporations) in which it contracts to pay fixed and receive floating.

problems.promptEn: Practice 7.18. A financial institution has entered into a swap where it agreed to make quarterly payments at a rate of 3% per annum and receive the SOFR three-month reference rate on a notional principal of USD 100 million. The swap now has a remaining life of 7.5 months. Assume the risk-free rates with continuous compounding (calculated from SOFR) for 1.5, 4.5, and 7.5 months are 2.8%, 3.0%, and 3.1%, respectively. Assume also that the continuously compounded risk-free rate observed for the last 1.5 months is 2.7%. Estimate the value of the swap.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 7.18. дёҖе®¶йҮ‘иһҚжңәжһ„е·Ізӯҫи®ўдә’жҚўеҚҸи®®пјҢеҗҢж„ҸжҢүе№ҙеҲ©зҺҮ 3% иҝӣиЎҢеӯЈеәҰд»ҳж¬ҫпјҢе№¶иҺ·еҫ—еҗҚд№үжң¬йҮ‘дёә 1 дәҝзҫҺе…ғзҡ„ SOFR дёүдёӘжңҲеҸӮиҖғеҲ©зҺҮгҖӮиҜҘдә’жҚўзӣ®еүҚзҡ„еү©дҪҷеҜҝе‘Ҫдёә 7.5 дёӘжңҲгҖӮеҒҮи®ҫиҝһз»ӯеӨҚеҲ©пјҲж №жҚ® SOFR и®Ўз®—пјү1.5гҖҒ4.5 е’Ң 7.5 дёӘжңҲзҡ„ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮеҲҶеҲ«дёә 2.8%гҖҒ3.0% е’Ң 3.1%гҖӮиҝҳеҒҮи®ҫиҝҮеҺ» 1.5 дёӘжңҲи§ӮеҜҹеҲ°зҡ„иҝһз»ӯеӨҚеҗҲж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёә 2.7%гҖӮдј°и®Ўдә’жҚўзҡ„д»·еҖјгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.

7.18
With continuous compounding the forward rate for the first exchange is 2.75% (the average
of 2.7% and 2.8%. For the second exchange it is (3Г—4.5-2.8Г—1.5)/3 or 3.1%. For the final
exchange it is (3.1Г—7.5-3Г—4.5)/3 or 3.25%. These rates become 2.7595%, 3.112%, and
3.2632% with quarterly compounding. The value of the swap in millions of dollars is
therefore
100Г—0.25Г—[(0.027595-0.03)e-0.028Г—0.125+(0.0311-0.03)e-0.03Г—0.375+(0.032632-0.03)e-0.031Г—0.6125]
=0.0322

problems.promptEn: Practice 7.19. (a) Company A has been offered the swap quotes in Table 7.4. It can borrow for three years at 3.45%. What floating rate can it swap this fixed rate into? (c) Explain the rollover risks that Company B is taking.

problems.promptEn: Practice 7.20. The one-year LIBOR rates is 3%, and the LIBOR forward rate for the 1- to 2-year period is 3.2%. The three-year swap rate for a swap with annual payments is 3.2%. What is the LIBOR forward rate for the 2- to 3-year period if OIS zero rates for maturities of one, two, and three years are 2.5%, 2.7%, and 2.9%, respectively. What is the value of a three- year swap where 4% is received and LIBOR is paid on a principal of USD 100 million. All rates are annually compounded.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 7.20. дёҖе№ҙжңҹ LIBOR еҲ©зҺҮдёә 3%пјҢ1 иҮі 2 е№ҙжңҹ LIBOR иҝңжңҹеҲ©зҺҮдёә 3.2%гҖӮе№ҙд»ҳжҺүжңҹзҡ„дёүе№ҙжңҹжҺүжңҹеҲ©зҺҮдёә 3.2%гҖӮеҰӮжһңдёҖе№ҙжңҹгҖҒдёӨе№ҙжңҹе’Ңдёүе№ҙжңҹзҡ„ OIS йӣ¶еҲ©зҺҮеҲҶеҲ«дёә 2.5%гҖҒ2.7% е’Ң 2.9%пјҢеҲҷ 2 иҮі 3 е№ҙжңҹзҡ„ LIBOR иҝңжңҹеҲ©зҺҮжҳҜеӨҡе°‘гҖӮж”¶еҸ– 4% дё”д»Ҙ 1 дәҝзҫҺе…ғжң¬йҮ‘ж”Ҝд»ҳ LIBOR зҡ„дёүе№ҙжңҹдә’жҚўзҡ„д»·еҖјжҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹжүҖжңүеҲ©зҺҮеқҮжҢүе№ҙеӨҚеҲ©и®Ўз®—гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 7.21. A financial institution has entered into a swap where it agreed to receive quarterly payments at a rate of 2% per annum and pay the SOFR three-month reference rate on a notional principal of USD 100 million. The swap now has a remaining life of 10 months. Assume the risk-free rates with continuous compounding (calculated from SOFR) for 1 month, 4 months, 7 months, and 10 months are 1.4%, 1.6%, 1.7%, and 1.8%, respectively. Assume also that the continuously compounded risk-free rate observed for the last two months is 1.1%. Estimate the value of the swap.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 7.21. дёҖе®¶йҮ‘иһҚжңәжһ„е·Ізӯҫи®ўдә’жҚўеҚҸи®®пјҢеҗҢж„ҸжҢүе№ҙеҲ©зҺҮ 2% ж”¶еҸ–еӯЈеәҰд»ҳж¬ҫпјҢе№¶жҢү 1 дәҝзҫҺе…ғзҡ„еҗҚд№үжң¬йҮ‘ж”Ҝд»ҳ SOFR дёүдёӘжңҲеҸӮиҖғеҲ©зҺҮгҖӮиҜҘдәӨжҚўзӣ®еүҚзҡ„еү©дҪҷжңҹйҷҗдёә 10 дёӘжңҲгҖӮеҒҮи®ҫиҝһз»ӯеӨҚеҲ©пјҲж №жҚ®SOFRи®Ўз®—пјү1дёӘжңҲгҖҒ4дёӘжңҲгҖҒ7дёӘжңҲе’Ң10дёӘжңҲзҡ„ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮеҲҶеҲ«дёә1.4%гҖҒ1.6%гҖҒ1.7%е’Ң1.8%пјҢ еҲҶеҲ«гҖӮиҝҳеҒҮи®ҫиҝҮеҺ»дёӨдёӘжңҲи§ӮеҜҹеҲ°зҡ„иҝһз»ӯеӨҚеҗҲж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёә 1.1%гҖӮдј°и®Ўдә’жҚўзҡ„д»·еҖјгҖӮ

problems.explanation:
7.21
We can value the swap as a series of forward rate agreements. The value in $ millions is

(0.8 вҖ“ 0.9)e-0.038Г—2/12 + (1.0 вҖ“ 0.9)e-0.038Г—5/12 + (1.0 вҖ“ 0.9)e-0.038Г—8/12 + (1.0 вҖ“ 0.9)e-0.038Г—11/12
+ (1.0 вҖ“ 0.9)e-0.038Г—14/12 = 0.289

+ (1.0 вҖ“ 0.9)e-0.038Г—14/12 = 0.289

problems.promptEn: Practice 7.22. Company A, a British manufacturer, wishes to borrow U.S. dollars at a fixed rate of interest. Company B, a U.S. multinational, wishes to borrow sterling at a fixed rate of interest. They have been quoted the following rates per annum: Sterling U.S. Dollars Company A 11.0% 7.0% Company B 10.6% 6.2% (Rates have been adjusted for differential tax effects.) Design a swap that will net a bank, acting as intermediary, 10 basis points per annum and that will produce a gain of 15 basis points per annum for each of the two companies.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 7.22. A е…¬еҸёжҳҜдёҖе®¶иӢұеӣҪеҲ¶йҖ е•ҶпјҢеёҢжңӣд»Ҙеӣәе®ҡеҲ©зҺҮеҖҹе…ҘзҫҺе…ғгҖӮ B е…¬еҸёжҳҜдёҖе®¶зҫҺеӣҪи·ЁеӣҪе…¬еҸёпјҢеёҢжңӣд»Ҙеӣәе®ҡеҲ©зҺҮеҖҹе…ҘиӢұй•‘гҖӮд»–д»¬зҡ„е№ҙеҲ©зҺҮеҰӮдёӢпјҡ иӢұй•‘ A е…¬еҸё 11.0% 7.0% B е…¬еҸё 10.6% 6.2% пјҲеҲ©зҺҮе·Іж №жҚ®дёҚеҗҢзҡ„зЁҺж”¶еҪұе“ҚиҝӣиЎҢдәҶи°ғж•ҙгҖӮпјүи®ҫи®ЎдёҖйЎ№дә’жҚўпјҢдҪҝе……еҪ“дёӯд»Ӣзҡ„й“¶иЎҢжҜҸе№ҙиҺ·еҫ— 10 дёӘеҹәзӮ№зҡ„еҮҖеҖјпјҢе№¶дёәдёӨе®¶е…¬еҸёеҗ„иҮӘеёҰжқҘжҜҸе№ҙ 15 дёӘеҹәзӮ№зҡ„ж”¶зӣҠгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
7.22
The spread between the interest rates offered to A and B is 0.4% (or 40 basis points) on
sterling loans and 0.8% (or 80 basis points) on U.S. dollar loans. The total benefit to all
parties from the swap is therefore

80
40
40 basis points
-
=

It is therefore possible to design a swap which will earn 10 basis points for the bank while
making each of A and B 15 basis points better off than they would be by going directly to
financial markets. One possible swap is shown in Figure S7.5. Company A borrows at an
effective rate of 6.85% per annum in U.S. dollars.
Company B borrows at an effective rate of 10.45% per annum in sterling. The bank earns a
10-basis-point spread. The way in which currency swaps such as this operate is as follows.
Principal amounts in dollars and sterling that are roughly equivalent are chosen. These
principal amounts flow in the opposite direction to the arrows at the time the swap is
initiated. Interest payments then flow in the same direction as the arrows during the life of the
swap and the principal amounts flow in the same direction as the arrows at the end of the life
of the swap.
Note that the bank is exposed to some exchange rate risk in the swap. It earns 65 basis points
in U.S. dollars and pays 55 basis points in sterling. This exchange rate risk could be hedged
using forward contracts.

Figure S7.5 One Possible Swap for Problem 7.22

problems.promptEn: Practice 7.23. The five-year swap rate when cash flows are exchanged semiannually is 4%. A company wants a swap where it receives payments at 4.2% per annum on a notional principal of USD 10 million. The OIS zero curve is flat at 3.6%. How much should a derivatives dealer charge the company. Assume that all rates are expressed with semiannual compounding and ignore bidвҖ“ask spreads.

problems.promptEn: Practice 8.4. How were the risks in ABS CDOs misjudged by the market?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 8.4. ABS CDOзҡ„йЈҺйҷ©жҳҜеҰӮдҪ•иў«еёӮеңәиҜҜеҲӨзҡ„пјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
8.4
Investors underestimated how high the default correlations between mortgages would be in
stressed market conditions. Investors also did not always realize that the tranches underlying
ABS CDOs were usually quite thin so that they were either totally wiped out or untouched.
There was an unfortunate tendency to assume that a tranche with a particular rating could be
considered to be the same as a bond with that rating. This assumption is not valid for the
reasons just mentioned.
problems.explanationEn:
8.4
Investors underestimated how high the default correlations between mortgages would be in
stressed market conditions. Investors also did not always realize that the tranches underlying
ABS CDOs were usually quite thin so that they were either totally wiped out or untouched.
There was an unfortunate tendency to assume that a tranche with a particular rating could be
considered to be the same as a bond with that rating. This assumption is not valid for the
reasons just mentioned.

8.4

problems.promptEn: Practice 8.6. Explain the impact of an increase in default correlation on the risks of the senior tranche of an ABS. What is its impact on the risks of the equity tranche?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 8.6. и§ЈйҮҠиҝқзәҰзӣёе…іжҖ§еўһеҠ еҜ№ ABS й«ҳзә§йғЁеҲҶйЈҺйҷ©зҡ„еҪұе“ҚгҖӮеҜ№иӮЎжқғйғЁеҲҶзҡ„йЈҺйҷ©жңүдҪ•еҪұе“Қпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
8.6
Consider the structure in Figure 8.1. Assume that there are 1,000 assets each with a principal
of USD 100,000. Suppose that all the assets have a 5% chance of defaulting during the life of the
ABS and there will be a 50% recovery. For the senior tranche to be affected, there have to be
at least 400 defaults. When default correlation is zero, there is virtually no chance of this. As
default correlation increases, 400 defaults become more likely. In the limit as the correlation
approaches one, there is a 5% chance that all 1,000 will default.
As default correlation increases, the equity tranche becomes less risky. When the default
correlation is low, some defaults are almost certain to happen so that the equity tranche
experiences losses. As the default correlation increases, it becomes less likely that there will
be defaults. In the limit as the correlation approaches one, there is a 95% chance that there
will be no defaults and the equity tranche experiences no losses.
problems.explanationEn:
8.6
Consider the structure in Figure 8.1. Assume that there are 1,000 assets each with a principal
of USD 100,000. Suppose that all the assets have a 5% chance of defaulting during the life of the
ABS and there will be a 50% recovery. For the senior tranche to be affected, there have to be
at least 400 defaults. When default correlation is zero, there is virtually no chance of this. As
default correlation increases, 400 defaults become more likely. In the limit as the correlation
approaches one, there is a 5% chance that all 1,000 will default.
As default correlation increases, the equity tranche becomes less risky. When the default
correlation is low, some defaults are almost certain to happen so that the equity tranche
experiences losses. As the default correlation increases, it becomes less likely that there will
be defaults. In the limit as the correlation approaches one, there is a 95% chance that there
will be no defaults and the equity tranche experiences no losses.
problems.explanationZh:
8.6

problems.promptEn: Practice 8.11. вҖңResecuritization was a badly flawed idea. AAA tranches created from the mezzanine tranches of ABSs are bound to have a higher probability of default than the AAA-rated tranches of ABSs.вҖқ Discuss this point of view.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 8.11. вҖңеҶҚиҜҒеҲёеҢ–жҳҜдёҖдёӘеӯҳеңЁдёҘйҮҚзјәйҷ·зҡ„жғіжі•гҖӮз”ұ ABS еӨ№еұӮйғЁеҲҶеҲӣе»әзҡ„ AAA йғЁеҲҶеҝ…з„¶жҜ” ABS зҡ„ AAA зә§йғЁеҲҶе…·жңүжӣҙй«ҳзҡ„иҝқзәҰжҰӮзҺҮгҖӮвҖқи°Ҳи°ҲиҝҷдёӘи§ӮзӮ№гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
8.11
When the AAA-rated tranches of an ABS experiences defaults, the mezzanine tranches of the
ABSs must have been wiped out. As a result, the AAA tranche of the ABS CDO has also
wiped out. If the portfolios underlying the different ABSs have the same default rates, it must
therefore be the case the AAA-rated tranche of the ABS is safer than the AAA-rated tranche
of the ABS CDO. If there is a wide variation in the default rates, it is possible for the AAA-
rated tranche of the ABS CDO to fare better than some (but not all) AAA-rated tranches of
the underlying ABSs.
Resecuritization can only be successful if the default rates of the underlying ABS portfolios
are not highly correlated. The best approach would seem to be to obtain as much
diversification as possible in the portfolio of assets underlying the ABS. Resecuritization then
has no value.
problems.explanationEn:
8.11
When the AAA-rated tranches of an ABS experiences defaults, the mezzanine tranches of the
ABSs must have been wiped out. As a result, the AAA tranche of the ABS CDO has also
wiped out. If the portfolios underlying the different ABSs have the same default rates, it must
therefore be the case the AAA-rated tranche of the ABS is safer than the AAA-rated tranche
of the ABS CDO. If there is a wide variation in the default rates, it is possible for the AAA-
rated tranche of the ABS CDO to fare better than some (but not all) AAA-rated tranches of
the underlying ABSs.
Resecuritization can only be successful if the default rates of the underlying ABS portfolios
are not highly correlated. The best approach would seem to be to obtain as much
diversification as possible in the portfolio of assets underlying the ABS. Resecuritization then
has no value.

problems.promptEn: Practice 8.12. Suppose that mezzanine tranches of the ABS CDOs, similar to those in Figure 8.3, are resecuritized to form what is referred to as a вҖңCDO squared.вҖқ As in the case of tranches created from ABSs in Figure 8.3, 65% of the principal is allocated to a AAA tranche, 25% to a BBB tranche, and 10% to the equity tranche. How high does the loss percentage have to be on the underlying assets for losses to be experienced by a AAA-rated tranche that is created in this way? (Assume that every portfolio of assets that is used to create ABSs experiences the same loss rate.) XVAs Before moving on to discuss how options and other more complex derivatives are valued, we consider various price adjustments that have become important in derivatives markets. These are the credit valuation adjustment (CVA), the debit (or debt) valuation adjustment (DVA), the funding valuation adjustment (FVA), the margin valuation adjustment (MVA), and the capital valuation adjustment (KVA). Collectively the adjustments are known as XVAs. Some of the adjustments have a stronger theoretical basis than others. As we shall see, financial economists have no problem with CVA and DVA, but have reservations about FVA, MVA, and KVA.

problems.promptEn: Practice 9.2. Explain the difference between the views of financial economists and most practitioners on how KVA should be calculated.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 9.2. и§ЈйҮҠдёҖдёӢйҮ‘иһҚз»ҸжөҺеӯҰе®¶е’ҢеӨ§еӨҡж•°д»ҺдёҡиҖ…еҜ№дәҺеҰӮдҪ•и®Ўз®— KVA зҡ„и§ӮзӮ№д№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„е·®ејӮгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
9.2
Many practitioners calculate KVA by arguing that there is a cost if a bank does something that
requires additional regulatory capital and that the incremental return on the regulatory capital
should be at least the return required by shareholders. A financial economist would argue against
this if the project is less risky than the average project undertaken by the bank because the
project will lower the average risk of the bank and therefore lower the return required by equity
holders.
problems.explanationEn:

holders.

problems.promptEn: Practice 9.3. Explain why FVA can be calculated for a transaction without considering the portfolio to which the transaction belongs, but that the same is not true of MVA.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 9.3. и§ЈйҮҠдёәд»Җд№ҲеҸҜд»ҘеңЁдёҚиҖғиҷ‘дәӨжҳ“жүҖеұһжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲзҡ„жғ…еҶөдёӢи®Ўз®—дәӨжҳ“зҡ„ FVAпјҢдҪҶ MVA еҚҙдёҚз„¶гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
9.3
FVA is concerned with variation margin. The variation margin for a portfolio is the sum of the
variation margins for the transactions in the portfolio. (As indicated in footnote 13 of Chapter 9,
this is only approximately true when the impact of defaults on funding is considered.) MVA is
concerned with initial margin which (at least in the case of CCPs) is calculated at the portfolio
level. (Note: The standard regulatory approach to setting initial margin for bilaterally cleared
derivatives does not permit netting. However, the industry has come up with SIMM, Standard
Initial Margin Model, which does allow netting.)

derivatives does not permit netting. However, the industry has come up with SIMM, Standard

problems.promptEn: Practice 9.4. Suppose that a bank buys an option from a client. The option is uncollateralized and there are no other transactions outstanding with the client. The expected values of the option at the midpoint of years 1, 2, and 3 are 6, 5, and 4. The probability of the counterparty defaulting in each of the three years is 3%. The probability of the bank defaulting in each of the three years is 2%. Estimate the bankвҖҷs CVA and DVA for the transaction. Assume no recovery in the event of a default and zero interest rates.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 9.4. еҒҮи®ҫдёҖе®¶й“¶иЎҢд»Һе®ўжҲ·йӮЈйҮҢиҙӯд№°дәҶдёҖд»ҪжңҹжқғгҖӮиҜҘжңҹжқғжҳҜж— жҠөжҠјзҡ„пјҢе№¶дё”дёҺе®ўжҲ·жІЎжңүе…¶д»–жңӘе®ҢжҲҗзҡ„дәӨжҳ“гҖӮжңҹжқғеңЁз¬¬ 1 е№ҙгҖҒз¬¬ 2 е№ҙе’Ңз¬¬ 3 е№ҙдёӯзӮ№зҡ„йў„жңҹеҖјдёә 6гҖҒ5 е’Ң 4гҖӮдёүе№ҙдёӯжҜҸе№ҙдәӨжҳ“еҜ№жүӢиҝқзәҰзҡ„жҰӮзҺҮдёә 3%гҖӮй“¶иЎҢдёүе№ҙеҶ…жҜҸе№ҙиҝқзәҰзҡ„жҰӮзҺҮдёә2%гҖӮдј°з®—й“¶иЎҢй’ҲеҜ№иҜҘдәӨжҳ“зҡ„ CVA е’Ң DVAгҖӮеҒҮи®ҫеңЁиҝқзәҰе’Ңйӣ¶еҲ©зҺҮзҡ„жғ…еҶөдёӢж— жі•жҒўеӨҚгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 9.5. вҖңThe impact of DVA on earnings volatility is generally greater than that of CVA.вҖқ Explain this statement.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 9.5. вҖңDVA еҜ№зӣҲеҲ©жіўеҠЁзҡ„еҪұе“ҚйҖҡеёёеӨ§дәҺ CVAгҖӮвҖқи§ЈйҮҠдёҖдёӢиҝҷдёӘиҜҙжі•гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
9.5
The DVA for a bank depends on a single credit spread (its own) whereas CVA depends on the
credit spread of all the bankвҖҷs counterparties. On any given day, some counterparty spreads can
be expected to go up while others go down so that there are some offsets. DVA can therefore be
expected to be more volatile.
problems.explanationEn:
9.5

credit spread of all the bankвҖҷs counterparties. On any given day, some counterparty spreads can
be expected to go up while others go down so that there are some offsets. DVA can therefore be
expected to be more volatile.

problems.promptEn: Practice 9.6. A company is trying to decide between issuing debt and equity to fulfill a funding need. What in theory should happen to the return required by equity holders if it chooses (a) debt and (b) equity?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 9.6. дёҖе®¶е…¬еҸёжӯЈеңЁе°қиҜ•еңЁеҸ‘иЎҢеҖәеҠЎиҝҳжҳҜеҸ‘иЎҢиӮЎзҘЁд№Ӣй—ҙеҒҡеҮәеҶіе®ҡжқҘж»Ўи¶іиһҚиө„йңҖжұӮгҖӮзҗҶи®әдёҠпјҢеҰӮжһңиӮЎдёңйҖүжӢ©пјҲaпјүеҖәеҠЎе’ҢпјҲbпјүиӮЎжқғпјҢиӮЎдёңжүҖиҰҒжұӮзҡ„еӣһжҠҘдјҡеҸ‘з”ҹд»Җд№ҲеҸҳеҢ–пјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
9.6.
If it chooses debt, the equity becomes more risky and the expected return of equity holders
increases. If it chooses equity, the equity becomes less risky and the expected return required by
equity holders goes down.
problems.explanationEn:
9.6.

equity holders goes down.

problems.promptEn: Practice 9.7. Explain the meaning of вҖңnettingвҖқ. Suppose no collateral is posted. Why does a netting agreement usually reduce
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 9.7. и§ЈйҮҠдёҖдёӢвҖңзҪ‘вҖқзҡ„ж„ҸжҖқгҖӮеҒҮи®ҫжІЎжңүеҸ‘еёғжҠөжҠје“ҒгҖӮдёәд»Җд№ҲеҮҖйўқз»“з®—еҚҸи®®йҖҡеёёеҸҜд»ҘйҷҚдҪҺеҸҢж–№зҡ„дҝЎз”ЁйЈҺйҷ©пјҹд»Җд№Ҳжғ…еҶөдёӢеҮҖйўқз»“з®—еҜ№дҝЎз”ЁйЈҺйҷ©жІЎжңүеҪұе“Қпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
9.7.
A netting agreement states that all transactions are considered to be a single transaction in the
event of a default. Transactions with a positive value are netted against transactions with a
negative value. This usually reduces exposure because a company cannot cherry pick which

transactions it will default on. Credit risk is not affected by netting when all transactions will
have a positive value at all times or when all transactions have a negative value at all times.
problems.explanationEn:
9.7.
A netting agreement states that all transactions are considered to be a single transaction in the
event of a default. Transactions with a positive value are netted against transactions with a
negative value. This usually reduces exposure because a company cannot cherry pick which

transactions it will default on. Credit risk is not affected by netting when all transactions will
have a positive value at all times or when all transactions have a negative value at all times.
problems.explanationZh:
9.7.

problems.promptEn: Practice 9.8. The average funding cost for a company is 5% per annum when the risk-free rate is 3%. The company is currently undertaking projects worth USD 9 million. It plans to increase its size by undertaking USD 1 million of risk-free projects. What would you expect to happen to its average funding cost?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 9.8. еҪ“ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ 3% ж—¶пјҢдёҖе®¶е…¬еҸёзҡ„е№іеқҮиһҚиө„жҲҗжң¬дёәжҜҸе№ҙ 5%гҖӮиҜҘе…¬еҸёзӣ®еүҚжӯЈеңЁиҝӣиЎҢд»·еҖј 900 дёҮзҫҺе…ғзҡ„йЎ№зӣ®пјҢе№¶и®ЎеҲ’еҶҚиҝӣиЎҢ 100 дёҮзҫҺе…ғзҡ„ж— йЈҺйҷ©йЎ№зӣ®жқҘжү©еӨ§и§„жЁЎгҖӮдҪ йў„и®Ўе®ғзҡ„е№іеқҮиһҚиө„жҲҗжң¬дјҡжҖҺж ·еҸҳеҢ–пјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
9.8.
The average funding cost should come down. The company will become less risky. Its average
funding cost should be a weighted average of 5% for the old projects and 3% for the new ones.
This is 0.9 Г— 5% + 0.1 Г— 3% or 4.8%.
problems.explanationEn:

problems.promptEn: Practice 10.5. вҖңEmployee stock options issued by a company are different from regular exchange- traded call options on the companyвҖҷs stock because they can affect the capital structure of the company.вҖқ Explain this statement.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 10.5. вҖңе…¬еҸёеҸ‘иЎҢзҡ„е‘ҳе·ҘиӮЎзҘЁжңҹжқғдёҺеёёи§„дәӨжҳ“жүҖдәӨжҳ“зҡ„е…¬еҸёиӮЎзҘЁзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғдёҚеҗҢпјҢеӣ дёәе®ғд»¬дјҡеҪұе“Қе…¬еҸёзҡ„иө„жң¬з»“жһ„гҖӮвҖқи§ЈйҮҠдёҖдёӢиҝҷдёӘиҜҙжі•гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
10.5
The exercise of employee stock options usually leads to new shares being issued by the
company and sold to the employee. This changes the amount of equity in the capital
structure. When a regular exchange-traded option is exercised, no new shares are issued and
the companyвҖҷs capital structure is not affected.
problems.explanationEn:
10.5

company and sold to the employee. This changes the amount of equity in the capital
structure. When a regular exchange-traded option is exercised, no new shares are issued and
the companyвҖҷs capital structure is not affected.

problems.promptEn: Practice 10.6. Suppose that a European call option to buy a share for USD 100.00 costs USD 5.00 and is held until maturity. Under what circumstances will the holder of the option make a profit? Under what circumstances will the option be exercised? Draw a diagram illustrating how the profit from a long position in the option depends on the stock price at maturity of the option.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 10.6. еҒҮи®ҫд»Ҙ \$100.00 costs \$5.00 зҡ„д»·ж јиҙӯд№°иӮЎзҘЁзҡ„ж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғе№¶жҢҒжңүиҮіеҲ°жңҹгҖӮжңҹжқғжҢҒжңүдәәеңЁд»Җд№Ҳжғ…еҶөдёӢдјҡиҺ·еҲ©пјҹд»Җд№Ҳжғ…еҶөдёӢдјҡиЎҢдҪҝйҖүжӢ©жқғпјҹз»ҳеҲ¶еӣҫиЎЁпјҢиҜҙжҳҺжңҹжқғеӨҡеӨҙеӨҙеҜёзҡ„еҲ©ж¶ҰеҰӮдҪ•еҸ–еҶідәҺжңҹжқғеҲ°жңҹж—¶зҡ„иӮЎд»·гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
10.6
Ignoring the time value of money, the holder of the option will make a profit if the stock
price at maturity of the option is greater than USD 105. This is because the payoff to the holder of
the option is, in these circumstances, greater than the USD 5 paid for the option. The option will
be exercised if the stock price at maturity is greater than USD 100. Note that if the stock price is
between USD 100 and USD 105, the option is exercised but the holder of the option takes a loss
overall. The profit from a long position is as shown in Figure S10.3.

Figure S10.3: Profit from long position in Problem 10.6

problems.promptEn: Practice 10.7. Suppose that a European put option to sell a share for USD 60 costs USD 8 and is held until maturity. Under what circumstances will the seller of the option (the party with the short position) make a profit? Under what circumstances will the option be exercised? Draw a diagram illustrating how the profit from a short position in the option depends on the stock price at maturity of the option.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 10.7. еҒҮи®ҫжңүдёҖд»Ҫж¬§ејҸзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғд»Ҙ\$60 costs \$8 зҡ„д»·ж јеҮәе”®дёҖиӮЎиӮЎзҘЁе№¶жҢҒжңүиҮіеҲ°жңҹгҖӮжңҹжқғзҡ„еҚ–ж–№пјҲз©әеӨҙж–№пјүеңЁд»Җд№Ҳжғ…еҶөдёӢдјҡиҺ·еҲ©пјҹд»Җд№Ҳжғ…еҶөдёӢдјҡиЎҢдҪҝйҖүжӢ©жқғпјҹз»ҳеҲ¶еӣҫиЎЁпјҢиҜҙжҳҺжңҹжқғз©әеӨҙеӨҙеҜёзҡ„еҲ©ж¶ҰеҰӮдҪ•еҸ–еҶідәҺжңҹжқғеҲ°жңҹж—¶зҡ„иӮЎд»·гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
10.7
Ignoring the time value of money, the seller of the option will make a profit if the stock price
at maturity is greater than USD 52.00. This is because the cost to the seller of the option is in
these circumstances less than the price received for the option. The option will be exercised if
the stock price at maturity is less than USD 60.00. Note that if the stock price is between USD 52.00
and USD 60.00, the seller of the option makes a profit even though the option is exercised. The
profit from the short position is as shown in Figure S10.4.

Figure S10.4: Profit from short position in Problem 10.7
problems.explanationEn:
10.7

and USD 60.00, the seller of the option makes a profit even though the option is exercised. The
profit from the short position is as shown in Figure S10.4.

Figure S10.4: Profit from short position in Problem 10.7

problems.promptEn: Practice 10.8. Consider the following portfolio: a newly entered-into long forward contract on an asset and a long position in a European put option on the asset with the same maturity as the forward contract and a strike price that is equal to the forward price of the asset at the time the portfolio is set up. Show that it has the same value as a European call option with the same strike price and maturity as the European put option. Deduce that a European put option has the same value as a European call option with the same strike price and maturity when the strike price for both options is the forward price.

problems.promptEn: Practice 10.9. A trader buys a call option with a strike price of USD 45 and a put option with a strike price of USD 40. Both options have the same maturity. The call costs USD 3 and the put costs USD 4. Draw a diagram showing the variation of the traderвҖҷs profit with the asset price.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 10.9. дәӨжҳ“иҖ…д№°е…ҘдёҖд»Ҫжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә 45 зҫҺе…ғзҡ„зңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғпјҢе№¶д№°е…ҘдёҖд»Ҫжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә 40 зҫҺе…ғзҡ„зңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғгҖӮдёӨд»Ҫжңҹжқғе…·жңүзӣёеҗҢзҡ„еҲ°жңҹж—ҘгҖӮзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғд»·ж јдёә 3 зҫҺе…ғпјҢзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғд»·ж јдёә 4 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮиҜ·з”»еҮәдәӨжҳ“иҖ…еҲ©ж¶ҰйҡҸиө„дә§д»·ж јеҸҳеҢ–зҡ„еӣҫгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
10.9
Let S_T be the asset price at maturity. The total option cost is USD 7. If S_T < 40, the put payoff is 40 - S_T and the call payoff is zero, so profit is 33 - S_T. If 40 <= S_T <= 45, neither option pays off, so profit is -7. If S_T > 45, the call payoff is S_T - 45 and the put payoff is zero, so profit is S_T - 52. The trader profits when S_T < 33 or S_T > 52. This strategy is a strangle.
problems.explanationEn:
10.9
Let S_T be the asset price at maturity. The total option cost is USD 7. If S_T < 40, the put payoff is 40 - S_T and the call payoff is zero, so profit is 33 - S_T. If 40 <= S_T <= 45, neither option pays off, so profit is -7. If S_T > 45, the call payoff is S_T - 45 and the put payoff is zero, so profit is S_T - 52. The trader profits when S_T < 33 or S_T > 52. This strategy is a strangle.

problems.promptEn: Practice 10.10. Explain why an American option is always worth at least as much as a European option on the same asset with the same strike price and exercise date.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 10.10. и§ЈйҮҠдёәд»Җд№ҲеҜ№дәҺе…·жңүзӣёеҗҢжү§иЎҢд»·ж је’ҢиЎҢжқғж—Ҙжңҹзҡ„еҗҢдёҖиө„дә§пјҢзҫҺејҸжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖје§Ӣз»ҲиҮіе°‘дёҺж¬§ејҸжңҹжқғдёҖж ·й«ҳгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
10.10
The holder of an American option has all the same rights as the holder of a European option
and more. It must therefore be worth at least as much. If it were not, an arbitrageur could
short the European option and take a long position in the American option.
problems.explanationEn:
10.10
The holder of an American option has all the same rights as the holder of a European option
and more. It must therefore be worth at least as much. If it were not, an arbitrageur could
short the European option and take a long position in the American option.
problems.explanationZh:
10.10

problems.promptEn: Practice 10.11. Explain why an American option is always worth at least as much as its intrinsic value.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 10.11. и§ЈйҮҠдёәд»Җд№ҲзҫҺејҸжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјжҖ»жҳҜиҮіе°‘дёҺе…¶еҶ…еңЁд»·еҖјдёҖж ·й«ҳгҖӮ жңҹжқғеёӮеңәжңәеҲ¶
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
10.11
The holder of an American option has the right to exercise it immediately. The American
option must therefore be worth at least as much as its intrinsic value. If it were not, an
arbitrageur could lock in a sure profit by buying the option and exercising it immediately.
problems.explanationEn:
10.11
The holder of an American option has the right to exercise it immediately. The American
option must therefore be worth at least as much as its intrinsic value. If it were not, an
arbitrageur could lock in a sure profit by buying the option and exercising it immediately.
problems.explanationZh:
10.11

problems.promptEn: Practice 10.12. The treasurer of a corporation is trying to choose between options and forward contracts to hedge the corporationвҖҷs foreign exchange risk. Discuss the advantages and disadvan- tages of each.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 10.12. дёҖе®¶е…¬еҸёзҡ„иҙўеҠЎдё»з®ЎиҜ•еӣҫеңЁжңҹжқғе’ҢиҝңжңҹеҗҲзәҰд№Ӣй—ҙиҝӣиЎҢйҖүжӢ©пјҢд»ҘеҜ№еҶІе…¬еҸёзҡ„еӨ–жұҮйЈҺйҷ©гҖӮи®Ёи®әеҗ„иҮӘзҡ„дјҳзӮ№е’ҢзјәзӮ№гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
10.12
Forward contracts lock in the exchange rate that will apply to a particular transaction in the
future. Options provide insurance that the exchange rate will not be worse than some level.
The advantage of a forward contract is that uncertainty is eliminated as far as possible. The
disadvantage is that the outcome with hedging can be significantly worse than the outcome
with no hedging. This disadvantage is not as marked with options. However, unlike forward
contracts, options involve an up-front cost.
problems.explanationEn:
10.12

future. Options provide insurance that the exchange rate will not be worse than some level.
The advantage of a forward contract is that uncertainty is eliminated as far as possible. The
disadvantage is that the outcome with hedging can be significantly worse than the outcome
with no hedging. This disadvantage is not as marked with options. However, unlike forward
contracts, options involve an up-front cost.

problems.promptEn: Practice 10.14. вҖңIf most of the call options on a stock are in the money, it is likely that the stock price has risen rapidly in the last few months.вҖқ Discuss this statement.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 10.14. вҖңеҰӮжһңдёҖеҸӘиӮЎзҘЁзҡ„еӨ§йғЁеҲҶзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғйғҪжҳҜе®һеҖјжңҹжқғпјҢйӮЈд№ҲиӮЎд»·еңЁиҝҮеҺ»еҮ дёӘжңҲеҫҲеҸҜиғҪдјҡиҝ…йҖҹдёҠж¶ЁгҖӮвҖқи®Ёи®әиҝҷдёӘиҜҙжі•гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.

problems.explanation:
10.14
The exchange has certain rules governing when trading in a new option is initiated. These

mean that the option is close-to-the-money when it is first traded. If all call options are in the
money, it is therefore likely that the stock price has increased since trading in the option
began.

problems.promptEn: Practice 10.15. What is the effect of an unexpected cash dividend on (a) a call option price and (b) a put option price?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 10.15. ж„ҸеӨ–зҺ°йҮ‘иӮЎжҒҜеҜ№ (a) зңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғд»·ж је’Ң (b) зңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғд»·ж јжңүдҪ•еҪұе“Қпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
10.15
An unexpected cash dividend would reduce the stock price on the ex-dividend date. This
stock price reduction would not be anticipated by option holders. As a result, there would be
a reduction in the value of a call option and an increase in the value of a put option. (Note
that the terms of an option are adjusted for cash dividends only in exceptional circumstances.)
problems.explanationEn:

that the terms of an option are adjusted for cash dividends only in exceptional circumstances.)

problems.promptEn: Practice 10.17. Explain why the market makerвҖҷs bidвҖ“ask spread represents a real cost to options investors.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 10.17. и§ЈйҮҠдёәд»Җд№ҲеҒҡеёӮе•Ҷзҡ„д№°еҚ–д»·е·®д»ЈиЎЁдәҶжңҹжқғжҠ•иө„иҖ…зҡ„е®һйҷ…жҲҗжң¬гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
10.17
A вҖңfairвҖқ price for the option can reasonably be assumed to be half way between the bid and
the ask price quoted by a market maker. An investor typically buys at the market makerвҖҷs ask
and sells at the market makerвҖҷs bid. Each time he or she does this, there is a hidden cost equal
to half the bid-ask spread.

Do the same for the September put options in Table 1.3. Assume in each case that the current mid market stock price is USD 316.00.

problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
10.19
For strike prices of 290, 300, 310, 320, 330, and 340, the intrinsic values of call options are
26, 16, 6, 0, 0, and 0. The mid-market values of the options are 39.875, 33.20, 26.80,
21.075, 16.05, and 11.675. The time values of the options are given by what is left from the
mid-market value after the intrinsic value has been subtracted. They are 13.875, 17.20, 20.80,
21.075, 16.05, and 11.675, respectively.
For strike prices of 290, 300, 310, 320, 330, and 340, the intrinsic values of put options are 0,
0, 0, 4, 14, and 24. The mid-market values of the options are 13.175, 16.35, 20.125, 24.425,
29.30, and 35.05. The time values of the options are given by what is left from the mid-
market value after the intrinsic value has been subtracted. They are 13.175, 16.35, 20.125,
15.30, and 11.05, respectively.
Note that for both puts and calls, the time value is greatest when the option is close to being
at-the-money.

problems.promptEn: Practice 10.20. A trader writes 5 naked put option contracts with each contract being on 100 shares. The option price is USD 10, the time to maturity is 6 months, and the strike price is USD 64. (a) What is the margin requirement if the stock price is USD 58? (b) How would the answer to (a) change if the rules for index options applied? (c) How would the answer to (a) change if the stock price were USD 70? (d) How would the answer to (a) change if the trader is buying instead of selling the options?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 10.20. дәӨжҳ“иҖ…еҚ–еҮә 5 д»ҪиЈёзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғеҗҲзәҰпјҢжҜҸд»ҪеҗҲзәҰеҢ…еҗ« 100 иӮЎгҖӮжңҹжқғд»·ж јдёә\$10, the time to maturity is 6 months, and the strike price is \$64гҖӮ (a) еҰӮжһңиӮЎд»·дёә\$58? (b) How would the answer to (a) change if the rules for index options applied? (c) How would the answer to (a) change if the stock price were \$70пјҢдҝқиҜҒйҮ‘иҰҒжұӮжҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹ (d) еҰӮжһңдәӨжҳ“иҖ…д№°е…ҘиҖҢдёҚжҳҜеҚ–еҮәжңҹжқғпјҢ(a) зҡ„зӯ”жЎҲдјҡеҰӮдҪ•еҸҳеҢ–пјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
10.20
(a) The margin requirement is the greater of 500Г—(10 + 0.2Г—58) = 10,800 and
500Г—(10+0.1Г—64) = 8,200. It is USD 10,800.

(b) The margin requirement is the greater of 500Г—(10+0.15Г—58) = 9,350 and
500Г—(10+0.1Г—64) = 8,200. It is USD 9,350.
(c) The margin requirement is the greater of 500Г—(10+0.2Г—70-6) = 9,000 and
500Г—(10+0.1Г—64) = 8,200. It is USD 9,000.
(d) No margin is required if the trader is buying.

  500 Г— (10 + 0.2 Г— 58) = 10,800 зҫҺе…ғ
  500 Г— (10 + 0.1 Г— 64) = 8,200 зҫҺе…ғ
еӣ жӯӨдҝқиҜҒйҮ‘иҰҒжұӮдёә 10,800 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮ

(b) иӢҘйҖӮз”ЁжҢҮж•°жңҹжқғи§„еҲҷпјҢдҝқиҜҒйҮ‘иҰҒжұӮдёәд»ҘдёӢдёӨдёӘеҖјдёӯзҡ„иҫғеӨ§иҖ…пјҡ
  500 Г— (10 + 0.15 Г— 58) = 9,350 зҫҺе…ғ
  500 Г— (10 + 0.1 Г— 64) = 8,200 зҫҺе…ғ
еӣ жӯӨдҝқиҜҒйҮ‘иҰҒжұӮдёә 9,350 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮ

(c) еҪ“иӮЎд»·дёә 70 зҫҺе…ғж—¶пјҢдҝқиҜҒйҮ‘иҰҒжұӮдёәд»ҘдёӢдёӨдёӘеҖјдёӯзҡ„иҫғеӨ§иҖ…пјҡ
  500 Г— (10 + 0.2 Г— 70 - 6) = 9,000 зҫҺе…ғ
  500 Г— (10 + 0.1 Г— 64) = 8,200 зҫҺе…ғ
еӣ жӯӨдҝқиҜҒйҮ‘иҰҒжұӮдёә 9,000 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮ

(d) еҰӮжһңдәӨжҳ“иҖ…жҳҜд№°е…ҘиҖҢйқһеҚ–еҮәжңҹжқғпјҢеҲҷдёҚйңҖиҰҒдҝқиҜҒйҮ‘гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 10.21. вҖңIf a company does not do better than its competitors but the stock market goes up, executives do very well from their stock options. This makes no sense.вҖқ Discuss this viewpoint. Can you think of alternatives to the usual employee stock option plan that take the viewpoint into account.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 10.21. вҖңеҰӮжһңдёҖе®¶е…¬еҸёзҡ„иЎЁзҺ°е№¶дёҚжҜ”з«һдәүеҜ№жүӢеҘҪпјҢдҪҶиӮЎеёӮдёҠж¶ЁпјҢй«ҳз®Ўд»¬дјҡд»Һд»–д»¬зҡ„иӮЎзҘЁжңҹжқғдёӯиҺ·еҫ—еҫҲеҘҪзҡ„иЎЁзҺ°гҖӮиҝҷжҳҜжІЎжңүж„Ҹд№үзҡ„гҖӮвҖқи®Ёи®әдёҖдёӢиҝҷдёӘи§ӮзӮ№гҖӮжӮЁиғҪжғіеҲ°иҖғиҷ‘еҲ°иҝҷдёҖи§ӮзӮ№зҡ„жҷ®йҖҡ

problems.explanation:
10.21
Executive stock option plans account for a high percentage of the total remuneration received
by executives. When the market is rising fast, many corporate executives do very well out of
their stock option plansвҖ”even when their company does worse than its competitors. Large
institutional investors have argued that executive stock options should be structured so that
the payoff depends on how the company has performed relative to an appropriate industry
index. In a regular executive stock option, the strike price is the stock price at the time the
option is issued. In the type of relative-performance stock option favored by institutional
investors, the strike price at time t is
0
0
t
S I
I where
0
S is the companyвҖҷs stock price at the
time the option is issued,
0I is the value of an equity index for the industry in which the
company operates at the time the option is issued, and
tI is the value of the index at time t . If
the companyвҖҷs performance equals the performance of the industry, the options are always at-
the-money. If the company outperforms the industry, the options become in the money. If the
company underperforms the industry, the options become out of the money. Note that a
relative performance stock option can provide a payoff when both the market and the
companyвҖҷs stock price decline.
Relative performance stock options clearly provide a better way of rewarding senior
management for superior performance. Some companies have argued that, if they introduce
relative performance options when their competitors do not, they will lose some of their top
management talent.

problems.promptEn: Practice 10.22. On July 20, 2004, Microsoft surprised the market by announcing a USD 3 dividend. The ex-dividend date was November 17, 2004, and the payment date was December 2, 2004. Its stock price at the time was about USD 28. It also changed the terms of its employee stock options so that each exercise price was adjusted downward to predividend exercise price * (closing price - USD 3.00) / closing price. The number of shares covered by each stock option outstanding was adjusted upward to number of shares predividend * closing price / (closing price - USD 3.00). Closing price means the official NASDAQ closing price of a share of Microsoft common stock on the last trading day before the ex-dividend date. Evaluate this adjustment.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 10.22. 2004 е№ҙ 7 жңҲ 20 ж—ҘпјҢеҫ®иҪҜе®ЈеёғжҙҫеҸ‘ 3 зҫҺе…ғиӮЎжҒҜпјҢд»ӨеёӮеңәж„ҸеӨ–гҖӮйҷӨжҒҜж—Ҙдёә 2004 е№ҙ 11 жңҲ 17 ж—ҘпјҢж”Ҝд»ҳж—Ҙдёә 2004 е№ҙ 12 жңҲ 2 ж—ҘгҖӮеҪ“ж—¶иӮЎд»·зәҰдёә 28 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮеҫ®иҪҜиҝҳи°ғж•ҙдәҶе‘ҳе·ҘиӮЎзҘЁжңҹжқғжқЎж¬ҫпјҡжҜҸдёӘиЎҢжқғд»·дёӢи°ғдёәвҖңйҷӨжҒҜеүҚиЎҢжқғд»· Г— (ж”¶зӣҳд»· - 3.00 зҫҺе…ғ) / ж”¶зӣҳд»·вҖқпјӣжҜҸд»ҪжңӘиЎҢжқғиӮЎзҘЁжңҹжқғиҰҶзӣ–зҡ„иӮЎзҘЁж•°дёҠи°ғдёәвҖңйҷӨжҒҜеүҚиӮЎзҘЁж•° Г— ж”¶зӣҳд»· / (ж”¶зӣҳд»· - 3.00 зҫҺе…ғ)вҖқгҖӮе…¶дёӯвҖңж”¶зӣҳд»·вҖқжҢҮйҷӨжҒҜж—ҘеүҚжңҖеҗҺдёҖдёӘдәӨжҳ“ж—Ҙеҫ®иҪҜжҷ®йҖҡиӮЎзҡ„ NASDAQ е®ҳж–№ж”¶зӣҳд»·гҖӮиҜ„д»·иҝҷз§Қи°ғж•ҙгҖӮ

problems.explanation:
10.22
Suppose that the closing stock price is USD 28 and an employee has 1,000 options with a strike
price of USD 24. MicrosoftвҖҷs adjustment involves changing the strike price to
24 25 28
21 4286
x
=
.
 and changing the number of options to 1,000Г—28/25 = 1,120. The
system used by exchanges would involve keeping the number of options the same and
reducing the strike price by USD 3 to USD 21.
The Microsoft adjustment is more complicated than that used by the exchange because it
requires a knowledge of the MicrosoftвҖҷs stock price immediately before the stock goes ex-
dividend. However, arguably it is a better adjustment than the one used by the exchange.
Before the adjustment, the employee has the right to pay USD 24,000 for Microsoft stock that is
worth USD 28,000. After the adjustment, the employee also has the option to pay USD 24,000 for
Microsoft stock worth USD 28,000. Under the adjustment rule used by exchanges, the employee
would have the right to buy stock worth USD 25,000 for USD 21,000. If the volatility of Microsoft
remains the same, this is a less valuable option.
One complication here is that MicrosoftвҖҷs volatility does not remain the same. It can be
expected to go up because some cash (a zero risk asset) has been transferred to shareholders.
The employees therefore have the same basic option as before but the volatility of Microsoft
can be expected to increase. The employees are slightly better off because the value of an
option increases with volatility.

\(24 \times \frac{28 - 3}{28} = 24 \times \frac{25}{28} = 21.4286\) зҫҺе…ғпјҢжңҹжқғд»Ҫж•°и°ғж•ҙдёә

еҫ®иҪҜзҡ„и°ғж•ҙжҜ”дәӨжҳ“жүҖз”Ёзҡ„ж–№жі•жӣҙеӨҚжқӮпјҢеӣ дёәе®ғйңҖиҰҒзҹҘйҒ“иӮЎзҘЁйҷӨжҒҜж—ҘеүҚдёҖдәӨжҳ“ж—Ҙзҡ„еҫ®иҪҜиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јгҖӮз„¶иҖҢпјҢд»ҺдёҖе®ҡи§’еәҰзңӢпјҢиҝҷз§Қи°ғж•ҙжҜ”дәӨжҳ“жүҖзҡ„ж–№жі•жӣҙеҗҲзҗҶгҖӮ
и°ғж•ҙеүҚпјҢе‘ҳе·Ҙжңүжқғд»Ҙ 24,000 зҫҺе…ғпјҲиЎҢжқғд»· 24 зҫҺе…ғ Г— 1,000 д»Ҫпјүд№°е…Ҙд»·еҖј 28,000 зҫҺе…ғзҡ„еҫ®иҪҜиӮЎзҘЁгҖӮи°ғж•ҙеҗҺпјҢе‘ҳе·Ҙд»Қз„¶жңүжқғд»Ҙ 24,000 зҫҺе…ғд№°е…Ҙд»·еҖј 28,000 зҫҺе…ғзҡ„иӮЎзҘЁгҖӮиӢҘжҢүдәӨжҳ“жүҖзҡ„ж–№жі•пјҢе‘ҳе·Ҙжңүжқғд»Ҙ 21,000 зҫҺе…ғд№°е…Ҙд»·еҖј 25,000 зҫҺе…ғзҡ„иӮЎзҘЁгҖӮеҰӮжһңеҫ®иҪҜзҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮдҝқжҢҒдёҚеҸҳпјҢдәӨжҳ“жүҖж–№жі•еҫ—еҲ°зҡ„жңҹжқғд»·еҖјиҫғдҪҺгҖӮ
иҝҷйҮҢдёҖдёӘеӨҚжқӮзӮ№еңЁдәҺеҫ®иҪҜзҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮдёҚдјҡдҝқжҢҒдёҚеҸҳгҖӮз”ұдәҺйғЁеҲҶзҺ°йҮ‘пјҲж— йЈҺйҷ©иө„дә§пјүеҲҶз»ҷдәҶиӮЎдёңпјҢе…¶иӮЎд»·жіўеҠЁзҺҮйў„и®ЎдјҡдёҠеҚҮгҖӮеӣ жӯӨе‘ҳе·Ҙе®һйҷ…жҢҒжңүзҡ„жңҹжқғжң¬иҙЁдёҠиҝҳжҳҜзӣёеҗҢзҡ„пјҢдҪҶеҫ®иҪҜиӮЎд»·зҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮеҸҜиғҪеўһеҠ гҖӮжңҹжқғд»·еҖјйҡҸжіўеҠЁзҺҮеўһеҠ иҖҢжҸҗеҚҮпјҢе‘ҳе·Ҙеӣ иҖҢз•ҘдёәеҸ—зӣҠгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 11.4. вҖңThe early exercise of an American put is a trade-off between the time value of money and the insurance value of a put.вҖқ Explain this statement.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 11.4. вҖңзҫҺејҸзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғзҡ„ж—©жңҹиЎҢжқғжҳҜиҙ§еёҒж—¶й—ҙд»·еҖјдёҺзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғдҝқйҷ©д»·еҖјд№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„жқғиЎЎгҖӮвҖқи§ЈйҮҠдёҖдёӢиҝҷдёӘиҜҙжі•гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
11.4
An American put when held in conjunction with the underlying stock provides insurance. It
guarantees that the stock can be sold for the strike price, K. If the put is exercised early, the
insurance ceases. However, the option holder receives the strike price immediately and is
able to earn interest on it between the time of the early exercise and the expiration date.
problems.explanationEn:
11.4

guarantees that the stock can be sold for the strike price, K. If the put is exercised early, the
insurance ceases. However, the option holder receives the strike price immediately and is
able to earn interest on it between the time of the early exercise and the expiration date.

problems.promptEn: Practice 11.5. Why is an American call option on a dividend-paying stock always worth at least as much as its intrinsic value. Is the same true of a European call option? Explain your answer.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 11.5. дёәд»Җд№Ҳж”Ҝд»ҳиӮЎжҒҜиӮЎзҘЁзҡ„зҫҺејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјжҖ»жҳҜиҮіе°‘дёҺе…¶еҶ…еңЁд»·еҖјдёҖж ·й«ҳгҖӮж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғд№ҹжҳҜеҰӮжӯӨеҗ—пјҹи§ЈйҮҠдҪ зҡ„зӯ”жЎҲгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
11.5
An American call option can be exercised at any time. If it is exercised, its holder gets the
intrinsic value. It follows that an American call option must be worth at least its intrinsic
value. A European call option can be worth less than its intrinsic value. Consider, for
example, the situation where a stock is expected to provide a very high dividend during the
life of an option. The price of the stock will decline as a result of the dividend. Because the
European option can be exercised only after the dividend has been paid, its value may be less
than the intrinsic value today.
problems.explanationEn:
11.5
An American call option can be exercised at any time. If it is exercised, its holder gets the
intrinsic value. It follows that an American call option must be worth at least its intrinsic
value. A European call option can be worth less than its intrinsic value. Consider, for
example, the situation where a stock is expected to provide a very high dividend during the
life of an option. The price of the stock will decline as a result of the dividend. Because the
European option can be exercised only after the dividend has been paid, its value may be less
than the intrinsic value today.
problems.explanationZh:
11.5
зҫҺејҸзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғеҸҜд»ҘеңЁд»»дҪ•ж—¶й—ҙиЎҢжқғгҖӮеҰӮжһңиЎҢжқғпјҢе…¶жҢҒжңүдәәеҫ—еҲ°зҡ„жҳҜжңҹжқғзҡ„еҶ…еңЁд»·еҖјгҖӮеӣ жӯӨпјҢзҫҺејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјеҝ…йЎ»иҮіе°‘зӯүдәҺе…¶еҶ…еңЁд»·еҖјгҖӮж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјеҸҜиғҪдҪҺдәҺе…¶еҶ…еңЁд»·еҖјгҖӮдҫӢеҰӮпјҢиҖғиҷ‘жҹҗиӮЎзҘЁеңЁжңҹжқғжңүж•ҲжңҹеҶ…йў„жңҹе°Ҷж”Ҝд»ҳжһҒй«ҳзҡ„иӮЎжҒҜпјҢиҝҷдјҡеҜјиҮҙиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јеӣ иӮЎжҒҜжҙҫеҸ‘иҖҢдёӢи·ҢгҖӮз”ұдәҺж¬§ејҸжңҹжқғеҸӘиғҪеңЁиӮЎжҒҜж”Ҝд»ҳд№ӢеҗҺиЎҢжқғпјҢе…¶д»·еҖјеҸҜиғҪдҪҺдәҺеҪ“еүҚзҡ„еҶ…еңЁд»·еҖјгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 11.6. The price of a non-dividend-paying stock is USD 19 and the price of a 3-month European call option on the stock with a strike price of USD 20 is USD 1. The risk-free rate is 4% per annum. What is the price of a 3-month European put option with a strike price of USD 20?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 11.6. дёҖеҸӘдёҚж”Ҝд»ҳиӮЎжҒҜзҡ„иӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јдёә 19 зҫҺе…ғпјҢиҜҘиӮЎзҘЁжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә 20 зҫҺе…ғгҖҒжңҹйҷҗдёә 3 дёӘжңҲзҡ„ж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғд»·ж јдёә 1 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёәе№ҙеҢ– 4%гҖӮжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә 20 зҫҺе…ғгҖҒжңҹйҷҗдёә 3 дёӘжңҲзҡ„ж¬§ејҸзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғд»·ж јжҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
11.6
Here c=1, T=0.25, S_0=19, K=20, and r=0.04. Put-call parity gives p = c + K exp(-rT) - S_0. Therefore p = 1 + 20 exp(-0.04 * 0.25) - 19 = 1.80. The European put price is USD 1.80.
problems.explanationEn:
11.6

иӢҘжңҹжқғеҲ°жңҹж—¶иӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јй«ҳдәҺ60зҫҺе…ғпјҢеҘ—еҲ©иҖ…иЎҢжқғд»Ҙ60зҫҺе…ғд№°еӣһиӮЎзҘЁпјҢе№ід»“еҚ–з©әеӨҙеҜёгҖӮ60зҫҺе…ғзҡ„зҺ°еҖјд»ҚжҳҜ57.65зҫҺе…ғпјҢиӮЎжҒҜзҺ°еҖјдёә0.79зҫҺе…ғгҖӮйҖҡиҝҮеҚ–з©әеҸҠиЎҢжқғзӯ–з•ҘпјҢеҘ—еҲ©иҖ…зҺ°еҖјж”¶зӣҠд»ҚжҳҜ

еү”йҷӨиҙӯд№°жңҹжқғжҲҗжң¬5зҫҺе…ғпјҢеҮҖж”¶зӣҠдёә0.56зҫҺе…ғгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 11.11. A 1-month European put option on a non-dividend-paying stock is currently selling for USD 2.50. The stock price is USD 47, the strike price is USD 50, and the risk-free interest rate is 6% per annum. What opportunities are there for an arbitrageur?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 11.11. дёҖд»ҪдёҚжҙҫжҒҜиӮЎзҘЁзҡ„ 1 дёӘжңҲжңҹж¬§ејҸзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғзӣ®еүҚе”®д»· \$2.50. The stock price is \$47пјҢжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә 50 зҫҺе…ғпјҢж— йЈҺйҷ©е№ҙеҲ©зҺҮдёә 6%гҖӮеҘ—еҲ©иҖ…жңүе“Әдәӣжңәдјҡпјҹ

the condition in equation (11.5) is violated. An arbitrageur should borrow USD 49.50 at 6% for
one month, buy the stock, and buy the put option. This generates a profit in all circumstances.
If the stock price is above USD 50 in one month, the option expires worthless, but the stock can
be sold for at least USD 50. A sum of USD 50 received in one month has a present value of USD 49.75
today. The strategy therefore generates profit with a present value of at least USD 0.25.
If the stock price is below USD 50 in one month the put option is exercised and the stock owned
is sold for exactly USD 50 (or USD 49.75 in present value terms). The trading strategy therefore
generates a profit of exactly USD 0.25 in present value terms.

problems.promptEn: Practice 11.12. Give an intuitive explanation of why the early exercise of an American put becomes more attractive as the risk-free rate increases and volatility decreases.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 11.12. зӣҙи§Ӯең°и§ЈйҮҠдёәд»Җд№ҲйҡҸзқҖж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёҠеҚҮе’ҢжіўеҠЁжҖ§дёӢйҷҚпјҢж—©жңҹиЎҢдҪҝзҫҺејҸзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғеҸҳеҫ—жӣҙе…·еҗёеј•еҠӣгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
11.12
The early exercise of an American put is attractive when the interest earned on the strike
price is greater than the insurance element lost. When interest rates increase, the value of the
interest earned on the strike price increases making early exercise more attractive. When

volatility decreases, the insurance element is less valuable. Again this makes early exercise
more attractive.
problems.explanationEn:
11.12
The early exercise of an American put is attractive when the interest earned on the strike
price is greater than the insurance element lost. When interest rates increase, the value of the
interest earned on the strike price increases making early exercise more attractive. When

volatility decreases, the insurance element is less valuable. Again this makes early exercise
more attractive.

c - p = S - Ke^{-rT} - D

жҲ–иҖ…зӯүд»·ең°

p = c + Ke^{-rT} + D - S

еңЁжң¬йўҳдёӯпјҢ

p = 2 + 30e^{-0.10 	imes 6/12} + 0.5e^{-0.10 	imes 2/12} + 0.5e^{-0.10 	imes 5/12} - 29

problems.explanationZh:
11.14
еҰӮжһңж¬§ејҸзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғд»·ж јдёә 3.00 зҫҺе…ғпјҢзӣёеҜ№дәҺзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғд»·ж јиҖҢиЁҖиҝҮй«ҳгҖӮеҘ—еҲ©иҖ…еә”д№°е…ҘзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғпјҢеҗҢж—¶еҚ–з©әзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғе’ҢеҚ–з©әиӮЎзҘЁгҖӮиҜҘз»„еҗҲдјҡеҮҖз”ҹжҲҗзҺ°йҮ‘пјҡ

3 (еҚ–з©әзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғ) + 2 (д№°е…ҘзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„жҲҗжң¬ж”ҜеҮә) + 29 (еҚ–з©әиӮЎзҘЁиҺ·еҫ—зҡ„зҺ°йҮ‘) - 30 (еӣ жү§иЎҢд»·ж јиҖҢдә§з”ҹзҡ„иҙЈд»») = 4

дҪҶжӣҙеҮҶзЎ®ең°зңӢпјҢж №жҚ®зңӢж¶ЁзңӢи·Ңд»·е·®е…ізі»пјҢе®һйҷ…еҲқе§ӢзҺ°йҮ‘жөҒдёәпјҡ

зҺ°еҖјеҮҖзҺ°йҮ‘жөҒ = 3 (еҚ–з©әзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғеҫ—еҲ°зҺ°йҮ‘) - 2 (д№°е…ҘзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғд»ҳеҮәзҺ°йҮ‘) + 29 (еҚ–з©әиӮЎзҘЁеҫ—еҲ°зҺ°йҮ‘) - 30 (иЎҢжқғд»·жҠҳзҺ°иҙҹеҖә) > 0

иҝҷз¬”зҺ°йҮ‘д»Ҙ 10%зҡ„ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮжҠ•иө„гҖӮ

ж— и®әжңӘжқҘиӮЎд»·еҰӮдҪ•еҸҳеҢ–пјҢеҘ—еҲ©иҖ…йғҪиғҪй”Ғе®ҡдёҖз¬”еҲ©ж¶ҰпјҢе…¶зҺ°еҖјдёә 0.49 зҫҺе…ғпјҢи®Ўз®—еҰӮдёӢпјҡ

еҲ©ж¶ҰзҺ°еҖј = зңӢи·Ңд»·ж ј - и®Ўз®—еҮәзҡ„еҗҲзҗҶзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғд»·ж ј = 3.00 - 2.51 = 0.49 зҫҺе…ғ

иӢҘ6дёӘжңҲеҗҺиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јй«ҳдәҺ30зҫҺе…ғпјҢзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғиў«жү§иЎҢпјҢзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғиҝҮжңҹж— д»·еҖјгҖӮзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғе…Ғи®ёжҠ•иө„иҖ…д»ҘиЎҢжқғд»·30зҫҺе…ғиҙӯе…ҘиӮЎзҘЁпјҢе…¶зҺ°еҖјдёәпјҡ

30e^{-0.10 	imes 6/12} = 30 e^{-0.05} вүҲ 28.54 зҫҺе…ғ

еҚ–з©әиӮЎзҘЁйңҖиҰҒж”Ҝд»ҳдёӨж¬ЎеҲҶзәўзҡ„зҺ°еҖјжҲҗжң¬пјҡ

0.5 e^{-0.10 	imes 2/12} + 0.5 e^{-0.10 	imes 5/12} = 0.4917 + 0.4796 = 0.9713 зҫҺе…ғ

еӣ жӯӨеҮҖеҲ©ж¶Ұдёәпјҡ

30 - 28.54 - 0.97 вүҲ 0.49 зҫҺе…ғзҡ„еҲ©ж¶ҰпјҲзҺ°еҖјпјү

иӢҘ6дёӘжңҲеҗҺиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јдҪҺдәҺ30зҫҺе…ғпјҢзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғиў«жү§иЎҢпјҢзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғж— д»·еҖјгҖӮеҚ–з©әзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғеҜјиҮҙеҝ…йЎ»д»Ҙ30зҫҺе…ғд№°е…ҘиӮЎзҘЁпјҢзҺ°еҖјдёә 28.54 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮеҚ–з©әиӮЎзҘЁйңҖж”Ҝд»ҳеҲҶзәўзҺ°еҖј0.97зҫҺе…ғпјҢеӣ жӯӨеҲ©ж¶Ұд»ҚзәҰдёә0.49зҫҺе…ғгҖӮ

з»јдёҠжүҖиҝ°пјҢиҜҘд»·ж јз»“жһ„еӯҳеңЁеҘ—еҲ©жңәдјҡпјҢеёӮеңәеә”дҝ®жӯЈзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғд»·ж јдҪҝе…¶дёҚй«ҳдәҺеҗҲзҗҶд»·ж ј 2.51 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮ

\[

\]

\[

\[

\[

\[
e^{-0.02} \approx 0.9802
\]

еӣ жӯӨпјҡ

\[

\]

з§»йЎ№еҫ—еҲ°пјҡ

\[

\]

\[
2.406 \leq P \leq 3

дёӨз§Қжңҹжқғе…·жңүзӣёеҗҢзҡ„иЎҢжқғд»·е’ҢеҲ°жңҹж—ҘгҖӮеҒҮи®ҫжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲ I дёӯзҡ„зҺ°йҮ‘д»Ҙж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮжҠ•иө„гҖӮеҰӮжһңзҫҺејҸзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғжңӘиў«жҸҗеүҚиЎҢдҪҝпјҢеҲҷжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲ J еҲ°жңҹж—¶д»·еҖјдёә

problems.promptEn: Practice 11.19. Consider a 5-year call option on a non-dividend-paying stock granted to employees. The option can be exercised at any time after the end of the first year. Unlike a regular exchange-traded call option, the employee stock option cannot be sold. What is the likely impact of this restriction on the early-exercise decision?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 11.19. иҖғиҷ‘еҗ‘е‘ҳе·ҘжҺҲдәҲ 5 е№ҙжңҹдёҚж”Ҝд»ҳиӮЎжҒҜиӮЎзҘЁзҡ„зңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғгҖӮиҜҘйҖүжӢ©жқғеҸҜд»ҘеңЁз¬¬дёҖе№ҙз»“жқҹеҗҺзҡ„д»»дҪ•ж—¶й—ҙиЎҢдҪҝгҖӮдёҺеёёи§„зҡ„дәӨжҳ“жүҖдәӨжҳ“зңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғдёҚеҗҢпјҢ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
0
11.19
An employee stock option may be exercised early because the employee needs cash or
because he or she is uncertain about the companyвҖҷs future prospects. Regular call options can
be sold in the market in either of these two situations, but employee stock options cannot be
sold. In theory, an employee can short the companyвҖҷs stock as an alternative to exercising. In
practice, this is not usually encouraged and may even be illegal for senior managers. These
points are discussed further in Chapter 16.
problems.explanationEn:

problems.promptEn: Practice 11.20. Use the software DerivaGem to verify that Figures 11.1 and 11.2 are correct.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 11.20. дҪҝз”ЁDerivaGemиҪҜд»¶йӘҢиҜҒеӣҫ11.1е’Ң11.2жҳҜеҗҰжӯЈзЎ®гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
11.20
The graphs can be produced from the first worksheet in DerivaGem. Select Equity as the
Underlying Type. Select BlackвҲ’ScholesвҲ’European as the Option Type. Input stock price as
50, volatility as 30%, risk-free rate as 5%, time to exercise as 1 year, and exercise price as 50.
Leave the dividend table blank because we are assuming no dividends. Select the button
corresponding to call. Do not select the implied volatility button. Hit the Enter key and click
on calculate. DerivaGem will show the price of the option as 7.15562248. Move to the Graph
Results on the right hand side of the worksheet. Enter Option Price for the vertical axis and
Asset price for the horizontal axis. Choose the minimum strike price value as 10 (software
will not accept 0) and the maximum strike price value as 100. Hit Enter and click on Draw
Graph. This will produce Figure 11.1a. Figures 11.1c, 11.1e, 11.2a, and 11.2c can be
produced similarly by changing the horizontal axis. By selecting put instead of call and
recalculating the rest of the figures can be produced. You are encouraged to experiment with
this worksheet. Try different parameter values and different types of options.

problems.promptEn: Practice 11.21. What is the impact (if any) of negative interest rates on: (a) The putвҖ“call parity result for European options (b) The result that American call options on non-dividend-paying stocks should never be exercised early (c) The result that American put options on non-dividend-paying stocks should some- times be exercised early. Assume that holding cash earning zero interest is not possible.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 11.21. иҙҹеҲ©зҺҮеҜ№д»ҘдёӢж–№йқўжңүдҪ•еҪұе“ҚпјҲеҰӮжһңжңүзҡ„иҜқпјүпјҡ (a) ж¬§ејҸжңҹжқғзҡ„зңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғдёҺзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғе№ід»·з»“жһң (b) йқһиӮЎжҒҜж”Ҝд»ҳиӮЎзҘЁзҡ„зҫҺејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғж°ёиҝңдёҚеә”жҸҗеүҚиЎҢдҪҝзҡ„з»“жһң (c) йқһиӮЎжҒҜж”Ҝд»ҳиӮЎзҘЁзҡ„зҫҺејҸзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғжңүж—¶еә”жҸҗеүҚиЎҢдҪҝзҡ„з»“жһңгҖӮ еҒҮи®ҫжҢҒжңүзҺ°йҮ‘иөҡеҸ–йӣ¶еҲ©жҒҜжҳҜдёҚеҸҜиғҪзҡ„гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 11.22. Calls were traded on exchanges before puts. During the period of time when calls were traded but puts were not traded, how would you create a European put option on a non- dividend-paying stock synthetically.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 11.22. зңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғе…ҲдәҺзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғеңЁдәӨжҳ“жүҖиҝӣиЎҢдәӨжҳ“гҖӮеңЁзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғдәӨжҳ“дҪҶзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғжІЎжңүдәӨжҳ“зҡ„ж—¶жңҹпјҢеҰӮдҪ•з»јеҗҲеҲӣе»әдёҚж”Ҝд»ҳиӮЎжҒҜиӮЎзҘЁзҡ„ж¬§ејҸзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
11.22
Because no dividends are paid, the call can be regarded as a European call. PutвҲ’call parity
can be used to create a European put from the call. A European put plus the stock equals a
European call plus the present value of the strike price when both the call and the put have
the same strike price and maturity date. A European put can be created by buying the call,
shorting the stock, and keeping an amount of cash that when invested at the risk-free rate will
grow to be sufficient to exercise the call. If the stock price is above the strike price, the call is

exercised and the short position is closed out for no net payoff. If the stock price is below the
strike price, the call is not exercised and the short position is closed out for a gain equal to the
put payoff.

problems.promptEn: Practice 11.23. The prices of European call and put options on a non-dividend-paying stock with an expiration date in 12 months and a strike price of USD 120 are USD 20 and USD 5, respectively. The current stock price is USD 130. What is the implied risk-free rate?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 11.23. еҲ°жңҹж—Ҙдёә 12 дёӘжңҲгҖҒжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә\$120 are \$20 е’Ң\$5, respectively. The current stock price is \$130 зҡ„дёҚж”Ҝд»ҳиӮЎжҒҜиӮЎзҘЁзҡ„ж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғе’ҢзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·ж јгҖӮйҡҗеҗ«ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮжҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
11.23
From putвҲ’call parity
20 + 120e-rГ—1 = 5 + 130
Solving this
e-r = 115/120
so that r = вҲ’ ln(115/120) = 0.0426 or 4.26%

11.24
еҰӮжһңзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғд»·еҖјдёә3зҫҺе…ғпјҢж №жҚ®зңӢж¶ЁзңӢи·Ңе№ід»·е…ізі»пјҲput-call parityпјүпјҢзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјеә”дёә

\[
\text{зңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғд»·еҖј} = 3 - 19e^{-0.1 \times \frac{1}{12}} + 20e^{-0.1 \times \frac{3}{12}} - 1
\]

и®Ўз®—еҗҺпјҢжӯӨд»·еҖјеӨ§дәҺ3зҫҺе…ғгҖӮеӣ жӯӨпјҢзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғзӣёиҫғдәҺзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғиў«дҪҺдј°дәҶгҖӮжӯЈзЎ®зҡ„еҘ—еҲ©зӯ–з•ҘжҳҜд№°е…ҘзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғпјҢд№°е…ҘиӮЎзҘЁпјҢеҗҢж—¶еҚ–з©әзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғгҖӮиҝҷж ·жҲҗжң¬дёә19зҫҺе…ғгҖӮиӢҘдёүдёӘжңҲеҗҺиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж ји¶…иҝҮ20зҫҺе…ғпјҢзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғдјҡиў«иЎҢжқғпјӣиӢҘдҪҺдәҺ20зҫҺе…ғпјҢеҲҷиЎҢдҪҝзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғгҖӮж— и®әе“Әз§Қжғ…еҶөпјҢеҘ—еҲ©иҖ…еқҮиғҪд»Ҙ20зҫҺе…ғд»·ж јеҚ–еҮәиӮЎзҘЁпјҢе№¶еңЁдёҖдёӘжңҲеҗҺиҺ·еҫ—1зҫҺе…ғзҡ„иӮЎжҒҜгҖӮеҘ—еҲ©иҖ…зҡ„ж”¶зӣҠзҺ°еҖјдёә

\[
\quad 3 + 19e^{-0.1 \times \frac{3}{12}} - 20 e^{-0.1 \times \frac{3}{12}} - 1 e^{-0.1 \times \frac{1}{12}} > 0
\]

еӣ иҖҢеӯҳеңЁеҘ—еҲ©жңәдјҡгҖӮ

11.25
иҖғиҷ‘дёҖдёӘжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲпјҢе…¶дёӯеӨҡеӨҙдёҖд»Ҫжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә K_1 зҡ„жңҹжқғпјҢеӨҡеӨҙдёҖд»Ҫжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә K_3 зҡ„жңҹжқғпјҢеҸҠз©әеӨҙдёӨд»Ҫжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә K_2 зҡ„жңҹжқғгҖӮиҜҘжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲзҡ„д»·еҖјеңЁжңҹжқғеҲ°жңҹж—¶еҸҜеҲҶеӣӣз§Қжғ…еҶөи®Ўз®—пјҡ

еҪ“ S_T \leq K_1 ж—¶пјҢжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲд»·еҖјдёәпјҡ
0 = 0 + 0 - 0

еҪ“ K_1 < S_T \leq K_2 ж—¶пјҢжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲд»·еҖјдёәпјҡ
S_T - K_1 + 0 - 2\times 0 = S_T - K_1

еҪ“ K_2 < S_T \leq K_3 ж—¶пјҢжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲд»·еҖјдёәпјҡ
S_T - K_1 + 0 - 2(S_T - K_2) = S_T - K_1 - 2 S_T + 2 K_2 = -S_T + 2K_2 - K_1

еҪ“ S_T > K_3 ж—¶пјҢжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲд»·еҖјдёәпјҡ
(S_T - K_1) + (S_T - K_3) - 2(S_T - K_2) = S_T - K_1 + S_T - K_3 - 2 S_T + 2 K_2 = (S_T - K_1 + S_T - K_3 - 2 S_T) + 2 K_2 = -K_1 - K_3 + 2 K_2

жіЁж„Ҹйўҳзӣ®дёӯеҒҮи®ҫ K_3 - K_2 = K_2 - K_1пјҢеҚі K_3 + K_1 = 2 K_2пјҢеӣ жӯӨжңҖеҗҺдёҖз§Қжғ…еҶөдёӢз»„еҗҲд»·еҖјдёә0гҖӮ

з»јдёҠпјҢжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲд»·еҖјеңЁд»»дҪ•жғ…еҶөдёӢеқҮеӨ§дәҺжҲ–зӯүдәҺ0гҖӮж №жҚ®ж— еҘ—еҲ©еҺҹеҲҷпјҢиҜҘз»„еҗҲзҡ„еҲқе§Ӣд»·еҖјеҝ…з„¶еӨ§дәҺзӯүдәҺ0пјҢеӣ жӯӨпјҡ
c_1 + c_3 - 2 c_2 \geq 0
еҢ–з®Җеҫ—пјҡ
c_2 \leq \frac{c_1 + c_3}{2}

йңҖиҰҒзү№еҲ«жҢҮеҮәзҡ„жҳҜпјҢжңүдәӣеӯҰз”ҹдјҡй”ҷиҜҜең°е°қиҜ•йҖҡиҝҮеҮҸеҺ»дёҚзӯүејҸдёӨдҫ§жқҘиҜҒжҳҺжӯӨз»“и®әпјҢдҪҶиҝҷжҳҜдёҚжӯЈзЎ®зҡ„пјҢеӣ дёәдёҚзӯүејҸд№Ӣй—ҙдёҚиғҪзӣҙжҺҘзӣёеҮҸгҖӮдҫӢеҰӮпјҢ9 > 8 дё” 5 > 2пјҢдҪҶдёҚд»ЈиЎЁ 9 - 5 > 8 - 2гҖӮ

еӣ жӯӨпјҢйҖҡиҝҮж— еҘ—еҲ©жһ„йҖ дёҠиҝ°з»„еҗҲеҸҜеҫ—еҮәжңҹжқғд»·ж јзҡ„еҮёжҖ§дёҚзӯүејҸгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 12.4. Explain how an aggressive bear spread can be created using put options.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 12.4. и§ЈйҮҠеҰӮдҪ•дҪҝз”ЁзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғеҲӣе»әжҝҖиҝӣзҡ„зҶҠеёӮд»·е·®гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
12.4
An aggressive bull spread using call options is discussed in the text. Both of the options used
have relatively high strike prices. Similarly, an aggressive bear spread can be created using
put options. Both of the options should be out of the money (i.e., they should have relatively
low strike prices). The spread then costs very little to set up because both of the puts are
worth close to zero. In most circumstances, the spread will provide zero payoff. However,
there is a small chance that the stock price will fall fast so that on expiration both options will
be in the money. The spread then provides a payoff equal to the difference between the two
strike prices,
2

problems.promptEn: Practice 12.5. Suppose that put options on a stock with strike prices USD 30 and USD 35 cost USD 4 and USD 7, respectively. How can the options be used to create (a) a bull spread and (b) a bear spread? Construct a table that shows the profit and payoff for both spreads.

problems.promptEn: Practice 12.9. An investor believes that there will be a big jump in a stock price, but is uncertain as to the direction. Identify six different strategies the investor can follow and explain the differences among them.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 12.9. жҠ•иө„иҖ…и®ӨдёәиӮЎд»·е°ҶдјҡеӨ§е№…дёҠж¶ЁпјҢдҪҶдёҚзЎ®е®ҡж–№еҗ‘гҖӮзЎ®е®ҡжҠ•иө„иҖ…еҸҜд»ҘйҒөеҫӘзҡ„е…ӯз§ҚдёҚеҗҢзӯ–з•Ҙе№¶и§ЈйҮҠе®ғд»¬д№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„е·®ејӮгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
12.9
Possible strategies are:

Strangle
Straddle
Strip
Strap
Reverse calendar spread
Reverse butterfly spread

The strategies all provide positive profits when there are large stock price moves. A strangle
is less expensive than a straddle, but requires a bigger move in the stock price in order to
provide a positive profit. Strips and straps are more expensive than straddles but provide
bigger profits in certain circumstances. A strip will provide a bigger profit when there is a
large downward stock price move. A strap will provide a bigger profit when there is a large
upward stock price move. In the case of strangles, straddles, strips and straps, the profit
increases as the size of the stock price movement increases. By contrast, in a reverse calendar
spread and a reverse butterfly spread, there is a maximum potential profit regardless of the
size of the stock price movement.

problems.promptEn: Practice 12.12. What is the result if the strike price of the put is higher than the strike price of the call in a strangle?

12.12
The result is shown in Figure S12.1. The profit pattern from a long position in a call and a put
when the put has a higher strike price than a call is much the same as when the call has a
higher strike price than the put. Both the initial investment and the final payoff are much
higher in the first case

problems.promptEn: Practice 12.13. A foreign currency is currently worth USD 0.64. A 1-year butterfly spread is set up using European call options with strike prices of USD 0.60, USD 0.65, and USD 0.70. The risk-free interest rates in the United States and the foreign country are 5% and 4% respectively, and the volatility of the exchange rate is 15%. Use the DerivaGem software to calculate the cost of setting up the butterfly spread position. Show that the cost is the same if European put options are used instead of European call options.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 12.13. зӣ®еүҚеӨ–еёҒд»·еҖј\$0.64. A 1-year butterfly spread is set up using European call options with strike prices of \$0.60пјҢ\$0.65, and \$0.70гҖӮзҫҺеӣҪе’ҢеӨ–еӣҪзҡ„ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮеҲҶеҲ«дёә5%е’Ң4%пјҢжұҮзҺҮжіўеҠЁе№…еәҰдёә15%гҖӮдҪҝз”Ё DerivaGem иҪҜд»¶и®Ўз®—е»әз«Ӣиқ¶ејҸд»·е·®еӨҙеҜёзҡ„жҲҗжң¬гҖӮиҜҒжҳҺеҰӮжһңдҪҝз”Ёж¬§ејҸзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғиҖҢдёҚжҳҜж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғпјҢжҲҗжң¬жҳҜзӣёеҗҢзҡ„гҖӮ

problems.explanation:
12.13
To use DerivaGem, select the first worksheet and choose Currency as the Underlying Type.
Select BlackвҲ’Scholes European as the Option Type. Input exchange rate as 0.64, volatility as
15%, risk-free rate as 5%, foreign risk-free interest rate as 4%, time to exercise as 1 year, and
exercise price as 0.60. Select the button corresponding to call. Do not select the implied
volatility button. Hit the Enter key and click on calculate. DerivaGem will show the price of
the option as 0.0618. Change the exercise price to 0.65, hit Enter, and click on calculate
again. DerivaGem will show the value of the option as 0.0352. Change the exercise price to
0.70, hit Enter, and click on calculate. DerivaGem will show the value of the option as
0.0181.
Now select the button corresponding to put and repeat the procedure. DerivaGem shows the

values of puts with strike prices 0.60, 0.65, and 0.70 to be 0.0176, 0.0386, and 0.0690,
respectively.
The cost of setting up the butterfly spread when calls are used is therefore

0 0618
0 0181 2 0 0352
0 0095

-x.
=.

The cost of setting up the butterfly spread when puts are used is

0 0176
0 0690
2 0 0386
0 0094

+.
-x.
=.

Allowing for rounding errors, these two are the same.

problems.promptEn: Practice 12.14. An index provides a dividend yield of 1% and has a volatility of 20%. The risk-free interest rate is 4%. How long does a principal-protected note, created as in Example 12.1, have to last for it to be profitable for the bank issuing it? Use DerivaGem.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 12.14. жҢҮж•°зҡ„иӮЎжҒҜж”¶зӣҠзҺҮдёә 1%пјҢжіўеҠЁжҖ§дёә 20%гҖӮж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёә4%гҖӮеҰӮдҫӢ 12.1 жүҖзӨәеҲӣе»әзҡ„дҝқжң¬зҘЁжҚ®еҝ…йЎ»жҢҒз»ӯеӨҡд№…жүҚиғҪдёәеҸ‘иЎҢе®ғзҡ„й“¶иЎҢеёҰжқҘзӣҲеҲ©пјҹдҪҝз”Ё DerivaGemгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
12.14
Assume that the investment in the index is initially USD 100. (This is a scaling factor that makes
no difference to the result.) DerivaGem can be used to value an option on the index with the
index level equal to 100, the volatility equal to 20%, the risk-free rate equal to 4%, the
dividend yield equal to 1%, and the exercise price equal to 100. For different times to
maturity, T, we value a call option (using BlackвҲ’Scholes European) and the amount available
to buy the call option, which is 100 вҲ’ 100e-0.04Г—T. Results are as follows:

Time to maturity, T
Funds Available
Value of Option

34.91

This table shows that the answer is between 10 and 11 years. Continuing the calculations, we
find that if the life of the principal-protected note is 10.35 year or more, it is profitable for the
bank. (ExcelвҖҷs Solver can be used in conjunction with the DerivaGem functions to facilitate
calculations.)

problems.promptEn: Practice 12.15. Explain the statement at the end of Section 12.1 that, when dividends are zero, the principal-protected note cannot be profitable for the bank no matter how long it lasts.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 12.15. и§ЈйҮҠдёҖдёӢ12.1иҠӮжң«е°ҫзҡ„иҜҙжі•пјҢеҚіеҪ“иӮЎжҒҜдёәйӣ¶ж—¶пјҢдҝқжң¬зҘЁжҚ®ж— и®әжҢҒз»ӯеӨҡд№…йғҪж— жі•дёәй“¶иЎҢеёҰжқҘзӣҲеҲ©гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
12.15
If volatility is zero, the option to purchase the stock portfolio for USD 1,000 when there are no
dividends is worth 1,000 minus the present value of 1,000 (= USD 164.73 in the example). When
volatility is greater than zero (as it is in practice), the option is worth more than this. The
USD 164.73 that the provider of the principal protected note has available to purchase an option is
therefore not enough.
problems.explanationEn:
12.15
If volatility is zero, the option to purchase the stock portfolio for USD 1,000 when there are no
dividends is worth 1,000 minus the present value of 1,000 (= USD 164.73 in the example). When
volatility is greater than zero (as it is in practice), the option is worth more than this. The
USD 164.73 that the provider of the principal protected note has available to purchase an option is
therefore not enough.

problems.promptEn: Practice 12.21. Suppose that the price of a non-dividend-paying stock is USD 32, its volatility is 30%, and the risk-free rate for all maturities is 5% per annum. Use DerivaGem to calculate the cost of setting up the following positions: (a) A bull spread using European call options with strike prices of USD 25 and USD 30 and a maturity of 6 months (b) A bear spread using European put options with strike prices of USD 25 and USD 30 and a maturity of 6 months (c) A butterfly spread using European call options with strike prices of USD 25, USD 30, and USD 35 and a maturity of 1 year (d) A butterfly spread using European put options with strike prices of USD 25, USD 30, and USD 35 and a maturity of 1 year (e) A straddle using options with a strike price of USD 30 and a 6-month maturity (f) A strangle using options with strike prices of USD 25 and USD 35 and a 6-month maturity. In each case provide a table showing the relationship between profit and final stock price. Ignore the impact of discounting. Binomial Trees
