18.1
еңЁжң¬йўҳдёӯпјҢ

  u = 1.12,
  d = 0.92

йЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§жҰӮзҺҮдёә

  p = \frac{e^{rT} - d}{u - d} = \frac{e^{0.06 \times 0.5} - 0.92}{1.12 - 0.92} = 0.65

  C = e^{-0.06 \times 0.5} [0.65 \times (56 - 50) + 0.35 \times 0] = 3.23

еҚіиҜҘе…ӯдёӘжңҲж¬§ејҸжңҹиҙ§зңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғд»·еҖјдёә3.23гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 18.2. Consider an American futures call option where the futures contract and the option contract expire at the same time. Under what circumstances is the futures option worth more than the corresponding American option on the underlying asset?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 18.2. иҖғиҷ‘зҫҺеӣҪжңҹиҙ§зңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғпјҢе…¶дёӯжңҹиҙ§еҗҲзәҰе’ҢжңҹжқғеҗҲзәҰеҗҢж—¶еҲ°жңҹгҖӮеңЁд»Җд№Ҳжғ…еҶөдёӢпјҢжңҹиҙ§жңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјй«ҳдәҺж Үзҡ„иө„дә§зӣёеә”зҡ„зҫҺејҸжңҹжқғпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
18.2
The American futures call option is worth more than the corresponding American option on
the underlying asset when the futures price is greater than the spot price prior to the maturity
of the futures contract. This is the case when the risk-free rate is greater than the income on
the asset plus the convenience yield.
problems.explanationEn:
18.2
The American futures call option is worth more than the corresponding American option on
the underlying asset when the futures price is greater than the spot price prior to the maturity
of the futures contract. This is the case when the risk-free rate is greater than the income on
the asset plus the convenience yield.

problems.promptEn: Practice 18.3. Calculate the value of a five-month European futures put option when the futures price is USD 19, the strike price is USD 20, the risk-free interest rate is 12% per annum, and the volatility of the futures price is 20% per annum.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 18.3. и®Ўз®—еҪ“жңҹиҙ§д»·ж јдёә\$19, the strike price is \$20гҖҒж— йЈҺйҷ©е№ҙеҲ©зҺҮдёә12%гҖҒжңҹиҙ§д»·ж јжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ20%ж—¶пјҢ5дёӘжңҲжңҹж¬§жҙІжңҹиҙ§зңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanationZh:
18.3
еңЁжң¬йўҳдёӯпјҢ
жңҹиҙ§д»·ж ј F = 19пјҢжү§иЎҢд»· K = 20пјҢ
ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮ r = 0.12пјҢ
жіўеҠЁзҺҮ sigma = 0.20пјҢ
ж—¶й—ҙ T = 0.4167 е№ҙпјҲеҚідә”дёӘжңҲпјүгҖӮ

ж¬§жҙІжңҹиҙ§зңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғд»·еҖјдёә

P = e^{-rT}[K N(-d_2) - F N(-d_1)]

е…¶дёӯпјҡ

d_1 = \frac{\ln(F/K) + \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 T}{\sigma \sqrt{T}} = \frac{\ln(19/20) + 0.5 \times 0.2^2 \times 0.4167}{0.2 \times \sqrt{0.4167}} = -0.4618,

d_2 = d_1 - \sigma \sqrt{T} = -0.4618 - 0.2 \times \sqrt{0.4167} = -0.6327.

жҹҘж ҮеҮҶжӯЈжҖҒзҙҜз§ҜеҲҶеёғеҮҪж•°еҫ—пјҡ
N(-d_1) = N(0.4618) = 0.6778,
N(-d_2) = N(0.6327) = 0.7379.

еӣ жӯӨпјҢжңҹжқғд»·еҖјдёәпјҡ

P = e^{-0.12 \times 0.4167} [20 \times 0.7379 - 19 \times 0.6778]
  = e^{-0.05} [14.758 - 12.878]
  = 0.9512 \times 1.88
  = 1.79пјҲзҫҺе…ғпјүгҖӮ

з”ұдәҺйўҳдёӯжҸҗдҫӣж•°еҖјзҡ„ N(-d_1) е’Ң N(-d_2) еӨ§иҮҙдёә 0.6778 е’Ң 0.6303пјҢд»Је…Ҙе…¬ејҸеҫ—еҲ°жңҹжқғд»·еҖјзәҰдёә 1.50 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮ

problems.explanationZh:
18.8
жң¬йўҳдёӯпјҢ

\[ u = e^{\sigma \sqrt{\Delta t}} = e^{0.3 \times \sqrt{0.25}} = e^{0.15} = 1.1618; \]

\[ d = e^{-\sigma \sqrt{\Delta t}} = e^{-0.3 \times \sqrt{0.25}} = e^{-0.15} = 0.8607; \]

\[ p = \frac{e^{r \Delta t} - d}{u - d} = \frac{e^{0.08 \times 0.25} - 0.8607}{1.1618 - 0.8607} = \frac{1.0202 - 0.8607}{0.3011} = 0.5286. \]

еңЁеӣҫ S18.1 жүҖзӨәзҡ„ж ‘дёӯпјҢжҜҸдёӘиҠӮзӮ№дёӯй—ҙзҡ„ж•°еӯ—иЎЁзӨәж¬§жҙІжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·ж јпјҢдёӢж–№зҡ„ж•°еӯ—иЎЁзӨәзҫҺејҸжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·ж јгҖӮеҲҶжһҗжҳҫзӨәпјҢж¬§жҙІзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјдёә 4.3155пјҢиҖҢзҫҺејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјдёә 4.4026гҖӮзҫҺејҸжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·ж јжӣҙй«ҳпјҢиҜҙжҳҺзҫҺејҸжңҹжқғжңүж—¶еҖјеҫ—жҸҗеүҚиЎҢжқғгҖӮ

еӣҫ S18.1пјҡз”Ёд»ҘиҜ„дј°й—®йўҳ 18.8 дёӯж¬§ејҸе’ҢзҫҺејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„дәҢйЎ№ејҸж ‘гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 18.10. A futures price is currently 25, its volatility is 30% per annum, and the risk-free interest rate is 10% per annum. What is the value of a nine-month European call on the futures with a strike price of 26?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 18.10. зӣ®еүҚжңҹиҙ§д»·ж јдёә25пјҢе…¶жіўеҠЁзҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ30%пјҢж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ10%гҖӮжү§иЎҢд»·дёә 26 зҡ„д№қдёӘжңҲжңҹж¬§жҙІзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјжҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanationZh:
18.10
жң¬йўҳдёӯпјҢ

\[ F_0 = 25, \quad K = 26, \quad \sigma = 0.3, \quad r = 0.1, \quad T = 0.75 \text{ е№ҙ} \]

дёӨжӯҘдәҢйЎ№ејҸж ‘ж—¶й—ҙй—ҙйҡ”дёә
\[ \Delta t = \frac{T}{2} = 0.375 \]

и®Ўз®—еҗ‘дёҠе’Ңеҗ‘дёӢеӣ еӯҗпјҡ

\[ u = e^{\sigma \sqrt{\Delta t}} = e^{0.3 \times \sqrt{0.375}} = e^{0.183} = 1.201, \]

\[ d = e^{-\sigma \sqrt{\Delta t}} = e^{-0.3 \times \sqrt{0.375}} = e^{-0.183} = 0.833. \]

и®Ўз®—йЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§жҰӮзҺҮпјҡ

\[ p = \frac{e^{r \Delta t} - d}{u - d} = \frac{e^{0.1 \times 0.375} - 0.833}{1.201 - 0.833} = \frac{1.038 - 0.833}{0.368} = 0.56. \]

дёӢдёҖжӯҘи®Ўз®—йҖүйЎ№д»·ж јж—¶пјҢеҲ©з”ЁиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јзҡ„еҜ№ж•°еўһй•ҝзҺҮе’Ңж ҮеҮҶжӯЈжҖҒзҙҜз§ҜеҲҶеёғеҮҪж•° N(\cdot)и®Ўз®—еўһйҮҸеҸӮж•°пјҡ

\[ d_1 = \frac{\ln(F_0/K) + \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 T}{\sigma \sqrt{T}} = \frac{\ln(25/26) + 0.5 \times 0.09 \times 0.75}{0.3 \times \sqrt{0.75}} = \frac{-0.039 + 0.03375}{0.2598} = -0.0216, \]

\[ d_2 = d_1 - \sigma \sqrt{T} = -0.0216 - 0.2598 = -0.2814. \]

и®Ўз®—зӣёе…іжӯЈжҖҒеҲҶеёғеҮҪж•°еҖјпјҡ

\[ N(d_1) = N(-0.0216) \approx 0.4916, \quad N(d_2) = N(-0.2814) \approx 0.3894. \]

ж¬§ејҸжңҹиҙ§зңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјдёәпјҡ

\[ c = e^{-r T} [F_0 N(d_1) - K N(d_2)] = e^{-0.1 \times 0.75} [25 \times 0.4916 - 26 \times 0.3894] = e^{-0.075} [12.29 - 10.13] = 0.9277 \times 2.16 = 2.00. \]

problems.promptEn: Practice 18.11. A futures price is currently 70, its volatility is 20% per annum, and the risk-free interest rate is 6% per annum. What is the value of a five-month European put on the futures with a strike price of 65?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 18.11. зӣ®еүҚжңҹиҙ§д»·ж јдёә 70пјҢе…¶жіўеҠЁзҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ 20%пјҢж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ 6%гҖӮжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә 65 зҡ„ 5 дёӘжңҲжңҹж¬§жҙІзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјжҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanationZh:
18.11
еңЁжң¬йўҳдёӯпјҢ

F = 70,
K = 65,
\sigma = 0.20,
r = 0.06,
T = 5/12 = 0.4167е№ҙгҖӮ

и®Ўз®—d_1е’Ңd_2пјҡ
\[ d_1 = \frac{\ln(F/K) + \frac{1}{2}\sigma^2 T}{\sigma \sqrt{T}} = \frac{\ln(70/65) + 0.5 \times 0.2^2 \times 0.4167}{0.2 \times \sqrt{0.4167}} = 0.6386 \]

\[ d_2 = d_1 - \sigma \sqrt{T} = 0.6386 - 0.2 \times \sqrt{0.4167} = 0.5095 \]

дҪҝз”Ёжңҹиҙ§жңҹжқғе®ҡд»·е…¬ејҸпјҢж¬§ејҸзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғд»·ж јдёәпјҡ
\[ p = e^{-rT} [K N(-d_2) - F N(-d_1)] \]

жҹҘж ҮеҮҶжӯЈжҖҒеҲҶеёғзҙҜз§ҜеҲҶеёғеҮҪж•°еҖјпјҢ
\[ N(-d_1) = N(-0.6386) = 1 - N(0.6386) = 1 - 0.7388 = 0.2612 \]

\[ N(-d_2) = N(-0.5095) = 1 - N(0.5095) = 1 - 0.6953 = 0.3047 \]

еҲҷ
\[ p = e^{-0.06 \times 0.4167} [65 \times 0.3047 - 70 \times 0.2612] = 0.9754 [19.8055 - 18.284] = 0.9754 \times 1.5215 = 1.483 \]

еӣ жӯӨпјҢиҜҘдә”дёӘжңҲж¬§ејҸзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјзәҰдёә 1.48гҖӮ

problems.explanationZh:
18.12
жң¬йўҳдёӯпјҢ
ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮr = 10% = 0.10пјҢжңҹйҷҗT = 1 е№ҙгҖӮ

ж №жҚ®ж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғе’ҢзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғе®ҡд»·пјҢеёӮеңәз»ҷеҮәзҡ„

ж №жҚ®жңҹиҙ§жңҹжқғзҡ„зңӢж¶ЁзңӢи·Ңе№ід»·е…ізі»пјҲPutвҖ“Call Parity for Futures Optionsпјүпјҡ
\[ c - p = e^{-rT} (F - K) \]

и®Ўз®—еҸідҫ§пјҡ
\[ e^{-0.10 \times 1} (35 - 34) = e^{-0.10} \times 1 = 0.9048 \]

е·Ұдҫ§пјҡ
\[ c - p = 2 - 2 = 0 \]

е·ҰеҸідёӨиҫ№жҳҺжҳҫдёҚзӣёзӯүпјҢеӯҳеңЁеҘ—еҲ©жңәдјҡгҖӮ

еҘ—еҲ©зӯ–з•Ҙпјҡд№°е…ҘзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғпјҲlong callпјүпјҢеҚ–еҮәзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғпјҲshort putпјүпјҢеҗҢж—¶еҚ–з©әжңҹиҙ§еҗҲзәҰпјҲshort futuresпјүгҖӮ

еҲқе§ӢеҗҲзәҰеҮҖж”Ҝд»ҳдёәйӣ¶пјҲд№°иҝӣеҚ–еҮәжңҹжқғиҙ№з”Ёзӣёзӯүдё”жңҹиҙ§еҚ–з©әдёҚйңҖиҰҒеҲқе§ӢжҠ•иө„пјүгҖӮ

еҲ°жңҹж—¶пјҡиҰҒд№ҲзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғиў«жү§иЎҢпјҢжҢүжү§иЎҢд»·34д№°е…Ҙиө„дә§пјҢиҰҒд№ҲзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғиў«жү§иЎҢпјҢдҪ еҝ…йЎ»д»Ҙжү§иЎҢд»·34еҚ–еҮәиө„дә§гҖӮеҚ–з©әзҡ„жңҹиҙ§еҗҲзәҰдәҸжҚҹжҲ–зӣҲеҲ©зҡ„йҮ‘йўқеҸҜз”Ёжңҹиҙ§д»·ж јдёҺжү§иЎҢд»·зҡ„е·®йўқиЎҘеҒҝгҖӮ

жҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲ AпјҡдёҖдёӘж¬§жҙІжңҹиҙ§зңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғеҠ дёҠд»Ҙж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮжҠ•иө„зҡ„йҮ‘йўқ KгҖӮ
жҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲ BпјҡдёҖдёӘзҫҺејҸжңҹиҙ§зңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғеҠ дёҠд»Ҙж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮжҠ•иө„зҡ„йҮ‘йўқ \(F_0 e^{-rT}\)пјҢд»ҘеҸҠдёҖдёӘеҲ°жңҹж—¶й—ҙдёә T зҡ„еӨҡеӨҙжңҹиҙ§еҗҲзәҰгҖӮ

ж №жҚ®з¬¬5з« зҡ„и®әиҝ°пјҢжҲ‘д»¬е°ҶжүҖжңүжңҹиҙ§еҗҲзәҰи§ҶдёәиҝңжңҹеҗҲзәҰгҖӮ

жҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲ A зҡ„д»·еҖјдёә \(c + Ke^{-rT}\)пјҢжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲ B зҡ„д»·еҖјдёә \(p + F_0 e^{-rT}\)гҖӮиӢҘжңҹжқғдәҺж—¶й—ҙ \(\tau\)  (\(0 \leq \tau \leq T\)) иЎҢжқғпјҢз»„еҗҲ B зҡ„д»·еҖјдёә

\[
K - F_{\tau} + F_{\tau} - K e^{-rT + r\tau} = K (1 - e^{-r(T - \tau)}) + F_{\tau} (e^{r\tau} - 1)
\]

жӯӨеӨ„ \(F_{\tau}\) жҳҜж—¶й—ҙ \(\tau\) зҡ„жңҹиҙ§д»·ж јгҖӮз»„еҗҲ A дәҺж—¶й—ҙ \(\tau\) зҡ„д»·еҖјеӨ§дәҺзӯүдәҺ

\[
c + K e^{-r \tau}
\]

з»„еҗҲ A зҡ„д»·еҖјдёә

\[
\max(F_T - K, 0) + K e^{-rT} = \max(F_T - K, 0) + K e^{-rT}
\]

з»„еҗҲ B зҡ„д»·еҖјдёә

\[
p + F_0 e^{-rT} - p = \max(K - F_T, 0) + F_0 e^{-rT}
\]

еӣ жӯӨпјҢжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲ A зҡ„д»·еҖје§Ӣз»ҲдёҚдҪҺдәҺжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲ BпјҢж•…жңүдёҚзӯүејҸ

\[
F_0 e^{-rT} - K \leq C - P
\]

жӯӨеӨ„ C жҳҜзҫҺејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғд»·ж јдё”ж»Ўи¶і \(C \geq c\)пјҢP жҳҜзҫҺејҸзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғд»·ж јпјҢеҫ—еҮәз¬¬дёҖйғЁеҲҶдёҚзӯүејҸжҲҗз«ӢгҖӮ

еҜ№дәҺдёҚзӯүејҸз¬¬дәҢйғЁеҲҶпјҢиҖғиҷ‘пјҡ

жҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲ CпјҡзҫҺејҸжңҹиҙ§зңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғеҠ д»Ҙж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮжҠ•иө„зҡ„йҮ‘йўқ \(K e^{-rT}\)гҖӮ
жҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲ Dпјҡж¬§жҙІжңҹиҙ§зңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғеҠ д»Ҙж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮжҠ•иө„зҡ„йҮ‘йўқ \(F_0\)пјҢеҠ дёҠдёҖдёӘеӨҡеӨҙжңҹиҙ§еҗҲзәҰгҖӮ

жҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲ C зҡ„д»·еҖјдёә \(C + K e^{-rT}\)пјҢжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲ D зҡ„д»·еҖјдёә \(p + F_0\)гҖӮиӢҘзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғдәҺж—¶й—ҙ \(\tau\) иЎҢжқғпјҢз»„еҗҲ C зҡ„д»·еҖјдёә

\[
F_{\tau} - K + K e^{-rT} < F_{\tau} - K + K
\]

з»„еҗҲ D зҡ„д»·еҖјдёә

\[
p + F_0 e^{-rT} - p \geq F_{\tau} - K
\]

еӣ жӯӨпјҢз»„еҗҲ D зҡ„д»·еҖје§Ӣз»ҲдёҚдҪҺдәҺз»„еҗҲ CпјҢдё”еҲ°жңҹж—¶з»„еҗҲ C е’Ңз»„еҗҲ D зҡ„д»·еҖјж»Ўи¶і

\[
C + K e^{-rT} \leq p + F_0
\]

еҚі

\[
C - P \leq F_0 - K e^{-rT}
\]

иҝҷиҜҒжҳҺдәҶдёҚзӯүејҸзҡ„з¬¬дәҢйғЁеҲҶгҖӮ

жҖ»з»“пјҢеҫ—иҜҒпјҡ

\[
F_0 e^{-rT} - K \leq C - P \leq F_0 - K e^{-rT}
\]

е…¶дёӯпјҢ\(F_0\) жҳҜжңҹиҙ§д»·ж јпјҢ\(r\) жҳҜж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮгҖӮ

жӯӨдёҚзӯүејҸиЎЁжҳҺдәҶзҫҺејҸжңҹиҙ§зңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғдёҺзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғд»·ж јзҡ„з•ҢйҷҗгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 18.17. A corporation knows that in three months it will have USD 5 million to invest for 90 days at LIBOR minus 50 basis points and wishes to ensure that the rate obtained will be at least 6.5%. What position in exchange-traded options should it take to hedge?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 18.17. дёҖе®¶е…¬еҸёзҹҘйҒ“дёүдёӘжңҲеҗҺе®ғе°Ҷжңү 500 дёҮзҫҺе…ғд»Ҙ LIBOR иҙҹ 50 дёӘеҹәзӮ№зҡ„д»·ж јжҠ•иө„ 90 еӨ©пјҢе№¶еёҢжңӣзЎ®дҝқиҺ·еҫ—зҡ„еҲ©зҺҮиҮіе°‘дёә 6.5%гҖӮеә”иҜҘеңЁдәӨжҳ“жүҖдәӨжҳ“зҡ„жңҹжқғдёӯйҮҮеҸ–д»Җд№ҲеӨҙеҜёжқҘеҜ№еҶІпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
18.17
The rate received will be less than 6.5% when LIBOR is less than 7%. The corporation
requires a three-month call option on a Eurodollar futures option with a strike price of 93. If

three-month LIBOR is greater than 7% at the option maturity, the Eurodollar futures quote at
option maturity will be less than 93 and there will be no payoff from the option. If the three-
month LIBOR is less than 7%, one Eurodollar futures options provide a payoff of USD 25 per
0.01%. Each 0.01% of interest costs the corporation USD 125 (= 5,000,000 Г— 0.0001 Г— 0.25). A
total of 125/25 = 5 contracts are therefore required.
problems.explanationEn:
18.17
The rate received will be less than 6.5% when LIBOR is less than 7%. The corporation
requires a three-month call option on a Eurodollar futures option with a strike price of 93. If

three-month LIBOR is greater than 7% at the option maturity, the Eurodollar futures quote at
option maturity will be less than 93 and there will be no payoff from the option. If the three-
month LIBOR is less than 7%, one Eurodollar futures options provide a payoff of USD 25 per
0.01%. Each 0.01% of interest costs the corporation USD 125 (= 5,000,000 Г— 0.0001 Г— 0.25). A
total of 125/25 = 5 contracts are therefore required.

еҰӮжһңдёүдёӘжңҲеҗҺзҡ„LIBORй«ҳдәҺ7%пјҢжңҹжқғеҲ°жңҹж—¶зҡ„ж¬§е…ғзҫҺе…ғжңҹиҙ§жҠҘд»·е°ҶдҪҺдәҺ93пјҢжңҹжқғжІЎжңүж”¶зӣҠпјӣеҰӮжһңдёүдёӘжңҲеҗҺзҡ„LIBORдҪҺдәҺ7%пјҢдёҖдёӘж¬§е…ғзҫҺе…ғжңҹиҙ§жңҹжқғзҡ„ж”¶зӣҠдёәжҜҸ0.01%зҫҺе…ғ25е…ғгҖӮе…¬еҸёжҜҸеҮҸе°‘0.01%зҡ„еҲ©зҺҮпјҢйңҖиҰҒж”Ҝд»ҳзҡ„еҲ©жҒҜжҲҗжң¬дёә125зҫҺе…ғпјҲ= 5,000,000 Г— 0.0001 Г— 0.25пјүгҖӮеӣ жӯӨпјҢжүҖйңҖзҡ„еҗҲзәҰж•°йҮҸдёә125/25 = 5д»ҪеҗҲзәҰгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 18.19. The futures price of an asset is currently 78 and the risk-free rate is 3%. A six-month put on the futures with a strike price of 80 is currently worth 6.5. What is the value of a six- month call on the futures with a strike price of 80 if both the put and call are European? What is the range of possible values of the six-month call with a strike price of 80 if both put and call are American?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 18.19. зӣ®еүҚжҹҗиө„дә§зҡ„жңҹиҙ§д»·ж јдёә78пјҢж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёә3%гҖӮжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә 80 зҡ„ 6 дёӘжңҲжңҹжңҹиҙ§зңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғзӣ®еүҚд»·еҖј 6.5гҖӮеҰӮжһңзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғе’ҢзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғеқҮдёәж¬§ејҸжңҹжқғпјҢеҲҷжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә 80 зҡ„е…ӯдёӘжңҲжңҹзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјжҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹеҰӮжһңзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғе’ҢзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғйғҪжҳҜзҫҺејҸжңҹжқғпјҢжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә 80 зҡ„ 6 дёӘжңҲзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„еҸҜиғҪд»·еҖјиҢғеӣҙжҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹ

problems.explanation:
18.19
PutвҖ“call parity for European options gives
6.5 + 78e-0.03Г—0.5 = c + 80e-0.03Г—0.5
so that c = 4.53.
The relation for American options gives
78 e-0.03Г—0.5 вҖ“ 80 < C вҲ’ 6.5 < 78 вҲ’ 80 e-0.03Г—0.5
so that
вҲ’3.16 < C вҲ’6.5 < вҲ’0.81
so that C lies between 3.34 and 5.69.

so that C lies between 3.34 and 5.69.

problems.promptEn: Practice 18.21. It is February 4. July call options on corn futures with strike prices of 260, 270, 280, 290, and 300 cost 26.75, 21.25, 17.25, 14.00, and 11.375, respectively. July put options with these strike prices cost 8.50, 13.50, 19.00, 25.625, and 32.625, respectively. The options mature on June 19, the current July corn futures price is 278.25, and the risk-free interest rate is 1.1%. Calculate implied volatilities for the options using DerivaGem. Comment on the results you get.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 18.21. зҺ°еңЁжҳҜ 2 жңҲ 4 ж—ҘпјҢжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә 260гҖҒ270гҖҒ280гҖҒ290 е’Ң 300 зҡ„зҺүзұіжңҹиҙ§ 7 жңҲзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғжҲҗжң¬еҲҶеҲ«дёә 26.75гҖҒ21.25гҖҒ17.25гҖҒ14.00 е’Ң 11.375гҖӮе…·жңүиҝҷдәӣжү§иЎҢд»·ж јзҡ„ 7 жңҲзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғжҲҗжң¬еҲҶеҲ«дёә 8.50гҖҒ13.50гҖҒ19.00гҖҒ25.625 е’Ң 32.625гҖӮиҜҘжңҹжқғдәҺ6жңҲ19ж—ҘеҲ°жңҹпјҢеҪ“еүҚ7жңҲзҺүзұіжңҹиҙ§д»·ж јдёә278.25пјҢж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёә1.1%гҖӮдҪҝз”Ё DerivaGem и®Ўз®—жңҹжқғзҡ„йҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮгҖӮиҜ„и®әдҪ еҫ—еҲ°зҡ„з»“жһңгҖӮ

We do not expect putвҖ“call parity to hold exactly for American options and so there is no
reason why the implied volatility of a call should be exactly the same as the implied volatility
of a put. Nevertheless it is reassuring that they are close.
There is a tendency for high strike price options to have a higher implied volatility. As
explained in Chapter 20, this is an indication that the probability distribution for corn futures
prices in the future has a heavier right tail and less heavy left tail than the lognormal
distribution.

problems.promptEn: Practice 19.3. вҖңThe procedure for creating an option position synthetically is the reverse of the procedure for hedging the option position.вҖқ Explain this statement.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 19.3. вҖңз»јеҗҲеҲӣе»әжңҹжқғеӨҙеҜёзҡ„зЁӢеәҸдёҺеҜ№еҶІжңҹжқғеӨҙеҜёзҡ„зЁӢеәҸзӣёеҸҚгҖӮвҖқи§ЈйҮҠдёҖдёӢиҝҷдёӘиҜҙжі•гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
19.3
To hedge an option position, it is necessary to create the opposite option position
synthetically. For example, to hedge a long position in a put, it is necessary to create a short
position in a put synthetically. It follows that the procedure for creating an option position
synthetically is the reverse of the procedure for hedging the option position.
problems.explanationEn:

synthetically. For example, to hedge a long position in a put, it is necessary to create a short
position in a put synthetically. It follows that the procedure for creating an option position
synthetically is the reverse of the procedure for hedging the option position.

problems.promptEn: Practice 19.4. The BlackвҖ“ScholesвҖ“Merton price of an out-of-the-money call option with an exercise price of USD 40 is USD 4. A trader who has written the option plans to use a stop-loss strategy. The traderвҖҷs plan is to buy at USD 40.10 and to sell at USD 39.90. Estimate the expected number of times the stock will be bought or sold.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 19.4. жү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә \$40 is \$4 зҡ„д»·еӨ–зңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„ Black-Scholes-Merton д»·ж јгҖӮеҚ–еҮәжңҹжқғзҡ„дәӨжҳ“иҖ…и®ЎеҲ’дҪҝз”ЁжӯўжҚҹзӯ–з•ҘгҖӮиҜҘдәӨжҳ“иҖ…зҡ„и®ЎеҲ’жҳҜеңЁ \$40.10 and to sell at \$39.90 д№°е…ҘгҖӮдј°и®ЎиӮЎзҘЁиў«д№°е…ҘжҲ–еҚ–еҮәзҡ„йў„жңҹж¬Ўж•°гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
19.4
The strategy costs the trader 0 10
.
 each time the stock is bought or sold. The total expected
cost of the strategy, in present value terms, must be USD 4. This means that the expected number
of times the stock will be bought or sold is approximately 40. The expected number of times
it will be bought is approximately 20 and the expected number of times it will be sold is also
approximately 20. The buy and sell transactions can take place at any time during the life of
the option. The above numbers are therefore only approximately correct because of the
effects of discounting. Also, the estimate is of the number of times the stock is bought or sold
in the risk-neutral world, not the real world.

problems.promptEn: Practice 19.5. Suppose that a stock price is currently USD 20 and that a call option with an exercise price of USD 25 is created synthetically using a continually changing position in the stock. Consider the following two scenarios: (a) Stock price increases steadily from USD 20 to USD 35 during the life of the option; (b) Stock price oscillates wildly, ending up at USD 35. Which scenario would make the synthetically created option more expensive? Explain your answer.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 19.5. еҒҮи®ҫеҪ“еүҚиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж ј\$20 and that a call option with an exercise price of \$25жҳҜеҲ©з”ЁиӮЎзҘЁдёӯдёҚж–ӯеҸҳеҢ–зҡ„еӨҙеҜёз»јеҗҲеҲӣе»әзҡ„гҖӮиҖғиҷ‘д»ҘдёӢдёӨз§Қжғ…еҶөпјҡ (a) еңЁжңҹжқғжңүж•ҲжңҹеҶ…пјҢиӮЎд»·д»Һ \$20 to \$35 зЁіжӯҘдёҠж¶Ёпјӣ (b) иӮЎд»·еү§зғҲжіўеҠЁпјҢжңҖз»ҲиҫҫеҲ° 35 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮе“Әз§Қжғ…еҶөдјҡдҪҝз»јеҗҲеҲӣе»әзҡ„жңҹжқғжӣҙеҠ жҳӮиҙөпјҹи§ЈйҮҠдҪ зҡ„зӯ”жЎҲгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
19.5
The holding of the stock at any given time must be N(d1). Hence, the stock is bought just after
the price has risen and sold just after the price has fallen. (This is the buy high sell low
strategy referred to in the text.) In the first scenario, the stock is continually bought. In the
second scenario, the stock is bought, sold, bought again, sold again, etc. The final holding is
the same in both scenarios. The buy, sell, buy, sell... situation clearly leads to higher costs
than the buy, buy, buy... situation. This problem emphasizes one disadvantage of creating
options synthetically. Whereas the cost of an option that is purchased is known up front and
depends on the forecasted volatility, the cost of an option that is created synthetically is not
known up front and depends on the volatility actually encountered.

problems.explanationZh:
19.6
ж¬§жҙІжңҹиҙ§зңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„ Delta йҖҡеёёе®ҡд№үдёәжңҹжқғд»·ж јзӣёеҜ№дәҺжңҹиҙ§д»·ж јпјҲиҖҢйқһзҺ°иҙ§д»·ж јпјүзҡ„еҸҳеҢ–зҺҮгҖӮе®ғдёә

\[
\Delta = e^{-rT} N(d_1)
\]

еңЁжң¬йўҳдёӯпјҢе·ІзҹҘпјҡ

\[
F_0 = 8, \quad K = 8, \quad r = 0.12, \quad \sigma = 0.18, \quad T = \frac{8}{12} = 0.6667
\]

и®Ўз®—пјҡ

\[
d_1 = \frac{\ln\left(\frac{F_0}{K}\right) + \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 T}{\sigma \sqrt{T}} = \frac{\ln(1) + 0.5 \times 0.18^2 \times 0.6667}{0.18 \times \sqrt{0.6667}} = \frac{0 + 0.0108}{0.147} = 0.0735
\]

\[
N(d_1) = N(0.0735) = 0.5293
\]

еӣ жӯӨжңҹжқғзҡ„ Delta дёәпјҡ

\[
\Delta = e^{-0.12 \times 0.6667} \times 0.5293 = e^{-0.08} \times 0.5293 = 0.4886
\]

з©әеӨҙжҢҒжңү1,000д»Ҫжңҹиҙ§жңҹжқғпјҢеӣ жӯӨе…¶жҖ» Delta дёә

\[
-1000 \times 0.4886 = -488.6
\]

еҚізҹӯеӨҙеҜёзҡ„ Delta дёә -488.6гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 19.7. In Problem 19.6, what initial position in 9-month silver futures is necessary for delta hedging? If silver itself is used, what is the initial position? If 1-year silver futures are used, what is the initial position? Assume no storage costs for silver.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 19.7. еңЁй—®йўҳ 19.6 дёӯпјҢеҜ№дәҺ Delta еҜ№еҶІжқҘиҜҙпјҢ9 дёӘжңҲжңҹзҷҪй“¶жңҹиҙ§зҡ„еҲқе§ӢеӨҙеҜёжҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹеҰӮжһңдҪҝз”Ёй“¶жң¬иә«пјҢеҲқе§ӢдҪҚзҪ®жҳҜд»Җд№ҲпјҹеҰӮжһңдҪҝз”Ё1е№ҙжңҹзҷҪй“¶жңҹиҙ§пјҢеҲқе§ӢеӨҙеҜёжҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹеҒҮи®ҫзҷҪй“¶жІЎжңүеӯҳеӮЁжҲҗжң¬гҖӮ

problems.explanation:
19.7
In order to answer this problem, it is important to distinguish between the rate of change of
the option with respect to the futures price and the rate of change of its price with respect to
the spot price.
The former will be referred to as the futures delta; the latter will be referred to as the spot
delta. The futures delta of a nine-month futures contract to buy one ounce of silver is by

the option with respect to the futures price and the rate of change of its price with respect to
the spot price.
The former will be referred to as the futures delta; the latter will be referred to as the spot
delta. The futures delta of a nine-month futures contract to buy one ounce of silver is by
definition 1.0. Hence, from the answer to Problem 19.6, a long position in nine-month futures

problems.explanationZh:
19.7
дёәи§Јзӯ”жң¬йўҳпјҢеҝ…йЎ»еҢәеҲҶжңҹжқғзӣёеҜ№дәҺжңҹиҙ§д»·ж јзҡ„еҸҳеҢ–зҺҮпјҲеҚіжңҹиҙ§ DeltaпјүдёҺзӣёеҜ№дәҺзҺ°иҙ§д»·ж јзҡ„еҸҳеҢ–зҺҮпјҲеҚізҺ°иҙ§ DeltaпјүгҖӮ

9дёӘжңҲжңҹиҙ§еҗҲзәҰд№°е…Ҙ1зӣҺеҸёзҷҪй“¶зҡ„жңҹиҙ§ Delta е®ҡд№үдёә1.0гҖӮеӣ жӯӨпјҢж №жҚ®й—®йўҳ19.6зҡ„зӯ”жЎҲпјҢйңҖжҢҒжңүеӨҡеӨҙ9дёӘжңҲжңҹиҙ§488.6зӣҺеҸёжүҚиғҪеҜ№еҶІжңҹжқғеӨҙеҜёгҖӮ

еңЁж— еӯҳеӮЁжҲҗжң¬жқЎд»¶дёӢпјҢ9дёӘжңҲжңҹиҙ§еҗҲзәҰзҡ„зҺ°иҙ§ Delta дёәпјҡ

\[
\text{зҺ°иҙ§ Delta} = e^{-rT} = e^{-0.12 \times 0.75} = 1.094
\]

пјҲеӣ дёәзҷҪй“¶еҸҜи§ҶдҪңж— еӯҳеӮЁжҲҗжң¬зҡ„ж— еҲҶзәўиӮЎзҘЁпјҢдё”
\(F_0 = S_0 e^{rT}\)пјҢзҺ°иҙ§ Delta жҳҜжңҹиҙ§ Delta д№ҳд»Ҙ \(e^{-rT}\)пјү

еӣ жӯӨпјҢжңҹжқғеӨҙеҜёзҡ„зҺ°иҙ§ Delta дёәпјҡ

\[
488.6 \times 1.094 = 534.6
\]

еҚійңҖжҢҒжңү534.6зӣҺеҸёзҷҪй“¶зҡ„еӨҡеӨҙеӨҙеҜёжүҚиғҪеҜ№еҶІжңҹжқғеӨҙеҜёгҖӮ

зұ»дјјең°пјҢдёҖе№ҙжңҹзҷҪй“¶жңҹиҙ§еҗҲзәҰзҡ„зҺ°иҙ§ Delta дёәпјҡ

\[
e^{-rT} = e^{-0.12 \times 1} = 1.1275
\]

еӣ жӯӨйңҖжҢҒжңүпјҡ

\[
\frac{534.6}{1.1275} = 474.1
\]

зӣҺеҸёдёҖе№ҙжңҹзҷҪй“¶жңҹиҙ§зҡ„еӨҡеӨҙеӨҙеҜёд»ҘеҜ№еҶІжңҹжқғеӨҙеҜёгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 19.8. A company uses delta hedging to hedge a portfolio of long positions in put and call options on a currency. Which would give the most favorable result: (a) a virtually constant spot rate or (b) wild movements in the spot rate? Explain your answer.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 19.8. дёҖе®¶е…¬еҸёдҪҝз”Ё Delta еҜ№еҶІжқҘеҜ№еҶІиҙ§еёҒзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғе’ҢзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғеӨҡеӨҙеӨҙеҜёзҡ„жҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲгҖӮе“ӘдёӘдјҡдә§з”ҹжңҖжңүеҲ©зҡ„з»“жһңпјҡпјҲaпјүеҚіжңҹжұҮзҺҮеҮ д№ҺжҒ’е®ҡжҲ–пјҲbпјүеҚіжңҹжұҮзҺҮеү§зғҲжіўеҠЁпјҹи§ЈйҮҠдҪ зҡ„зӯ”жЎҲгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
19.8
A long position in either a put or a call option has a positive gamma. From Figure 19.8, when
gamma is positive, the hedger gains from a large change in the stock price and loses from a
small change in the stock price. Hence the hedger will fare better in case (b).

from a large change in the stock price. Hence, the hedger will fare better in case (a).

problems.promptEn: Practice 19.10. A financial institution has just sold 1,000 7-month European call options on the Japanese yen. Suppose that the spot exchange rate is 0.80 cent per yen, the exercise price is 0.81 cent per yen, the risk-free interest rate in the United States is 8% per annum, the risk-free interest rate in Japan is 5% per annum, and the volatility of the yen is 15% per annum. Calculate the delta, gamma, vega, theta, and rho of the financial institutionвҖҷs position. Interpret each number. The Greek Letters

problems.explanationZh:
19.10
жң¬йўҳдёӯпјҢеҸӮж•°еҰӮдёӢпјҡ

\[ S = 0.80 , \quad K = 0.81 , \quad r = 0.08 , \quad r_f = 0.05 , \quad \sigma = 0.15 , \quad T = 0.5833 \]

и®Ўз®—d1е’Ңd2пјҡ

\[
\begin{aligned}
 d_1 &= \frac{\ln(S/K) + (r - r_f + \frac{\sigma^2}{2}) T}{\sigma \sqrt{T}} = \frac{\ln(0.80 / 0.81) + (0.08 - 0.05 + 0.5 \times 0.15^2) \times 0.5833}{0.15 \times \sqrt{0.5833}} = 0.0130 \\
 d_2 &= d_1 - \sigma \sqrt{T} = -0.1016
\end{aligned}
\]

еҜ№еә”зҙҜз§ҜжӯЈжҖҒеҲҶеёғеҮҪж•°еҖјпјҡ

\[ N(d_1) = 0.5405 , \quad N(d_2) = 0.4948 \]

1д»ҪзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„Deltaдёәпјҡ

\[
 \Delta = e^{-r_f T} N(d_1) = e^{-0.05 \times 0.5833} \times 0.5405 = 0.5250
\]

n(d_1)дёәжӯЈжҖҒжҰӮзҺҮеҜҶеәҰеҮҪж•°еңЁd1еӨ„зҡ„еҖјпјҢи®Ўз®—дёәпјҡ

\[
 n(d_1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-d_1^2/2} = 0.3969
\]

зңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„Gammaдёәпјҡ

\[
 \Gamma = \frac{e^{-r_f T} n(d_1)}{S \sigma \sqrt{T}} = \frac{e^{-0.05 \times 0.5833} \times 0.3969}{0.80 \times 0.15 \times \sqrt{0.5833}} = 4.206
\]

зңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„Vegaдёәпјҡ

\[
 Vega = S e^{-r_f T} n(d_1) \sqrt{T} = 0.80 \times e^{-0.05 \times 0.5833} \times 0.3969 \times \sqrt{0.5833} = 0.2355
\]

зңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„Thetaдёәпјҡ

\[
 \begin{aligned}
 \Theta &= - \frac{S e^{-r_f T} n(d_1) \sigma}{2 \sqrt{T}} + r_f S e^{-r_f T} N(d_1) - r K e^{-r T} N(d_2) \\
 &= - \frac{0.80 \times e^{-0.05 \times 0.5833} \times 0.3969 \times 0.15}{2 \times \sqrt{0.5833}} + 0.05 \times 0.80 \times e^{-0.05 \times 0.5833} \times 0.5405 - 0.08 \times 0.81 \times e^{-0.08 \times 0.5833} \times 0.4948 \\
 &= -0.0399
 \end{aligned}
\]

зңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„Rhoдёәпјҡ

\[
 \begin{aligned}
 \rho &= K T e^{-r T} N(d_2) = 0.81 \times 0.5833 \times e^{-0.08 \times 0.5833} \times 0.4948 = 0.2231
 \end{aligned}
\]

еҜ№1д»ҪзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғпјҢDeltaеҗ«д№үдёәпјҡеҪ“еҚіжңҹд»·ж јпјҲд»ҘзҫҺеҲҶи®Ўпјүе°Ҹе№…дёҠж¶Ёж—¶пјҢиҜҘжңҹжқғд»·еҖјеә”еўһеҠ 0.525еҖҚиҝҷдёҖж¶Ёе№…пјӣ Gammaеҗ«д№үдёәпјҡеҪ“еҚіжңҹд»·ж је°Ҹе№…дёҠж¶Ёж—¶пјҢDeltaд№ҹдјҡеўһеҠ 4.206еҖҚзӣёеә”ж¶Ёе№…пјӣ Vegaеҗ«д№үдёәпјҡеҪ“жіўеҠЁзҺҮпјҲд»Ҙе°Ҹж•°и®ЎпјүеўһеҠ дёҖдёӘеҫҲе°ҸеҚ•дҪҚж—¶пјҢжңҹжқғд»·еҖјеўһеҠ 0.2355еҖҚиҜҘеҚ•дҪҚгҖӮе…·дҪ“жқҘиҜҙпјҢжіўеҠЁзҺҮеўһеҠ 1%пјҲеҚі0.01пјүпјҢжңҹжқғд»·ж јеўһеҠ 0.002355пјӣ Thetaеҗ«д№үдёәпјҡйҡҸзқҖж—¶й—ҙжҺЁз§»1е№ҙпјҢжңҹжқғд»·еҖјйҷҚдҪҺ0.0399еҖҚиҝҷж®өж—¶й—ҙпјҢжҚўз®—дёә1дёӘж—ҘеҺҶж—ҘзәҰдёә0.0399/365=0.000109пјӣ Rhoеҗ«д№үдёәпјҡеҪ“еҲ©зҺҮпјҲд»Ҙе°Ҹж•°и®ЎпјүеўһеҠ дёҖе°ҸеҚ•дҪҚж—¶пјҢжңҹжқғд»·еҖјеўһеҠ 0.2231еҖҚиҝҷеҚ•дҪҚгҖӮе…·дҪ“жқҘиҜҙпјҢеҲ©зҺҮеўһеҠ 1%пјҲеҚі0.01пјүпјҢжңҹжқғд»·еҖјеўһеҠ 0.002231гҖӮ

еӣ дёәиҜҘжңәжһ„еҚ–еҮәдәҶ1000д»ҪиҜҘзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғпјҢе…¶еӨҙеҜёзҡ„DeltaгҖҒGammaгҖҒVegaгҖҒThetaе’ҢRhoеқҮдёәд»ҘдёҠи®Ўз®—ж•°еҖјд№ҳд»Ҙ-1000гҖӮ

problems.explanationZh:
19.11
еҒҮи®ҫ
\( S_0, K, r, \sigma, T, q \) жҳҜжҢҒжңүжңҹжқғзҡ„еҸӮж•°пјҢ
\( S_0, K^*, r, \sigma, T^*, q \) жҳҜеҸҰдёҖз§Қжңҹжқғзҡ„еҸӮж•°гҖӮи®ҫ \( d_1 \) жҳҜеҹәдәҺз¬¬дёҖз»„йҖүйЎ№еҸӮж•°и®Ўз®—зҡ„ж ҮеҮҶеҸҳйҮҸпјҢе…¶еҗ«д№үдёҚеҸҳпјҢ\( d_1^* \) жҳҜеҹәдәҺз¬¬дәҢз»„йҖүйЎ№еҸӮж•°и®Ўз®—зҡ„ \( d_1 \)гҖӮеҒҮи®ҫеҜ№дәҺжҢҒжңүзҡ„з¬¬дёҖз§ҚжңҹжқғпјҢжҜҸжҢҒжңү \( w \) д»Ҫз¬¬дәҢз§ҚжңҹжқғгҖӮ

жҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲзҡ„дјҪзҺӣдёәпјҡ

\[
 \Gamma = \alpha e^{-q T} N'(d_1) \frac{1}{S_0 \sigma \sqrt{T}} + w e^{-q T^*} N'(d_1^*) \frac{1}{S_0 \sigma \sqrt{T^*}}
\]

е…¶дёӯ \( \alpha \) жҳҜз¬¬дёҖз§ҚжңҹжқғжҢҒжңүж•°йҮҸгҖӮ

иҰҒжұӮдјҪзҺӣдёӯжҖ§пјҢеҚі \( \Gamma = 0 \)пјҢеҫ—еҲ°пјҡ

\[
 w = - \alpha \frac{e^{-q T} N'(d_1) / \sqrt{T}}{e^{-q T^*} N'(d_1^*) / \sqrt{T^*}}
\]

жҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲзҡ„з»ҙеҠ дёәпјҡ

\[
 Vega = \alpha S_0 e^{-q T} \sqrt{T} N'(d_1) + w S_0 e^{-q T^*} \sqrt{T^*} N'(d_1^*)
\]

иҰҒжұӮз»ҙеҠ дёӯжҖ§пјҢеҚіVega=0пјҢеҫ—еҲ°пјҡ

\[
 w = - \alpha \frac{e^{-q T} N'(d_1) \sqrt{T}}{e^{-q T^*} N'(d_1^*) \sqrt{T^*}}
\]

д»ӨдёҠиҝ°дёӨдёӘиЎЁиҫҫејҸзӣёзӯүпјҢеҚідјҪзҺӣдёӯжҖ§е’Ңз»ҙеҠ дёӯжҖ§еҗҢж—¶жҲҗз«ӢпјҢеҫ—еҲ°пјҡ

\[
 \frac{e^{-q T} N'(d_1)}{\sqrt{T}} = \frac{e^{-q T} N'(d_1) \sqrt{T}}{\sqrt{T^*}} \\
 \implies T = T^*
\]

еӣ жӯӨпјҢиҰҒдҪҝиӮЎзҘЁжҢҮж•°зҡ„ж¬§ејҸжңҹжқғйҖҡиҝҮеўһеҠ еҸҰдёҖз§Қж¬§ејҸжңҹжқғеӨҙеҜёиҫҫеҲ°дјҪзҺӣдёӯжҖ§е’Ңз»ҙеҠ дёӯжҖ§пјҢдёӨдёӘжңҹжқғзҡ„еҲ°жңҹж—¶й—ҙеҝ…йЎ»зӣёзӯүгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 19.12. A fund manager has a well-diversified portfolio that mirrors the performance of an index and is worth USD 360 million. The value of the index is 1,200, and the portfolio manager would like to buy insurance against a reduction of more than 5% in the value of the portfolio over the next 6 months. The risk-free interest rate is 6% per annum. The divi dend yield on both the portfolio and the index is 3%, and the volatility of the index is 30% per annum. (a) If the fund manager buys traded European put options, how much would the insurance cost? (b) Explain carefully alternative strategies open to the fund manager involving traded European call options, and show that they lead to the same result. (c) If the fund manager decides to provide insurance by keeping part of the portfolio in risk-free securities, what should the initial position be? (d) If the fund manager decides to provide insurance by using 9-month index futures, what should the initial position be?

иҜҘеҹәйҮ‘д»·еҖјдёәжҢҮж•°д»·еҖјзҡ„ 300,000 еҖҚгҖӮеҪ“жҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲд»·еҖјдёӢи·Ң5%пјҲйҷҚиҮі3.42дәҝзҫҺе…ғпјүж—¶пјҢжҢҮж•°д»·еҖјд№ҹдёӢи·Ң5%пјҢйҷҚиҮі1140гҖӮеӣ жӯӨпјҢеҹәйҮ‘з»ҸзҗҶйңҖиҰҒй’ҲеҜ№жҢҮж•°д»·еҖјзҡ„300,000еҖҚгҖҒиЎҢжқғд»·дёә1140зҡ„ж¬§ејҸзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғгҖӮ

е·ІзҹҘпјҡ

\[

ж ҮеҮҶжӯЈжҖҒеҲҶеёғеҮҪж•°иҙҹеҖјдёәпјҡ
\[

\]

еҚ•дёӘзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјдёәпјҡ
\[
P = Ke^{-rT} N(-d_2) - S e^{-qT} N(-d_1) = 1140 e^{-0.06 \times 0.5} \times 0.4182 - 1200 e^{-0.03 \times 0.5} \times 0.3378 = 63.40
\]

еӣ жӯӨпјҢжҖ»дҝқйҷ©иҙ№з”Ёдёәпјҡ
\[
300,000 \times 63.40 = 19,020,000 \text{зҫҺе…ғ}
\]

b) ж №жҚ®зңӢи·ҢзңӢж¶Ёе№ід»·е…ізі»пјҡ

\]

\]

иҝҷиҜҙжҳҺеҸҜд»ҘйҖҡиҝҮеҚ–еҮәжҢҮж•°зҡ„ \( S e^{-qT} \) еҜ№еә”ж•°йҮҸпјҢд№°е…ҘзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғе№¶д»Ҙж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮжҠ•иө„еү©дҪҷиө„йҮ‘пјҢжқҘеӨҚеҲ¶дёҖдёӘзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғгҖӮ
е…·дҪ“ж“ҚдҪңеҰӮдёӢпјҡ

2. д№°е…ҘиЎҢжқғд»·дёә 1140гҖҒжңҹйҷҗ6дёӘжңҲзҡ„зңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғпјҢж Үзҡ„жҢҮж•°ж•°йҮҸдёә300,000еҖҚпјҢ
3. е°Ҷеү©дҪҷиө„йҮ‘д»Ҙ6%зҡ„ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮиҝӣиЎҢжҠ•иө„гҖӮ

problems.explanationZh:
19.14
a) еҜ№дәҺдёҚж”Ҝд»ҳиӮЎжҒҜиӮЎзҘЁзҡ„ж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғпјҢ

\( \Delta = N(d_1) \)
\( \Gamma = \frac{N'(d_1)}{S \sigma \sqrt{T}} \)
\( \Theta = - \frac{S N'(d_1) \sigma}{2 \sqrt{T}} - r K e^{-r T} N(d_2) \)

еӣ жӯӨпјҢзӯүејҸпјҲ19.4пјүе·Ұиҫ№иЎЁиҫҫејҸдёәпјҡ

\[
\frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 S^2 \Gamma + r S \Delta + \Theta =
\frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 S^2 \frac{N'(d_1)}{S \sigma \sqrt{T}} + r S N(d_1) - \frac{S N'(d_1) \sigma}{2 \sqrt{T}} - r K e^{-r T} N(d_2) =
- r K e^{-r T} N(d_2) = -r \Pi
\]

b) еҜ№дәҺдёҚж”Ҝд»ҳиӮЎжҒҜиӮЎзҘЁзҡ„ж¬§ејҸзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғпјҢ

\( \Delta = - N(-d_1) = N(d_1) - 1 \)
\( \Gamma = \frac{N'(d_1)}{S \sigma \sqrt{T}} \)
\( \Theta = - \frac{S N'(d_1) \sigma}{2 \sqrt{T}} + r K e^{-r T} N(-d_2) \)

еӣ жӯӨпјҢзӯүејҸпјҲ19.4пјүе·Ұиҫ№иЎЁиҫҫејҸдёәпјҡ

\[
\frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 S^2 \Gamma + r S \Delta + \Theta =
\frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 S^2 \frac{N'(d_1)}{S \sigma \sqrt{T}} + r S (N(d_1) - 1) - \frac{S N'(d_1) \sigma}{2 \sqrt{T}} + r K e^{-r T} N(-d_2) =
- r (S - K e^{-r T}) + r \Pi = -r \Pi
\]

c) еҜ№дәҺдёҖдёӘжңҹжқғжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲ\( \Pi \)пјҢе…¶\( \Delta \)гҖҒ\( \Theta \)е’Ң\( \Gamma \)йғҪжҳҜе…¶еҚ•дёӘжңҹжқғзҡ„еҜ№еә”еҖјзҡ„еҠ е’ҢгҖӮз”ұжӯӨеҸҜзҹҘпјҢзӯүејҸпјҲ19.4пјүеҜ№дәҺд»»дҪ•дёҚж”Ҝд»ҳиӮЎжҒҜиӮЎзҘЁзҡ„ж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғе’ҢзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғз»„еҗҲеқҮжҲҗз«ӢгҖӮ

иҙ§еёҒзұ»дјјдәҺж”Ҝд»ҳиҝһз»ӯиӮЎжҒҜж”¶зӣҠзҺҮдёә fr зҡ„иӮЎзҘЁгҖӮдҫқиө–иҙ§еёҒзҡ„иЎҚз”ҹе“Ғз»„еҗҲзҡ„еҫ®еҲҶж–№зЁӢпјҲи§Ғж–№зЁӢ 17.6пјүжҳҜ

\[
\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 S^2 \frac{\partial^2 \Pi}{\partial S^2} + (r - f_r) S \frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial S} - r \Pi = 0
\]

еӣ жӯӨпјҢ

\[
\Theta + (r - f_r) S \Delta - r \Pi + \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 S^2 \Gamma = 0
\]

зұ»дјјең°пјҢеҜ№дәҺдҫқиө–жңҹиҙ§д»·ж ј F зҡ„иЎҚз”ҹе“Ғз»„еҗҲпјҲи§Ғж–№зЁӢ 18.6пјүпјҢеҫ®еҲҶж–№зЁӢдёә

\[
\Theta + \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 F^2 \Gamma - r \Pi = 0
\]

problems.promptEn: Practice 19.16. Suppose that USD 70 billion of equity assets are the subject of portfolio insurance schemes. Assume that the schemes are designed to provide insurance against the value of the assets declining by more than 5% within 1 year. Making whatever estimates you find necessary, use the DerivaGem software to calculate the value of the stock or futures contracts that the administrators of the portfolio insurance schemes will attempt to sell if the market falls by 23% in a single day.

problems.promptEn: Practice 19.18. A bankвҖҷs position in options on the dollar/CAD exchange rate has a delta of 30,000 and a gamma of -80,000. Explain how these numbers can be interpreted. The exchange rate (dollars per CAD) is 0.90. What position would you take to make the position delta neutral? After a short period of time, the exchange rate moves to 0.93. Estimate the new delta. What additional trade is necessary to keep the position delta neutral? Assuming the bank did set up a delta-neutral position originally, has it gained or lost money from the exchange rate movement?

problems.explanationZh:
19.19
(a) еҜ№дәҺдёҚж”Ҝд»ҳиӮЎжҒҜзҡ„иӮЎзҘЁпјҢputвҖ“call parity еңЁдёҖиҲ¬ж—¶еҲ» t з»ҷеҮәе…ізі»пјҡ

c + Ke^{-r(T-t)} = p + S

еҜ№ S жұӮеҒҸеҜјж•°еҫ—еҲ°пјҡ

\frac{\partial p}{\partial S} + 0 = \frac{\partial c}{\partial S} + 1

еҚі

\frac{\partial p}{\partial S} = \frac{\partial c}{\partial S} - 1

иҝҷиЎЁжҳҺж¬§ејҸзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғзҡ„ delta зӯүдәҺеҜ№еә”ж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„ delta еҮҸеҺ» 1гҖӮ

(b) еҜ№ S еҶҚж¬ЎжұӮдәҢйҳ¶еҜјж•°пјҡ

\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial S^2} = \frac{\partial^2 c}{\partial S^2}

еӣ жӯӨпјҢж¬§ејҸзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғзҡ„ gamma зӯүдәҺж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„ gammaгҖӮ

(c) еҜ№жіўеҠЁзҺҮ \sigma жұӮеҒҸеҜјж•°пјҡ

\frac{\partial p}{\partial \sigma} = \frac{\partial c}{\partial \sigma}

иҝҷиЎЁжҳҺж¬§ејҸзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғзҡ„ vega зӯүдәҺж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„ vegaгҖӮ

(d) еҜ№ж—¶й—ҙ t жұӮеҒҸеҜјж•°пјҡ

- rKe^{-r(T-t)} + \frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial c}{\partial t}

иҝҷдёҖе…ізі»з¬ҰеҗҲз¬¬ 19.5 иҠӮдёӯз»ҷеҮәзҡ„ж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғе’ҢзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғзҡ„ thetaгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 19.20. A financial institution has the following portfolio of over-the-counter options on sterling: Type Position Delta of option Gamma of option Vega of option Call -1,000 0.50 2.2 1.8 Call -500 0.80 0.6 0.2 Put -2,000 -0.40 1.3 0.7 Call -500 0.70 1.8 1.4 A traded option is available with a delta of 0.6, a gamma of 1.5, and a vega of 0.8. (a) What position in the traded option and in sterling would make the portfolio both gamma neutral and delta neutral? (b) What position in the traded option and in sterling would make the portfolio both vega neutral and delta neutral? Assume that all implied volatilities change by the same amount so that vegas can be aggregated.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 19.20. йҮ‘иһҚжңәжһ„жӢҘжңүд»ҘдёӢиӢұй•‘еңәеӨ–жңҹжқғжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲпјҡ зұ»еһӢ еӨҙеҜё жңҹжқғ Delta жңҹжқғ Gamma жңҹжқғ Vega зңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғ -1,000 0.50 2.2 1.8 зңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғ -500 0.80 0.6 0.2 зңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғ -2,000 -0.40 1.3 0.7 зңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғ -500 0.70 1.8 1.4 дәӨжҳ“жңҹжқғзҡ„ Delta дёә 0.6пјҢGamma дёә 1.5пјҢVega дёә 0.8гҖӮ (a) дәӨжҳ“жңҹжқғе’ҢиӢұй•‘зҡ„д»Җд№ҲеӨҙеҜёдјҡдҪҝжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲеҗҢж—¶иҫҫеҲ°дјҪзҺӣдёӯжҖ§е’Ңеҫ·е°”еЎ”дёӯжҖ§пјҹ (b) дәӨжҳ“жңҹжқғе’ҢиӢұй•‘зҡ„д»Җд№ҲеӨҙеҜёдјҡдҪҝжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲеҗҢж—¶е…·жңү vega дёӯжҖ§е’Ң delta дёӯжҖ§пјҹеҒҮи®ҫжүҖжңүйҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮеҸҳеҢ–зӣёеҗҢзҡ„йҮҸпјҢд»Ҙдҫҝ vegas еҸҜд»ҘиҒҡеҗҲгҖӮ

problems.explanationZh:
19.20
з»„еҗҲзҡ„ delta дёәпјҡ

-1,000 Г— 0.50 + (-500) Г— 0.80 + (-2,000) Г— (-0.40) + (-500) Г— 0.70 = -500 - 400 + 800 - 350 = -450гҖӮ

з»„еҗҲзҡ„ gamma дёәпјҡ

-1,000 Г— 2.2 + (-500) Г— 0.6 + (-2,000) Г— 1.3 + (-500) Г— 1.8 = -2,200 - 300 - 2,600 - 900 = -6,000гҖӮ

з»„еҗҲзҡ„ vega дёәпјҡ

-1,000 Г— 1.8 + (-500) Г— 0.2 + (-2,000) Г— 0.7 + (-500) Г— 1.4 = -1,800 - 100 - 1,400 - 700 = -4,000гҖӮ

(a) дёәдәҶдҪҝжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲ gamma дёӯжҖ§пјҢйЎ»жҢҒжңүдәӨжҳ“жңҹжқғзҡ„еӨҡеӨҙеӨҙеҜё xпјҢдҪҝеҫ—пјҡ

6,000 + 1.5x = 0 вҶ’ x = 4,000гҖӮ

жҢҒжңү 4,000 дёӘдәӨжҳ“жңҹжқғеҗҺпјҢз»„еҗҲзҡ„ delta дёәпјҡ

-450 + 0.6 Г— 4,000 = -450 + 2,400 = 1,950гҖӮ

дёәдәҶдҪҝз»„еҗҲ delta дёӯжҖ§пјҢйңҖеҚ–з©ә 1,950 иӢұй•‘гҖӮ

еӣ жӯӨпјҢз»„еҗҲеңЁдәӨжҳ“жңҹжқғдёӯжҢҒжңүеӨҡеӨҙ 4,000 д»ҪпјҢеҗҢж—¶еҚ–з©ә 1,950 иӢұй•‘пјҢд»Ҙе®һзҺ° gamma е’Ң delta еҸҢдёӯжҖ§гҖӮ

(b) дёәдәҶдҪҝжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲ vega дёӯжҖ§пјҢйЎ»жҢҒжңүдәӨжҳ“жңҹжқғзҡ„еӨҡеӨҙеӨҙеҜё yпјҢдҪҝеҫ—пјҡ

-4,000 + 0.8y = 0 вҶ’ y = 5,000гҖӮ

жҢҒжңү 5,000 дёӘдәӨжҳ“жңҹжқғеҗҺпјҢз»„еҗҲзҡ„ delta дёәпјҡ

-450 + 0.6 Г— 5,000 = -450 + 3,000 = 2,550гҖӮ

дёәдәҶдҪҝз»„еҗҲ delta дёӯжҖ§пјҢйңҖеҚ–з©ә 2,550 иӢұй•‘гҖӮ

еӣ жӯӨпјҢз»„еҗҲеңЁдәӨжҳ“жңҹжқғдёӯжҢҒжңүеӨҡеӨҙ 5,000 д»ҪпјҢеҗҢж—¶еҚ–з©ә 2,550 иӢұй•‘пјҢд»Ҙе®һзҺ° vega е’Ң delta еҸҢдёӯжҖ§гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 19.22. A deposit instrument offered by a bank guarantees that investors will receive a return during a 6-month period that is the greater of (a) zero and (b) 40% of the return provided by a market index. An investor is planning to put USD 100,000 in the instrument. Describe the payoff as an option on the index. Assuming that the risk-free rate of interest is 8% per annum, the dividend yield on the index is 3% per annum, and the volatility of the index is 25% per annum, is the product a good deal for the investor?

problems.promptEn: Practice 19.23. The formula for the price c of a European call futures option in terms of the futures price F0 is given in Chapter 18 as c = e-rT 3F0N1d12 - K N1d224 where d1 = ln1F0>K2 + s2T>2 s2T and d2 = d1 - s2T The Greek Letters 449 and K, r, T, and s are the strike price, interest rate, time to maturity, and volatility, respectively. (a) Prove that F0NвҖІ1d12 = K NвҖІ1d22. (b) Prove that the delta of the call price with respect to the futures price is e-rT N1d12. (c) Prove that the vega of the call price is F01T NвҖІ1d12e-rT. (d) Prove the formula for the rho of a call futures option given in Section 19.12. The delta, gamma, theta, and vega of a call futures option are the same as those for a call option on a stock paying dividends at rate q, with q replaced by r and S0 replaced by F0. Explain why the same is not true of the rho of a call futures option.

problems.explanationZh:
19.23
(a)

иҜҒжҳҺ F_0 N'(d_1) = K N'(d_2)гҖӮ

жҲ‘д»¬дҪҝз”ЁеҜјж•°з¬ҰеҸ·пјҡ

N'(d) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-d^2/2}.

е…¶дёӯ

d_1 = \frac{\ln(F_0 / K) + \frac{Пғ^2}{2} T}{Пғ \sqrt{T}},

d_2 = d_1 - Пғ \sqrt{T}.

и®Ўз®—е·Ұдҫ§пјҡ

F_0 N'(d_1) = F_0 \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-d_1^2 / 2}.

еҸідҫ§пјҡ

K N'(d_2) = K \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-d_2^2 / 2}.

еӣ дёә

ln(F_0 / K) + \frac{Пғ^2}{2} T = d_1 Пғ \sqrt{T},

дё”

-d_1^2/2 + ln(F_0/K) + \frac{Пғ^2}{2} T = -d_2^2/2,

дёӨиҫ№зӣёзӯүпјҢеҫ—иҜҒгҖӮ

(b)

зңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғд»·ж јдёә

c = e^{-rT} (F_0 N(d_1) - K N(d_2)).

и®Ўз®—еҜ№ F_0 зҡ„еҒҸеҜјпјҡ

\frac{\partial c}{\partial F_0} = e^{-rT} \left( N(d_1) + F_0 N'(d_1) \frac{\partial d_1}{\partial F_0} - K N'(d_2) \frac{\partial d_2}{\partial F_0} \right).

еӣ дёә

\frac{\partial d_1}{\partial F_0} = \frac{1}{F_0 Пғ \sqrt{T}},

\frac{\partial d_2}{\partial F_0} = \frac{\partial d_1}{\partial F_0}.

еҲ©з”Ё(a)дёӯз»“жһңпјҢйЎ№ F_0 N'(d_1) е’Ң K N'(d_2) жҠөж¶ҲпјҢж•…

\frac{\partial c}{\partial F_0} = e^{-rT} N(d_1).

(c)

и®Ўз®—еҜ№жіўеҠЁзҺҮ Пғ зҡ„еҒҸеҜјпјҡ

\frac{\partial c}{\partial Пғ} = e^{-rT} \left( F_0 N'(d_1) \frac{\partial d_1}{\partial Пғ} - K N'(d_2) \frac{\partial d_2}{\partial Пғ} \right).

еӣ дёә

\frac{\partial d_1}{\partial Пғ} = \sqrt{T} / 2 - \frac{\ln(F_0 / K) + (r - q + \frac{Пғ^2}{2})T}{Пғ^2 \sqrt{T}} = \sqrt{T} / 2 - \frac{d_1 Пғ \sqrt{T}}{Пғ^2 \sqrt{T}} = \frac{\sqrt{T}}{2} - \frac{d_1}{Пғ},

дё”

\frac{\partial d_2}{\partial Пғ} = \frac{\partial d_1}{\partial Пғ} - \sqrt{T}.

е°ҶдёҠиҝ°еёҰе…ҘпјҢеҲ©з”Ё(a)дёӯзҡ„е…ізі»пјҢжңҖз»Ҳеҫ—еҲ°

\frac{\partial c}{\partial Пғ} = F_0 e^{-rT} \sqrt{T} N'(d_1).

(d)

Rho е®ҡд№үдёәеҜ№еҲ©зҺҮ r зҡ„еҒҸеҜјж•°пјҢи®Ўз®—

\frac{\partial c}{\partial r} = e^{-rT} (F_0 N(d_1) - K N(d_2)) (-T) + e^{-rT} \left( F_0 N'(d_1) \frac{\partial d_1}{\partial r} - K N'(d_2) \frac{\partial d_2}{\partial r} \right).

жңҹиҙ§жңҹжқғдёӯпјҢq = rпјҢж•… rho жқҘиҮӘдёӨдёӘйғЁеҲҶпјҡеҜ№ r зҡ„зӣҙжҺҘеҒҸеҜјд»ҘеҸҠеҜ№ q зҡ„й—ҙжҺҘеҒҸеҜјгҖӮ

з”ұдәҺжңҹиҙ§жңҹжқғзҡ„ж Үзҡ„иҙ№з”Ёз»“жһ„дёҚеҗҢдәҺеёҰиӮЎжҒҜзҺҮ q зҡ„иӮЎзҘЁжңҹжқғпјҢеӣ жӯӨзңӢж¶Ёжңҹиҙ§жңҹжқғзҡ„ rho дёҚеҗҢдәҺиӮЎзҘЁзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғгҖӮз»јдёҠиҜҒжҳҺдәҶй—®йўҳдёӯзҡ„е…¬ејҸе’Ңзү№жҖ§гҖӮ

20.2
Jumps tend to make both tails of the stock price distribution heavier than those of the
lognormal distribution. This creates a volatility smile similar to that in Figure 20.1. The
volatility smile is likely to be more pronounced for the three-month option.

problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.

20.3

buy the put, buy the stock, and sell the call.

problems.promptEn: Practice 20.4. Explain carefully why a distribution with a heavier left tail and less heavy right tail than the lognormal distribution gives rise to a downward sloping volatility smile.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 20.4. д»”з»Ҷи§ЈйҮҠдёәд»Җд№ҲдёҺеҜ№ж•°жӯЈжҖҒеҲҶеёғзӣёжҜ”пјҢе·Ұе°ҫиҫғйҮҚдё”еҸіе°ҫиҫғиҪ»зҡ„еҲҶеёғдјҡдә§з”ҹеҗ‘дёӢеҖҫж–ңзҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮеҫ®з¬‘гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
20.4
The heavier left tail should lead to high prices, and therefore high implied volatilities, for out-
of-the-money (low-strike-price) puts. Similarly, the less heavy right tail should lead to low
prices, and therefore low volatilities for out-of-the-money (high-strike-price) calls. A
volatility smile where volatility is a decreasing function of strike price results.
problems.explanationEn:
20.4

of-the-money (low-strike-price) puts. Similarly, the less heavy right tail should lead to low
prices, and therefore low volatilities for out-of-the-money (high-strike-price) calls. A
volatility smile where volatility is a decreasing function of strike price results.

problems.promptEn: Practice 20.6. Explain what is meant by вҖңcrashophobia.вҖқ
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 20.6. и§ЈйҮҠдёҖдёӢвҖңcrashophobiaвҖқжҳҜд»Җд№Ҳж„ҸжҖқгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
20.6
The crashophobia argument is an attempt to explain the pronounced volatility skew in equity
markets since 1987. (This was the year equity markets shocked everyone by crashing more
than 20% in one day). The argument is that traders are concerned about another crash and as
a result increase the price of out-of-the-money puts. This creates the volatility skew.
problems.explanationEn:

markets since 1987. (This was the year equity markets shocked everyone by crashing more
than 20% in one day). The argument is that traders are concerned about another crash and as
a result increase the price of out-of-the-money puts. This creates the volatility skew.

problems.promptEn: Practice 20.7. A stock price is currently USD 20. Tomorrow, news is expected to be announced that will either increase the price by USD 5 or decrease the price by USD 5. What are the problems in using BlackвҖ“ScholesвҖ“Merton to value 1-month options on the stock?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 20.7. зӣ®еүҚиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јдёә \$20. Tomorrow, news is expected to be announced that will either increase the price by \$5 жҲ–е°Ҷд»·ж јйҷҚдҪҺ 5 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮдҪҝз”ЁеёғиҺұе…Ӣ-ж–Ҝз§‘е°”ж–Ҝ-й»ҳйЎҝжі•еҜ№иӮЎзҘЁ 1 дёӘжңҲжңҹжқғиҝӣиЎҢдј°еҖјжңүе“Әдәӣй—®йўҳпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
20.7
The probability distribution of the stock price in one month is not lognormal. Possibly, it
consists of two lognormal distributions superimposed upon each other and is bimodal. BlackвҖ“
Scholes is clearly inappropriate, because it assumes that the stock price at any future time is
lognormal.

problems.promptEn: Practice 20.8. What volatility smile is likely to be observed for 6-month options when the volatility is uncertain and positively correlated with the stock price?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 20.8. еҪ“жіўеҠЁжҖ§дёҚзЎ®е®ҡдё”дёҺиӮЎд»·жӯЈзӣёе…іж—¶пјҢ6 дёӘжңҲжңҹжңҹжқғеҸҜиғҪдјҡеҮәзҺ°д»Җд№Ҳж ·зҡ„жіўеҠЁжҖ§еҫ®з¬‘пјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
20.8
When the asset price is positively correlated with volatility, the volatility tends to increase as

the asset price increases, producing less heavy left tails and heavier right tails. Implied
volatility then increases with the strike price.
problems.explanationEn:
20.8
When the asset price is positively correlated with volatility, the volatility tends to increase as

the asset price increases, producing less heavy left tails and heavier right tails. Implied
volatility then increases with the strike price.

problems.promptEn: Practice 20.9. Explain the problems in testing a stock option pricing model empirically.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 20.9. и§ЈйҮҠе®һиҜҒжЈҖйӘҢиӮЎзҘЁжңҹжқғе®ҡд»·жЁЎеһӢзҡ„й—®йўҳгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
20.9
There are a number of problems in testing an option pricing model empirically. These include
the problem of obtaining synchronous data on stock prices and option prices, the problem of
estimating the dividends that will be paid on the stock during the optionвҖҷs life, the problem of
distinguishing between situations where the market is inefficient and situations where the
option pricing model is incorrect, and the problems of estimating stock price volatility.

problems.promptEn: Practice 20.10. Suppose that a central bank's policy is to allow an exchange rate to fluctuate between 0.97 and 1.03. What pattern of implied volatilities for options on the exchange rate would you expect to see?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 20.10. еҒҮи®ҫдёӯеӨ®й“¶иЎҢзҡ„ж”ҝзӯ–жҳҜе…Ғи®ёжұҮзҺҮеңЁ0.97 е’Ң1.03 д№Ӣй—ҙжіўеҠЁгҖӮжӮЁжңҹжңӣзңӢеҲ°жұҮзҺҮжңҹжқғзҡ„йҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮжҳҜд»Җд№Ҳж ·зҡ„пјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
20.10
In this case, the probability distribution of the exchange rate has a thin left tail and a thin right
tail relative to the lognormal distribution. We are in the opposite situation to that described
for foreign currencies in Section 20.2. Both out-of-the-money and in-the-money calls and
puts can be expected to have lower implied volatilities than at-the-money calls and puts. The
pattern of implied volatilities is likely to be similar to Figure 20.7.

problems.promptEn: Practice 20.11. Option traders sometimes refer to deep-out-of-the-money options as being options on volatility. Why do you think they do this?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 20.11. жңҹжқғдәӨжҳ“иҖ…жңүж—¶е°Ҷж·ұеәҰиҷҡеҖјжңҹжқғз§°дёәжіўеҠЁжҖ§жңҹжқғгҖӮдҪ и®Өдёәд»–д»¬дёәд»Җд№Ҳиҝҷж ·еҒҡпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
20.11
A deep-out-of-the-money option has a low value. Decreases in its volatility reduce its value.
However, this reduction is small because the value can never go below zero. Increases in its
volatility, on the other hand, can lead to significant percentage increases in the value of the
option. The option does, therefore, have some of the same attributes as an option on
volatility.
problems.explanationEn:

problems.promptEn: Practice 20.12. A European call option on a certain stock has a strike price of USD 30, a time to maturity of 1 year, and an implied volatility of 30%. A European put option on the same stock has a strike price of USD 30, a time to maturity of 1 year, and an implied volatility of 33%. What is the arbitrage opportunity open to a trader? Does the arbitrage work only when the lognormal assumption underlying BlackвҖ“ScholesвҖ“Merton holds? Explain carefully the reasons for your answer.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 20.12. жҹҗиӮЎзҘЁзҡ„ж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„жү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә\$30, a time to maturity of 1 year, and an implied volatility of 30%. A European put option on the same stock has a strike price of \$30пјҢеҲ°жңҹж—¶й—ҙдёә1е№ҙпјҢйҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә33%гҖӮеҗ‘дәӨжҳ“иҖ…ејҖж”ҫзҡ„еҘ—еҲ©жңәдјҡжҳҜд»Җд№ҲпјҹеҸӘжңүеҪ“еёғиҺұе…Ӣ-ж–Ҝз§‘е°”ж–Ҝ-й»ҳйЎҝзҡ„еҜ№ж•°жӯЈжҖҒеҒҮи®ҫжҲҗз«Ӣж—¶пјҢеҘ—еҲ©жүҚжңүж•Ҳеҗ—пјҹд»”з»Ҷи§ЈйҮҠдҪ зҡ„зӯ”жЎҲзҡ„еҺҹеӣ гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
20.12
PutвҖ“call parity implies that European put and call options have the same implied volatility. If
a call option has an implied volatility of 30% and a put option has an implied volatility of
33%, the call is priced too low relative to the put. The correct trading strategy is to buy the
call, sell the put and short the stock. This does not depend on the lognormal assumption
underlying BlackвҖ“ScholesвҖ“Merton. PutвҖ“call parity is true for any set of assumptions.

problems.promptEn: Practice 20.13. Suppose that the result of a major lawsuit affecting a company is due to be announced tomorrow. The companyвҖҷs stock price is currently USD 60. If the ruling is favorable to the company, the stock price is expected to jump to USD 75. If it is unfavorable, the stock is expected to jump to USD 50. What is the risk-neutral probability of a favorable ruling? Assume that the volatility of the companyвҖҷs stock will be 25% for 6 months after the ruling if the ruling is favorable and 40% if it is unfavorable. Use DerivaGem to calculate the relationship between implied volatility and strike price for 6-month European call options today. The company pays no dividends. Assume that the 6-month risk-free interest rate is 6%. Consider strike prices of USD 30, USD 40, USD 50, USD 60, USD 70, and USD 80.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 20.13. еҒҮи®ҫеҪұе“Қжҹҗе…¬еҸёзҡ„йҮҚеӨ§иҜүи®јз»“жһңе°ҶеңЁжҳҺеӨ©е…¬еёғгҖӮиҜҘе…¬еҸёеҪ“еүҚиӮЎд»·дёә 60 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮеҰӮжһңиЈҒеҶіеҜ№е…¬еҸёжңүеҲ©пјҢйў„и®ЎиӮЎд»·е°Ҷи·іеҚҮиҮі 75 зҫҺе…ғпјӣеҰӮжһңиЈҒеҶідёҚеҲ©пјҢйў„и®ЎиӮЎд»·е°ҶеҸҳдёә 50 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮжңүеҲ©иЈҒеҶізҡ„йЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§жҰӮзҺҮжҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹеҒҮи®ҫиӢҘиЈҒеҶіжңүеҲ©пјҢиЈҒеҶіеҗҺ 6 дёӘжңҲеҶ…е…¬еҸёиӮЎзҘЁжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә 25%пјӣиӢҘиЈҒеҶідёҚеҲ©пјҢжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә 40%гҖӮе…¬еҸёдёҚж”Ҝд»ҳиӮЎжҒҜпјҢ6 дёӘжңҲж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёә 6%гҖӮдҪҝз”Ё DerivaGem и®Ўз®—д»ҠеӨ© 6 дёӘжңҲжңҹж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„йҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮдёҺжү§иЎҢд»·ж јд№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„е…ізі»пјҢжү§иЎҢд»·ж јеҲҶеҲ«дёә 30гҖҒ40гҖҒ50гҖҒ60гҖҒ70 е’Ң 80 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
20.13
Let p be the risk-neutral probability of a favorable ruling. Ignoring one day's expected return, the current stock price must equal the expected stock price tomorrow: 75p + 50(1-p) = 60. Hence p=0.4.

For K=50, if the ruling is favorable, use S_0=75, r=0.06, T=0.5, and sigma=25%; DerivaGem gives a call price of 26.502. If the ruling is unfavorable, use S_0=50, r=0.06, T=0.5, and sigma=40%; DerivaGem gives a call price of 6.310. The current call value is the weighted average 0.4*26.502 + 0.6*6.310 = 14.387. With S_0=60, K=50, T=0.5, r=0.06, and c=14.387, the implied volatility is 47.76%.

Repeating this calculation across strike prices gives the smile shown in the official solution: implied volatilities are approximately 46.67%, 47.78%, 47.76%, 46.05%, 43.22%, and 40.36% for strikes 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, and 80 respectively.

problems.promptEn: Practice 20.14. An exchange rate is currently 0.8000. The volatility of the exchange rate is quoted as 12% and interest rates in the two countries are the same. Using the lognormal assumption, estimate the probability that the exchange rate in 3 months will be (a) less than 0.7000, (b) between 0.7000 and 0.7500, (c) between 0.7500 and 0.8000, (d) between 0.8000 and 0.8500, (e) between 0.8500 and 0.9000, and (f) greater than 0.9000. Based on the volatility smile usually observed in the market for exchange rates, which of these estimates would you expect to be too low and which would you expect to be too high?

problems.promptEn: Practice 20.15. A stock price is USD 40. A 6-month European call option on the stock with a strike price of USD 30 has an implied volatility of 35%. A 6-month European call option on the stock with a strike price of USD 50 has an implied volatility of 28%. The 6-month risk-free rate is 5% and no dividends are expected. Explain why the two implied volatilities are different. Use DerivaGem to calculate the prices of the two options. Use putвҖ“call parity to calculate the prices of 6-month European put options with strike prices of USD 30 and USD 50. Use DerivaGem to calculate the implied volatilities of these two put options.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 20.15. иӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јдёә\$40. A 6-month European call option on the stock with a strike price of \$30пјҢйҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә35%гҖӮиӮЎзҘЁзҡ„ 6 дёӘжңҲж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғпјҢжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә \$50 has an implied volatility of 28%. The 6-month risk-free rate is 5% and no dividends are expected. Explain why the two implied volatilities are different. Use DerivaGem to calculate the prices of the two options. Use putвҖ“call parity to calculate the prices of 6-month European put options with strike prices of \$30 е’Ң 50 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮдҪҝз”Ё DerivaGem и®Ўз®—иҝҷдёӨдёӘзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғзҡ„йҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
20.15
The difference between the two implied volatilities is consistent with Figure 20.3 in the text.
For equities, the volatility smile is downward sloping. A high strike price option has a lower
implied volatility than a low strike price option. The reason is that traders consider the

probability of a large downward movement in the stock price is higher than that predicted by
the lognormal probability distribution. The implied distribution assumed by traders is shown
in Figure 20.4.
To use DerivaGem to calculate the price of the first option, proceed as follows. Select Equity
as the Underlying Type in the first worksheet. Select BlackвҖ“Scholes European as the Option
Type. Input the stock price as 40, volatility as 35%, risk-free rate as 5%, time to exercise as
0.5 year, and exercise price as 30. Leave the dividend table blank because we are assuming
no dividends. Select the button corresponding to call. Do not select the implied volatility
button. Hit the Enter key and click on calculate. DerivaGem will show the price of the option
as 11.155. Change the volatility to 28% and the strike price to 50. Hit the Enter key and click
on calculate. DerivaGem will show the price of the option as 0.725.

To use DerivaGem to calculate the implied volatility of the first put option, input the stock
price as 40, the risk-free rate as 5%, time to exercise as 0.5 year, and the exercise price as 30.
Input the price as 0.414 in the second half of the Option Data table. Select the buttons for a
put option and implied volatility. Hit the Enter key and click on calculate. DerivaGem will
show the implied volatility as 34.99%.
Similarly, to use DerivaGem to calculate the implied volatility of the first put option, input
the stock price as 40, the risk-free rate as 5%, time to exercise as 0.5 year, and the exercise
price as 50. Input the price as 9.490 in the second half of the Option Data table. Select the
buttons for a put option and implied volatility. Hit the Enter key and click on calculate.
DerivaGem will show the implied volatility as 27.99%.
These results are what we would expect. DerivaGem gives the implied volatility of a put with
strike price 30 to be almost exactly the same as the implied volatility of a call with a strike
price of 30. Similarly, it gives the implied volatility of a put with strike price 50 to be almost
exactly the same as the implied volatility of a call with a strike price of 50.

20.15
дёӨдёӘйҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮзҡ„е·®ејӮдёҺиҜҫжң¬дёӯеӣҫ 20.3 дёҖиҮҙгҖӮ
еҜ№дәҺиӮЎзҘЁзұ»иө„дә§пјҢжіўеҠЁзҺҮеҫ®з¬‘е‘ҲдёӢйҷҚи¶ӢеҠҝгҖӮй«ҳиЎҢжқғд»·жңҹжқғзҡ„йҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮдҪҺдәҺдҪҺиЎҢжқғд»·жңҹжқғгҖӮеҺҹеӣ еңЁдәҺдәӨжҳ“иҖ…и®ӨдёәиӮЎд»·еҮәзҺ°еӨ§е№…дёӢи·Ңзҡ„жҰӮзҺҮжҜ”еҜ№ж•°жӯЈжҖҒеҲҶеёғйў„жөӢзҡ„иҰҒй«ҳгҖӮдәӨжҳ“иҖ…еҒҮи®ҫзҡ„йҡҗеҗ«еҲҶеёғеҰӮеӣҫ 20.4 жүҖзӨәгҖӮ

дҪҝз”Ё DerivaGem и®Ўз®—з¬¬дёҖдёӘжңҹжқғд»·ж јпјҢжӯҘйӘӨеҰӮдёӢгҖӮз¬¬дёҖдёӘе·ҘдҪңиЎЁдёӯйҖүжӢ©вҖңEquityвҖқдҪңдёәж Үзҡ„иө„дә§зұ»еһӢгҖӮйҖүжӢ©вҖңBlackвҖ“Scholes EuropeanвҖқдҪңдёәжңҹжқғзұ»еһӢгҖӮиҫ“е…ҘиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јдёә 40пјҢжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә 35%пјҢж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёә 5%пјҢеҲ°жңҹж—¶й—ҙдёә 0.5 е№ҙпјҢиЎҢжқғд»·дёә 30гҖӮз”ұдәҺеҒҮи®ҫж— иӮЎжҒҜпјҢиӮЎжҒҜиЎЁз•ҷз©әгҖӮйҖүжӢ©зңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғжҢүй’®пјҢдёҚйҖүйҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮжҢүй’®гҖӮжҢүеӣһиҪҰеҗҺзӮ№еҮ»и®Ўз®—пјҢDerivaGem жҳҫзӨәжңҹжқғд»·ж јдёә 11.155гҖӮе°ҶжіўеҠЁзҺҮж”№дёә 28%пјҢиЎҢжқғд»·ж”№дёә 50пјҢжҢүеӣһиҪҰзӮ№еҮ»и®Ўз®—пјҢDerivaGem жҳҫзӨәжңҹжқғд»·ж јдёә 0.725гҖӮ

зңӢж¶ЁзңӢи·Ңе№ід»·е…ізі»дёә

\[ c + Ke^{-rT} = p + S \]

еҚі

\[ p = c + Ke^{-rT} - S \]

еҜ№дәҺз¬¬дёҖдёӘжңҹжқғпјҢ
\[ c=11.155, \quad S=40, \quad r=0.05, \quad K=30, \quad T=0.5 \]пјҢи®Ўз®—еҫ—

\[ p = 11.155 + 30 e^{-0.05 \times 0.5} - 40 = 0.414 \]

еҜ№дәҺз¬¬дәҢдёӘжңҹжқғпјҢ
\[ c=0.725, \quad S=40, \quad r=0.06, \quad K=50, \quad T=0.5 \]пјҢи®Ўз®—еҫ—

\[ p = 0.725 + 50 e^{-0.05 \times 0.5} - 40 = 9.490 \]


дҪҝз”Ё DerivaGem и®Ўз®—з¬¬дёҖдёӘзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғйҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮпјҢиҫ“е…ҘиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж ј 40пјҢж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮ 5%пјҢж—¶й—ҙ 0.5 е№ҙпјҢиЎҢжқғд»· 30пјҢеңЁжңҹжқғж•°жҚ®иЎЁз¬¬дәҢйғЁеҲҶдёӯиҫ“е…Ҙд»·ж ј 0.414пјҢйҖүжӢ©зңӢи·Ңе’Ңйҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮжҢүй’®пјҢеӣһиҪҰе№¶и®Ўз®—пјҢDerivaGem жҳҫзӨәйҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә 34.99%гҖӮ

зұ»дјјең°пјҢи®Ўз®—з¬¬дәҢдёӘзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғйҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮпјҢиҫ“е…ҘиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж ј 40пјҢж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮ 5%пјҢж—¶й—ҙ 0.5 е№ҙпјҢиЎҢжқғд»· 50пјҢеңЁж•°жҚ®иЎЁдёӯиҫ“е…Ҙд»·ж ј 9.490пјҢйҖүжӢ©зңӢи·Ңе’Ңйҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮжҢүй’®пјҢеӣһиҪҰе№¶и®Ўз®—пјҢDerivaGem жҳҫзӨәйҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә 27.99%гҖӮ

иҝҷдәӣз»“жһңз¬ҰеҗҲйў„жңҹгҖӮDerivaGem жҳҫзӨәиЎҢжқғд»· 30 зҡ„зңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғйҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮеҮ д№ҺзӯүдәҺиЎҢжқғд»· 30 зҡ„зңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғйҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮпјҢеҗҢзҗҶиЎҢжқғд»· 50 зҡ„зңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғйҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮд№ҹеҮ д№ҺзӯүдәҺеҜ№еә”зңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„йҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 20.16. вҖңThe BlackвҖ“ScholesвҖ“Merton model is used by traders as an interpolation tool.вҖқ Discuss this view.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 20.16. вҖңеёғиҺұе…Ӣ-ж–Ҝз§‘е°”ж–Ҝ-й»ҳйЎҝжЁЎеһӢиў«дәӨжҳ“иҖ…з”ЁдҪңжҸ’еҖје·Ҙе…·гҖӮвҖқи®Ёи®әиҝҷдёӘи§ӮзӮ№гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
20.16
When plain vanilla call and put options are being priced, traders do use the BlackвҖ“ScholesвҖ“
Merton model as an interpolation tool. They calculate implied volatilities for the options
whose prices they can observe in the market. By interpolating between strike prices and
between times to maturity, they estimate implied volatilities for other options. These implied
volatilities are then substituted into BlackвҖ“ScholesвҖ“Merton to calculate prices for these
options. In practice, much of the work in producing a table such as Table 20.2 in the over-the-
counter market is done by interdealer brokers. These brokers often act as intermediaries
between participants in the over-the-counter market and usually have more information on
the trades taking place than any individual financial institution. The brokers provide a table
such as Table 20.2 to their clients as a service.

1.00 and 1.05.

problems.explanation:
20.18
In liquidation, the companyвҖҷs stock price must be at least 300,000/100,000 = USD 3. The
companyвҖҷs stock price should therefore always be at least USD 3. This means that the stock price
distribution that has a thinner left tail and fatter right tail than the lognormal distribution. An
upward sloping volatility smile can be expected.

еҸҜд»ҘйҖҡиҝҮжҸ’еҖји®Ўз®—еҮәд»·ж јдёә 0.75 зҡ„ 6 дёӘжңҲжңҹжқғзҡ„йҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә 12.5%пјҢиҝҷж„Ҹе‘ізқҖжү§иЎҢд»·ж јеҲҶеҲ«дёә 0.7гҖҒ0.75 е’Ң 0.8 зҡ„жңҹжқғзҡ„йҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә 13%гҖҒ12.5% е’Ң 12%гҖӮ

ж №жҚ® DerivaGem иҪҜд»¶пјҢиҝҷдәӣжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·ж јеҲҶеҲ«дёә 0.2963 зҫҺе…ғгҖҒ0.2469 зҫҺе…ғе’Ң 0.1976 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮ

еҲ©з”ЁзӯүејҸ(20A.1)пјҢеҜ№дәҺжү§иЎҢд»·ж ј K=0.75пјҢд»·е·®О”=0.05пјҢи®Ўз®—еҫ—еҲ°пјҡ

\[
\frac{0.025}{0.5} \left(0.2963 + 0.1976 - 2 \times 0.2469 \right) = 0.0315
\]

зұ»дјји®Ўз®—еҫ—пјҡ

\[g_2 = 0.7241, \quad g_3 = 4.0788, \quad g_4 = 3.6766, \quad g_5 = 0.7285, \quad g_6 = 0.0898.\]

еҢәй—ҙ 0.7 еҲ° 1.3 зҡ„жҖ»жҰӮзҺҮдёәиҝҷдәӣеҖјзҡ„е’Ңд№ҳд»Ҙ 0.1пјҢеҚіпјҡ

\[
0.1 \times (0.0315 + 0.7241 + 4.0788 + 3.6766 + 0.7285 + 0.0898) = 0.9329.

еҰӮжһңжіўеҠЁзҺҮдҝқжҢҒе№іеқҰдёә 11.5%пјҢеҲҷеҜ№еә”зҡ„жҰӮзҺҮеҖјеҲҶеҲ«дёәпјҡ

problems.promptEn: Practice 20.23. Using Table 20.2, calculate the implied volatility a trader would use for an 11-month option with K>S0 = 0.98.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 20.23. дҪҝз”ЁиЎЁ 20.2пјҢи®Ўз®—дәӨжҳ“иҖ…з”ЁдәҺ K>S0 = 0.98 зҡ„ 11 дёӘжңҲжңҹжқғзҡ„йҡҗеҗ«жіўеҠЁзҺҮгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
20.23
Interpolation gives the volatility for a six-month option with a strike price of 98 as 12.82%.
Interpolation also gives the volatility for a 12-month option with a strike price of 98 as
13.7%. A final interpolation gives the volatility of an 11-month option with a strike price of
98 as 13.55%. The same answer is obtained if the sequence in which the interpolations are
done is reversed.

problems.promptEn: Practice 21.1. Which of the following can be estimated for an American option by constructing a single binomial tree: delta, gamma, vega, theta, rho?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 21.1. йҖҡиҝҮжһ„йҖ дёҖжЈөдәҢйЎ№ејҸж ‘еҸҜд»Ҙдј°и®ЎзҫҺејҸжңҹжқғзҡ„д»ҘдёӢе“ӘйЎ№пјҡdeltaгҖҒgammaгҖҒvegaгҖҒthetaгҖҒrhoпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
21.1
Delta, gamma, and theta can be determined from a single binomial tree. Vega is determined
by making a small change to the volatility and recomputing the option price using a new tree.
Rho is calculated by making a small change to the interest rate and recomputing the option
price using a new tree.

21.4
еңЁжӯӨжғ…еҶөдёӢпјҢ
еҪ“еүҚжңҹиҙ§д»·ж ј F_0 = 198 зҫҺеҲҶпјҢ
жү§иЎҢд»· K = 200 зҫҺеҲҶпјҢ
ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮ r = 0.08пјҢ
жіўеҠЁзҺҮ Пғ = 0.3пјҢ

еҚ•жӯҘж—¶й—ҙй—ҙйҡ” О”t = 0.25 е№ҙпјҲ3 дёӘжңҲпјүгҖӮ
еҗҢж—¶пјҢ

u = e^{ПғвҲҡ{О”t}} = 1.1618пјҢ
d = e^{-ПғвҲҡ{О”t}} = 0.8607пјҢ

problems.promptEn: Practice 21.5. Consider an option that pays off the amount by which the final stock price exceeds the average stock price achieved during the life of the option. Can this be valued using the binomial tree approach? Explain your answer.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 21.5. иҖғиҷ‘дёҖдёӘжңҹжқғпјҢе®ғж”Ҝд»ҳжңҖз»ҲиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж ји¶…иҝҮжңҹжқғжңүж•ҲжңҹеҶ…е®һзҺ°зҡ„е№іеқҮиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јзҡ„йҮ‘йўқгҖӮеҸҜд»ҘдҪҝз”ЁдәҢйЎ№ејҸж ‘ж–№жі•еҜ№е…¶иҝӣиЎҢиҜ„дј°еҗ—пјҹи§ЈйҮҠдҪ зҡ„зӯ”жЎҲгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
21.5
A binomial tree cannot be used in the way described in this chapter. This is an example of
what is known as a history-dependent option. The payoff depends on the path followed by the
stock price as well as its final value. The option cannot be valued by starting at the end of the
tree and working backward since the payoff at the final branches is not known
unambiguously. Chapter 27 describes an extension of the binomial tree approach that can be
used to handle options where the payoff depends on the average value of the stock price.

In Monte Carlo simulation, sample values for the derivative security in a risk-neutral world

for the underlying variables are first determined at time

In short, Monte Carlo simulation works by moving forward from time t to time T . Other
numerical procedures which accommodate early exercise work by moving backwards from
time T to time t .

numerical procedures which accommodate early exercise work by moving backwards from
time T to time t .

и®Ўз®—еҫ—еҲ°пјҡ
\begin{align*}
u &= e^{\sigma \sqrt{\Delta t}} = e^{0.30 \times \sqrt{0.25}} = 1.1618, \\
d &= e^{-\sigma \sqrt{\Delta t}} = e^{-0.30 \times \sqrt{0.25}} = 0.8607, \\
a &= e^{r \Delta t} = e^{0.05 \times 0.25} = 1.0126, \\
p &= \frac{a - d}{u - d} = \frac{1.0126 - 0.8607}{1.1618 - 0.8607} = 0.5043.
\end{align*}

problems.promptEn: Practice 21.13. A 1-year American put option on a non-dividend-paying stock has an exercise price of USD 18. The current stock price is USD 20, the risk-free interest rate is 15% per annum, and the volatility of the stock price is 40% per annum. Use the DerivaGem software with four 3-month time steps to estimate the value of the option. Display the tree and verify that the option prices at the final and penultimate nodes are correct. Use DerivaGem to value the European version of the option. Use the control variate technique to improve your estimate of the price of the American option.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 21.13. дёҖд»ҪдёҚжҙҫжҒҜиӮЎзҘЁзҡ„дёҖе№ҙжңҹзҫҺејҸзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғпјҢиЎҢжқғд»·ж јдёә\$18. The current stock price is \$20пјҢж— йЈҺйҷ©е№ҙеҲ©зҺҮдёә15%пјҢиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ40%гҖӮдҪҝз”Ёе…·жңүеӣӣдёӘ 3 дёӘжңҲж—¶й—ҙжӯҘй•ҝзҡ„ DerivaGem иҪҜд»¶жқҘдј°и®Ўжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјгҖӮжҳҫзӨәж ‘е№¶йӘҢиҜҒжңҖеҗҺдёҖдёӘе’ҢеҖ’ж•°з¬¬дәҢдёӘиҠӮзӮ№зҡ„жңҹжқғд»·ж јжҳҜеҗҰжӯЈзЎ®гҖӮдҪҝз”Ё DerivaGem еҜ№ж¬§жҙІзүҲжң¬зҡ„жңҹжқғиҝӣиЎҢдј°еҖјгҖӮдҪҝз”ЁжҺ§еҲ¶еҸҳйҮҸжҠҖжңҜжқҘж”№иҝӣжӮЁеҜ№зҫҺејҸжңҹжқғд»·ж јзҡ„дј°и®ЎгҖӮ

problems.explanationZh:
21.13
еңЁжң¬йўҳдёӯпјҡ

\[ S=20, \quad K=18, \quad r=0.15, \quad \sigma=0.40, \quad T=1, \quad \Delta t = 0.25 \]

ж ‘зҡ„еҸӮж•°дёәпјҡ

\[ u = e^{\sigma \sqrt{\Delta t}} = 1.2214 \]
\[ d = \frac{1}{u} = 0.8187 \]
\[ a = e^{r \Delta t} = 1.0382 \]
\[ p = \frac{a - d}{u - d} = 0.545 \]

DerivaGem дә§з”ҹзҡ„зҫҺејҸжңҹжқғж ‘еҰӮеӣҫ S21.7 жүҖзӨәгҖӮиҜҘзҫҺејҸжңҹжқғзҡ„дј°и®Ўд»·еҖјдёә 1.29 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮ

жҜҸдёӘиҠӮзӮ№пјҡ
дёҠж–№еҖјдёәж Үзҡ„иө„дә§д»·ж ј
дёӢж–№еҖјдёәжңҹжқғд»·ж ј
еҠ зІ—зҡ„еҖјиЎЁзӨәжңҹжқғеңЁиҜҘиҠӮзӮ№иў«иЎҢжқғ

е…·дҪ“иҠӮзӮ№иө„дә§д»·ж јзӨәдҫӢпјҡ44.51082, 36.44238, 29.83649, 24.42806, 20, 16.37462, 13.4064, 10.97623 зӯү

иҠӮзӮ№ж—¶й—ҙпјҡ0.0000пјҢ0.2500пјҢ0.5000пјҢ0.7500пјҢ1.0000

еӣҫ S21.7пјҡй—®йўҳ 21.13 зҡ„зҫҺејҸжңҹжқғдј°еҖјж ‘

еҗҢдёҖйў—ж ‘еҸҜз”ЁдәҺдј°еҖјж¬§ејҸзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғпјҢз»“жһңеҰӮеӣҫ S21.8гҖӮ
иҜҘж¬§ејҸжңҹжқғдј°и®Ўд»·еҖјдёә 1.14 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮ

ж¬§ејҸжңҹжқғзҡ„еҮҶзЎ®д»·ж јйҖҡиҝҮд»ҘдёӢи®Ўз®—еҫ—еҲ°пјҡ

\[
 d_1 = \frac{\ln(20 / 18) + (0.15 + 0.4^2 / 2) \times 1}{0.4 \sqrt{1}} = 0.8384
\]

\[
 d_2 = d_1 - 0.4 \sqrt{1} = 0.4384
\]

з”Ёж ҮеҮҶжӯЈжҖҒеҲҶеёғеҮҪж•°и®°дёә \( N(\cdot) \)пјҢж¬§ејҸзңӢи·Ңд»·дёәпјҡ

\[
 P = 18 e^{-0.15} N(-d_2) - 20 N(-d_1) = 1.10
\]

иҝҷд№ҹеҸҜд»Ҙз”Ё DerivaGem и®Ўз®—еҫ—еҲ°гҖӮжҺ§еҲ¶еҸҳйҮҸжі•дј°и®Ўзҡ„зҫҺејҸжңҹжқғд»·ж јдёәпјҡ

\[
1.29 + 1.10 - 1.14 = 1.25 \text{ зҫҺе…ғ}
\]

problems.promptEn: Practice 21.14. A 2-month American put option on a stock index has an exercise price of 480. The current level of the index is 484, the risk-free interest rate is 10% per annum, the dividend yield on the index is 3% per annum, and the volatility of the index is 25% per annum. Divide the life of the option into four half-month periods and use the tree approach to estimate the value of the option.

problems.promptEn: Practice 21.16. Suppose that Monte Carlo simulation is being used to evaluate a European call option on a non-dividend-paying stock when the volatility is stochastic. How could the control variate and antithetic variable technique be used to improve numerical efficiency? Explain why it is necessary to calculate six values of the option in each simulation trial when both the control variate and the antithetic variable technique are used.

problems.explanationZh:
21.17
еҜ№дәҺиҙ§еёҒзҡ„зҫҺејҸзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғпјҢеҒҸеҫ®еҲҶж–№зЁӢдёәпјҡ

\[ \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial S^2} \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 S^2 + \frac{\partial f}{\partial S} (r_d - r_f)S - \frac{\partial f}{\partial t} + r_d f = 0 \]

ж №жҚ®ж–Үдёӯзҡ„з¬ҰеҸ·иЎЁзӨәжі•пјҢжӯӨж–№зЁӢзҰ»ж•ЈеҢ–еҗҺеҸҳдёәпјҡ

\[ a_{i,j} f_{i,j-1} + b_{i,j} f_{i,j} + c_{i,j} f_{i,j+1} = d_{i,j} \]

е…¶дёӯпјҢ

\[ a_{j} = \frac{\Delta t}{2 \Delta S^2} \sigma^2 j^2 - \frac{\Delta t}{2 \Delta S} (r_d - r_f) j \\
   b_{j} = 1 + \Delta t r_d + \frac{\Delta t}{\Delta S^2} \sigma^2 j^2 \\
   c_{j} = - \frac{\Delta t}{2 \Delta S^2} \sigma^2 j^2 - \frac{\Delta t}{2 \Delta S} (r_d - r_f) j \]

дёҠиҝ°дёәйҡҗејҸжңүйҷҗе·®еҲҶжі•зҡ„зі»ж•°гҖӮ

иҫ№з•ҢжқЎд»¶пјҲеҜ№еә”еҺҹд№Ұдёӯж–№зЁӢпјҲ21.28пјүпјҢпјҲ21.29пјүе’ҢпјҲ21.30пјүпјүдёәпјҡ

\[ f_{0} = \max[0, S_0 - K] \]
\[ f_{M} = \max[0, S_M - K] \]

д»ҘеҸҠеҲқе§ӢжқЎд»¶е’Ңз»ҲжӯўжқЎд»¶зҡ„жҳ е°„гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 21.18. An American put option on a non-dividend-paying stock has 4 months to maturity. The exercise price is USD 21, the stock price is USD 20, the risk-free rate of interest is 10% per annum, and the volatility is 30% per annum. Use the explicit version of the finite difference approach to value the option. Use stock price intervals of USD 4 and time intervals of 1 month.

problems.promptEn: Practice 21.19. The spot price of copper is USD 0.60 per pound. Suppose that the futures prices (dollars per pound) are as follows: 3 months 0.59 6 months 0.57 9 months 0.54 12 months 0.50 The volatility of the price of copper is 40% per annum and the risk-free rate is 6% per annum. Use a binomial tree to value an American call option on copper with an exercise price of USD 0.60 and a time to maturity of 1 year. Divide the life of the option into four 3-month periods for the purposes of constructing the tree. (Hint: As explained in Section 18.6, the futures price of a variable is its expected future price in a risk- neutral world.)

problems.explanationZh:
21.19
еңЁжң¬йўҳдёӯпјҢ
\(\Delta t = 0.25\) е№ҙпјҢ
\(\sigma = 0.4\)пјҢеӣ жӯӨ

\[
u = e^{\sigma\sqrt{\Delta t}} = e^{0.4 \times \sqrt{0.25}} = e^{0.2} = 1.2214,\]

\[
d = e^{-\sigma\sqrt{\Delta t}} = e^{-0.2} = 0.8187.\]

жңҹиҙ§д»·ж јжҸҗдҫӣдәҶй“ңд»·еңЁйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§дё–з•ҢдёӢзҡ„еўһй•ҝзҺҮдј°и®ЎгҖӮ
з¬¬дёҖеӯЈеәҰпјҲдёүдёӘжңҲпјүжңҹй—ҙпјҢиҜҘеўһй•ҝзҺҮпјҲиҝһз»ӯеӨҚеҲ©пјүдёә

\[
\frac{1}{0.25} \ln\left(\frac{0.59}{0.60}\right) = 4 \ln\left(\frac{0.59}{0.60}\right) = -0.0672 = -6.72\%\text{жҜҸе№ҙ}.
\]

еӣ жӯӨпјҢз¬¬дёҖеӯЈеәҰзҡ„еҸӮж•°\(p\)дёә

\[
p = \frac{e^{rt} - d}{u - d} = \frac{e^{-0.0672 \times 0.25} - 0.8187}{1.2214 - 0.8187} = \frac{e^{-0.0168} - 0.8187}{0.4027} = \frac{0.9833 - 0.8187}{0.4027} = 0.4088.
\]

еҗҺз»ӯдёүдёӘеӯЈеәҰй“ңд»·зҡ„еўһй•ҝзҺҮеҲҶеҲ«дёә-13.79%гҖҒ-21.63%е’Ң-30.78%гҖӮ
еӣ жӯӨпјҢз¬¬дәҢеӯЈеәҰзҡ„еҸӮж•°\(p\)дёә

\[
p = \frac{e^{-0.1379 \times 0.25} - d}{u - d} = \frac{e^{-0.0345} - 0.8187}{0.4027} = \frac{0.9661 - 0.8187}{0.4027} = 0.3660.
\]

з¬¬дёүеӯЈеәҰзҡ„еҸӮж•°дёә

\[
p = \frac{e^{-0.2163 \times 0.25} - d}{u - d} = \frac{e^{-0.0541} - 0.8187}{0.4027} = \frac{0.9473 - 0.8187}{0.4027} = 0.3195.
\]

з¬¬еӣӣеӯЈеәҰзҡ„еҸӮж•°дёә

\[
p = \frac{e^{-0.3078 \times 0.25} - d}{u - d} = \frac{e^{-0.07695} - 0.8187}{0.4027} = \frac{0.9260 - 0.8187}{0.4027} = 0.2663.
\]

problems.promptEn: Practice 21.21. When do the boundary conditions for S = 0 and S -> infinity affect the estimates of derivative prices in the explicit finite difference method?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 21.21. еңЁжҳҫејҸжңүйҷҗе·®еҲҶжі•дёӯпјҢS = 0 е’Ң S -> infinity зҡ„иҫ№з•ҢжқЎд»¶д»Җд№Ҳж—¶еҖҷдјҡеҪұе“ҚиЎҚз”ҹе“Ғд»·ж јдј°и®Ўпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
21.21

Let S_t be the current asset price, S_max the highest asset price included in the grid, and S_min the lowest asset price included in the grid. Let Delta S be the stock-price step size and define Q_1=(S_t-S_min)/Delta S and Q_2=(S_max-S_t)/Delta S.

In the explicit finite difference method, information propagates one grid node per time step through the triangular calculation structure. The lower boundary at S_min can affect the current node only if the number of time intervals N is at least Q_1. The upper boundary at S_max can affect it only if N is at least Q_2.

Therefore the assumed boundary values affect the derivative price estimate when N >= min(Q_1,Q_2) for at least one boundary, and both boundaries can affect it when N >= max(Q_1,Q_2). If the grid boundaries are sufficiently far away relative to N, their assumed values do not influence the estimate at the current asset price.
problems.explanationEn:
21.21

In the explicit finite difference method, information propagates one grid node per time step through the triangular calculation structure. The lower boundary at S_min can affect the current node only if the number of time intervals N is at least Q_1. The upper boundary at S_max can affect it only if N is at least Q_2.

Therefore the assumed boundary values affect the derivative price estimate when N >= min(Q_1,Q_2) for at least one boundary, and both boundaries can affect it when N >= max(Q_1,Q_2). If the grid boundaries are sufficiently far away relative to N, their assumed values do not influence the estimate at the current asset price.
problems.explanationZh:
21.21

и®ҫ S_t дёәеҪ“еүҚиө„дә§д»·ж јпјҢS_max дёәзҪ‘ж јдёӯиҖғиҷ‘зҡ„жңҖй«ҳиө„дә§д»·ж јпјҢS_min дёәзҪ‘ж јдёӯиҖғиҷ‘зҡ„жңҖдҪҺиө„дә§д»·ж јгҖӮд»Ө Delta S дёәд»·ж јжӯҘй•ҝпјҢе№¶е®ҡд№ү Q_1=(S_t-S_min)/Delta SпјҢQ_2=(S_max-S_t)/Delta SгҖӮ

еңЁжҳҫејҸжңүйҷҗе·®еҲҶжі•дёӯпјҢз”ұдәҺи®Ўз®—з»“жһ„е‘Ҳдёүи§’еҪўпјҢиҫ№з•ҢдҝЎжҒҜжҜҸз»ҸиҝҮдёҖдёӘж—¶й—ҙжӯҘжңҖеӨҡеҗ‘еҶ…дј ж’ӯдёҖдёӘзҪ‘ж јиҠӮзӮ№гҖӮдёӢиҫ№з•Ң S_min еҸӘжңүеңЁж—¶й—ҙжӯҘж•° N иҮіе°‘иҫҫеҲ° Q_1 ж—¶жүҚдјҡеҪұе“ҚеҪ“еүҚиҠӮзӮ№пјӣдёҠиҫ№з•Ң S_max еҸӘжңүеңЁ N иҮіе°‘иҫҫеҲ° Q_2 ж—¶жүҚдјҡеҪұе“ҚеҪ“еүҚиҠӮзӮ№гҖӮ

еӣ жӯӨпјҢеҸӘиҰҒ N >= min(Q_1,Q_2)пјҢиҮіе°‘жңүдёҖдёӘиҫ№з•Ңзҡ„еҒҮи®ҫеҖјеҸҜиғҪеҪұе“ҚеҪ“еүҚд»·ж јеӨ„зҡ„дј°и®ЎпјӣиӢҘ N >= max(Q_1,Q_2)пјҢдёӨдёӘиҫ№з•ҢйғҪеҸҜиғҪеҪұе“Қдј°и®ЎгҖӮиӢҘдёҠдёӢиҫ№з•ҢзӣёеҜ№дәҺж—¶й—ҙжӯҘж•°и¶іеӨҹиҝңпјҢе®ғд»¬зҡ„еҒҮи®ҫеҖјдёҚдјҡдј йҖ’еҲ°еҪ“еүҚиө„дә§д»·ж јеӨ„гҖӮ

еҸҜд»ҘеҒҡеҰӮдёӢжӣҙж”№гҖӮи®ҫзҪ®еҲ—Lдёӯзҡ„еҚ•е…ғж јL1дёәпјҡ
\[\text{L1} = \text{NORMSINV} (\text{RAND}())\]
еҲ—Aдёӯзҡ„еҚ•е…ғж јA1и®ҫдёәпјҡ
\[A1 = C * \exp\big( (EUSD2 - \frac{FUSD2^2}{2}) * GUSD2 + FUSD2 * L1 * \sqrt{GUSD2} \big)\]
еҲ—Hдёӯзҡ„еҚ•е…ғж јH1и®ҫдёәпјҡ
\[H1 = C * \exp\big( (EUSD2 - \frac{FUSD2^2}{2}) * GUSD2 - FUSD2 * L1 * \sqrt{GUSD2} \big)\]
еҲ—Iдёӯзҡ„еҚ•е…ғж јI1и®ҫдёәпјҡ
\[I1 = \exp(-EUSD2 * GUSD2) * \max(H1 - DUSD2, 0)\]
еҲ—Jдёӯзҡ„еҚ•е…ғж јJ1дёәпјҡ
\[J1 = 0.5 * (B1 + I1)\]

problems.promptEn: Practice 21.23. A company has issued a 3-year convertible bond that has a face value of USD 25 and can be exchanged for two of the companyвҖҷs shares at any time. The company can call the issue, forcing conversion, when the share price is greater than or equal to USD 18. Assuming that the company will force conversion at the earliest opportunity, what are the boundary conditions for the price of the convertible? Describe how you would use finite difference methods to value the convertible assuming constant interest rates. Assume there is no risk of the company defaulting.

problems.promptEn: Practice 21.24. Provide formulas that can be used for obtaining three random samples from standard normal distributions when the correlation between sample i and sample j is ri, j.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 21.24. жҸҗдҫӣеҪ“ж ·жң¬ i е’Ңж ·жң¬ j д№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„зӣёе…іжҖ§дёә ri, j ж—¶еҸҜз”ЁдәҺд»Һж ҮеҮҶжӯЈжҖҒеҲҶеёғиҺ·еҸ–дёүдёӘйҡҸжңәж ·жң¬зҡ„е…¬ејҸгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
21.24

Let x_1, x_2, and x_3 be independent standard normal random variables. To create three standard normal variables epsilon_1, epsilon_2, epsilon_3 with correlations rho_12, rho_13, and rho_23, use a Cholesky-style construction.

Set epsilon_1 = x_1.

Set epsilon_2 = rho_12 x_1 + sqrt(1-rho_12^2) x_2.

Set epsilon_3 = rho_13 x_1 + alpha_2 x_2 + alpha_3 x_3, where alpha_2 = (rho_23 - rho_12 rho_13) / sqrt(1-rho_12^2) and alpha_3 = sqrt(1 - rho_13^2 - alpha_2^2).

These formulas give Corr(epsilon_1,epsilon_2)=rho_12, Corr(epsilon_1,epsilon_3)=rho_13, and Corr(epsilon_2,epsilon_3)=rho_23, provided the correlation matrix is positive semidefinite.
problems.explanationEn:
21.24

Let x_1, x_2, and x_3 be independent standard normal random variables. To create three standard normal variables epsilon_1, epsilon_2, epsilon_3 with correlations rho_12, rho_13, and rho_23, use a Cholesky-style construction.

Set epsilon_1 = x_1.

Set epsilon_2 = rho_12 x_1 + sqrt(1-rho_12^2) x_2.

Set epsilon_3 = rho_13 x_1 + alpha_2 x_2 + alpha_3 x_3, where alpha_2 = (rho_23 - rho_12 rho_13) / sqrt(1-rho_12^2) and alpha_3 = sqrt(1 - rho_13^2 - alpha_2^2).

These formulas give Corr(epsilon_1,epsilon_2)=rho_12, Corr(epsilon_1,epsilon_3)=rho_13, and Corr(epsilon_2,epsilon_3)=rho_23, provided the correlation matrix is positive semidefinite.
problems.explanationZh:
21.24

и®ҫ x_1гҖҒx_2гҖҒx_3 дёәзӣёдә’зӢ¬з«Ӣзҡ„ж ҮеҮҶжӯЈжҖҒйҡҸжңәеҸҳйҮҸгҖӮиӢҘиҰҒз”ҹжҲҗдёүдёӘж ҮеҮҶжӯЈжҖҒеҸҳйҮҸ epsilon_1гҖҒepsilon_2гҖҒepsilon_3пјҢдҪҝе®ғд»¬зҡ„зӣёе…ізі»ж•°еҲҶеҲ«дёә rho_12гҖҒrho_13гҖҒrho_23пјҢеҸҜд»ҘдҪҝз”Ё Cholesky еҲҶи§ЈејҸжһ„йҖ гҖӮ

д»Ө epsilon_1 = x_1гҖӮ

д»Ө epsilon_2 = rho_12 x_1 + sqrt(1-rho_12^2) x_2гҖӮ

д»Ө epsilon_3 = rho_13 x_1 + alpha_2 x_2 + alpha_3 x_3пјҢе…¶дёӯ alpha_2 = (rho_23 - rho_12 rho_13) / sqrt(1-rho_12^2)пјҢalpha_3 = sqrt(1 - rho_13^2 - alpha_2^2)гҖӮ

иҝҷж ·еҸҜеҫ—еҲ° Corr(epsilon_1,epsilon_2)=rho_12пјҢCorr(epsilon_1,epsilon_3)=rho_13пјҢCorr(epsilon_2,epsilon_3)=rho_23пјҢеүҚжҸҗжҳҜз»ҷе®ҡзӣёе…ізҹ©йҳөдёәеҚҠжӯЈе®ҡгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 21.25. An American put option to sell a Swiss franc for dollars has a strike price of USD 0.80 and a time to maturity of 1 year. The Swiss francвҖҷs volatility is 10%, the dollar interest rate is 6%, the Swiss franc interest rate is 3%, and the current exchange rate is 0.81. Use a three- step binomial tree to value the option. Estimate the delta of the option from your tree.

problems.promptEn: Practice 21.26. A 1-year American call option on silver futures has an exercise price of USD 9.00. The current futures price is USD 8.50, the risk-free rate of interest is 12% per annum, and the volatility of the futures price is 25% per annum. Use the DerivaGem software with four 3-month time steps to estimate the value of the option. Display the tree and verify that the option prices at the final and penultimate nodes are correct. Use DerivaGem to value the European version of the option. Use the control variate technique to improve your estimate of the price of the American option.

problems.promptEn: Practice 21.27. Answer the following questions concerned with the alternative procedures for construct- ing trees in Section 21.4: (a) Show that the binomial model in Section 21.4 is exactly consistent with the mean and variance of the change in the logarithm of the stock price in time вҲҶt. (b) Show that the trinomial model in Section 21.4 is consistent with the mean and variance of the change in the logarithm of the stock price in time вҲҶt when terms of order 1вҲҶt22 and higher are ignored. (c) Construct an alternative to the trinomial model in Section 21.4 so that the prob abilities are 1/6, 2/3, and 1/6 on the upper, middle, and lower branches emanating from each node. Assume that the branching is from S to Su, Sm, or Sd with m2 = ud. Match the mean and variance of the change in the logarithm of the stock price exactly.
