problems.explanationZh:
21.27
(a)
еҜ№дәҺз¬¬ 21.4 иҠӮдёӯзҡ„дәҢйЎ№ејҸжЁЎеһӢпјҢиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јеҜ№ж•°еңЁж—¶й—ҙжӯҘйӘӨ О”t еҶ…зҡ„еҸҳеҢ–жңүдёӨдёӘзӯүжҰӮзҺҮзҡ„еҸҜиғҪеҖјпјҢеҲҶеҲ«жҳҜ
( r - \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma^2 ) \Delta t + \sigma \sqrt{\Delta t}
е’Ң
( r - \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma^2 ) \Delta t - \sigma \sqrt{\Delta t}гҖӮ
иӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јеҜ№ж•°еҸҳеҢ–зҡ„жңҹжңӣдёә
0.5 \left[ ( r - \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma^2 ) \Delta t + \sigma \sqrt{\Delta t} \right] + 0.5 \left[ ( r - \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma^2 ) \Delta t - \sigma \sqrt{\Delta t} \right] = ( r - \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma^2 ) \Delta tгҖӮ
иҝҷжҳҜжӯЈзЎ®зҡ„гҖӮиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јеҜ№ж•°еҸҳеҢ–зҡ„ж–№е·®дёә
0.5 \sigma^2 \Delta t + 0.5 \sigma^2 \Delta t = \sigma^2 \Delta tгҖӮ
иҝҷд№ҹжҳҜжӯЈзЎ®зҡ„гҖӮ

(b)
еҜ№дәҺз¬¬ 21.4 иҠӮдёӯзҡ„дёүйЎ№ејҸж ‘жЁЎеһӢпјҢиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јеҜ№ж•°еңЁж—¶й—ҙжӯҘйӘӨ О”t еҶ…зҡ„еҸҳеҢ–еҲҶеҲ«жҳҜ
\sqrt{3} \sigma \sqrt{\Delta t}, 0 е’Ң -\sqrt{3} \sigma \sqrt{\Delta t},
еҜ№еә”зҡ„жҰӮзҺҮеҲҶеҲ«жҳҜ
\frac{1}{6}, \frac{2}{3}, \frac{1}{6}гҖӮ
е…¶жңҹжңӣеҸҳеҢ–дёә
( r - \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma^2 ) \Delta tпјҢ
ж–№е·®дёә
\sigma^2 \Delta t + O( (\Delta t)^2 )гҖӮ
еҝҪз•Ҙй«ҳйҳ¶йЎ№ ( й«ҳдәҺ (\Delta t)^2 ) ж—¶пјҢиҝҷдәӣжҳҜжӯЈзЎ®зҡ„гҖӮ

(c)
жһ„е»әдёҖдёӘдёүйЎ№ејҸжӣҝд»ЈжЁЎеһӢпјҢдҪҝеҫ—жҜҸдёӘиҠӮзӮ№еҗ‘дёҠгҖҒдёӯгҖҒдёӢеҲҶж”Ҝзҡ„жҰӮзҺҮеҲҶеҲ«дёә
\frac{1}{6}, \frac{2}{3}, \frac{1}{6}пјҢдё”еҒҮи®ҫж ‘зҡ„дёүдёӘд»·ж јеҲҶж”Ҝдёә S_u, S_m, S_dпјҢе№¶дё”ж»Ўи¶і m^2 = u dгҖӮ
иҰҒжұӮзІҫзЎ®еҢ№й…ҚиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јеҜ№ж•°еҸҳеҢ–зҡ„еқҮеҖје’Ңж–№е·®гҖӮ

д»ӨОҪ = ln uпјҢm = ln mпјҢd = ln dпјҢеҲҷжңүжқЎд»¶пјҡ
\frac{1}{6} \nu + \frac{2}{3} m + \frac{1}{6} d = ( r - \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma^2 ) \Delta tгҖӮ
з”ұдәҺ m^2 = u dпјҢеҸ–еҜ№ж•°еҫ— 2 m = \nu + dпјҢеӣ жӯӨ
0.5 ( \nu + d ) = mпјҢ\nu = 2 m - dгҖӮ
еёҰе…ҘеқҮеҖјжқЎд»¶пјҢж•ҙзҗҶеҫ—
\frac{1}{6} \nu + \frac{2}{3} m + \frac{1}{6} d = \frac{1}{6} ( 2 m - d ) + \frac{2}{3} m + \frac{1}{6} d = \frac{2}{3} m + \frac{2}{3} m = \frac{4}{3} mгҖӮ
иҝҷеә”зӯүдәҺ ( r - \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma^2 ) \Delta tпјҢж•…
m = \frac{3}{4} ( r - \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma^2 ) \Delta tгҖӮ

дёәдәҶеҢ№й…Қж–№е·®пјҢиҰҒжұӮ
\frac{1}{6} ( \nu - m )^2 + \frac{2}{3} ( m - m )^2 + \frac{1}{6} ( d - m )^2 = \sigma^2 \Delta tгҖӮ
з”ұдәҺ m жҳҜжңҹжңӣпјҢ ( m - m )^2 = 0пјҢдё” \nu - m = m - dпјҢж•…
\frac{1}{6} ( \nu - m )^2 + \frac{1}{6} ( d - m )^2 = \frac{1}{6} z^2 + \frac{1}{6} z^2 = \frac{1}{3} z^2 = \sigma^2 \Delta tпјҢ
е…¶дёӯ z = \nu - m = m - dгҖӮ
еӣ жӯӨ
z^2 = 3 \sigma^2 \Delta t,
еҚі
z = \sqrt{3} \sigma \sqrt{\Delta t}гҖӮ

з»“еҗҲдёҠиҝ°з»“жһңпјҢеҸҜд»ҘзЎ®е®ҡ u, m, dпјҡ
ln u = \nu = m + z = \frac{3}{4} ( r - \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma^2 ) \Delta t + \sqrt{3} \sigma \sqrt{\Delta t},
ln m = m = \frac{3}{4} ( r - \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma^2 ) \Delta t,
ln d = m - z = \frac{3}{4} ( r - \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma^2 ) \Delta t - \sqrt{3} \sigma \sqrt{\Delta t}гҖӮ

иҝҷж ·жһ„йҖ зҡ„дёүйЎ№ејҸжЁЎеһӢпјҢж»Ўи¶іжҰӮзҺҮдёә 1/6гҖҒ2/3гҖҒ1/6 дё”зІҫзЎ®еҢ№й…ҚиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јеҜ№ж•°еҸҳеҢ–зҡ„еқҮеҖје’Ңж–№е·®гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 21.28. The DerivaGem Application Builder functions enable you to investigate how the prices of options calculated from a binomial tree converge to the correct value as the number of time steps increases. (See Figure 21.4 and Sample Application A in DerivaGem.) Consider a put option on a stock index where the index level is 900, the strike price is 900, the risk-free rate is 5%, the dividend yield is 2%, and the time to maturity is 2 years. (a) Produce results similar to Sample Application A on convergence for the situation where the option is European and the volatility of the index is 20%. (b) Produce results similar to Sample Application A on convergence for the situation where the option is American and the volatility of the index is 20%. (c) Produce a chart showing the pricing of the American option when the volatility is 20% as a function of the number of time steps when the control variate technique is used. (d) Suppose that the price of the American option in the market is 85.0. Produce a chart showing the implied volatility estimate as a function of the number of time steps. Basic Numerical Procedures

problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
21.30
Without early exercise, the option is worth 0.2535 at the lowest node at the 9 month point.
With early exercise, it is worth 0.2552. The gain from early exercise is therefore 0.0017.

Assume that the daily volatilities of both assets are 1% and that the coefficient of correlation between their returns is 0.3. Estimate the 5-day 99% VaR and ES for the portfolio assuming normally distributed returns.

model building approach is used to calculate VaR involve (a) the use of the duration model,
(b) the use of cash flow mapping, and (c) the use of principal components analysis. When
historical simulation is used, we need to assume that the change in the zero-coupon yield
curve between Day m and Day

change in each of these between Day m and Day

(c) дҪҝз”Ёдё»жҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗпјҲprincipal components analysisпјүпјҢеҜ№еҲ©зҺҮжңҹйҷҗз»“жһ„зҡ„еҸҳеҢ–иҝӣиЎҢз»ҹи®ЎйҷҚз»ҙпјҢжҚ•жҚүеҲ©зҺҮжӣІзәҝзҡ„дё»иҰҒеҸҳеҠЁеӣ зҙ гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 22.5. Suppose that the daily change in the value of a portfolio is, to a good approximation, linearly dependent on two factors, calculated from a principal components analysis. The delta of a portfolio with respect to the first factor is 6 and the delta with respect to the second factor is -4. The standard deviations of the factors are 20 and 8, respectively. What is the 5-day 90% VaR? 22.6 Suppose that a company has a portfolio consisting of positions in stocks and bonds. Assume that there are no derivatives. Explain the assumptions underlying (a) the linear model and (b) the historical simulation model for calculating VaR.

problems.promptEn: Practice 22.9. Explain why the linear model can provide only approximate estimates of VaR for a portfolio containing options.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 22.9. и§ЈйҮҠдёәд»Җд№ҲзәҝжҖ§жЁЎеһӢеҸӘиғҪдёәеҢ…еҗ«жңҹжқғзҡ„жҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲжҸҗдҫӣ VaR зҡ„иҝ‘дјјдј°и®ЎгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
22.9
The change in the value of an option is not linearly related to the change in the value of the
underlying variables. When the change in the values of underlying variables is normal, the
change in the value of the option is non-normal. The linear model assumes that it is normal
and is, therefore, only an approximation.
problems.explanationEn:
22.9

underlying variables. When the change in the values of underlying variables is normal, the
change in the value of the option is non-normal. The linear model assumes that it is normal
and is, therefore, only an approximation.

problems.promptEn: Practice 22.10. Some time ago a company entered into a forward contract to buy ВЈ1 million for USD 1.5 million. The contract now has 6 months to maturity. The daily volatility of a 6-month zero-coupon sterling bond (when its price is translated to dollars) is 0.06% and the daily volatility of a 6-month zero-coupon dollar bond is 0.05%. The correlation between returns from the two bonds is 0.8. The current exchange rate is 1.53. Calculate the standard deviation of the change in the dollar value of the forward contract in 1 day. What is the 10-day 99% VaR? Assume that the 6-month interest rate in both sterling and dollars is 5% per annum with continuous compounding.

problems.promptEn: Practice 22.11. The text calculates a VaR estimate for the example in Table 22.11 assuming two factors. How does the estimate change if you assume (a) one factor and (b) three factors.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 22.11. жң¬ж–Үи®Ўз®—дәҶиЎЁ 22.11 дёӯзӨәдҫӢзҡ„ VaR дј°и®ЎеҖјпјҢеҒҮи®ҫжңүдёӨдёӘеӣ зҙ гҖӮеҰӮжһңжӮЁеҒҮи®ҫ (a) дёҖдёӘеӣ зҙ е’Ң (b) дёүдёӘеӣ зҙ пјҢдј°и®ЎеҖјдјҡеҰӮдҪ•еҸҳеҢ–гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
22.11
If we assume only one factor, the model is
DeltaP = вҖ“1.99f1

The standard deviation of
1f is 11.54. The standard deviation of P
Delta is therefore
1.99 Г— 11.54 = 22.965 and the 1-day 99 percent value at risk is 22.965Г—2.326=53.42. If we
assume three factors, our exposure to the third factor is
10 Г— (0.376) + 4 Г— (0.006) вҖ“ 8 Г— (вҲ’0.332) вҲ’ 7 Г— (вҲ’0.349) +2 Г— (вҖ“0.153) = 8.58

The model is therefore,
DeltaP = вҲ’1.99f1вҲ’3.06f2+8.58f3
The variance of P
Delta is
1.992 Г— 11.542 + 3.062 Г— 3.552 + 8.582 Г— 1.782 = 878.62

The standard deviation of P
Delta is the square root of this or 29.64 and the 1-day 99% value at
risk is 29.64Г— 2.326 = USD 68.95..
The example illustrates that the relative importance of different factors depends on the
portfolio being considered. Normally, the second factor is more important than the third, but
in this case it is less important.

problems.promptEn: Practice 22.12. A bank has a portfolio of options on an asset. The delta of the options is -30 and the gamma is -5. Explain how these numbers can be interpreted. The asset price is 20 and its volatility is 1% per day. Adapt Sample Application E in the DerivaGem Application Builder software to calculate VaR.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 22.12. й“¶иЎҢжӢҘжңүиө„дә§зҡ„жңҹжқғз»„еҗҲгҖӮйҖүйЎ№зҡ„ delta дёә -30пјҢgamma дёә -5гҖӮи§ЈйҮҠеҰӮдҪ•и§ЈйҮҠиҝҷдәӣж•°еӯ—гҖӮиө„дә§д»·ж јдёә20пјҢжҜҸеӨ©жіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә1%гҖӮи°ғж•ҙ DerivaGem Application Builder иҪҜд»¶дёӯзҡ„зӨәдҫӢеә”з”ЁзЁӢеәҸ E жқҘи®Ўз®— VaRгҖӮ

problems.explanation:
22.12
The delta of the options is the rate of change of the value of the options with respect to the
price of the asset. When the asset price increases by a small amount, the value of the options
decrease by 30 times this amount. The gamma of the options is the rate of change of their
delta with respect to the price of the asset. When the asset price increases by a small amount,
the delta of the portfolio decreases by five times this amount.

problems.promptEn: Practice 22.14. The 1-day 99% VaR is calculated using historical simulation for the four-index example in Section 22.2 as USD 422,291. Look at the underlying spreadsheets on the authorвҖҷs website and calculate using historical simulation: (a) the 1-day 95% VaR, (b) the 1-day 95% ES, (c) the 1-day 97% VaR, and (d) the 1-day 97% ES. Repeat your calculations using the model-building approach.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 22.14. 1 еӨ© 99% VaR дҪҝз”Ёз¬¬ 22.2 иҠӮдёӯеӣӣжҢҮж•°зӨәдҫӢзҡ„еҺҶеҸІжЁЎжӢҹи®Ўз®—дёә 422,291 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮжҹҘзңӢдҪңиҖ…зҪ‘з«ҷдёҠзҡ„еҹәзЎҖз”өеӯҗиЎЁж јпјҢе№¶дҪҝз”ЁеҺҶеҸІжЁЎжӢҹиҝӣиЎҢи®Ўз®—пјҡ(a) 1 еӨ© 95% VaRпјҢ(b) 1 еӨ© 95% ESпјҢ(c) 1 еӨ© 97% VaRпјҢд»ҘеҸҠ (d) 1 еӨ© 97% ESгҖӮдҪҝз”ЁжЁЎеһӢжһ„е»әж–№жі•йҮҚеӨҚи®Ўз®—гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
22.14
The 95% one-day VaR is the 25th worst loss. This is USD 163,620. The 95% one-day ES is the
average of the 25 largest losses. It is USD 323,690. The 97% one-day VaR is the 15th worst loss.
This is USD 229,683. The 97% one-day ES is the average of the 15 largest losses. It is USD 415,401.
The model building approach gives the 95% one-day VaR as USD 197,425 and the 95% one-day
ES as USD 247,579. The model building approach gives the 97% one-day VaR as USD 225,744 and
the 95% one-day ES as USD 272,226.

problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 22.15. еҰӮжһңз¬¬ 22.2 иҠӮдёӯиҖғиҷ‘зҡ„еӣӣжҢҮж•°жҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲеңЁеӣӣдёӘжҢҮж•°д№Ӣй—ҙе№іеқҮеҲҶй…ҚпјҢеҲҷдҪҝз”ЁдҪңиҖ…зҪ‘з«ҷдёҠзҡ„з”өеӯҗиЎЁж јпјҢйҮҮз”Ёз¬¬ 22.2 иҠӮдёӯзҡ„еҹәжң¬ж–№жі•жқҘи®Ўз®— 1 еӨ© 99% VaR е’Ң ESгҖӮдҪҝз”ЁжЁЎеһӢжһ„е»әж–№жі•йҮҚеӨҚи®Ўз®—гҖӮ

problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
22.15
For the historical simulation approach, in the вҖңScenariosвҖқ worksheet the portfolio investments
are changed to 2,500 in cells L2:O2. The losses are then sorted from the largest to the
smallest. The fifth worst loss is USD 394,437. This is the one-day 99% VaR. The average of the
five worst losses is USD 633,716. This is the one-day 99% ES. For the model building approach,
we make a similar change to the equal weights sheet and find that the value at risk is
USD 275,757 while the expected shortfall is USD 315,926.

problems.promptEn: Practice 22.16. A company has a position in bonds worth USD 6 million. The modified duration of the portfolio is 5.2 years. Assume that only parallel shifts in the yield curve can take place and that the standard deviation of the daily yield change (when yield is measured in percent) is 0.09. Use the duration model to estimate the 20-day 90% VaR for the portfolio. Explain carefully the weaknesses of this approach to calculating VaR. Explain two alternatives that give more accuracy.

problems.promptEn: Practice 22.17. Consider a portfolio of options on a single asset. Suppose that the delta of the portfolio is 12, the value of the asset is USD 10, and the daily volatility of the asset is 2%. Estimate the 1-day 95% VaR for the portfolio from the delta. Suppose next that the gamma of the portfolio is -2.6. Derive a quadratic relationship between the change in the portfolio value and the percentage change in the underlying asset price in one day. How would you use this in a Monte Carlo simulation?

problems.promptEn: Practice 22.18. A company has a long position in a 2-year bond and a 3-year bond, as well as a short position in a 5-year bond. Each bond has a principal of USD 100 and pays a 5% coupon annually. Calculate the companyвҖҷs exposure to the 1-year, 2-year, 3-year, 4-year, and 5-year rates. Use the data in Tables 22.9 and 22.10 to calculate a 20-day 95% VaR on the assumption that rate changes are explained by (a) one factor, (b) two factors, and (c) three factors. Assume that the zero-coupon yield curve is flat at 5%.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 22.18. дёҖе®¶е…¬еҸёжӢҘжңү2е№ҙжңҹеҖәеҲёе’Ң3е№ҙжңҹеҖәеҲёзҡ„еӨҡеӨҙеӨҙеҜёпјҢд»ҘеҸҠ5е№ҙжңҹеҖәеҲёзҡ„з©әеӨҙеӨҙеҜёгҖӮжҜҸеј еҖәеҲёзҡ„жң¬йҮ‘дёә 100 зҫҺе…ғпјҢжҜҸе№ҙж”Ҝд»ҳ 5% зҡ„жҒҜзҘЁгҖӮи®Ўз®—е…¬еҸёзҡ„ 1 е№ҙжңҹгҖҒ2 е№ҙжңҹгҖҒ3 е№ҙжңҹгҖҒ4 е№ҙжңҹе’Ң 5 е№ҙжңҹеҲ©зҺҮйЈҺйҷ©ж•һеҸЈгҖӮдҪҝз”ЁиЎЁ 22.9 е’Ң 22.10 дёӯзҡ„ж•°жҚ®и®Ўз®— 20 еӨ© 95% VaRпјҢеҒҮи®ҫеҲ©зҺҮеҸҳеҢ–з”ұ (a) дёҖдёӘеӣ зҙ гҖҒ(b) дёӨдёӘеӣ зҙ е’Ң (c) дёүдёӘеӣ зҙ и§ЈйҮҠгҖӮеҒҮи®ҫйӣ¶жҒҜзҘЁж”¶зӣҠзҺҮжӣІзәҝе№іеқҰдәҺ 5%гҖӮ

The duration relationship is used to calculate the last row of the table. When the one-year rate
increases by one basis point, the value of the cash flow in year 1 decreases by 1 Г— 0.0001 Г—
4.756 = 0.0005; when the two year rate increases by one basis point, the value of the cash
flow in year 2 decreases by 2Г—0.0001Г—95.008 = 0.0190; and so on.

The sensitivity to the first factor is
вҲ’0.0005 Г— 0.083 вҲ’ 0.0190 Г— 0.210 вҲ’ 0.0258 Г— 0.286+ 0.0016 Г— 0.336 + 0.0409 Г— 0.386
or 0.004915. (We assume that PC1 for 4 years is the average of that for 3 and 5 years.)
Similarly, the sensitivity to the second and third factors are 0.007496 and вҲ’0.02148.
Assuming one factor, the standard deviation of the one-day change in the portfolio value is
0.004915 Г— 11.54 = 0.05672. The 20-day 95% VaR is therefore 0.05672 Г— 1.645
20 =
0.417. Assuming two factors, the variance of the one-day change in the portfolio value is
0.0049152 Г— 11.542 + 0.0074962 Г— 3.552 = 0.003925
so that the standard deviation is 0.06267.
The 20-day 95% VaR is therefore 0.06267 Г— 1.645
20 = 0.461.
Assuming three factors, the variance of the one-day change in the portfolio value is
0.0049152 Г— 11.542 + 0.0074962 Г— 3.552 + 0.021482Г—1.782 = 0.005387
so that the standard deviation is 0.07339.
The 20-day 95% VaR is therefore 0.07339 Г— 1.645
20 = 0.540.

tags: Value at Risk and Expected Shortfall
problems.promptEn: Practice 22.19. A bank has written a call option on one stock and a put option on another stock. For the first option the stock price is 50, the strike price is 51, the volatility is 28% per annum, and the time to maturity is 9 months. For the second option the stock price is 20, the strike price is 19, the volatility is 25% per annum, and the time to maturity is 1 year. Neither stock pays a dividend, the risk-free rate is 6% per annum, and the correlation between stock price returns is 0.4. Calculate a 10-day 99% VaR: (a) using only deltas, (b) using the partial simulation approach, and (c) using the full simulation approach.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 22.19. дёҖе®¶й“¶иЎҢдёәдёҖз§ҚиӮЎзҘЁеҲ¶е®ҡдәҶзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғпјҢе№¶дёәеҸҰдёҖз§ҚиӮЎзҘЁеҲ¶е®ҡдәҶзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғгҖӮз¬¬дёҖдёӘжңҹжқғзҡ„иӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јдёә 50пјҢжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә 51пјҢжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ 28%пјҢеҲ°жңҹж—¶й—ҙдёә 9 дёӘжңҲгҖӮеҜ№дәҺз¬¬дәҢдёӘжңҹжқғпјҢиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јдёә 20пјҢжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә 19пјҢжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ 25%пјҢеҲ°жңҹж—¶й—ҙдёә 1 е№ҙгҖӮдёӨеҸӘиӮЎзҘЁйғҪдёҚжҙҫжҒҜпјҢж— йЈҺйҷ©е№ҙеҲ©зҺҮдёә 6%пјҢиӮЎд»·еӣһжҠҘд№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„зӣёе…іжҖ§дёә 0.4гҖӮи®Ўз®— 10 еӨ© 99% VaRпјҡ(a) д»…дҪҝз”ЁеўһйҮҸпјҢ(b) дҪҝз”ЁйғЁеҲҶжЁЎжӢҹж–№жі•пјҢд»ҘеҸҠ (c) дҪҝз”Ёе®Ңж•ҙжЁЎжӢҹж–№жі•гҖӮ

to calculating VaR, but it is calculation intensive. Realistically, students need some
programming skills to make the assignment feasible. My answer follows the usual practice of

е®ҡд№ү
\[ u_i = \frac{S_i - S_{i-1}}{S_{i-1}}, \]
е…¶дёӯ \(S_i\) жҳҜз¬¬ \(i\) еӨ©еёӮеңәеҸҳйҮҸзҡ„ж•°еҖјгҖӮеңЁжҢҮж•°еҠ жқғз§»еҠЁе№іеқҮпјҲEWMAпјүжЁЎеһӢдёӯпјҢеёӮеңәеҸҳйҮҸзҡ„ж–№е·®зҺҮпјҲеҚіжіўеҠЁзҺҮзҡ„е№іж–№пјүеңЁз¬¬ \(n\) еӨ©зҡ„и®Ўз®—жҳҜз¬¬ \(n\) еӨ©жүҖжңү \(u_i^2\) зҡ„еҠ жқғе№іеқҮпјҲе…¶дёӯ \(i=1,2,3,\dots,n\)пјүгҖӮеҜ№дәҺжҹҗеёёж•° \(\lambda\) ж»Ўи¶і \(0 < \lambda < 1\)пјҢжқғйҮҚж»Ўи¶іпјҡз»ҷз¬¬ \(n-1\) еӨ©зҡ„жқғйҮҚжҳҜз»ҷз¬¬ \(n\) еӨ©жқғйҮҚзҡ„ \(\lambda\) еҖҚгҖӮ

з¬¬ \(n\) еӨ©зҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮдј°и®Ўдёә \(\sigma_n\)пјҢе®ғдёҺеүҚдёҖеӨ©зҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮдј°и®Ў \(\sigma_{n-1}\) зҡ„е…ізі»дёәпјҡ
\[
\sigma_n^2 = \lambda \sigma_{n-1}^2 + (1-\lambda) u_{n-1}^2.
\]
иҜҘе…¬ејҸжҳҫзӨәпјҢEWMA жЁЎеһӢжңүдёҖдёӘжһҒе…·еҗёеј•еҠӣзҡ„зү№жҖ§пјҡи®Ўз®—з¬¬ \(n\) еӨ©зҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮеҸӘйңҖзҹҘйҒ“з¬¬ \(n-1\) еӨ©зҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮдј°и®Ўе’Ңз¬¬ \(n-1\) еӨ©зҡ„ж”¶зӣҠ \(u_{n-1}\)гҖӮ

problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
23.2
The EWMA model produces a forecast of the daily variance rate for day n which is a
weighted average of (i) the forecast for day
1
n -, and (ii) the square of the proportional
change on day
1
n -. The GARCH (1,1) model produces a forecast of the daily variance for
day n which is a weighted average of (i) the forecast for day
1
n -, (ii) the square of the
proportional change on day
1
n -, and (iii) a long run average variance rate. GARCH (1,1)
adapts the EWMA model by giving some weight to a long run average variance rate.
Whereas the EWMA has no mean reversion, GARCH (1,1) is consistent with a mean-
reverting variance rate model.

problems.promptEn: Practice 23.3. The most recent estimate of the daily volatility of an asset is 1.5% and the price of the asset at the close of trading yesterday was USD 30.00. problems.promptEn: Practice 23.8. Assume that S&P 500 at close of trading yesterday was 3,040 and the daily volatility of the index was estimated as 1% per day at that time. problems.promptEn: Practice 23.10. The parameters of a GARCH(1,1) model are estimated as v = 0.000004, a = 0.05, and b = 0.92. What is the long-run average volatility and what is the equation describing the way that the variance rate reverts to its long-run average? If the current volatility is 20% per year, what is the expected volatility in 20 days?

problems.explanationZh:
23.10
й•ҝжңҹе№іеқҮж–№е·®зҺҮдёә
\((\omega) / (1 - \alpha - \beta) = 0.000004 / (1 - 0.05 - 0.92) = 0.0001333\)гҖӮ

й•ҝжңҹе№іеқҮжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә\(\sqrt{0.0001333} = 1.155\%\)гҖӮ

ж–№е·®зҺҮжҒўеӨҚеҲ°й•ҝжңҹе№іеқҮеҖјзҡ„ж–№зЁӢжҳҜе…¬ејҸ (23.13):

\[
\sigma_{n+1}^2 = \omega + \alpha V_n + \beta \sigma_n^2
\]

д»Је…Ҙз»ҷе®ҡеҸӮж•°пјҢеҸҳдёәпјҡ

\[
\sigma_{n+1}^2 = 0.0001333 + 0.97(\sigma_n^2 - 0.0001333)
\]

еҰӮжһңеҪ“еүҚжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ 20%пјҢеҲҷж–№е·®дёә

\(0.2^2 / 252 = 0.0126\)пјҢ

20 еӨ©еҗҺзҡ„йў„жңҹж–№е·®дёә

\[ 0.0001333 + 0.97(0.0126 - 0.0001333) = 0.0001471 \]

еӣ жӯӨпјҢ20 еӨ©еҗҺзҡ„йў„жңҹжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә \(\sqrt{0.0001471} = 0.0121\)пјҢеҚіжҜҸеӨ© 1.21%гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 23.11. Suppose that the current daily volatilities of asset X and asset Y are 1.0% and 1.2%, respectively. The prices of the assets at close of trading yesterday were USD 30 and USD 50 and the estimate of the coefficient of correlation between the returns on the two assets made at this time was 0.50. Correlations and volatilities are updated using a GARCH(1,1) model. The estimates of the modelвҖҷs parameters are a = 0.04 and b = 0.94. For the correlation v = 0.000001, and for the volatilities v = 0.000003. If the prices of the two assets at close of trading today are USD 31 and USD 51, how is the correlation estimate updated?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 23.11. еҒҮи®ҫиө„дә§Xе’Ңиө„дә§YеҪ“еүҚзҡ„ж—ҘжіўеҠЁзҺҮеҲҶеҲ«дёә1.0%е’Ң1.2%гҖӮжҳЁеӨ©ж”¶зӣҳж—¶иҜҘиө„дә§зҡ„д»·ж јдёә\$30 and \$50пјҢжӯӨж—¶еҜ№дёӨз§Қиө„дә§ж”¶зӣҠд№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„зӣёе…ізі»ж•°зҡ„дј°и®Ўдёә0.50гҖӮдҪҝз”Ё GARCH(1,1) жЁЎеһӢжӣҙж–°зӣёе…іжҖ§е’ҢжіўеҠЁжҖ§гҖӮжЁЎеһӢеҸӮж•°зҡ„дј°и®ЎеҖјдёә a = 0.04 е’Ң b = 0.94гҖӮеҜ№дәҺзӣёе…іжҖ§ v = 0.000001пјҢеҜ№дәҺжіўеҠЁжҖ§ v = 0.000003гҖӮеҰӮжһңд»ҠеӨ©ж”¶зӣҳж—¶дёӨз§Қиө„дә§зҡ„д»·ж јдёә \$31 and \$51пјҢйӮЈд№Ҳзӣёе…іжҖ§дј°и®ЎеҖјеҰӮдҪ•жӣҙж–°пјҹ

problems.explanationZh:
23.11
дҪҝз”Ёж–Үдёӯз¬ҰеҸ·пјҢ
иө„дә§Xзҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә1.0%пјҢеҚі\(\sigma_u = 0.01\)пјҢиө„дә§Yзҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә1.2%пјҢеҚі\(\sigma_v = 0.012\)гҖӮ

дёӨиҖ…ж”¶зӣҠзҡ„зӣёе…ізі»ж•°дј°и®Ўдёә0.50пјҢеӣ жӯӨеҚҸж–№е·®дј°и®Ўдёә

\(0.01 \times 0.012 \times 0.50 = 0.00006\)гҖӮ

иө„дә§Xзҡ„жҜ”дҫӢеҸҳеҠЁдёә

\(\frac{31 - 30}{30} = 0.03333\)пјҢиө„дә§Yзҡ„жҜ”дҫӢеҸҳеҠЁдёә

\(\frac{51 - 50}{50} = 0.02\)гҖӮ

ж–°зҡ„еҚҸж–№е·®дј°и®Ўдёә

\(0.000001 + 0.04 \times 0.03333 \times 0.02 + 0.94 \times 0.00006 = 0.0000841\)гҖӮ

иө„дә§Xзҡ„ж–°ж–№е·®дј°и®Ўдёә

\(0.000003 + 0.04 \times 0.03333^2 + 0.94 \times 0.01^2 = 0.0001414\)пјҢеҚіжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә

\(\sqrt{0.0001414} = 0.01189\)пјҢеҚі1.189%гҖӮ

иө„дә§Yзҡ„ж–°ж–№е·®дј°и®Ўдёә

\(0.000003 + 0.04 \times 0.02^2 + 0.94 \times 0.012^2 = 0.0001544\)пјҢеҚіжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә

\(\sqrt{0.0001544} = 0.01242\)пјҢеҚі1.242%гҖӮ

ж–°дј°и®Ўзҡ„зӣёе…ізі»ж•°дёә

\(\frac{0.0000841}{0.01189 \times 0.01242} = 0.569\)гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 23.12. Suppose that the daily volatility of the FTSE 100 stock index (measured in pounds sterling) is 1.8% and the daily volatility of the dollar/sterling exchange rate is 0.9%. Suppose further that the correlation between the FTSE 100 and the dollar/sterling exchange rate is 0.4. What is the volatility of the FTSE 100 when it is translated to U.S. dollars? Assume that the dollar/sterling exchange rate is expressed as the number of U.S. dollars per pound sterling. (Hint: When Z = XY, the percentage daily change in Z is approximately equal to the percentage daily change in X plus the percentage daily change in Y.)
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 23.12. еҒҮи®ҫеҜҢж—¶ 100 иӮЎзҘЁжҢҮж•°пјҲд»ҘиӢұй•‘иЎЎйҮҸпјүзҡ„жҜҸж—ҘжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә 1.8%пјҢзҫҺе…ғ/иӢұй•‘жұҮзҺҮзҡ„жҜҸж—ҘжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә 0.9%гҖӮиҝӣдёҖжӯҘеҒҮи®ҫеҜҢж—¶ 100 жҢҮж•°дёҺзҫҺе…ғ/иӢұй•‘жұҮзҺҮд№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„зӣёе…іжҖ§дёә 0.4гҖӮжҚўз®—жҲҗзҫҺе…ғеҗҺпјҢеҜҢж—¶ 100 жҢҮж•°зҡ„жіўеҠЁжҖ§жҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹеҒҮи®ҫзҫҺе…ғ/иӢұй•‘жұҮзҺҮиЎЁзӨәдёәжҜҸиӢұй•‘е…‘жҚўеӨҡе°‘зҫҺе…ғгҖӮ пјҲжҸҗзӨәпјҡеҪ“ Z = XY ж—¶пјҢZ зҡ„жҜҸж—ҘеҸҳеҢ–зҷҫеҲҶжҜ”еӨ§зәҰзӯүдәҺ X зҡ„жҜҸж—ҘеҸҳеҢ–зҷҫеҲҶжҜ”еҠ дёҠ Y зҡ„жҜҸж—ҘеҸҳеҢ–зҷҫеҲҶжҜ”гҖӮпјү

ix + iyгҖӮix зҡ„ж ҮеҮҶе·®дёә 0.018пјҢiy зҡ„ж ҮеҮҶе·®дёә 0.009пјҢдёӨиҖ…зӣёе…ізі»ж•°дёә 0.4гҖӮдәҺжҳҜ ix + iy зҡ„ж–№е·®дёә
0.018^2 + 0.009^2 + 2 Г— 0.018 Г— 0.009 Г— 0.4 = 0.0005346гҖӮ
еӣ жӯӨ ix + iy зҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә sqrt(0.0005346) = 0.0231 жҲ– 2.31%гҖӮиҝҷе°ұжҳҜд»ҘзҫҺе…ғи®Ўзҡ„еҜҢж—¶жҢҮж•°жіўеҠЁзҺҮгҖӮжіЁж„ҸпјҢиҝҷдёӘжіўеҠЁзҺҮжҜ”д»ҘиӢұй•‘и®Ўзҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮжӣҙй«ҳпјҢиҝҷеҸҚжҳ дәҶжӯЈзӣёе…іжҖ§зҡ„еҪұе“ҚгҖӮеҪ“еҜҢж—¶жҢҮж•°дёҠж¶Ёж—¶пјҢд»ҘзҫҺе…ғи®Ўзҡ„иӢұй•‘д»·еҖјеҫҖеҫҖд№ҹдјҡдёҠж¶ЁпјҢд»ҺиҖҢдҪҝд»ҘзҫҺе…ғи®Ўзҡ„еҜҢж—¶жҢҮж•°д»·еҖјеўһй•ҝеҫ—жӣҙеӨ§пјӣеҗҢзҗҶпјҢеҜҢж—¶жҢҮж•°дёӢи·Ңж—¶пјҢд№ҹдјҡжңүзұ»дјјж”ҫеӨ§зҡ„ж•ҲжһңгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 23.14. Show that the GARCH (1,1) model s2 n = v + au2 n-1 + bs2 n-1 in equation (23.9) is equivalent to the stochastic volatility model dV = a1VL - V2dt + jV dz, where time is measured in days, V is the square of the volatility of the asset price, and a = 1 - a - b, VL = v 1 - a - b, j = a22 What is the stochastic volatility model when time is measured in years? (Hint: The variable un-1 is the return on the asset price in time вҲҶt. It can be assumed to be normally distributed with mean zero and standard deviation sn-1. It follows from the moments of the normal distribution that the mean and variance of u2 n-1 are s2 n-1 and 2s4 n-1, respectively.)

problems.explanationZh:
23.14
GARCH(1,1)жЁЎеһӢдёәпјҡ
s_n^2 = v + a u_{n-1}^2 + b s_{n-1}^2
е°Ҷе…¶еҜ№еә”дёәйҡҸжңәжіўеҠЁзҺҮжЁЎеһӢпјҢе®ҡд№үиө„дә§д»·ж јжіўеҠЁзҺҮзҡ„е№іж–№дёә VпјҢж—¶й—ҙеҚ•дҪҚдёәвҖңеӨ©вҖқгҖӮ
еҸҳйҮҸ u_{n-1} жҳҜж—¶й—ҙй—ҙйҡ” О”t еҶ…зҡ„иө„дә§еӣһжҠҘпјҢеҒҮи®ҫжңҚд»ҺеқҮеҖјдёә0пјҢж ҮеҮҶе·®дёә s_{n-1} зҡ„жӯЈжҖҒеҲҶеёғгҖӮж №жҚ®жӯЈжҖҒеҲҶеёғзҹ©пјҢu_{n-1}^2 зҡ„еқҮеҖјдёә s_{n-1}^2пјҢж–№е·®дёә 2 s_{n-1}^4гҖӮ
и®ҫ a_1 = 1 - a - bпјҢV_L = v / (1 - a - b)пјҢj = a^2гҖӮ
жҺЁеҜјеҰӮдёӢпјҡ
ж №жҚ®е®ҡд№үпјҢ
sigma_n^2 - sigma_{n-1}^2 = v + a u_{n-1}^2 + b s_{n-1}^2 - sigma_{n-1}^2
д»Је…ҘеҜ№еә”еҸҳйҮҸпјҢеҫ—еҲ°
V_{n} - V_{n-1} = a_1 (V_L - V_{n-1}) + a (u_{n-1}^2 - V_{n-1})
еӣ дёә u_{n-1}^2 зҡ„еқҮеҖјдёә V_{n-1}пјҢж–№е·®дёә 2 V_{n-1}^2пјҢж•…жңүдёҖдёӘйҡҸжңәйЎ№ ZпјҢ
Z зҡ„еқҮеҖјдёә0пјҢж ҮеҮҶе·®дёә j VгҖӮ
еҶҷдҪңе·®еҲҶеҪўејҸпјҡ
О”V = a_1 (V_L - V) О”t + j V Оө sqrt(О”t)пјҢе…¶дёӯ Оө ~ N(0,1)
д»Ө О”t = 1еӨ©пјҢеҸ–жһҒйҷҗеҸҜеҫ—йҡҸжңәеҫ®еҲҶж–№зЁӢпјҡ
dV = a_1 (V_L - V) dt + j V dz
е…¶дёӯ dz жҳҜж ҮеҮҶеёғжң—иҝҗеҠЁеўһйҮҸгҖӮ
ж—¶й—ҙеҚ•дҪҚж”№дёәвҖңе№ҙвҖқж—¶пјҢи®ҫдёҖе№ҙжңү252дёӘдәӨжҳ“ж—ҘпјҢж–№зЁӢеҸҳдёәпјҡ
dV = 252 a_1 (V_L - V) dt + sqrt(252) j V dz
еҚіеҜ№еә”е№ҙеҢ–зҡ„йҡҸжңәжіўеҠЁзҺҮжЁЎеһӢгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 23.18. Suppose that in Problem 23.17 the price of silver at the close of trading yesterday was USD 16, its volatility was estimated as 1.5% per day, and its correlation with gold was estimated as 0.8. The price of silver at the close of trading today is unchanged at USD 16. Update the volatility of silver and the correlation between silver and gold using the two models in Problem 23.17. In practice, is the v parameter likely to be the same for gold and silver?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 23.18. еҒҮи®ҫеңЁй—®йўҳ 23.17 дёӯпјҢжҳЁеӨ©ж”¶зӣҳж—¶зҡ„зҷҪй“¶д»·ж јдёә\$16, its volatility was estimated as 1.5% per day, and its correlation with gold was estimated as 0.8. For a given alpha and beta, the omega parameter defines the long run average value of a variance or a
covariance. There is no reason why we should expect the long run average daily variance for
gold and silver should be the same. There is also no reason why we should expect the long
run average covariance between gold and silver to be the same as the long run average
variance of gold or the long run average variance of silver. In practice, therefore, we are
likely to want to allow omega in a GARCH(1,1) model to vary from market variable to market
variable. (Some instructors may want to use this problem as a lead in to multivariate GARCH
models.)

problems.explanationZh:
23.18
зҷҪй“¶д»·ж јзҡ„жҜ”дҫӢеҸҳеҠЁдёәйӣ¶гҖӮдҪҝз”ЁEWMAжЁЎеһӢпјҢж–№е·®жӣҙж–°дёә

0.94 Г— 0.000225 + 0.06 Г— 0 = 0.0002115гҖӮ

еӣ жӯӨж–°зҡ„ж—ҘжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә\(\sqrt{0.0002115} = 0.01454\)пјҢеҚіжҜҸеӨ©1.454%гҖӮдҪҝз”ЁGARCH(1,1)жЁЎеһӢпјҢж–№е·®жӣҙж–°дёә

0.000002 + 0.94 Г— 0.000225 + 0.04 Г— 0 = 0.0002135гҖӮ

еӣ жӯӨж–°ж—ҘжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә\(\sqrt{0.0002135} = 0.01461\)пјҢеҚіжҜҸеӨ©1.461%гҖӮ

еҲқе§ӢеҚҸж–№е·®дёә\(0.8 \times 0.013 \times 0.015 = 0.000156\)гҖӮдҪҝз”ЁEWMAжЁЎеһӢпјҢеҚҸж–№е·®жӣҙж–°дёә

0.94 Г— 0.000156 + 0.06 Г— 0 = 0.00014664гҖӮ

еӣ жӯӨж–°зӣёе…ізі»ж•°дёә
\(
\frac{0.00014664}{0.01454 \times 0.01271} = 0.7934
\)гҖӮдҪҝз”ЁGARCH(1,1)жЁЎеһӢпјҢеҚҸж–№е·®жӣҙж–°дёә

0.000002 + 0.94 Г— 0.000156 + 0.04 Г— 0 = 0.00014864гҖӮ

ж–°зӣёе…ізі»ж•°дёә
\(
\frac{0.00014864}{0.01461 \times 0.01275} = 0.7977
\)гҖӮ

еҜ№дәҺз»ҷе®ҡзҡ„\(\alpha\)е’Ң\(\beta\)пјҢ\(\omega\)еҸӮж•°е®ҡд№үдәҶж–№е·®жҲ–еҚҸж–№е·®зҡ„й•ҝжңҹе№іеқҮеҖјгҖӮжІЎжңүзҗҶз”ұжңҹжңӣй»„йҮ‘е’ҢзҷҪй“¶зҡ„й•ҝжңҹе№іеқҮжҜҸж—Ҙж–№е·®зӣёеҗҢгҖӮеҗҢж ·пјҢд№ҹжІЎжңүзҗҶз”ұжңҹжңӣй»„йҮ‘дёҺзҷҪй“¶д№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„й•ҝжңҹе№іеқҮеҚҸж–№е·®дёҺй»„йҮ‘жҲ–зҷҪй“¶зҡ„й•ҝжңҹе№іеқҮж–№е·®зӣёеҗҢгҖӮеӣ жӯӨеңЁе®һйҷ…дёӯпјҢжҲ‘д»¬еҫҲеҸҜиғҪеёҢжңӣе…Ғи®ёеңЁGARCH(1,1)жЁЎеһӢдёӯдёҚеҗҢеёӮеңәеҸҳйҮҸжӢҘжңүдёҚеҗҢзҡ„\(\omega\)еҸӮж•°гҖӮпјҲжңүдәӣи®ІеёҲеҸҜиғҪдјҡз”ЁжӯӨй—®йўҳдҪңдёәеӨҡе…ғGARCHжЁЎеһӢзҡ„еј•е…ҘгҖӮпјү

problems.promptEn: Practice 23.19. An Excel spreadsheet containing over 900 days of daily data on a number of different exchange rates and stock indices can be downloaded from the authorвҖҷs website: www-2.rotman.utoronto.ca/~hull/data. Choose one exchange rate and one stock index. Estimate the value of l in the EWMA model that minimizes the value of ОЈi1vi - bi22, where vi is the variance forecast made at the end of day i - 1 and bi is the variance calculated from data between day i and day i + 25. Use the Solver tool in Excel. Set the variance forecast at the end of the first day equal to the square of the return on that day to start the EWMA calculations.

24.6
The seven-year historical hazard rate for AAA-rated companies is

вҲ’ [ln(1 вҲ’ 0.0051)]/7 = 0.00073

or 0.073% per year. Similarly, the historical hazard rates for AA, A, BBB, BB, B, and CCC/C
companies are 0.072%, 0.113%, 0.337%, 1.349%, 3.252%, 9.413% , respectively. The spread
to compensate for default are 60% of these. In basis points, the spreads for AAA, AA, A,

problems.promptEn: Practice 24.7. Describe how netting works. A bank already has one transaction with a counterparty on its books. Explain why a new transaction by a bank with a counterparty can have the effect of increasing or reducing the bankвҖҷs credit exposure to the counterparty.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 24.7. жҸҸиҝ°зҪ‘з»ңзҡ„е·ҘдҪңеҺҹзҗҶгҖӮй“¶иЎҢиҙҰз°ҝдёҠе·Із»ҸжңүдёҖз¬”дёҺдәӨжҳ“еҜ№жүӢзҡ„дәӨжҳ“гҖӮи§ЈйҮҠдёәд»Җд№Ҳй“¶иЎҢдёҺдәӨжҳ“еҜ№жүӢзҡ„ж–°дәӨжҳ“дјҡеўһеҠ жҲ–еҮҸе°‘й“¶иЎҢеҜ№дәӨжҳ“еҜ№жүӢзҡ„
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
24.7
Suppose company A goes bankrupt when it has a number of outstanding contracts with
company B. Netting means that the contracts with a positive value to A are netted against
those with a negative value in order to determine how much, if anything, company A owes

company B. Company A is not allowed to вҖңcherry pickвҖқ by keeping the positive-value
contracts and defaulting on the negative-value contracts.
The new transaction will increase the bankвҖҷs exposure to the counterparty if the contract
tends to have a positive value whenever the existing contract has a positive value and a
negative value whenever the existing contract has a negative value. However, if the new
transaction tends to offset the existing transaction, it is likely to have the incremental effect of
reducing credit risk.
problems.explanationEn:
24.7

The new transaction will increase the bankвҖҷs exposure to the counterparty if the contract

transaction tends to offset the existing transaction, it is likely to have the incremental effect of

problems.promptEn: Practice 24.8. вҖңDVA can improve the bottom line when a bank is experiencing financial difficulties.вҖқ Explain why this statement is true.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 24.8. вҖңеҪ“й“¶иЎҢйҒҮеҲ°иҙўеҠЎеӣ°йҡҫж—¶пјҢDVA еҸҜд»ҘжҸҗй«ҳеҲ©ж¶ҰгҖӮвҖқи§ЈйҮҠдёәд»Җд№ҲиҝҷдёӘиҜҙжі•жҳҜжӯЈзЎ®зҡ„гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
24.8
When a bank is experiencing financial difficulties, its credit spread is likely to increase. This
increases qi* and DVA increases. This is a benefit to the bank: the fact that it is more likely to
default means that its derivatives are worth less.
problems.explanationEn:
24.8
When a bank is experiencing financial difficulties, its credit spread is likely to increase. This
increases qi* and DVA increases. This is a benefit to the bank: the fact that it is more likely to
default means that its derivatives are worth less.

problems.promptEn: Practice 24.9. Explain the difference between the Gaussian copula model for the time to default and CreditMetrics as far as the following are concerned: (a) the definition of a credit loss and (b) the way in which default correlation is modeled.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 24.9. д»Һд»ҘдёӢж–№йқўи§ЈйҮҠиҝқзәҰж—¶й—ҙй«ҳж–ҜиҒ”з»“жЁЎеһӢдёҺ CreditMetrics д№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„е·®ејӮпјҡ(a) дҝЎз”ЁжҚҹеӨұзҡ„е®ҡд№үе’Ң (b) иҝқзәҰзӣёе…іжҖ§зҡ„е»әжЁЎж–№ејҸгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
24.9
(a)
In the Gaussian copula model for time to default, a credit loss is recognized only
when a default occurs. In CreditMetrics, it is recognized when there is a credit
downgrade as well as when there is a default.
(b)
In the Gaussian copula model of time to default, the default correlation arises because
the value of the factor M . This defines the default environment or average default
rate in the economy. In CreditMetrics, a copula model is applied to credit ratings
migration and this determines the joint probability of particular changes in the credit
ratings of two companies.
problems.explanationEn:
24.9
(a)
In the Gaussian copula model for time to default, a credit loss is recognized only
when a default occurs. In CreditMetrics, it is recognized when there is a credit
downgrade as well as when there is a default.
(b)
In the Gaussian copula model of time to default, the default correlation arises because
the value of the factor M . This defines the default environment or average default
rate in the economy. In CreditMetrics, a copula model is applied to credit ratings
migration and this determines the joint probability of particular changes in the credit
ratings of two companies.

problems.promptEn: Practice 24.11. A 4-year corporate bond provides a coupon of 4% per year payable semiannually and has a yield of 5% expressed with continuous compounding. The risk-free yield curve is flat at 3% with continuous compounding. Assume that defaults can take place at the end of each year (immediately before a coupon or principal payment) and that the recovery rate is 30%. Estimate the risk-neutral default probability on the assumption that it is the same each year.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 24.11. еӣӣе№ҙжңҹе…¬еҸёеҖәеҲёзҡ„зҘЁйқўеҲ©зҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ 4%пјҢжҜҸеҚҠе№ҙж”Ҝд»ҳдёҖж¬ЎпјҢж”¶зӣҠзҺҮдёә 5%пјҢд»Ҙиҝһз»ӯеӨҚеҲ©иЎЁзӨәгҖӮж— йЈҺйҷ©ж”¶зӣҠзҺҮжӣІзәҝе№іеқҰдәҺ 3%пјҢиҝһз»ӯеӨҚеҲ©гҖӮеҒҮи®ҫиҝқзәҰеҸҜиғҪеҸ‘з”ҹеңЁжҜҸе№ҙе№ҙеә•пјҲеңЁжҒҜзҘЁжҲ–жң¬йҮ‘ж”Ҝд»ҳд№ӢеүҚпјүпјҢе№¶дё”еӣһж”¶зҺҮдёә 30%гҖӮеҒҮи®ҫжҜҸе№ҙйғҪзӣёеҗҢпјҢдј°и®ЎйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§иҝқзәҰжҰӮзҺҮгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 24.12. A company has issued 3- and 5-year bonds with a coupon of 4% per annum payable annually. The yields on the bonds (expressed with continuous compounding) are 4.5% and 4.75%, respectively. Risk-free rates are 3.5% with continuous compounding for all maturities. The recovery rate is 40%. Defaults can take place halfway through each year. The risk-neutral default rates per year are Q1 for years 1 to 3 and Q2 for years 4 and 5. Estimate Q1 and Q2.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 24.12. дёҖе®¶е…¬еҸёеҸ‘иЎҢдәҶ 3 е№ҙжңҹе’Ң 5 е№ҙжңҹеҖәеҲёпјҢе№ҙеҲ©зҺҮдёә 4%пјҢжҜҸе№ҙж”Ҝд»ҳдёҖж¬ЎгҖӮеҖәеҲёж”¶зӣҠзҺҮпјҲд»Ҙиҝһз»ӯеӨҚеҲ©иЎЁзӨәпјүеҲҶеҲ«дёә 4.5% е’Ң 4.75%гҖӮж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёә 3.5%пјҢжүҖжңүжңҹйҷҗеқҮиҝһз»ӯеӨҚеҲ©гҖӮеӣһж”¶зҺҮдёә40%гҖӮиҝқзәҰеҸҜиғҪдјҡеңЁжҜҸе№ҙзҡ„дёӯй—ҙеҸ‘з”ҹгҖӮжҜҸе№ҙзҡ„йЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§иҝқзәҰзҺҮдёәз¬¬ 1 иҮіз¬¬ 3 е№ҙзҡ„ Q1пјҢз¬¬ 4 е’Ңз¬¬ 5 е№ҙзҡ„ Q2гҖӮдј°и®Ў Q1 е’Ң Q2гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 24.13. Suppose that a financial institution has entered into a swap dependent on the sterling interest rate with counterparty X and an exactly offsetting swap with counterparty Y. Which of the following statements are true and which are false? Explain your answers. (a) The total present value of the cost of defaults is the sum of the present value of the cost of defaults on the contract with X plus the present value of the cost of defaults on the contract with Y. (b) The expected exposure in 1 year on both contracts is the sum of the expected exposure on the contract with X and the expected exposure on the contract with Y.

problems.promptEn: Practice 24.14. вҖңA long forward contract subject to credit risk is a combination of a short position in a no-default put and a long position in a call subject to credit risk.вҖқ Explain this statement.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 24.14. вҖңжүҝеҸ—дҝЎз”ЁйЈҺйҷ©зҡ„еӨҡеӨҙиҝңжңҹеҗҲзәҰжҳҜж— иҝқзәҰзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғзҡ„з©әеӨҙеӨҙеҜёе’ҢжүҝеҸ—дҝЎз”ЁйЈҺйҷ©зҡ„зңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„еӨҡеӨҙеӨҙеҜёзҡ„з»„еҗҲгҖӮвҖқи§ЈйҮҠдёҖдёӢиҝҷдёӘиҜҙжі•гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
24.14
Assume that defaults happen only at the end of the life of the forward contract. In a default-
free world, the forward contract is the combination of a long European call and a short
European put where the strike price of the options equals the delivery price and the maturity
of the options equals the maturity of the forward contract. If the no-default value of the
contract is positive at maturity, the call has a positive value and the put is worth zero. The
impact of defaults on the forward contract is the same as that on the call. If the no-default
value of the contract is negative at maturity, the call has a zero value and the put has a
positive value. In this case, defaults have no effect. Again, the impact of defaults on the
forward contract is the same as that on the call. It follows that the contract has a value equal
to a long position in a call that is subject to default risk and short position in a default-free
put.
problems.explanationEn:
24.14
Assume that defaults happen only at the end of the life of the forward contract. In a default-
free world, the forward contract is the combination of a long European call and a short
European put where the strike price of the options equals the delivery price and the maturity
of the options equals the maturity of the forward contract. If the no-default value of the
contract is positive at maturity, the call has a positive value and the put is worth zero. The
impact of defaults on the forward contract is the same as that on the call. If the no-default
value of the contract is negative at maturity, the call has a zero value and the put has a
positive value. In this case, defaults have no effect. Again, the impact of defaults on the
forward contract is the same as that on the call. It follows that the contract has a value equal
to a long position in a call that is subject to default risk and short position in a default-free
put.

problems.promptEn: Practice 24.17. вҖңWhen a bank is negotiating currency swaps, it should try to ensure that it is receiving the lower interest rate currency from companies with low
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 24.17. вҖңеҪ“й“¶иЎҢиҝӣиЎҢиҙ§еёҒдә’жҚўи°ҲеҲӨж—¶пјҢеә”е°ҪеҠӣзЎ®дҝқд»ҺдҝЎз”ЁйЈҺйҷ©иҫғдҪҺзҡ„е…¬еҸёиҺ·еҫ—иҫғдҪҺеҲ©зҺҮзҡ„иҙ§еёҒгҖӮвҖқи§ЈйҮҠдёҖдёӢдёәд»Җд№ҲгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
24.17
As time passes, there is a tendency for the currency which has the lower interest rate to
strengthen. This means that a swap where we are receiving this currency will tend to move in
the money (i.e., have a positive value). Similarly, a swap where we are paying the currency
will tend to move out of the money (i.e., have a negative value). From this, it follows that our
expected exposure on the swap where we are receiving the low-interest currency is much
greater than our expected exposure on the swap where we are receiving the high-interest
currency. We should therefore look for counterparties with a low credit risk on the side of the
swap where we are receiving the low-interest currency. On the other side of the swap, we are
far less concerned about the creditworthiness of the counterparty.

problems.promptEn: Practice 24.19. In what is known as an asset swap, the promised coupons on a bond that is worth par are exchanged for a floating rate plus a spread. Show that the cost of default on the bond is the present value of the spread payments.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 24.19. еңЁжүҖи°“зҡ„иө„дә§дә’жҚўдёӯпјҢе°ҶйқўеҖјеҖәеҲёзҡ„жүҝиҜәжҒҜзҘЁдәӨжҚўдёәжө®еҠЁеҲ©зҺҮеҠ еҲ©е·®гҖӮиҜҒжҳҺеҖәеҲёзҡ„иҝқзәҰжҲҗжң¬жҳҜеҲ©е·®ж”Ҝд»ҳзҡ„зҺ°еҖјгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
24.19
We can assume that the principal is paid and received at the end of the life of the swap

without changing the swapвҖҷs value. Because the coupons on the bond are paid regardless of
whether the bond defaults, the value of what one side pays is the default-free value of the
bond. This is the current market value of the bond plus the present value of defaults. The
value of what the other side pays is the value of a floating rate bond plus the present value of
the spreads. Hence, the current market value of the bond plus present value of cost of defaults
equals value of floating rate bond plus present value of the spreads. We are told that the bond
is worth par. The floating rate bond is also worth par. It follows that the present value of the
cost of defaults equals the present value of the spread. (Note that if the bond is not worth par,
the asset swap is structured so that one side initially pays the difference between its value and
par to the other side. This preserves our result that the present value of the spreads equals the
present value of the cost of defaults.)

problems.promptEn: Practice 24.22. Give an example of (a) right-way risk and (b) wrong-way risk.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 24.22. дёҫдҫӢиҜҙжҳҺ (a) жӯЈеҗ‘йЈҺйҷ©е’Ң (b) йҖҶеҗ‘йЈҺйҷ©гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
24.22
Right way risk describes the situation when a default by the counterparty is most likely to
occur when the contract has a positive value to the counterparty. An example of right way
risk would be when a counterpartyвҖҷs future depends on the price of a commodity and it enters
into a contract to partially hedging that exposure.
Wrong way risk describes the situation when a default by the counterparty is most likely to
occur when the contract has a negative value to the counterparty. An example of right way
risk would be when a counterparty is a speculator and the contract has the same exposure as
the rest of the counterpartyвҖҷs portfolio.
problems.explanationEn:
24.22
Right way risk describes the situation when a default by the counterparty is most likely to
occur when the contract has a positive value to the counterparty. An example of right way
risk would be when a counterpartyвҖҷs future depends on the price of a commodity and it enters
into a contract to partially hedging that exposure.
Wrong way risk describes the situation when a default by the counterparty is most likely to
occur when the contract has a negative value to the counterparty. An example of right way
risk would be when a counterparty is a speculator and the contract has the same exposure as
the rest of the counterpartyвҖҷs portfolio.

problems.promptEn: Practice 24.24. Suppose a 3-year corporate bond provides a coupon of 7% per year payable semiannually and has a yield of 5% (expressed with semiannual compounding). The yields for all maturities on risk-free bonds is 4% per annum (expressed with semiannual compound- ing). Assume that defaults can take place every 6 months (immediately before a coupon payment) and the recovery rate is 45%. Estimate the hazard rate (assumed constant) for the three years. Assume that the probability of default immediately before a coupon payment is the default probability given by the hazard rate for the previous six months.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 24.24. еҒҮи®ҫ3е№ҙжңҹе…¬еҸёеҖәеҲёзҡ„зҘЁйқўеҲ©зҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ7%пјҢжҜҸеҚҠе№ҙж”Ҝд»ҳдёҖж¬ЎпјҢж”¶зӣҠзҺҮдёә5%пјҲд»ҘеҚҠе№ҙеӨҚеҲ©иЎЁзӨәпјүгҖӮжүҖжңүжңҹйҷҗзҡ„ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҖәеҲёзҡ„е№ҙж”¶зӣҠзҺҮеқҮдёә 4%пјҲд»ҘеҚҠе№ҙеӨҚеҲ©иЎЁзӨәпјүгҖӮеҒҮи®ҫжҜҸ 6 дёӘжңҲеҸ‘з”ҹдёҖж¬ЎиҝқзәҰпјҲеңЁжҒҜзҘЁж”Ҝд»ҳд№ӢеүҚпјүпјҢеӣһж”¶зҺҮдёә 45%гҖӮдј°и®Ўдёүе№ҙзҡ„еҚұйҷ©зҺҮпјҲеҒҮи®ҫжҒ’е®ҡпјүгҖӮеҒҮи®ҫжҒҜзҘЁж”Ҝд»ҳеүҚзҡ„иҝқзәҰжҰӮзҺҮжҳҜеүҚе…ӯдёӘжңҲйЈҺйҷ©зҺҮз»ҷеҮәзҡ„иҝқзәҰжҰӮзҺҮгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 24.25. Explain carefully the distinction between real-world and risk-neutral default probabil- ities. Which is higher? A bank enters into a credit derivative where it agrees to pay USD 100 at the end of 1 year if a certain companyвҖҷs credit rating falls from A to BBB or lower during the year. The 1-year risk-free rate is 5%. Using Table 24.4, estimate a value for the derivative. What assumptions are you making? Do they tend to overstate or under- state the value of the derivative.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 24.25. д»”з»Ҷи§ЈйҮҠзҺ°е®һдё–з•ҢиҝқзәҰжҰӮзҺҮдёҺйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§иҝқзәҰжҰӮзҺҮд№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„еҢәеҲ«гҖӮе“ӘдёҖдёӘжӣҙй«ҳпјҹдёҖе®¶й“¶иЎҢиҝӣе…ҘдёҖд»ҪдҝЎз”ЁиЎҚз”ҹе“ҒеҗҲзәҰпјҡеҰӮжһңжҹҗе…¬еҸёзҡ„дҝЎз”ЁиҜ„зә§еңЁдёҖе№ҙеҶ…д»Һ A йҷҚиҮі BBB жҲ–жӣҙдҪҺпјҢй“¶иЎҢе°ҶеңЁдёҖе№ҙжң«ж”Ҝд»ҳ 100 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮ1 е№ҙжңҹж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёә 5%гҖӮдҪҝз”ЁиЎЁ 24.4 дј°и®ЎиҜҘиЎҚз”ҹе“Ғзҡ„д»·еҖјгҖӮдҪ дҪңеҮәдәҶе“ӘдәӣеҒҮи®ҫпјҹиҝҷдәӣеҒҮи®ҫеҖҫеҗ‘дәҺй«ҳдј°иҝҳжҳҜдҪҺдј°иҜҘиЎҚз”ҹе“Ғзҡ„д»·еҖјпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
24.25
Real world default probabilities are the true probabilities of defaults. They can be estimated
from historical data. Risk-neutral default probabilities are the probabilities of defaults in a
world where all market participants are risk neutral. They can be estimated from bond prices.
Risk-neutral default probabilities are higher. This means that returns in the risk-neutral world
are lower. From Table 24.4, the probability of a company moving from A to BBB or lower in
one year is 5.74%. An estimate of the value of the derivative is therefore
0.0574Г—USD 100Г—e-0.05Г—1 = USD 5.46. The approximation in this is that we are using the real-world
probability of a downgrade. To value the derivative correctly, we should use the risk-neutral
probability of a downgrade. Since the risk-neutral probability of a default is higher than the
real-world probability, it seems likely that the same is true of a downgrade. This means that
USD 5.46 is likely to be too low as an estimate of the value of the derivative.

problems.promptEn: Practice 24.26. The value of a companyвҖҷs equity is USD 4 million and the volatility of its equity is 60%. The debt that will have to be repaid in 2 years is USD 15 million. The risk-free interest rate is 6% per annum. Use MertonвҖҷs model to estimate the expected loss from default, the probability of default, and the recovery rate in the event of default. (Hint: The Solver function in Excel can be used for this question, as indicated in footnote 10.)

problems.promptEn: Practice 24.27. Suppose that a bank has a total of USD 10 million of exposures of a certain type. The 1-year probability of default averages 1% and the recovery rate averages 40%. The copula correlation parameter is 0.2. Estimate the 99.5% 1-year credit VaR.

иҝҷз»ҷеҮәдәҶ 99.5% дҝЎз”Ё VaR дёә

\[ 10 \times (1 - 0.4) \times 0.0946 = 0.568 \]

зҷҫдёҮзҫҺе…ғпјҢеҚі 568,000 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 25.1. Explain the difference between a regular credit default swap and a binary credit default swap.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 25.1. и§ЈйҮҠеёёи§„дҝЎз”ЁиҝқзәҰжҺүжңҹе’ҢдәҢе…ғдҝЎз”ЁиҝқзәҰжҺүжңҹд№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„еҢәеҲ«гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
25.1
Both provide insurance against a particular company defaulting during a period of time. In a
credit default swap, the payoff is the notional principal amount multiplied by one minus the
recovery rate. In a binary swap, the payoff is the notional principal.

problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 25.3. и§ЈйҮҠзҺ°йҮ‘ CDO е’ҢеҗҲжҲҗ CDO д№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„еҢәеҲ«гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
25.3
A cash CDO is created by buying bonds and tranching out the risks. A synthetic CDO is
created from a portfolio of short CDSs (i.e., CDS that are selling protection).

problems.promptEn: Practice 25.4. Explain the term вҖңsingle-tranche trading.вҖқ
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 25.4. и§ЈйҮҠвҖңеҚ•з¬”дәӨжҳ“вҖқдёҖиҜҚгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
25.4
Single tranche trading occurs when a tranche of a synthetic CDO is traded without the
underlying portfolios of short CDSs being created. The underlying portfolio is simply used as
a reference point to determine cash flows on the tranche.

problems.promptEn: Practice 25.5. What is a first-to-default credit default swap? Does its value increase or decrease as the default correlation between the companies in the basket increases? Explain your answer.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 25.5. д»Җд№ҲжҳҜе…ҲиҝқзәҰдҝЎз”ЁиҝқзәҰжҺүжңҹпјҹйҡҸзқҖзҜ®еӯҗйҮҢе…¬еҸёд№Ӣй—ҙиҝқзәҰзӣёе…іжҖ§зҡ„еўһеҠ пјҢе®ғзҡ„д»·еҖјдјҡеўһеҠ иҝҳжҳҜеҮҸе°‘пјҹи§ЈйҮҠдҪ зҡ„зӯ”жЎҲгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
25.5
In a first-to-default basket CDS there are a number of reference entities. When the first one
defaults, there is a payoff (calculated in the usual way for a CDS) and basket CDS terminates.
The value of a first-to-default basket CDS decreases as the correlation between the reference
entities in the basket increases. This is because the probability of a default is high when the
correlation is zero and decreases as the correlation increases. In the limit when the correlation
is one, there is in effect only one company and the probability of a default is quite low.
problems.explanationEn:
25.5

defaults, there is a payoff (calculated in the usual way for a CDS) and basket CDS terminates.
The value of a first-to-default basket CDS decreases as the correlation between the reference
entities in the basket increases. This is because the probability of a default is high when the
correlation is zero and decreases as the correlation increases. In the limit when the correlation
is one, there is in effect only one company and the probability of a default is quite low.

problems.promptEn: Practice 25.6. Explain the difference between risk-neutral and real-world default probabilities. Which should be used for valuing CDSs?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 25.6. и§ЈйҮҠйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§е’ҢзҺ°е®һдё–з•ҢиҝқзәҰжҰӮзҺҮд№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„е·®ејӮгҖӮеә”иҜҘдҪҝз”Ёе“ӘдёӘжқҘеҜ№ CDS иҝӣиЎҢдј°еҖјпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
25.6
Risk-neutral default probabilities are backed out from credit default swaps or bond prices.
Real-world default probabilities are calculated from historical data. Risk-neutral probabilities
should be used for valuation (e.g., the valuations of CDSs). Real world probabilities should
be used for scenario analysis.
problems.explanationEn:
25.6
Risk-neutral default probabilities are backed out from credit default swaps or bond prices.
Real-world default probabilities are calculated from historical data. Risk-neutral probabilities
should be used for valuation (e.g., the valuations of CDSs). Real world probabilities should
be used for scenario analysis.

problems.promptEn: Practice 25.7. Explain why a total return swap can be useful as a financing tool.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 25.7. и§ЈйҮҠдёәд»Җд№ҲжҖ»еӣһжҠҘжҺүжңҹеҸҜд»ҘдҪңдёәдёҖз§Қжңүз”Ёзҡ„иһҚиө„е·Ҙе…·гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
25.7
Suppose a company wants to buy some assets. If a total return swap is used, a financial
institution buys the assets and enters into a swap with the company where it pays the
company the return on the assets and receives from the company LIBOR plus a spread. The
financial institution has less risk than it would have if it lent the company money and used the
assets as collateral. This is because, in the event of a default by the company, it owns the

assets.
problems.explanationEn:

assets.

problems.promptEn: Practice 25.8. Suppose that the risk-free zero curve is flat at 7% per annum with continuous compounding and that defaults can occur halfway through each year in a new 5-year credit default swap. Suppose that the recovery rate is 30% and the hazard rate is 3%. Estimate the credit default swap spread. Assume payments are made annually.

problems.promptEn: Practice 25.9. What is the value of the swap in Problem 25.8 per dollar of notional principal to the protection buyer if the credit default swap spread is 150 basis points?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 25.9. еҰӮжһңдҝЎз”ЁиҝқзәҰжҺүжңҹеҲ©е·®дёә 150 дёӘеҹәзӮ№пјҢй—®йўҳ 25.8 дёӯзҡ„жҜҸзҫҺе…ғеҗҚд№үжң¬йҮ‘еҜ№дҝқжҠӨд№°ж–№зҡ„жҺүжңҹд»·еҖјжҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
25.9
If the credit default swap spread is 150 basis points, the value of the swap to the buyer of
protection is:
0.0826 в”Җ (3.7412 + 0.0590) Г— 0.0150 = 0.0256

per dollar of notional principal.

problems.promptEn: Practice 25.11. How does a 5-year nth-to-default credit default swap work? Consider a basket of 100 reference entities where each reference entity has a probability of defaulting in each year of 1%. As the default correlation between the reference entities increases what would you expect to happen to the value of the swap when (a) n = 1 and (b) n = 25. Explain your answer.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 25.11. 5е№ҙжңҹз¬¬nж¬ЎиҝқзәҰдҝЎз”ЁиҝқзәҰжҺүжңҹеҰӮдҪ•иҝҗдҪңпјҹиҖғиҷ‘дёҖзҜ®еӯҗ 100 дёӘеҸӮиҖғе®һдҪ“пјҢе…¶дёӯжҜҸдёӘеҸӮиҖғе®һдҪ“жҜҸе№ҙзҡ„иҝқзәҰжҰӮзҺҮдёә 1%гҖӮйҡҸзқҖеҸӮиҖғе®һдҪ“д№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„й»ҳи®Өзӣёе…іжҖ§еўһеҠ пјҢеҪ“ (a) n = 1 е’Ң (b) n = 25 ж—¶пјҢжӮЁйў„и®Ўдә’жҚўд»·еҖјдјҡеҸ‘з”ҹд»Җд№ҲгҖӮиҜ·и§ЈйҮҠжӮЁзҡ„зӯ”жЎҲгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
25.11
A five-year n th to default credit default swap works in the same way as a regular credit
default swap except that there is a basket of companies. The payoff occurs when the n th
default from the companies in the basket occurs. After the n th default has occurred, the swap
ceases to exist. When
1
n = (so that the swap is a вҖңfirst to defaultвҖқ), an increase in the default
correlation lowers the value of the swap. When the default correlation is zero, there are 100
independent events that can lead to a payoff. As the correlation increases, the probability of a
payoff decreases. In the limit when the correlation is perfect, there is in effect only one
company and therefore only one event that can lead to a payoff.
When
25
n =
 (so that the swap is a 25th to default), an increase in the default correlation
increases the value of the swap. When the default correlation is zero, there is virtually no

chance that there will be 25 defaults and the value of the swap is very close to zero. As the
correlation increases, the probability of multiple defaults increases. In the limit when the
correlation is perfect, there is in effect only one company and the value of a 25th-to-default
credit default swap is the same as the value of a first-to-default swap.

problems.promptEn: Practice 25.16. Explain how forward contracts and options on credit default swaps are structured.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 25.16. и§ЈйҮҠдҝЎз”ЁиҝқзәҰжҺүжңҹиҝңжңҹеҗҲзәҰе’Ңжңҹжқғзҡ„з»“жһ„гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
25.16
When a company enters into a long (short) forward contract, it is obligated to buy (sell) the
protection given by a specified credit default swap with a specified spread at a specified
future time. When a company buys a call (put) option contract, it has the option to buy (sell)
the protection given by a specified credit default swap with a specified spread at a specified

future time. Both contracts are normally structured so that they cease to exist if a default
occurs during the life of the contract.
problems.explanationEn:
25.16

future time. When a company buys a call (put) option contract, it has the option to buy (sell)
the protection given by a specified credit default swap with a specified spread at a specified

future time. Both contracts are normally structured so that they cease to exist if a default
occurs during the life of the contract.

problems.promptEn: Practice 25.17. вҖңThe position of a buyer of a credit default swap is similar to the position of someone who is long a risk-free bond and short a corporate bond.вҖқ Explain this statement.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 25.17. вҖңдҝЎз”ЁиҝқзәҰдә’жҚўд№°е®¶зҡ„з«Ӣеңәзұ»дјјдәҺеҒҡеӨҡж— йЈҺйҷ©еҖәеҲёе№¶еҒҡз©әе…¬еҸёеҖәеҲёзҡ„дәәзҡ„з«ӢеңәгҖӮвҖқи§ЈйҮҠдёҖдёӢиҝҷдёӘиҜҙжі•гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
25.17
A credit default swap insures a corporate bond issued by the reference entity against default.
Its approximate effect is to convert the corporate bond into a risk-free bond. The buyer of a
credit default swap has therefore chosen to exchange a corporate bond for a risk-free bond.
This means that the buyer is long a risk-free bond and short a similar corporate bond.
problems.explanationEn:
25.17
A credit default swap insures a corporate bond issued by the reference entity against default.
Its approximate effect is to convert the corporate bond into a risk-free bond. The buyer of a
credit default swap has therefore chosen to exchange a corporate bond for a risk-free bond.
This means that the buyer is long a risk-free bond and short a similar corporate bond.

problems.promptEn: Practice 25.22. Explain the difference between base correlation and compound correlation.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 25.22. и§ЈйҮҠеҹәжң¬зӣёе…іжҖ§е’ҢеӨҚеҗҲзӣёе…іжҖ§д№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„еҢәеҲ«гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
25.22
Compound correlation for a tranche is the correlation which when substituted into the one-
factor Gaussian copula model produces the market quote for the tranche. Base correlation is
the correlation which is consistent with the one-factor Gaussian copula and market quotes for
the 0 to X% tranche where X% is a detachment point. It ensures that the expected loss on the
0 to X% tranche equals the sum of the expected losses on the underlying traded tranches.
problems.explanationEn:
25.22
Compound correlation for a tranche is the correlation which when substituted into the one-
factor Gaussian copula model produces the market quote for the tranche. Base correlation is
the correlation which is consistent with the one-factor Gaussian copula and market quotes for
the 0 to X% tranche where X% is a detachment point. It ensures that the expected loss on the
0 to X% tranche equals the sum of the expected losses on the underlying traded tranches.

problems.promptEn: Practice 25.24. Suppose that the risk-free zero curve is flat at 6% per annum with continuous compounding and that defaults can occur at times 0.25 years, 0.75 years, 1.25 years, and 1.75 years in a 2-year plain vanilla credit default swap with semiannual payments. Suppose that the recovery rate is 20% and the unconditional probabilities of default (as seen at time zero) are 1% at times 0.25 years and 0.75 years, and 1.5% at times 1.25 years and 1.75 years. What is the credit default swap spread? What would the credit default spread be if the instrument were a binary credit default swap?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 25.24. еҒҮи®ҫж— йЈҺйҷ©йӣ¶жӣІзәҝе№іеқҰпјҢе№ҙеҲ©зҺҮдёә 6%пјҢиҝһз»ӯеӨҚеҲ©пјҢе№¶дё”еңЁеҚҠе№ҙд»ҳж¬ҫзҡ„ 2 е№ҙжңҹжҷ®йҖҡдҝЎз”ЁиҝқзәҰжҺүжңҹдёӯпјҢиҝқзәҰеҸҜиғҪеңЁ 0.25 е№ҙгҖҒ0.75 е№ҙгҖҒ1.25 е№ҙе’Ң 1.75 е№ҙеҸ‘з”ҹгҖӮеҒҮи®ҫеӣһж”¶зҺҮдёә 20%пјҢж— жқЎд»¶иҝқзәҰжҰӮзҺҮпјҲеҰӮйӣ¶ж—¶еҲ»жүҖзӨәпјүеңЁ 0.25 е№ҙе’Ң 0.75 е№ҙж—¶дёә 1%пјҢеңЁ 1.25 е№ҙе’Ң 1.75 е№ҙж—¶дёә 1.5%гҖӮд»Җд№ҲжҳҜдҝЎз”ЁиҝқзәҰжҺүжңҹеҲ©е·®пјҹеҰӮжһңиҜҘе·Ҙе…·жҳҜдәҢе…ғдҝЎз”ЁиҝқзәҰдә’жҚўпјҢйӮЈд№ҲдҝЎз”ЁиҝқзәҰеҲ©е·®жҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹ

problems.promptEn: Practice 25.25. Assume that the hazard rate for a company is l and the recovery rate is R. The risk-free interest rate is 5% per annum. Default always occurs halfway through a year. The spread for a 5-year plain vanilla CDS where payments are made annually is 120 basis points and the spread for a 5-year binary CDS where payments are made annually is 160 basis points. Estimate R and l.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 25.25. еҒҮи®ҫдёҖе®¶е…¬еҸёзҡ„йЈҺйҷ©зҺҮдёәlпјҢеӣһж”¶зҺҮдёәRгҖӮж— йЈҺйҷ©е№ҙеҲ©зҺҮдёә5%гҖӮиҝқзәҰжҖ»жҳҜеҸ‘з”ҹеңЁдёҖе№ҙзҡ„дёӯй—ҙгҖӮжҜҸе№ҙд»ҳж¬ҫзҡ„ 5 е№ҙжңҹжҷ®йҖҡ CDS зҡ„еҲ©е·®дёә 120 дёӘеҹәзӮ№пјҢжҜҸе№ҙд»ҳж¬ҫзҡ„ 5 е№ҙжңҹдәҢе…ғ CDS зҡ„еҲ©е·®дёә 160 дёӘеҹәзӮ№гҖӮдј°и®Ў R е’Ң lгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
25.25
The spread for a binary credit default swap is equal to the spread for a regular credit default
swap divided by 1
R
-
 where R is the recovery rate. This means that 1
R
-
 equals 0.75 so
that the recovery rate is 25%. To find lambda, we search for the conditional annual default rate
that leads to the present value of payments being equal to the present value of payoffs. The
answer is lambda = 0.0156. The present value of payoffs (per dollar of principal) is then 0.0499.
The present value of regular payments is 4.1245. The present value of accrual payments is
0.0332.

problems.promptEn: Practice 25.26. Explain how you would expect the returns offered on the various tranches in a synthetic CDO to change when the correlation between the bonds in the portfolio increases.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 25.26. и§ЈйҮҠеҪ“жҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲдёӯеҖәеҲёд№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„зӣёе…іжҖ§еўһеҠ ж—¶пјҢжӮЁйў„и®ЎеҗҲжҲҗ CDO дёӯеҗ„дёӘйғЁеҲҶжҸҗдҫӣзҡ„еӣһжҠҘе°ҶеҰӮдҪ•еҸҳеҢ–гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
25.26
As the correlation increases, the yield on the equity tranche decreases and the yield on the
senior tranches increases. To understand this, consider what happens as the correlation
increases from zero to one. Initially, the equity tranche is much more risky than the senior
tranche. But as the correlation approaches one, the companies become essentially the same.
We are then in the position where either all companies default or no companies default and
the tranches have similar risk.

problems.promptEn: Practice 25.27. Suppose that: (a) The yield on a 5-year risk-free bond is 7%. (b) The yield on a 5-year corporate bond issued by company X is 9.5%. (c) A 5-year credit default swap providing insurance against company X defaulting costs 150 basis points per year. What arbitrage opportunity is there in this situation? What arbitrage opportunity would there be if the credit default spread were 300 basis points instead of 150 basis points?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 25.27. еҒҮи®ҫпјҡ (a) 5 е№ҙжңҹж— йЈҺйҷ©еҖәеҲёзҡ„ж”¶зӣҠзҺҮдёә 7%гҖӮ (b) X е…¬еҸёеҸ‘иЎҢзҡ„ 5 е№ҙжңҹе…¬еҸёеҖәеҲёзҡ„ж”¶зӣҠзҺҮдёә 9.5%гҖӮ (c) дёә X е…¬еҸёиҝқзәҰжҸҗдҫӣдҝқйҷ©зҡ„ 5 е№ҙжңҹдҝЎз”ЁиҝқзәҰжҺүжңҹиҙ№з”ЁдёәжҜҸе№ҙ 150 дёӘеҹәзӮ№гҖӮ иҝҷз§Қжғ…еҶөдёӢжңүд»Җд№ҲеҘ—еҲ©жңәдјҡе‘ўпјҹеҰӮжһңдҝЎз”ЁиҝқзәҰеҲ©е·®жҳҜ300дёӘеҹәзӮ№иҖҢдёҚжҳҜ150дёӘеҹәзӮ№пјҢдјҡжңүд»Җд№ҲеҘ—еҲ©жңәдјҡпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
25.27
When the credit default swap spread is 150 basis points, an arbitrageur can earn more than
the risk-free rate by buying the corporate bond and buying protection. If the arbitrageur can
finance trades at the risk-free rate (by shorting the riskless bond), it is possible to lock in an
almost certain profit of 100 basis points. When the credit spread is 300 basis points, the
arbitrageur can short the corporate bond, sell protection and buy a risk free bond. This will
lock in an almost certain profit of 50 basis points. The arbitrage is not perfect for a number of
reasons:
(a) It assumes that both the corporate bond and the riskless bond are par yield bonds and that
interest rates are constant. In practice, the riskless bond may be worth more or less than
par at the time of a default so that a credit default swap under protects or overprotects the
bond holder relative to the position with a riskless bond.
(b) There is uncertainty created by the cheapest-to-delivery bond option.
(c) To be a perfect hedge, the credit default swap would have to give the buyer of protection
the right to sell the bond for face value plus accrued interest, not just face value.
The arbitrage opportunities assume that market participants can short corporate bonds and
borrow at the risk-free rate. The definition of the credit event in the ISDA agreement is also
occasionally a problem. It can occasionally happen that there is a "credit event" but promised
payments on the bond are made.

problems.explanationZh:
25.27
еҪ“дҝЎз”ЁиҝқзәҰжҺүжңҹеҲ©е·®дёә150дёӘеҹәзӮ№ж—¶пјҢеҘ—еҲ©иҖ…еҸҜд»ҘйҖҡиҝҮд№°е…Ҙе…¬еҸёеҖәеҲёе’Ңд№°е…ҘдҝқжҠӨиҖҢиөҡеҸ–й«ҳдәҺж— йЈҺйҷ©ж”¶зӣҠзҺҮзҡ„еӣһжҠҘгҖӮеҰӮжһңеҘ—еҲ©иҖ…иғҪд»Ҙж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮиһҚиө„пјҲйҖҡиҝҮеҚ–з©әж— йЈҺйҷ©еҖәеҲёпјүпјҢе°ұжңүеҸҜиғҪй”Ғе®ҡеӨ§зәҰ100дёӘеҹәзӮ№зҡ„еҮ д№ҺзЎ®е®ҡзҡ„еҲ©ж¶ҰгҖӮеҪ“дҝЎз”ЁеҲ©е·®дёә300дёӘеҹәзӮ№ж—¶пјҢеҘ—еҲ©иҖ…еҲҷеҸҜд»ҘеҚ–з©әе…¬еҸёеҖәеҲёгҖҒеҚ–еҮәдҝқжҠӨе№¶д№°е…Ҙж— йЈҺйҷ©еҖәеҲёпјҢй”Ғе®ҡеӨ§зәҰ50дёӘеҹәзӮ№зҡ„еҮ д№ҺзЎ®е®ҡеҲ©ж¶ҰгҖӮиҝҷдёӘеҘ—еҲ©дёҚе®ҢзҫҺпјҢеҺҹеӣ жңүеҮ дёӘпјҡ
(a) е®ғеҒҮи®ҫе…¬еҸёеҖәе’Ңж— йЈҺйҷ©еҖәеҲёйғҪжҳҜзҘЁйқўж”¶зӣҠеҖәеҲёдё”еҲ©зҺҮдҝқжҢҒдёҚеҸҳгҖӮе®һйҷ…дёҠпјҢж— йЈҺйҷ©еҖәеҲёеңЁиҝқзәҰж—¶зҡ„д»·еҖјеҸҜиғҪй«ҳдәҺжҲ–дҪҺдәҺзҘЁйқўеҖјпјҢеӣ жӯӨдҝЎз”ЁиҝқзәҰжҺүжңҹеҜ№еҖәеҲёжҢҒжңүдәәзҡ„дҝқжҠӨеҸҜиғҪдёҚи¶іжҲ–иҝҮеәҰпјҢзӣёиҫғдәҺжҢҒжңүж— йЈҺйҷ©еҖәеҲёзҡ„жғ…еҶөгҖӮ
(b) жңҖдҪҺдәӨеүІеҖәеҲёжңҹжқғеёҰжқҘдёҚзЎ®е®ҡжҖ§гҖӮ
(c) дёәдәҶжҲҗдёәе®ҢзҫҺеҜ№еҶІпјҢдҝЎз”ЁиҝқзәҰжҺүжңҹеә”иөӢдәҲдҝқжҠӨд№°ж–№жқғеҲ©д»ҘзҘЁйқўеҖјеҠ еә”и®ЎеҲ©жҒҜд»·ж јеҮәе”®еҖәеҲёпјҢиҖҢдёҚд»…д»…жҳҜзҘЁйқўеҖјгҖӮ
еҘ—еҲ©жңәдјҡеҒҮи®ҫеёӮеңәеҸӮдёҺиҖ…иғҪеӨҹеҚ–з©әе…¬еҸёеҖәеҲёе№¶д»Ҙж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮеҖҹж¬ҫгҖӮISDAеҚҸи®®дёӯдҝЎз”ЁдәӢд»¶зҡ„е®ҡд№үжңүж—¶д№ҹдјҡеёҰжқҘй—®йўҳпјҢжҜ”еҰӮдјҡеҮәзҺ°вҖңдҝЎз”ЁдәӢд»¶вҖқдҪҶеҖәеҲёд»ҚжӯЈеёёж”Ҝд»ҳжүҝиҜәж¬ҫйЎ№зҡ„жғ…еҶөгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 25.30. Table 25.6 shows the 5-year iTraxx index was 77 basis points on January 31, 2008. Assume the risk-free rate is 5% for all maturities, the recovery rate is 40%, and payments are quarterly. Assume also that the spread of 77 basis points applies to all maturities. Use the DerivaGem CDS worksheet to calculate a hazard rate consistent with the spread. Use this in the CDO worksheet with 10 integration points to imply base correlations for each tranche from the quotes for January 31, 2008. 614 Exotic Options Derivatives such as European and American call and put options are what are termed plain vanilla products. They have standard well-defined properties and trade actively. Their prices or implied volatilities are quoted by exchanges or by interdealer brokers on a regular basis. One of the exciting aspects of the over-the-counter derivatives market is the number of nonstandard products that have been created by financial engineers. These products are termed exotic options, or simply exotics. Although they usually constitute a relatively small part of its portfolio, exotics are important to a derivatives dealer because they are generally much more profitable than plain vanilla products. Exotic products are developed for a number of reasons. Sometimes they meet a genuine hedging need in the market; sometimes there are tax, accounting, legal, or regulatory reasons why corporate treasurers, fund managers, and financial institutions find exotic products attractive; sometimes the products are designed to reflect a view on potential future movements in particular market variables; occasionally an exotic product is designed by a derivatives dealer to appear more attractive than it is to an unwary corporate treasurer or fund manager. In this chapter, we describe some of the more commonly occurring exotic options and discuss their valuation. We assume that the underlying asset provides a yield at rate q. As discussed in Chapters 17 and 18, for an option on a stock index q should be set equal to the dividend yield on the index, for an option on a currency it should be set equal to the foreign risk-free rate, and for an option on a futures contract it should be set equal to the domestic risk-free rate. Many of the options discussed in this chapter can be valued using the DerivaGem software. 26 C H A P T E R

problems.promptEn: Practice 26.1. Explain the difference between a forward start option and a chooser option.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 26.1. и§ЈйҮҠеҗ‘еүҚеҗҜеҠЁйҖүйЎ№е’ҢйҖүжӢ©еҷЁйҖүйЎ№д№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„еҢәеҲ«гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
26.1
A forward start option is an option that is paid for now but will start at some time in the
future. The strike price is usually equal to the price of the asset at the time the option starts. A
chooser option is an option where, at some time in the future, the holder chooses whether the
option is a call or a put.
problems.explanationEn:
26.1

future. The strike price is usually equal to the price of the asset at the time the option starts. A
chooser option is an option where, at some time in the future, the holder chooses whether the
option is a call or a put.

problems.promptEn: Practice 26.3. Consider a chooser option where the holder has the right to choose between a European call and a European put at any time during a 2-year period. The maturity dates and strike prices for the calls and puts are the same regardless of when the choice is made. Is it ever optimal to make the choice before the end of the 2-year period? Explain your answer.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 26.3. иҖғиҷ‘дёҖз§ҚйҖүжӢ©еҷЁжңҹжқғпјҢе…¶дёӯжҢҒжңүдәәжңүжқғеңЁдёӨе№ҙжңҹй—ҙзҡ„д»»дҪ•ж—¶й—ҙеңЁж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғе’Ңж¬§ејҸзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғд№Ӣй—ҙиҝӣиЎҢйҖүжӢ©гҖӮж— и®әдҪ•ж—¶еҒҡеҮәйҖүжӢ©пјҢзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғе’ҢзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғзҡ„еҲ°жңҹж—Ҙе’Ңжү§иЎҢд»·ж јйғҪжҳҜзӣёеҗҢзҡ„гҖӮеңЁдёӨе№ҙжңҹз»“жқҹд№ӢеүҚеҒҡеҮәйҖүжӢ©жҳҜеҗҰжҳҜжңҖдҪійҖүжӢ©пјҹи§ЈйҮҠдҪ зҡ„зӯ”жЎҲгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
26.3
No, it is never optimal to choose early. The resulting cash flows are the same regardless of
when the choice is made. There is no point in the holder making a commitment earlier than
necessary. This argument applies when the holder chooses between two American options
providing the options cannot be exercised before the 2-year point. If the early exercise period
starts as soon as the choice is made, the argument does not hold. For example, if the stock
price fell to almost nothing in the first six months, the holder would choose a put option at
this time and exercise it immediately.
problems.explanationEn:
26.3
No, it is never optimal to choose early. The resulting cash flows are the same regardless of
when the choice is made. There is no point in the holder making a commitment earlier than
necessary. This argument applies when the holder chooses between two American options
providing the options cannot be exercised before the 2-year point. If the early exercise period
starts as soon as the choice is made, the argument does not hold. For example, if the stock
price fell to almost nothing in the first six months, the holder would choose a put option at
this time and exercise it immediately.

problems.promptEn: Practice 26.4. Suppose that c1 and p1 are the prices of a European average price call and a European average price put with strike price K and maturity T, c2 and p2 are the prices of a European average strike call and European average strike put with maturity T, and c3 and p3 are the prices of a regular European call and a regular European put with strike price K and maturity T. Show that c1 + c2 - c3 = p1 + p2 - p3.

26.4
c1гҖҒc2гҖҒc3гҖҒp1гҖҒp2гҖҒp3 зҡ„еҜ№еә”жңҹжқғзҡ„ж”Ҝд»ҳеҲҶеҲ«еҰӮдёӢпјҡ

c1 зҡ„ж”Ҝд»ҳдёә max(0, S - K)пјҢе…¶дёӯ S жҳҜе№іеқҮд»·ж јпјҢK жҳҜжү§иЎҢд»·гҖӮ
c2 зҡ„ж”Ҝд»ҳдёә max(0, S_T - S)пјҢе…¶дёӯ S жҳҜе№іеқҮжү§иЎҢд»·пјҢS_T жҳҜеҲ°жңҹж—¶зҡ„д»·ж јгҖӮ
c3 зҡ„ж”Ҝд»ҳдёә max(0, S_T - K)пјҢеёёи§„ж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғж”Ҝд»ҳгҖӮ
p1 зҡ„ж”Ҝд»ҳдёә max(0, K - S)пјҢе№іеқҮд»·ж јзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғж”Ҝд»ҳгҖӮ
p2 зҡ„ж”Ҝд»ҳдёә max(0, S - S_T)пјҢе№іеқҮжү§иЎҢд»·зңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғж”Ҝд»ҳгҖӮ
p3 зҡ„ж”Ҝд»ҳдёә max(0, K - S_T)пјҢеёёи§„ж¬§ејҸзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғж”Ҝд»ҳгҖӮ

д»ҺдёӯжңүдёҖдёӘжҒ’зӯүе…ізі»пјҡ

c1 - p1 = S - K
c2 - p2 = S_T - S
c3 - p3 = S_T - K

еӣ жӯӨпјҢжңү

(c1 - p1) + (c2 - p2) = (c3 - p3)

еҚі

c1 + c2 - c3 = p1 + p2 - p3

problems.explanationZh:
26.6
иҖғиҷ‘еҪ“ H вүҘ K ж—¶пјҢdo_c зҡ„е…¬ејҸпјҡ

\[
do_c = S_0 e^{-qT} N(x_1) - K e^{-rT} N(x_2) - S_0 e^{-qT} \left(\frac{H}{S_0}\right)^{2\lambda} N(y_1) + K e^{-rT} \left(\frac{H}{S_0}\right)^{2\lambda - 2} N(y_2)\]

е°Ҷ H = K д»Је…ҘпјҢе№¶жіЁж„ҸеҲ°

\[
\lambda = \frac{r - q}{\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2}
\]

з”ұжӯӨеҸҜд»ҘжҺЁеҜјеҫ—

\[
x_1 = y_1, \quad x_2 = y_2
\]

еӣ жӯӨ

\[
do_c = c - S_0 e^{-qT} \left(\frac{H}{S_0}\right)^{2\lambda} N(y_1) + K e^{-rT} \left(\frac{H}{S_0}\right)^{2\lambda - 2} N(y_2)
\]

иҖҢеҪ“ H вүӨ K ж—¶пјҢdi_c зҡ„е…¬ејҸдёә

\[
di_c = c - S_0 e^{-qT} \left(\frac{H}{S_0}\right)^{2\lambda} N(y_1) + K e^{-rT} \left(\frac{H}{S_0}\right)^{2\lambda - 2} N(y_2)\]

еӣ дёә do_c е’Ң di_c зӣёе·®з¬ҰеҸ·жҠөж¶ҲпјҢдё”еҜ№дәҺ H = KпјҢжңү y_1 е’Ң y_2 ж»Ўи¶ізӣёеҗҢжқЎд»¶пјҢж•…дёӨејҸзӣёеҗҢгҖӮ

ж №жҚ®д№Ұдёӯзҡ„е…¬ејҸпјҢеҪ“ H = K ж—¶пјҢжңү 1 + y = yпјҢеӣ жӯӨдёӨдёӘе…ідәҺ do_c зҡ„иЎЁиҫҫејҸеҪ“ H = K ж—¶жҳҜзӯүд»·зҡ„гҖӮ

problems.explanation:
26.7
The option is in the money only when the asset price is less than the strike price. However, in
these circumstances the barrier has been hit and the option has ceased to exist.

26.8
иҜҘй—®йўҳзҡ„и®әиҜҒзұ»дјјдәҺз¬¬11з« е…ідәҺдёҚж”Ҝд»ҳиӮЎжҒҜиӮЎзҘЁзҡ„жҷ®йҖҡжңҹжқғзҡ„и®әиҜҒгҖӮиҖғиҷ‘дёҖдёӘз”ұжңҹжқғе’ҢзҺ°йҮ‘з»„жҲҗзҡ„жҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲпјҢзҺ°йҮ‘йҮ‘йўқзӣёеҪ“дәҺз»Ҳжӯўжү§иЎҢд»·ж јзҡ„зҺ°еҖјгҖӮ

жҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲеҲқе§ӢзҺ°йҮ‘йҮ‘йўқдёә

\[
K e^{-gT}
\]

еңЁж—¶й—ҙ \( \tau \)пјҲ\( 0 \leq \tau \leq T \)пјүж—¶пјҢзҺ°йҮ‘дјҡеўһй•ҝдёә

\[
K e^{-gT} e^{r \tau} = K e^{-g(T - \tau)} e^{(r - g) \tau}
\]

еӣ дёә \( r > g \)пјҢжүҖд»ҘиҝҷйҮ‘йўқе°ҸдәҺ \( K e^{-g (T - \tau)} \)пјҢд№ҹеҚіе°ҸдәҺиЎҢжқғжүҖйңҖйҮ‘йўқгҖӮеӣ жӯӨпјҢеҰӮжһңжҸҗеүҚиЎҢжқғпјҢд»ҘжӯӨжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲжһ„е»әзҡ„жңҖз»Ҳиө„дә§еңЁж—¶й—ҙ T е°ҸдәҺ \( S_T \)гҖӮ

еңЁж—¶й—ҙ TпјҢзҺ°йҮ‘иҙҰжҲ·еү©дҪҷйҮ‘йўқдёә \( K e^{-gT} \)пјҢиҜҘйҮ‘йўқжӯЈеҘҪж»Ўи¶ідә«еҸ—жңҹжқғзҡ„жү§иЎҢд»·ж јйңҖжұӮгҖӮеҰӮжһңйҖүжӢ©жҺЁиҝҹеҲ°ж—¶й—ҙ T еҶҚиЎҢжқғпјҢеҲҷжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲзҡ„з»Ҳжӯўд»·еҖјдёә

\[
\max(S_T - K, 0)
\]

иҝҷиҮіе°‘дёҺжӯӨеүҚжҸҗеүҚиЎҢжқғзҡ„з»“жһңдёҖж ·еҘҪпјҢз”ҡиҮіжӣҙеҘҪгҖӮз”ұжӯӨеҸҜи§ҒпјҢеҪ“жү§иЎҢд»·ж јд»ҘдҪҺдәҺж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮзҡ„йҖҹеәҰеўһй•ҝж—¶пјҢжҸҗеүҚиЎҢдҪҝжңҹжқғдёҚжҳҜжңҖдјҳзӯ–з•ҘгҖӮ

that the stock price, the value of the option is proportional to the stock price. The same
argument as that given in the text for forward start options shows that if 1t is the time when
the option starts and 2t is the time when it finishes, the option has the same value as an
option starting today with a life of 2

price.

problems.promptEn: Practice 26.11. Explain why delta hedging is easier for Asian options than for regular options.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 26.11. и§ЈйҮҠдёәд»Җд№ҲдәҡжҙІжңҹжқғзҡ„ Delta еҜ№еҶІжҜ”еёёи§„жңҹжқғжӣҙе®№жҳ“гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
26.11
In an Asian option, the payoff becomes more certain as time passes and the delta always
approaches zero as the maturity date is approached. This makes delta hedging easy. Barrier
options cause problems for delta hedgers when the asset price is close to the barrier because
delta is discontinuous.
problems.explanationEn:
26.11
In an Asian option, the payoff becomes more certain as time passes and the delta always
approaches zero as the maturity date is approached. This makes delta hedging easy. Barrier
options cause problems for delta hedgers when the asset price is close to the barrier because
delta is discontinuous.

Ignore storage costs.

е…¶дёӯ

\[
\begin{aligned}
U_0 &= 1520, \\
V_0 &= 1600, \\
q_1 &= 0, \\
q_2 &= 0, \\
T &= 1, \\
d_1 &= \frac{\ln\frac{V_0}{q V_0} + (q_1 - q_2 + \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2) T}{\sigma \sqrt{T}}, \\
d_2 &= d_1 - \sigma \sqrt{T}, \\
\sigma &= \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + q^2 \sigma_2^2 - 2 \rho q \sigma_1 \sigma_2},
\end{aligned}
\]

иҝҷйҮҢпјҢ

\[
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= 0.2, \\
\sigma_2 &= 0.2, \\
q &= 100, \\
\rho &= 0.7.
\end{aligned}
\]

и®Ўз®—еҫ—пјҡ

\[
\sigma = \sqrt{(0.2)^2 + 100^2 (0.2)^2 - 2 \times 0.7 \times 100 \times 0.2 \times 0.2} = 0.1549.
\]

жҺҘзқҖпјҢи®Ўз®—пјҡ

\[
d_1 = -0.2536, \quad d_2 = d_1 - \sigma \sqrt{T} = -0.4086.
\]

еӣ жӯӨпјҢжңҹжқғд»·ж јдёәпјҡ

\[
V = 1600 \times N(-0.4086) - 1520 \times N(-0.2536) = 54.61
\]

problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 26.13. иө„дә§зҡ„ж¬§ејҸи·Ңд»·жңҹжқғдёҺдёҺиҜҘжңҹжқғеҗҢж—¶еҲ°жңҹзҡ„жңҹиҙ§еҗҲзәҰзҡ„иө„дә§жңҹиҙ§д»·ж јзҡ„ж¬§ејҸи·Ңд»·жңҹжқғд»·еҖјзӣёеҗҢеҗ—пјҹ

problems.explanation:
26.13
No. If the futureвҖҷs price is above the spot price during the life of the option, it is possible that
the spot price will hit the barrier when the futures price does not.

е…¶дёӯпјҢcc жҳҜж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„зңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғд»·ж јпјҢpc жҳҜж¬§ејҸзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғзҡ„зңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғд»·ж јпјҢc жҳҜжңҹжқғеҲ°жңҹж—¶еҸҜиЎҢдҪҝзҡ„еҶ…еөҢзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғеҪ“еүҚд»·ж јпјҢK_1 жҳҜ cc е’Ң pc зҡ„жү§иЎҢд»·ж јгҖӮиҜҒжҳҺзұ»дјјдәҺз¬¬11з« дёӯйҖҡеёёзҡ„зңӢи·ҢвҖ“зңӢж¶Ёе№ід»·е…ізі»гҖӮж–№зЁӢдёӨиҫ№йғҪд»ЈиЎЁеңЁж—¶й—ҙ T_1 ж—¶д»·еҖјдёә

\max(c - K_1, 0)

зҡ„жҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲд»·еҖјгҖӮ

еҲ©з”Ёе…¬ејҸ

\nabla(\rho; a, b) = N(a) - N(b),

еҸҠ

N(x) + N(-x) = 1,

жҲ‘д»¬еҫ—еҲ°

\begin{aligned}
& e^{-q T_1} S N(b_2) - K e^{-r T_1} N(b_1) - \left(e^{-q T_1} S N(b_2 - \sigma \sqrt{T_1}) - K e^{-r T_1} N(b_1 - \sigma \sqrt{T_1})\right) \\
= & e^{-q T_1} S N(b_2) - K e^{-r T_1} N(b_1) - c
\end{aligned}

з”ұдәҺ

c = e^{-q T_1} S N(d_1) - K e^{-r T_1} N(d_2),

еӣ жӯӨзңӢи·ҢвҖ“зңӢж¶Ёе№ід»·дёҺж–Үдёӯе…¬ејҸдёҖиҮҙгҖӮ

(b) зңӢи·ҢвҖ“зңӢж¶Ёе…ізі»жҳҜ

cp - pp = p - K e^{-r T_1}

е…¶дёӯ cp жҳҜж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„зңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғд»·ж јпјҢpp жҳҜж¬§ејҸзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғзҡ„зңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғд»·ж јпјҢp жҳҜеҶ…еөҢзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғеҪ“еүҚд»·ж јпјҢK_1 жҳҜ cp е’Ң pp зҡ„жү§иЎҢд»·ж јгҖӮиҜҒжҳҺзұ»дјјдәҺз¬¬11з« дёӯйҖҡеёёзҡ„зңӢи·ҢвҖ“зңӢж¶Ёе№ід»·е…ізі»гҖӮж–№зЁӢдёӨиҫ№йғҪд»ЈиЎЁеңЁж—¶й—ҙ T_1 ж—¶д»·еҖјдёә

\max(p - K_1, 0)

зҡ„жҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲд»·еҖјгҖӮ

еҲ©з”Ёзӣёдјјзҡ„ж•°еӯҰе…ізі»

\nabla(\rho; a, b) = N(a) - N(b),

д»ҘеҸҠ

N(x) + N(-x) = 1,

жҺЁеҜјеҫ—еҲ°

\begin{aligned}
& e^{-q T_1} S N(b_2) - K e^{-r T_1} N(b_1) - \left( e^{-q T_1} S N(b_2 - \sigma \sqrt{T_1}) - K e^{-r T_1} N(b_1 - \sigma \sqrt{T_1}) \right) \\
= & cp - pp
\end{aligned}

иҖҢ

p = K e^{-r T_1} N(-d_2) - S e^{-q T_1} N(-d_1),

з”ұжӯӨиҜҒжҳҺзңӢи·ҢвҖ“зңӢж¶Ёе№ід»·дёҺж–Үдёӯе…¬ејҸдёҖиҮҙгҖӮ
