problems.promptEn: Practice 26.16. Does a down-and-out call become more valuable or less valuable as we increase the frequency with which we observe the asset price in determining whether the barrier has been crossed? What is the answer to the same question for a down-and-in call?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 26.16. еҪ“жҲ‘д»¬еўһеҠ и§ӮеҜҹиө„дә§д»·ж јд»ҘзЎ®е®ҡйҡңзўҚжҳҜеҗҰе·Іиў«и·Ёи¶Ҡзҡ„йў‘зҺҮж—¶пјҢдёӢи·ҢзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјжҳҜеҗҰдјҡеҸҳеҫ—жӣҙй«ҳжҲ–жӣҙдҪҺпјҹеҜ№дәҺеӨұиҙҘзҡ„е‘јеҸ«пјҢеҗҢдёҖй—®йўҳзҡ„зӯ”жЎҲжҳҜд»Җд№Ҳпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
26.16
As we increase the frequency with which the asset price is observed, the asset price becomes
more likely to hit the barrier and the value of a down-and-out call goes down. For a similar
reason the value of a down-and-in call goes up. The adjustment mentioned in the text,

suggested by Broadie, Glasserman, and Kou, moves the barrier further out as the asset price
is observed less frequently. This increases the price of a down-and-out option and reduces the
price of a down-and-in option.
problems.explanationEn:
26.16
As we increase the frequency with which the asset price is observed, the asset price becomes
more likely to hit the barrier and the value of a down-and-out call goes down. For a similar
reason the value of a down-and-in call goes up. The adjustment mentioned in the text,

suggested by Broadie, Glasserman, and Kou, moves the barrier further out as the asset price
is observed less frequently. This increases the price of a down-and-out option and reduces the
price of a down-and-in option.

problems.promptEn: Practice 26.17. Explain why a regular European call option is the sum of a down-and-out European call and a down-and-in European call. Is the same true for American call options? Exotic Options
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 26.17. и§ЈйҮҠдёәд»Җд№Ҳеёёи§„ж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғжҳҜи·Ңе…Ҙж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғе’Ңи·Ңе…Ҙж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„жҖ»е’ҢгҖӮзҫҺејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғд№ҹжҳҜеҰӮжӯӨеҗ—пјҹ ејӮеӣҪжғ…и°ғзҡ„йҖүжӢ©
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
26.17
If the barrier is reached, the down-and-out option is worth nothing while the down-and-in
option has the same value as a regular option. If the barrier is not reached, the down-and-in
option is worth nothing while the down-and-out option has the same value as a regular
option. This is why a down-and-out call option plus a down-and-in call option is worth the
same as a regular option. A similar argument cannot be used for American options.
problems.explanationEn:
26.17
If the barrier is reached, the down-and-out option is worth nothing while the down-and-in
option has the same value as a regular option. If the barrier is not reached, the down-and-in
option is worth nothing while the down-and-out option has the same value as a regular
option. This is why a down-and-out call option plus a down-and-in call option is worth the
same as a regular option. A similar argument cannot be used for American options.

problems.promptEn: Practice 26.22. Use DerivaGem to calculate the value of: (a) A regular European call option on a non-dividend-paying stock where the stock price is USD 50, the strike price is USD 50, the risk-free rate is 5% per annum, the volatility is 30%, and the time to maturity is one year (b) A down-and-out European call which is as in (a) with the barrier at USD (c) A down-and-in European call which is as in (a) with the barrier at USD 45. Show that the option in (a) is worth the sum of the values of the options in (b) and (c).

problems.promptEn: Practice 26.26. Consider an up-and-out barrier call option on a non-dividend-paying stock when the stock price is 50, the strike price is 50, the volatility is 30%, the risk-free rate is 5%, the time to maturity is 1 year, and the barrier at USD 80. Use the DerivaGem software to value the option and graph the relationship between (a) the option price and the stock price, (b) the delta and the stock price, (c) the option price and the time to maturity, and (d) the option price and the volatility. Provide an intuitive explanation for the results you get. Show that the delta, gamma, theta, and vega for an up-and-out barrier call option can be either positive or negative.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 26.26. иҖғиҷ‘дёҖдёӘдёҚж”Ҝд»ҳиӮЎжҒҜзҡ„иӮЎзҘЁзҡ„еҗ‘дёҠе’ҢйҖҖеҮәйҡңзўҚзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғпјҢеҪ“иӮЎд»·дёә 50гҖҒжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә 50гҖҒжіўеҠЁжҖ§дёә 30%гҖҒж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёә 5%гҖҒеҲ°жңҹж—¶й—ҙдёә 1 е№ҙгҖҒйҡңзўҚдёә 80 зҫҺе…ғж—¶гҖӮдҪҝз”Ё DerivaGem иҪҜд»¶еҜ№жңҹжқғиҝӣиЎҢдј°еҖјпјҢе№¶з»ҳеҲ¶ (a) жңҹжқғд»·ж је’ҢиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јгҖҒ(b) Delta е’ҢиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јгҖҒ(c) жңҹжқғд»·ж је’ҢеҲ°жңҹж—¶й—ҙд»ҘеҸҠ (d) жңҹжқғд»·ж је’ҢжіўеҠЁзҺҮд№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„е…ізі»еӣҫгҖӮдёәжӮЁеҫ—еҲ°зҡ„з»“жһңжҸҗдҫӣзӣҙи§Ӯзҡ„и§ЈйҮҠгҖӮиҜҒжҳҺеҗ‘дёҠе’Ңеҗ‘еӨ–йҡңзўҚзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„ deltaгҖҒgammaгҖҒtheta е’Ң vega еҸҜд»ҘжҳҜжӯЈеҖјпјҢд№ҹеҸҜд»ҘжҳҜиҙҹеҖјгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
26.26
The price of the option is 3.528.
(a) The option price is a humped function of the stock price with the maximum option
price occurring for a stock price of about USD 57. If you could choose the stock price
there would be a trade off. High stock prices give a high probability that the option
will be knocked out. Low stock prices give a low potential payoff. For a stock price
less than USD 57, delta is positive (as it is for a regular call option); for a stock price
greater than USD 57, delta is negative.
(b) Delta increases up to a stock price of about 45 and then decreases. This shows that
gamma can be positive or negative.
(c) The option price is a humped function of the time to maturity with the maximum
option price occurring for a time to maturity of 0.5 years. This is because too long a
time to maturity means that the option has a high probability of being knocked out;
too short a time to maturity means that the option has a low potential payoff. For a
time to maturity less than 0.5 years, theta is negative (as it is for a regular call option);
for a time to maturity greater than 0.5 years, the theta of the option is positive.
(d) The option price is also a humped function of volatility with the maximum option
price being obtained for a volatility of about 20%. This is because too high a volatility
means that the option has a high probability of being knocked out; too low volatility
means that the option has a low potential payoff. For volatilities less than 20%, vega
is positive (as it is for a regular option); for volatilities above 20% vega is negative.

(c) жңҹжқғд»·ж јжҳҜеҲ°жңҹж—¶й—ҙзҡ„еҮ№еі°еҮҪж•°пјҢжңҖеӨ§жңҹжқғд»·ж јеҸ‘з”ҹеңЁеҲ°жңҹж—¶й—ҙдёә 0.5 е№ҙж—¶гҖӮиҝҷжҳҜеӣ дёәж—¶й—ҙиҝҮй•ҝж„Ҹе‘ізқҖжңҹжқғиў«ж•ІеҮәзҡ„жҰӮзҺҮй«ҳпјӣж—¶й—ҙиҝҮзҹӯж„Ҹе‘ізқҖжңҹжқғжҪңеңЁзҡ„ж”¶зӣҠдҪҺгҖӮеҪ“еҲ°жңҹж—¶й—ҙе°‘дәҺ 0.5 е№ҙж—¶пјҢTheta дёәиҙҹпјҲдёҺжҷ®йҖҡзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғзӣёеҗҢпјүпјӣеҪ“еҲ°жңҹж—¶й—ҙи¶…иҝҮ 0.5 е№ҙж—¶пјҢжңҹжқғзҡ„ Theta дёәжӯЈгҖӮ
(d) жңҹжқғд»·ж јд№ҹжҳҜжіўеҠЁзҺҮзҡ„еҮ№еі°еҮҪж•°пјҢжңҖеӨ§жңҹжқғд»·ж јеӨ§зәҰеңЁ 20% жіўеҠЁзҺҮж—¶гҖӮиҝҷжҳҜеӣ дёәжіўеҠЁзҺҮиҝҮй«ҳж„Ҹе‘ізқҖжңҹжқғиў«ж•ІеҮәзҡ„жҰӮзҺҮй«ҳпјӣжіўеҠЁзҺҮиҝҮдҪҺж„Ҹе‘ізқҖжҪңеңЁж”¶зӣҠдҪҺгҖӮеҪ“жіўеҠЁзҺҮе°ҸдәҺ 20% ж—¶пјҢVega дёәжӯЈпјҲдёҺжҷ®йҖҡжңҹжқғзӣёеҗҢпјүпјӣеҪ“жіўеҠЁзҺҮи¶…иҝҮ 20% ж—¶пјҢVega дёәиҙҹгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 26.27. Sample Application F in the DerivaGem Application Builder Software considers the static options replication example in Section 26.17. It shows the way a hedge can be constructed using four options (as in Section 26.17) and two ways a hedge can be constructed using 16 options. (a) Explain the difference between the two ways a hedge can be constructed using 16 options. Explain intuitively why the second method works better. (b) Improve on the four-option hedge by changing Tmat for the third and fourth options. (c) Check how well the 16-option portfolios match the delta, gamma, and vega of the barrier option.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 26.27. DerivaGem Application Builder иҪҜд»¶дёӯзҡ„ Sample Application F иҖғеҜҹз¬¬ 26.17 иҠӮзҡ„йқҷжҖҒжңҹжқғеӨҚеҲ¶зӨәдҫӢгҖӮе®ғеұ•зӨәдәҶеҰӮдҪ•з”Ё 4 дёӘжңҹжқғжһ„йҖ еҜ№еҶІпјҲеҰӮз¬¬ 26.17 иҠӮпјүпјҢд»ҘеҸҠеҰӮдҪ•з”ЁдёӨз§Қж–№жі•йҖҡиҝҮ 16 дёӘжңҹжқғжһ„йҖ еҜ№еҶІгҖӮ(a) и§ЈйҮҠдёӨз§Қ 16 жңҹжқғеҜ№еҶІж–№жі•зҡ„е·®ејӮпјҢе№¶зӣҙи§ӮиҜҙжҳҺдёәд»Җд№Ҳз¬¬дәҢз§Қж–№жі•ж•ҲжһңжӣҙеҘҪгҖӮ(b) йҖҡиҝҮж”№еҸҳз¬¬ 3 дёӘе’Ңз¬¬ 4 дёӘжңҹжқғзҡ„ Tmat ж”№иҝӣ 4 жңҹжқғеҜ№еҶІгҖӮ(c) жЈҖжҹҘ 16 жңҹжқғз»„еҗҲдёҺйҡңзўҚжңҹжқғзҡ„ deltaгҖҒgamma е’Ң vega еҢ№й…ҚзЁӢеәҰгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
26.27
(a) Both approaches use a one call option with a strike price of 50 and a maturity of 0.75.
In the first approach, the other 15 call options have strike prices of 60 and equally
spaced times to maturity. In the second approach, the other 15 call options have strike

prices of 60, but the spacing between the times to maturity decreases as the maturity
of the barrier option is approached. The second approach to setting times to maturity
produces a better hedge. This is because the chance of the barrier being hit at time t is
an increasing function of t . As t increases, it therefore becomes more important to
replicate the barrier at time t .
(b) By using either trial and error or the Solver tool, we see that we come closest to
matching the price of the barrier option when the maturities of the third and fourth
options are changed from 0.25 and 0.5 to 0.39 and 0.65.
(c) To calculate delta for the two 16-option hedge strategies, it is necessary to change the
last argument of EPortfolio from 0 to 1 in cells L42 and X42. To calculate delta for
the barrier option, it is necessary to change the last argument of BarrierOption in cell
F12 from 0 to 1. To calculate gamma and vega, the arguments must be changed to 2
and 3, respectively. The delta, gamma, and vega of the barrier option are вҖ“0.0221, вҖ“
0.0035, and вҖ“0.0254. The delta, gamma, and vega of the first 16-option portfolio are вҖ“
0.0262, вҖ“0.0045, and вҖ“0.1470. The delta, gamma, and vega of the second 16-option
portfolio are вҖ“0.0199, вҖ“0.0037, and вҖ“0.1449. The second of the two 16-option
portfolios provides Greek letters that are closest to the Greek letters of the barrier
option. Interestingly, neither of the two portfolios does particularly well on vega.

problems.promptEn: Practice 26.28. Consider a down-and-out call option on a foreign currency. The initial exchange rate is 0.90, the time to maturity is 2 years, the strike price is 1.00, the barrier is 0.80, the domestic risk-free interest rate is 5%, the foreign risk-free interest rate is 6%, and the volatility is 25% per annum. Use DerivaGem to develop a static option replication strategy involving five options.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 26.28. иҖғиҷ‘еӨ–еёҒзҡ„дёӢи·ҢзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғгҖӮеҲқе§ӢжұҮзҺҮдёә0.90пјҢеҲ°жңҹж—¶й—ҙдёә2е№ҙпјҢжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә1.00пјҢйҡңзўҚдёә0.80пјҢеӣҪеҶ…ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёә5%пјҢеӣҪеӨ–ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёә6%пјҢе№ҙжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә25%гҖӮдҪҝз”Ё DerivaGem ејҖеҸ‘ж¶үеҸҠдә”дёӘжңҹжқғзҡ„йқҷжҖҒжңҹжқғеӨҚеҲ¶зӯ–з•ҘгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 26.29. Suppose that a stock index is currently 900. The dividend yield is 2%, the risk-free rate is 5%, and the volatility is 40%. Use the results in Technical Note 27 on the authorвҖҷs website to calculate the value of a 1-year average price call where the strike price is 900 and the index level is observed at the end of each quarter for the purposes of the averaging. Compare this with the price calculated by DerivaGem for a 1-year average price option where the price is observed continuously. Provide an intuitive explanation for any differences between the prices.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 26.29. еҒҮи®ҫжҹҗиӮЎжҢҮзӣ®еүҚдёә900пјҢиӮЎжҒҜж”¶зӣҠзҺҮдёә2%пјҢж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёә5%пјҢжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә40%гҖӮдҪҝз”ЁдҪңиҖ…зҪ‘з«ҷдёҠжҠҖжңҜиҜҙжҳҺ 27 дёӯзҡ„з»“жһңжқҘи®Ўз®— 1 е№ҙжңҹе№іеқҮзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјпјҢе…¶дёӯжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә 900пјҢе№¶дё”дёәдәҶжұӮе№іеқҮиҖҢеңЁжҜҸдёӘеӯЈеәҰжң«и§ӮеҜҹжҢҮж•°ж°ҙе№ігҖӮе°ҶжӯӨдёҺ DerivaGem и®Ўз®—зҡ„ 1 е№ҙе№іеқҮд»·ж јжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·ж јиҝӣиЎҢжҜ”иҫғпјҢе…¶дёӯд»·ж јжҳҜиҝһз»ӯи§ӮеҜҹзҡ„гҖӮеҜ№д»·ж јд№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„д»»дҪ•е·®ејӮжҸҗдҫӣзӣҙи§Ӯзҡ„и§ЈйҮҠгҖӮ

DerivaGem gives the price as 86.77 (set option type =Asian). The higher price for the first
option arises because the average is calculated from prices at times 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, and 1.00.
The mean of these times is 0.625. By contrast, the corresponding mean when the price is
observed continuously is 0.50. The later a price is observed the more uncertain it is and the
more it contributes to the value of the option.

problems.promptEn: Practice 26.30. Use the DerivaGem Application Builder software to compare the effectiveness of daily delta hedging for (a) the option considered in Tables 19.2 and 19.3 and (b) an average price call with the same parameters. Use Sample Application C. For the average price option you will find it necessary to change the calculation of the option price in cell C16, the payoffs in cells H15 and H16, and the deltas (cells G46 to G186 and N46 to N186). Carry out 20 Monte Carlo simulation runs for each option by repeatedly pressing F9. On each run record the cost of writing and hedging the option, the volume of trading over the whole 20 weeks and the volume of trading between weeks 11 and 20. Comment on the results.

problems.explanation:
26.30
For the regular option, the theoretical price is about USD 240,000. For the average price option,
the theoretical price is about 115,000. My 20 simulation runs (40 outcomes because of the
antithetic calculations) gave results as shown in the following table.

Regular Call
Ave Price Call
Ave Hedging Cost
247,628
114,837
SD Hedging Cost
17,833
12,123
Ave Trading Vol (20 wks)
412,440
291,237
Ave Trading Vol (last 10 wks)
187,074
51,658

These results show that the standard deviation of using delta hedging for an average price
option is lower than that for a regular option. However, using the criterion in Chapter 19
(standard deviation divided by value of option) hedge performance is better for the regular
option. Hedging the average price option requires less trading, particularly in the last 10
weeks. This is because we become progressively more certain about the average price as the
maturity of the option is approached.

problems.promptEn: Practice 26.31. In the DerivaGem Application Builder Software modify Sample Application D to test the effectiveness of delta and gamma hedging for a call on call compound option on a 100,000 units of a foreign currency where the exchange rate is 0.67, the domestic risk-free rate is 5%, the foreign risk-free rate is 6%, the volatility is 12%. The time to maturity of the first option is 20 weeks, and the strike price of the first option is 0.015. The second option matures 40 weeks from today and has a strike price of 0.68. Explain how you modified the cells. Comment on hedge effectiveness.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 26.31. еңЁ DerivaGem еә”з”ЁзЁӢеәҸз”ҹжҲҗеҷЁиҪҜд»¶дёӯдҝ®ж”№зӨәдҫӢеә”з”ЁзЁӢеәҸ DпјҢд»ҘжөӢиҜ• 100,000 еҚ•дҪҚеӨ–еёҒзҡ„зңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғеӨҚеҗҲжңҹжқғзҡ„ delta е’Ң gamma еҜ№еҶІжңүж•ҲжҖ§пјҢе…¶дёӯжұҮзҺҮдёә 0.67пјҢеӣҪеҶ…ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёә 5%пјҢеӣҪеӨ–ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёә 6%пјҢжіўеҠЁжҖ§дёә 12%гҖӮз¬¬дёҖдёӘжңҹжқғзҡ„еҲ°жңҹж—¶й—ҙдёә20е‘ЁпјҢз¬¬дёҖдёӘжңҹжқғзҡ„жү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә0.015гҖӮз¬¬дәҢдёӘжңҹжқғд»Һд»ҠеӨ©иө· 40 е‘ЁеҗҺеҲ°жңҹпјҢжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә 0.68гҖӮи§ЈйҮҠдёҖдёӢдҪ жҳҜеҰӮдҪ•дҝ®ж”№з»Ҷиғһзҡ„гҖӮиҜ„и®әеҜ№еҶІжңүж•ҲжҖ§гҖӮ

problems.explanation:
26.31
The value of the option is 1093. It is necessary to change cells F20 and F46 to 0.67. Cells
G20 to G39 and G46 to G65 must be changed to calculate delta of the compound option.
Cells H20 to H39 and H46 to H65 must be changed to calculate gamma of the compound
option. Cells I20 to I40 and I46 to I66 must be changed to calculate the BlackвҖ“Scholes price
of the call option expiring in 40 weeks. Similarly, cells J20 to J40 and J46 to J66 must be
changed to calculate the delta of this option; cells K20 to K40 and K46 to K66 must be
changed to calculate the gamma of the option. The payoffs in cells N9 and N10 must be
calculated as MAX(I40-0.015,0)*100,000 and MAX(I66-0.015,0)*100,000. Delta plus
gamma hedging works relatively poorly for the compound option. On 20 simulation runs the
cost of writing and hedging the option ranged from 200 to 2,500.

problems.promptEn: Practice 26.32. Outperformance certificates (also called вҖңsprint certificates,вҖқ вҖңaccelerator certificates,вҖқ or вҖңspeedersвҖқ) are offered to investors by many European banks as a way of investing in a companyвҖҷs stock. The initial investment equals the stock price, S0. If the stock price goes up between time 0 and time T, the investor gains k times the increase at time T, where k is a constant greater than 1.0. However, the stock price used to calculate the gain at time T is capped at some maximum level M. If the stock price goes down, the investorвҖҷs loss is equal to the decrease. The investor does not receive dividends. (a) Show that an outperformance certificate is a package. (b) Calculate using DerivaGem the value of a one-year outperformance certificate when the stock price is 50 euros, k = 1.5, M = 70 euros, the risk-free rate is 5%, and the stock price volatility is 25%. Dividends equal to 0.5 euros are expected in 2 months, 5 months, 8 months, and 11 months. Exotic Options

price equal to M.

price equal to the current stock price.

problems.promptEn: Practice 26.35. Produce a formula for valuing a cliquet option where an amount Q is invested to produce a payoff at the end of n periods. The return earned each period is the greater of the return on an index, excluding dividends, and zero.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 26.35. жҺЁеҜјдёҖдёӘ cliquet жңҹжқғдј°еҖје…¬ејҸпјҡжҠ•иө„йҮ‘йўқ QпјҢеңЁ n дёӘжңҹй—ҙз»“жқҹеҗҺдә§з”ҹж”¶зӣҠгҖӮжҜҸдёӘжңҹй—ҙиҺ·еҫ—зҡ„ж”¶зӣҠзҺҮзӯүдәҺжҢҮж•°ж”¶зӣҠзҺҮпјҲдёҚеҗ«иӮЎжҒҜпјүдёҺ 0 дёӯзҡ„иҫғеӨ§иҖ…гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
26.35

Let each period have length tau. Let r be the risk-free rate, q the dividend yield on the index, sigma the volatility, and R_i the index return excluding dividends in period i. The payoff multiplier in period i is max(1+R_i,1), so the value is Q exp(-r n tau) E_hat[ product_{i=1}^n max(1+R_i,1) ].

Assuming the successive index returns are independent under the risk-neutral measure, this becomes Q exp(-r n tau) [ E_hat max(S_i/S_{i-1},1) ]^n.

Now max(S_i/S_{i-1},1) = 1 + max(S_i/S_{i-1}-1,0). The expectation of max(S_i-S_{i-1},0) over one period is obtained from the Black-Scholes-Merton call formula with strike S_{i-1}. Therefore E_hat max(S_i/S_{i-1},1) = 1 + exp((r-q)tau) N(d_1) - N(d_2), where d_1 = [(r-q+0.5 sigma^2)tau] / [sigma sqrt(tau)] and d_2=d_1-sigma sqrt(tau).

Thus the cliquet value is Q exp(-r n tau) [1 + exp((r-q)tau) N(d_1) - N(d_2)]^n.
problems.explanationEn:
26.35

Let each period have length tau. Let r be the risk-free rate, q the dividend yield on the index, sigma the volatility, and R_i the index return excluding dividends in period i. The payoff multiplier in period i is max(1+R_i,1), so the value is Q exp(-r n tau) E_hat[ product_{i=1}^n max(1+R_i,1) ].

Assuming the successive index returns are independent under the risk-neutral measure, this becomes Q exp(-r n tau) [ E_hat max(S_i/S_{i-1},1) ]^n.

Now max(S_i/S_{i-1},1) = 1 + max(S_i/S_{i-1}-1,0). The expectation of max(S_i-S_{i-1},0) over one period is obtained from the Black-Scholes-Merton call formula with strike S_{i-1}. Therefore E_hat max(S_i/S_{i-1},1) = 1 + exp((r-q)tau) N(d_1) - N(d_2), where d_1 = [(r-q+0.5 sigma^2)tau] / [sigma sqrt(tau)] and d_2=d_1-sigma sqrt(tau).

Thus the cliquet value is Q exp(-r n tau) [1 + exp((r-q)tau) N(d_1) - N(d_2)]^n.

26.35

и®ҫжҜҸдёӘжңҹй—ҙй•ҝеәҰдёә tauпјҢж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёә rпјҢжҢҮж•°иӮЎжҒҜж”¶зӣҠзҺҮдёә qпјҢжҢҮж•°жіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә sigmaпјҢR_i дёәз¬¬ i дёӘжңҹй—ҙдёҚеҗ«иӮЎжҒҜзҡ„жҢҮж•°ж”¶зӣҠзҺҮгҖӮз¬¬ i жңҹзҡ„ж”¶зӣҠд№ҳж•°дёә max(1+R_i,1)пјҢеӣ жӯӨжңҹжқғд»·еҖјдёә Q exp(-r n tau) E_hat[ product_{i=1}^n max(1+R_i,1) ]гҖӮ

иӢҘеңЁйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§жөӢеәҰдёӢеҗ„жңҹй—ҙжҢҮж•°ж”¶зӣҠзӣёдә’зӢ¬з«ӢпјҢеҲҷд»·еҖјеҸҜеҶҷдёә Q exp(-r n tau) [ E_hat max(S_i/S_{i-1},1) ]^nгҖӮ

жүҖд»Ҙ cliquet жңҹжқғд»·еҖјдёә Q exp(-r n tau) [1 + exp((r-q)tau) N(d_1) - N(d_2)]^nгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 27.4. Suppose that the volatility of an asset will be 20% from month 0 to month 6, 22% from month 6 to month 12, and 24% from month 12 to month 24. What volatility should be used in BlackвҖ“ScholesвҖ“Merton to value a 2-year option?

problems.promptEn: Practice 27.5. Consider the case of MertonвҖҷs jumpвҖ“diffusion model where jumps always reduce the asset price to zero. Assume that the average number of jumps per year is l. Show that the price of a European call option is the same as in a world with no jumps except that the risk-free rate is r + l rather than r. Does the possibility of jumps increase or reduce the value of the call option in this case? (Hint: Value the option assuming no jumps and assuming one or more jumps. The probability of no jumps in time T is e -lT).

problems.explanationZh:
27.5
еңЁйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§дё–з•ҢдёӯпјҢеҒҮи®ҫи·іи·ғдёҚи®ЎпјҢиө„дә§д»·ж јиҝҮзЁӢдёә

\[
dS = S\left[(r - q - \lambda k) dt + \sigma dz\right]
\]

еңЁиҝҷйҮҢпјҢ\(k = -1\)пјҢеҚіи·іи·ғдјҡе°Ҷиө„дә§д»·ж јйҷҚиҮійӣ¶пјҢеҲҷиҝҮзЁӢжҲҗдёә

\[
dS = S\left[(r - q + \lambda) dt + \sigma dz\right]
\]

иҜҘиө„дә§иЎЁзҺ°еҫ—еғҸжҳҜдёҖеҸӘж”Ҝд»ҳиӮЎжҒҜж”¶зӣҠзҺҮдёә \(q - \lambda\) зҡ„иӮЎзҘЁгҖӮиҝҷж„Ҹе‘ізқҖпјҢжқЎд»¶жҳҜжІЎжңүи·іи·ғж—¶пјҢж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·ж јдёә

\[
C = e^{-qT} S_0 N(d_1) - K e^{-(r + \lambda) T} N(d_2)
\]

е…¶дёӯ

\[
d_1 = \frac{\ln \left( \frac{S_0}{K} \right) + (r - q + \lambda + \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2) T}{\sigma \sqrt{T}}, \quad d_2 = d_1 - \sigma \sqrt{T}
\]

жІЎжңүи·іи·ғзҡ„жҰӮзҺҮдёә \(e^{-\lambda T}\)пјҢжңүдёҖж¬ЎжҲ–еӨҡж¬Ўи·іи·ғж—¶пјҢиө„дә§д»·ж јдёәйӣ¶пјҢжңҹжқғд»·еҖјд№ҹдёәйӣ¶гҖӮеӣ жӯӨпјҢжңҹжқғзҡ„жңҹжңӣд»·еҖјдёә

\[
C = e^{-\lambda T} \left( e^{-qT} S_0 N(d_1) - K e^{-rT} N(d_2) \right) = e^{-qT} S_0 N(d_1) - K e^{-(r + \lambda) T} N(d_2)
\]

иҝҷиҜҙжҳҺж¬§ејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјдёҺж— и·іи·ғжғ…еҶөдёӢзӣёеҗҢпјҢеҸӘжҳҜж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮеҸҳдёә \(r + \lambda\)гҖӮ

и·іи·ғжң¬иә«дёҚеёҰзі»з»ҹжҖ§йЈҺйҷ©пјҢеӣ жӯӨж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮзҡ„жҸҗеҚҮеҜјиҮҙжңҹжқғд»·еҖјеўһеҠ гҖӮж•…и·іи·ғзҡ„еҸҜиғҪжҖ§еңЁиҝҷйҮҢжҸҗеҚҮдәҶзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 27.6. At time 0 the price of a non-dividend-paying stock is S0. Suppose that the time interval between 0 and T is divided into two subintervals of length t1 and t2. During the first subinterval, the risk-free interest rate and volatility are r1 and s1, respectively. During the second subinterval, they are r2 and s2, respectively. Assume that the world is risk neutral. (a) Use the results in Chapter 15 to determine the stock price distribution at time T in terms of r1, r2, s1, s2, t1, t2, and S0. (b) Suppose that r is the average interest rate between time zero and T and that V is the average variance rate between times zero and T. What is the stock price distribution as a function of T in terms of r, V, T, and S0? (c) What are the results corresponding to (a) and (b) when there are three subintervals with different interest rates and volatilities? (d) Show that if the risk-free rate, r, and the volatility, s, are known functions of time, the stock price distribution at time T in a risk-neutral world is ln ST вҲјf3ln S0 + 1r - 1 2V2T, VT4 where r is the average value of r, V is equal to the average value of s2, and S0 is the stock price today and f1m, v2 is a normal distribution with mean m and variance v.

problems.explanationZh:
27.6
и®ҫ \(S_1\) жҳҜеңЁж—¶й—ҙ \(t_1\) зҡ„иӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јпјҢ\(S_T\) жҳҜеңЁж—¶й—ҙ \(T\) зҡ„иӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јгҖӮж №жҚ®з¬¬15з« ж–№зЁӢ(15.3)пјҢйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§дё–з•ҢдёӢжңүпјҡ

\[
\ln S_1 \sim \mathcal{N} \left( \ln S_0 + \left(r_1 - \frac{1}{2} \sigma_1^2 \right) t_1, \sigma_1^2 t_1 \right)
\]

\[
\ln S_T \sim \mathcal{N} \left( \ln S_1 + \left(r_2 - \frac{1}{2} \sigma_2^2 \right) t_2, \sigma_2^2 t_2 \right)
\]

дёӨдёӘзӢ¬з«ӢжӯЈжҖҒеҲҶеёғзҡ„е’Ңд»ҚдёәжӯЈжҖҒеҲҶеёғпјҢеқҮеҖјдёәдёӨеқҮеҖјд№Ӣе’ҢпјҢж–№е·®дёәдёӨж–№е·®д№Ӣе’ҢгҖӮжүҖд»ҘпјҢ

\[
\ln S_T \sim \mathcal{N} \left( \ln S_0 + \left(r_1 t_1 + r_2 t_2\right) - \frac{1}{2} \left( \sigma_1^2 t_1 + \sigma_2^2 t_2 \right), \sigma_1^2 t_1 + \sigma_2^2 t_2 \right)
\]

(a) еӣ жӯӨпјҢиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јеңЁж—¶й—ҙ\(T\)зҡ„еҲҶеёғдёә

\[
\ln S_T \sim \mathcal{N} \left( \ln S_0 + \left(r_1 t_1 + r_2 t_2 \right) - \frac{1}{2} \left( \sigma_1^2 t_1 + \sigma_2^2 t_2 \right), \sigma_1^2 t_1 + \sigma_2^2 t_2 \right)
\]

(b) е®ҡд№үе№іеқҮеҲ©зҺҮ

\[
r = \frac{r_1 t_1 + r_2 t_2}{T},
\]

е’Ңе№іеқҮж–№е·®зҺҮ

\[
V = \frac{\sigma_1^2 t_1 + \sigma_2^2 t_2}{T},
\]

еҲҷиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јзҡ„еҲҶеёғдёә

\[
\ln S_T \sim \mathcal{N} \left( \ln S_0 + (r - \frac{1}{2} V) T, V T \right)
\]

(c) еҪ“ж—¶й—ҙеҢәй—ҙеҲҶжҲҗдёүдёӘеӯҗеҢәй—ҙй•ҝеәҰдёә \(t_1, t_2, t_3\)пјҢеҲҶеҲ«еҜ№еә” \(r_1, r_2, r_3\) е’Ң \(\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3\)пјҢзұ»дјјжҺЁеҜјеҸҜеҫ—

\[
\ln S_T \sim \mathcal{N} \left( \ln S_0 + \sum_{i=1}^3 r_i t_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^3 \sigma_i^2 t_i, \sum_{i=1}^3 \sigma_i^2 t_i \right)
\]

еҗҢж ·пјҢз”Ёе№іеқҮеҲ©зҺҮе’Ңе№іеқҮж–№е·®зҺҮе®ҡд№ү

\[
r = \frac{\sum r_i t_i}{T}, \quad V = \frac{\sum \sigma_i^2 t_i}{T}
\]

еҚіеҸҜеҫ—еҲ°зұ»дјј(b)дёӯзҡ„иЎЁиҫҫгҖӮ

(d) еҪ“ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮ \(r\) е’ҢжіўеҠЁзҺҮ \(\sigma\) жҳҜж—¶й—ҙзҡ„е·ІзҹҘеҮҪж•°пјҢеҲҷйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§дё–з•Ңдёӯж—¶й—ҙ \(T\) зҡ„иӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јеҜ№ж•°жңҚд»ҺжӯЈжҖҒеҲҶеёғпјҡ

\[
\ln S_T \sim \mathcal{N} \left( \ln S_0 + \int_0^T r(t) dt - \frac{1}{2} \int_0^T \sigma(t)^2 dt, \int_0^T \sigma(t)^2 dt \right)
\]

е®ҡд№ү

\[
r = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T r(t) dt, \quad V = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \sigma(t)^2 dt
\]

еҲҷ

\[
\ln S_T \sim \mathcal{N} \left( \ln S_0 + (r - \frac{1}{2} V) T, V T \right)
\]

иҝҷиЎЁжҳҺпјҢеңЁйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§жқЎд»¶дёӢпјҢе…·еӨҮж—¶й—ҙеҸҳеҠЁеҲ©зҺҮе’ҢжіўеҠЁзҺҮзҡ„иө„дә§д»·ж јиЎЁзҺ°дёҺдҪҝз”Ёе№іеқҮеҲ©зҺҮе’Ңе№іеқҮж–№е·®зҺҮзҡ„еёёж•°еҲ©зҺҮжіўеҠЁзҺҮиө„дә§д»·ж јз»ҹи®Ўзү№еҫҒзӣёеҗҢгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 27.8. вҖңThe IVF model does not necessarily get the evolution of the volatility surface correct.вҖқ Explain this statement.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 27.8. вҖңIVF жЁЎеһӢдёҚдёҖе®ҡиғҪеӨҹжӯЈзЎ®ең°еҫ—еҮәжіўеҠЁзҺҮиЎЁйқўзҡ„жј”еҸҳгҖӮвҖқи§ЈйҮҠдёҖдёӢиҝҷдёӘиҜҙжі•гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
27.8
The IVF model is designed to match the volatility surface today. There is no guarantee that the
volatility surface given by the model at future times will reflect the true evolution of the
volatility surface.
problems.explanationEn:
27.8
The IVF model is designed to match the volatility surface today. There is no guarantee that the
volatility surface given by the model at future times will reflect the true evolution of the
volatility surface.

problems.promptEn: Practice 27.9. вҖңThe IVF model correctly values any derivative whose payoff depends on the value of the underlying asset at only one time.вҖқ Explain why.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 27.9. вҖңIVF жЁЎеһӢеҸҜд»ҘжӯЈзЎ®иҜ„дј°д»»дҪ•иЎҚз”ҹе“ҒпјҢе…¶ж”¶зӣҠд»…дёҖж¬ЎеҸ–еҶідәҺж Үзҡ„иө„дә§зҡ„д»·еҖјгҖӮвҖқи§ЈйҮҠдёҖдёӢдёәд»Җд№ҲгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
27.9
The IVF model ensures that the risk-neutral probability distribution of the asset price at any
future time conditional on its value today is correct (or at least consistent with the market prices
of options). When a derivativeвҖҷs payoff depends on the value of the asset at only one time the
IVF model therefore calculates the expected payoff from the asset correctly.
problems.explanationEn:
27.9
The IVF model ensures that the risk-neutral probability distribution of the asset price at any
future time conditional on its value today is correct (or at least consistent with the market prices
of options). When a derivativeвҖҷs payoff depends on the value of the asset at only one time the
IVF model therefore calculates the expected payoff from the asset correctly.

27.10
еңЁжң¬йўҳдёӯпјҢ
\(S_0 = 1.6,\)
\(r = 0.05,\)
\(r_f = 0.08,\)
\(\sigma = 0.15,\)
\(T = 1.5,\)
\(\Delta t = 0.5.\)
иҝҷж„Ҹе‘ізқҖ
\[
u = e^{\sigma \sqrt{\Delta t}} = e^{0.15 \times \sqrt{0.5}} = 1.1119,\]
\[
d = \frac{1}{\nu} = 0.8994,\]
\[
a = e^{(r - r_f) \Delta t} = e^{(0.05 - 0.08) \times 0.5} = 0.9851,\]
\[
p_u = \frac{a - d}{u - d} = \frac{0.9851 - 0.8994}{1.1119 - 0.8994} = 0.4033,\]
\[
p_d = 1 - p_u = 0.5967.\]

жңҹжқғзҡ„ж”Ҝд»ҳдёә
\[S_T - \min_{0 \leq t \leq T} S_t.\]

problems.promptEn: Practice 27.16. Consider a European put option on a non-dividend paying stock when the stock price is USD 100, the strike price is USD 110, the risk-free rate is 5% per annum, and the time to maturity is one year. Suppose that the average variance rate during the life of an option has a 0.20 probability of being 0.06, a 0.5 probability of being 0.09, and a 0.3 probability of being 0.12. The volatility is uncorrelated with the stock price. Estimate the value of the option. Use DerivaGem.

еҰӮжһңе№іеқҮж–№е·®зҺҮдёә 0.06пјҢеҲҷжңҹжқғд»·еҖјз”ұжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә \(\sqrt{0.06} = 24.495\%\) зҡ„ BlackвҖ“Scholes е…¬ејҸз»ҷеҮәпјӣе…¶д»·еҖјдёә 12.460гҖӮеҰӮжһңе№іеқҮж–№е·®зҺҮдёә 0.09пјҢеҲҷжңҹжқғд»·еҖјз”ұжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә \(\sqrt{0.09} = 30.000\%\) зҡ„ BlackвҖ“Scholes е…¬ејҸз»ҷеҮәпјӣе…¶д»·еҖјдёә 14.655гҖӮеҰӮжһңе№іеқҮж–№е·®зҺҮдёә 0.12пјҢеҲҷжңҹжқғд»·еҖјз”ұжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә \(\sqrt{0.12} = 34.641\%\) зҡ„ BlackвҖ“ScholesвҖ“Merton е…¬ејҸз»ҷеҮәпјӣе…¶д»·еҖјдёә 16.506гҖӮжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјжҳҜ BlackвҖ“ScholesвҖ“Merton д»·ж јд»Ҙе№іеқҮж–№е·®зҺҮзҡ„жҰӮзҺҮеҲҶеёғеҠ жқғзҡ„з§ҜеҲҶпјҢи®Ўз®—еҰӮдёӢпјҡ

&= 14.77.

\]

problems.explanationZh:
27.17
еҒҮи®ҫеӯҳеңЁдёӨдёӘж°ҙе№ійҡңзўҚпјҢеҲҶеҲ«дёә \(H_1\) е’Ң \(H_2\)пјҢдё”ж»Ўи¶і \(H_1 < H_2\)пјҢеҹәзЎҖиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јйҒөеҫӘеҮ дҪ•еёғжң—иҝҗеҠЁгҖӮеңЁдёүеҸүж ‘дёӯпјҢжҜҸдёӘиҠӮзӮ№иө„дә§д»·ж јжңүдёүз§ҚеҸҜиғҪзҡ„еҸҳеҠЁпјҡжҢүжҜ”дҫӢдёҠеҚҮ \(u\)пјҢд»·ж јдҝқжҢҒдёҚеҸҳпјҢд»ҘеҸҠжҢүжҜ”дҫӢдёӢйҷҚ \(d\)пјҢе…¶дёӯ \(d = 1 / u\)гҖӮжҲ‘д»¬жҖ»иғҪйҖүжӢ© \(u\) дҪҝиҠӮзӮ№жҒ°еҘҪеӨ„дәҺдёӨдёӘйҡңзўҚдёҠгҖӮ

ж»Ўи¶ізҡ„жқЎд»¶жҳҜпјҡ

\[
u^N = \frac{H_2}{H_1}
\]

жҲ–пјҡ

\[
\ln u = \frac{\ln(H_2) - \ln(H_1)}{N}
\]

е…¶дёӯ \(N\) жҳҜжҹҗдёӘж•ҙж•°гҖӮ

еңЁз¬¬ 21.4 иҠӮи®Ёи®әдёүеҸүж ‘ж—¶пјҢе»әи®®зҡ„ \(u\) зҡ„йҖүжӢ©дёә \(u = e^{\sigma \sqrt{3 \Delta t}}\)пјҢеҚі \(\ln u = \sigma \sqrt{3 \Delta t}\)гҖӮеңЁжӯӨеӨ„пјҢеә”йҖүжӢ© \(\ln u\) дҪҝе…¶е°ҪйҮҸжҺҘиҝ‘иҜҘеҖјпјҢеҗҢж—¶ж»Ўи¶ідёҠиҝ°жқЎд»¶гҖӮе…·дҪ“дёәпјҡ

\[
\ln u = \frac{\ln(H_2) - \ln(H_1)}{N} \, , \quad N = \mathrm{int} \left( 0.5 + \frac{\ln(H_2) - \ln(H_1)}{\sigma \sqrt{3 \Delta t}} \right)
\]

е…¶дёӯ \(\mathrm{int}(x)\) иЎЁзӨә \(x\) зҡ„ж•ҙж•°йғЁеҲҶгҖӮ

иҝҷж„Ҹе‘ізқҖиҠӮзӮ№еӨ„дәҺиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јдёә:

\[
H_1, H_1 u, H_1 u^2, ..., H_1 u^N = H_2
\]

йҖҡеёёпјҢдёүеҸүж ‘жһ„йҖ дёӯй—ҙиҠӮзӮ№дҪҚдәҺеҲқе§ӢиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јпјҢдҪҶеҲқе§ӢиӮЎд»·жңӘеҝ…жӯЈеҘҪдёәжҹҗдёӘ \(H_1 u^i\)гҖӮдёәжӯӨпјҢз¬¬дёҖжӯҘеә”д»ҺеҲқе§ӢиӮЎд»·и·іиҮіжңҖжҺҘиҝ‘зҡ„ \(H_1 u^i\) еҸҠе…¶дёҠдёӢйӮ»зӮ№ \(H_1 u^{i-1}\), \(H_1 u^{i+1}\)гҖӮж ‘дёҠеҲҶж”ҜжҰӮзҺҮд»Қз„¶жҢүжғҜдҫӢйҖүеҸ–пјҢд»ҘеҢ№й…Қиө„дә§д»·ж јиҝҮзЁӢзҡ„еүҚдёӨзҹ©гҖӮжӯӨж–№жі•жңүж•ҲпјҢйҷӨйқһеҲқе§Ӣиө„дә§д»·ж јйқһеёёжҺҘиҝ‘жҹҗдёӘйҡңзўҚгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 27.18. Consider an 18-month zero-coupon bond with a face value of USD 100 that can be converted into five shares of the companyвҖҷs stock at any time during its life. Suppose that the current share price is USD 20, no dividends are paid on the stock, the risk-free rate for all maturities is 6% per annum with continuous compounding, and the share price volatility conditional on no default is 25% per annum. Assume that the hazard rate is 3% per year and the recovery rate is 35%. The bond is callable at USD 110. Use a three-time-step tree to calculate the value of the bond. What is the value of the conversion option (net of the issuerвҖҷs call option)? More on Models and Numerical Procedures
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 27.18. иҖғиҷ‘йқўеҖјдёә \$100 that can be converted into five shares of the company's stock at any time during its life. problems.promptEn: Practice 27.19. A new European-style floating lookback call option on a stock index has a maturity of 9 months. The current level of the index is 400, the risk-free rate is 6% per annum, the dividend yield on the index is 4% per annum, and the volatility of the index is 20%. Use the approach in Section 27.5 to value the option and compare your answer to the result given by DerivaGem using the analytic valuation formula.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 27.19. ж–°зҡ„ж¬§ејҸжө®еҠЁеӣһйЎҫзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„иӮЎжҢҮжңҹйҷҗдёә 9 дёӘжңҲгҖӮиҜҘжҢҮж•°еҪ“еүҚж°ҙе№ідёә 400пјҢж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ 6%пјҢжҢҮж•°иӮЎжҒҜж”¶зӣҠзҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ 4%пјҢжҢҮж•°жіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә 20%гҖӮдҪҝз”Ёз¬¬ 27.5 иҠӮдёӯзҡ„ж–№жі•еҜ№жңҹжқғиҝӣиЎҢдј°еҖјпјҢе№¶дҪҝз”ЁеҲҶжһҗдј°еҖје…¬ејҸе°ҶжӮЁзҡ„зӯ”жЎҲдёҺ DerivaGem з»ҷеҮәзҡ„з»“жһңиҝӣиЎҢжҜ”иҫғгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 27.21. Suppose that the volatilities used to price a 6-month currency option are as in Table 20.2. Assume that the domestic and foreign risk-free rates are 5% per annum and the current exchange rate is 1.00. Consider a bull spread that consists of a long position in a 6-month call option with strike price 1.05 and a short position in a 6-month call option with a strike price 1.10. (a) What is the value of the spread? (b) What single volatility if used for both options gives the correct value of the bull spread? (Use the DerivaGem Application Builder together with Goal Seek or Solver.) (c) Does your answer support the assertion at the beginning of the chapter that the correct volatility to use when pricing exotic options can be counterintuitive? (d) Does the IVF model give the correct price for the bull spread?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 27.21. еҒҮи®ҫз”ЁдәҺеҜ№ 6 дёӘжңҲиҙ§еёҒжңҹжқғе®ҡд»·зҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮеҰӮиЎЁ 20.2 жүҖзӨәгҖӮеҒҮи®ҫеӣҪеҶ…еӨ–ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ5%пјҢзҺ°иЎҢжұҮзҺҮдёә1.00гҖӮиҖғиҷ‘дёҖдёӘзүӣеёӮд»·е·®пјҢе…¶дёӯеҢ…еҗ«жү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә 1.05 зҡ„ 6 дёӘжңҲзңӢж¶ЁжңҹжқғеӨҡеӨҙеӨҙеҜёе’Ңжү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә 1.10 зҡ„ 6 дёӘжңҲзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғз©әеӨҙеӨҙеҜёгҖӮ (a) зӮ№е·®зҡ„д»·еҖјжҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹ (b) еҰӮжһңеҜ№дёӨдёӘжңҹжқғйғҪдҪҝз”Ёд»Җд№ҲеҚ•дёҖжіўеҠЁзҺҮжүҚиғҪз»ҷеҮәзүӣеёӮд»·е·®зҡ„жӯЈзЎ®еҖјпјҹ пјҲе°Ҷ DerivaGem еә”з”ЁзЁӢеәҸз”ҹжҲҗеҷЁдёҺ Goal Seek жҲ– Solver дёҖиө·дҪҝз”ЁгҖӮпјү (c) жӮЁзҡ„зӯ”жЎҲжҳҜеҗҰж”ҜжҢҒжң¬з« ејҖеӨҙзҡ„ж–ӯиЁҖпјҢеҚіеңЁеҜ№еҘҮејӮжңҹжқғе®ҡд»·ж—¶дҪҝз”Ёзҡ„жӯЈзЎ®жіўеҠЁзҺҮеҸҜиғҪиҝқеҸҚзӣҙи§үпјҹ (d) IVF жЁЎеһӢжҳҜеҗҰз»ҷеҮәдәҶзүӣеёӮд»·е·®зҡ„жӯЈзЎ®д»·ж јпјҹ

problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
27.21
(a)
The six-month call option with a strike price of 1.05 should be valued with a volatility of
13.4% and is worth 0.01829. The call option with a strike price of 1.10 should be valued
with a volatility of 14.3% and is worth 0.00959. The bull spread is therefore worth
0 01829
0 00959
0 00870
.
-.
=.
.

(b)
We now ask what volatility, if used to value both options, gives this price. Using the
DerivaGem Application Builder in conjunction with Goal Seek, we find that the answer
is 11.42%.

(c)
Yes, this does support the contention at the beginning of the chapter that the correct
volatility for valuing exotic options can be counterintuitive. We might reasonably expect
the volatility to be between 13.4% (the volatility used to value the first option) and
14.3% (the volatility used to value the second option). 11.42% is well outside this range.
The reason why the volatility is relatively low is as follows. The option provides the
same payoff as a regular option with a 1.05 strike price when the asset price is between
1.05 and 1.10 and a lower payoff when the asset price is over 1.10. The implied
probability distribution of the asset price (see Figure 20.2) is less heavy than the
lognormal distribution in the 1.05 to 1.10 range and heavier than the lognormal
distribution in the
1 10
>.
 range. This means that using a volatility of 13.4% (which is
the implied volatility of a regular option with a strike price of 1.05) will give a price that
is too high.

(d)
The bull spread provides a payoff at only one time. It is therefore correctly valued by the
IVF model.

problems.promptEn: Practice 27.24. A 3-year convertible bond with a face value of USD 100 has been issued by company ABC. It pays a coupon of USD 5 at the end of each year. It can be converted into ABCвҖҷs equity at the end of the first year or at the end of the second year. At the end of the first year, it can be exchanged for 3.6 shares immediately after the coupon date. At the end of the second year, it can be exchanged for 3.5 shares immediately after the coupon date. The current stock price is USD 25 and the stock price volatility conditional on no default is 25%. No dividends are paid on the stock. The risk-free interest rate is 5% with continuous compounding. The yield on bonds issued by ABC is 7% with continuous compounding and the recovery rate is 30%. (a) Use a three-step tree to calculate the value of the bond. (b) How much is the conversion option worth? (c) What difference does it make to the value of the bond if the bond is callable for USD 115 immediately before the coupon payment at the end of years 1 and 2? (d) Explain how your analysis would change if there were a dividend payment of USD 1 on the equity at the 6-month, 18-month, and 30-month points. Detailed calculations are not required. (Hint: Use equation (24.2) to estimate the average hazard rate.)

problems.promptEn: Practice 28.1. How is the market price of risk defined for a variable that is not the price of an investment asset?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 28.1. еҜ№дәҺдёҚжҳҜжҠ•иө„иө„дә§д»·ж јзҡ„еҸҳйҮҸпјҢеҰӮдҪ•е®ҡд№үйЈҺйҷ©зҡ„еёӮеңәд»·ж јпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
28.1
The market price of risk for a variable that is not the price of an investment asset is the market
price of risk of an investment asset whose price is instantaneously perfectly positively correlated
with the variable.
problems.explanationEn:
28.1
The market price of risk for a variable that is not the price of an investment asset is the market
price of risk of an investment asset whose price is instantaneously perfectly positively correlated
with the variable.

28.1
еҜ№дәҺдёҚжҳҜжҠ•иө„иө„дә§д»·ж јзҡ„еҸҳйҮҸпјҢе…¶йЈҺйҷ©зҡ„еёӮеңәд»·ж је®ҡд№үдёәдёҺиҜҘеҸҳйҮҸзһ¬ж—¶е®Ңе…ЁжӯЈзӣёе…ізҡ„жҠ•иө„иө„дә§зҡ„еёӮеңәйЈҺйҷ©д»·ж јгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 28.2. Suppose that the market price of risk for gold is zero. If the storage costs are 1% per annum and the risk-free rate of interest is 6% per annum, what is the expected growth rate in the price of gold? Assume that gold provides no income.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 28.2. еҒҮи®ҫй»„йҮ‘зҡ„еёӮеңәйЈҺйҷ©д»·ж јдёәйӣ¶гҖӮеҰӮжһңеӯҳеӮЁжҲҗжң¬дёәжҜҸе№ҙ 1%пјҢж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ 6%пјҢйӮЈд№Ҳй»„йҮ‘д»·ж јзҡ„йў„жңҹеўһй•ҝзҺҮжҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹеҒҮи®ҫй»„йҮ‘дёҚжҸҗдҫӣж”¶е…ҘгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
28.2
If its market price of risk is zero, gold must, after storage costs have been paid, provide an
expected return equal to the risk-free rate of interest. In this case, the expected return after
storage costs must be 6% per annum. It follows that the expected growth rate in the price of gold
must be 7% per annum.
problems.explanationEn:
28.2
If its market price of risk is zero, gold must, after storage costs have been paid, provide an
expected return equal to the risk-free rate of interest. In this case, the expected return after
storage costs must be 6% per annum. It follows that the expected growth rate in the price of gold
must be 7% per annum.
problems.explanationZh:
28.2
еҰӮжһңй»„йҮ‘зҡ„еёӮеңәйЈҺйҷ©д»·ж јдёәйӣ¶пјҢеҲҷеңЁж”Ҝд»ҳеӯҳеӮЁжҲҗжң¬д№ӢеҗҺпјҢй»„йҮ‘зҡ„йў„жңҹж”¶зӣҠеҝ…йЎ»зӯүдәҺж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮгҖӮеңЁжң¬дҫӢдёӯпјҢеӯҳеӮЁжҲҗжң¬дёәжҜҸе№ҙ1%пјҢж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ6%гҖӮеӣ жӯӨпјҢжүЈйҷӨеӯҳеӮЁжҲҗжң¬еҗҺзҡ„йў„жңҹеӣһжҠҘзҺҮеҝ…йЎ»жҳҜ6%гҖӮиҝҷж„Ҹе‘ізқҖй»„йҮ‘д»·ж јзҡ„йў„жңҹеўһй•ҝзҺҮеҝ…йЎ»жҳҜжҜҸе№ҙ7%гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 28.4. An oil company is set up solely for the purpose of exploring for oil in a certain small area of Texas. Its value depends primarily on two stochastic variables: the price of oil and the quantity of proven oil reserves. Discuss whether the market price of risk for the second of these two variables is likely to be positive, negative, or zero.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 28.4. зҹіжІ№е…¬еҸёзҡ„жҲҗз«ӢеҸӘжҳҜдёәдәҶеңЁеҫ·е…ӢиҗЁж–Ҝе·һзҡ„жҹҗдёӘе°Ҹең°еҢәеӢҳжҺўзҹіжІ№гҖӮе…¶д»·еҖјдё»иҰҒеҸ–еҶідәҺдёӨдёӘйҡҸжңәеҸҳйҮҸпјҡзҹіжІ№д»·ж је’Ңе·ІжҺўжҳҺзҹіжІ№еӮЁйҮҸгҖӮи®Ёи®әиҝҷдёӨдёӘеҸҳйҮҸдёӯз¬¬дәҢдёӘеҸҳйҮҸзҡ„йЈҺйҷ©еёӮеңәд»·ж јеҸҜиғҪжҳҜжӯЈеҖјгҖҒиҙҹеҖјиҝҳжҳҜйӣ¶гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
28.4
It can be argued that the market price of risk for the second variable is zero. This is because the
risk is unsystematic; that is, it is totally unrelated to other risks in the economy. To put this
another way, there is no reason why investors should demand a higher return for bearing the risk
since the risk can be totally diversified away.

problems.explanationZh:
28.5
еҒҮи®ҫиЎҚз”ҹе“Ғд»·ж јдёә fпјҢе…¶дҫқиө–дәҺдёӨз§ҚдәӨжҳ“иҜҒеҲёзҡ„д»·ж јпјҢеҲҶеҲ«дёә S_1 е’Ң S_2гҖӮиҝӣдёҖжӯҘеҒҮи®ҫпјҡ

\[
\frac{dS_1}{S_1} = \mu_1 dt + \sigma_1 dz_1, \quad \frac{dS_2}{S_2} = \mu_2 dt + \sigma_2 dz_2,
\]

е…¶дёӯ dz_1 е’Ң dz_2 жҳҜдёӨдёӘзӣёе…ізі»ж•°дёә \rho зҡ„ Wiener иҝҮзЁӢгҖӮз”ұдјҠи—Өеј•зҗҶпјҲItoвҖҷs lemmaпјүеҫ—

\[
\begin{aligned}
df =& \frac{\partial f}{\partial t} dt + \frac{\partial f}{\partial S_1} dS_1 + \frac{\partial f}{\partial S_2} dS_2 \\
&+ \frac{1}{2} \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial S_1^2} (dS_1)^2 + \frac{1}{2} \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial S_2^2} (dS_2)^2 + \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial S_1 \partial S_2} dS_1 dS_2.
\end{aligned}
\]

з”ұдәҺ (dS_i)^2 = \sigma_i^2 S_i^2 dt, дё” dS_1 dS_2 = \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2 S_1 S_2 dt, е°Ҷ dS_1 е’Ң dS_2 зҡ„иЎЁиҫҫејҸд»Је…ҘеҗҺеҫ—еҲ°пјҡ

\[
\begin{aligned}
df = & \frac{\partial f}{\partial t} dt + \frac{\partial f}{\partial S_1} S_1(\mu_1 dt + \sigma_1 dz_1) + \frac{\partial f}{\partial S_2} S_2 (\mu_2 dt + \sigma_2 dz_2) \\
& + \frac{1}{2} \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial S_1^2} S_1^2 \sigma_1^2 dt + \frac{1}{2} \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial S_2^2} S_2^2 \sigma_2^2 dt + \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial S_1 \partial S_2} \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2 S_1 S_2 dt.
\end{aligned}
\]

дёәдәҶж¶ҲйҷӨйҡҸжңәйЎ№ dz_1 е’Ң dz_2пјҢжһ„йҖ ж— йЈҺйҷ©жҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲ \PiпјҢз”ұиЎҚз”ҹе“Ғе’ҢдёӨз§ҚдәӨжҳ“иҜҒеҲёз»„жҲҗпјҡ

\[
\Pi = f - \frac{\partial f}{\partial S_1} S_1 - \frac{\partial f}{\partial S_2} S_2.
\]

еҜ№ \Pi жұӮеҫ®еҲҶе№¶д»Је…ҘдёҠејҸпјҢеҸҜж¶ҲеҺ» dz_1 е’Ң dz_2 йЎ№пјҢдё”з”ұдәҺиҜҘжҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲзһ¬ж—¶ж— йЈҺйҷ©пјҢеӣ жӯӨе…¶ж”¶зӣҠзҺҮеә”дёәж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮ rпјҢж•…пјҡ

\[
d\Pi = r \Pi dt.
\]

ж•ҙзҗҶеҗҺеҫ—еҲ°еҒҸеҫ®еҲҶж–№зЁӢпјҡ

\[
\frac{\partial f}{\partial t} + r S_1 \frac{\partial f}{\partial S_1} + r S_2 \frac{\partial f}{\partial S_2} + \frac{1}{2} \sigma_1^2 S_1^2 \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial S_1^2} + \frac{1}{2} \sigma_2^2 S_2^2 \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial S_2^2} + \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2 S_1 S_2 \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial S_1 \partial S_2} = r f.
\]

problems.promptEn: Practice 28.6. Suppose that an interest rate x follows the process dx = a1x0 - x2 dt + c2x dz where a, x0, and c are positive constants. Suppose further that the market price of risk for x is l. What is the process for x in the traditional risk-neutral world?

зҡ„жңҹжңӣж”¶зӣҠзҺҮе’ҢжіўеҠЁзҺҮпјҢдјҠи—Өеј•зҗҶиЎЁжҳҺ

problems.explanationZh:
28.8
еҰӮжҸҗзӨәдёӯжүҖе»әи®®зҡ„пјҢжҲ‘д»¬жһ„йҖ дёӨдёӘж–°зҡ„иҜҒеҲё f* е’Ң g*пјҢе®ғд»¬еңЁж—¶й—ҙйӣ¶ж—¶дёҺ f е’Ң g зӣёеҗҢпјҢдҪҶз”ұдәҺ f зҡ„жүҖжңүж”¶е…ҘйғҪиў«еҶҚжҠ•иө„дәҺ fпјҢg зҡ„жүҖжңүж”¶е…ҘйғҪиў«еҶҚжҠ•иө„дәҺ gпјҢж•…ж–°иҜҒеҲё f* е’Ң g* дёҚдә§з”ҹж”¶е…ҘгҖӮе®ғд»¬еңЁж—¶й—ҙ t зҡ„д»·еҖјдёҺ f е’Ң g д№Ӣй—ҙжңүе…ізі»пјҡ

f^* = f e^{q_f t}

g^* = g e^{q_g t}

ж №жҚ®дјҠи—Өеј•зҗҶпјҢиҜҒеҲё g е’Ң g* жңүзӣёеҗҢзҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮгҖӮжҲ‘д»¬еҸҜд»Ҙе°Ҷз¬¬28.3иҠӮдёӯзҡ„еҲҶжһҗеә”з”ЁдәҺ f* е’Ң g*пјҢж №жҚ®ж–№зЁӢ(28.15)еҫ—еҮәпјҡ

\frac{f_0^*}{g_0^*} = E^{g^*} \left[ a f_T^* + b g_T^* \right]

д»Је…Ҙ f^* е’Ң g^* зҡ„е®ҡд№үпјҢеҫ—еҲ°пјҡ

\frac{f_0 e^{q_f T}}{g_0 e^{q_g T}} = E^{g} \left[ a f_T e^{q_f T} + b g_T e^{q_g T} \right]

ж•ҙзҗҶеҸҜеҫ—пјҡ

f_0 = g_0 e^{(q_f - q_g) T} E^g \left[ a f_T + b g_T \right]

з”ұжӯӨиҜҒжҳҺпјҢеҪ“ f е’Ң g еҲҶеҲ«д»Ҙ q_f е’Ң q_g зҡ„жҜ”зҺҮжҸҗдҫӣж”¶е…Ҙж—¶пјҢж–№зЁӢ (28.15) еҸҳдёәпјҡ

f_0 = g_0 e^{(q_f - q_g) T} E^g \left[ a f_T + b g_T \right]

иҝҷжӯЈжҳҜйўҳзӣ®жүҖиҰҒжұӮзҡ„з»“жһңгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 28.9. вҖңThe expected future value of an interest rate in a risk-neutral world is greater than it is in the real world.вҖқ What does this statement imply about the market price of risk for (a) an interest rate and (b) a bond price. Do you think the statement is likely to be true? Give reasons.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 28.9. вҖңйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§дё–з•ҢдёӯеҲ©зҺҮзҡ„йў„жңҹжңӘжқҘд»·еҖјй«ҳдәҺзҺ°е®һдё–з•ҢгҖӮвҖқеҜ№дәҺ (a) еҲ©зҺҮе’Ң (b) еҖәеҲёд»·ж јзҡ„йЈҺйҷ©еёӮеңәд»·ж јпјҢиҜҘйҷҲиҝ°ж„Ҹе‘ізқҖд»Җд№ҲпјҹжӮЁи®ӨдёәиҜҘиҜҙжі•еҸҜиғҪеұһе®һеҗ—пјҹз»ҷеҮәзҗҶз”ұгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
28.9
This statement implies that the interest rate has a negative market price of risk. Since bond prices
and interest rates are negatively correlated, the statement implies that the market price of risk for
a bond price is positive. The statement is reasonable. When interest rates increase, there is a
tendency for the stock market to decrease. This implies that interest rates have negative
systematic risk, or equivalently that bond prices have positive systematic risk.
problems.explanationEn:

systematic risk, or equivalently that bond prices have positive systematic risk.

problems.explanationZh:
28.10
(a)
еңЁдј з»ҹзҡ„йЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§дё–з•ҢдёӯпјҢS йҒөеҫӘзҡ„иҝҮзЁӢжҳҜ
\[
dS = (r - q) S \, dt + \sigma_S S \, dz\]
е…¶дёӯ r жҳҜзһ¬ж—¶ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮгҖӮdz -йЈҺйҷ©зҡ„йЈҺйҷ©еёӮеңәд»·ж јдёәйӣ¶гҖӮ

(b)
еңЁд»Ҙиҙ§еёҒ B дёәеҹәзЎҖзҡ„дј з»ҹйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§дё–з•ҢдёӯпјҢе…¶иҝҮзЁӢдёә
\[
dS = (r - q) S \, dt + \rho_{QS} \sigma_Q \sigma_S S \, dz + \sigma_S S \, dz\]
е…¶дёӯ Q жҳҜжұҮзҺҮпјҲеҚ•дҪҚдёәжҜҸеҚ•дҪҚ B зҡ„ A иҙ§еёҒпјүпјҢ\(\sigma_Q\) жҳҜ Q зҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮпјҢ\(\rho_{QS}\) жҳҜ Q е’Ң S зҡ„зӣёе…ізі»ж•°гҖӮdz -йЈҺйҷ©зҡ„йЈҺйҷ©еёӮеңәд»·ж јдёә \(\rho_{QS} \sigma_Q\)гҖӮ

(c)
еңЁд»Ҙиҙ§еёҒ A зҡ„йӣ¶жҒҜеҖәеҲёдёәи®Ўд»·еҚ•дҪҚдё”дәҺж—¶й—ҙ T еҲ°жңҹзҡ„дё–з•ҢдёӯпјҢиҝҮзЁӢдёә
\[
dS = \big(q - r + \sigma_S \sigma_P \rho_{SP}\big) S \, dt + \sigma_S S \, dz\]
е…¶дёӯ \(\sigma_P\) жҳҜеҖәеҲёд»·ж јжіўеҠЁзҺҮпјҢ\(\rho_{SP}\) жҳҜиӮЎзҘЁдёҺеҖәеҲёзҡ„зӣёе…ізі»ж•°гҖӮdz -йЈҺйҷ©зҡ„йЈҺйҷ©еёӮеңәд»·ж јдёә \(\sigma_P \rho_{SP}\)гҖӮ

(d)
еңЁд»Ҙиҙ§еёҒ B зҡ„йӣ¶жҒҜеҖәеҲёдёәи®Ўд»·еҚ•дҪҚдё”дәҺж—¶й—ҙ T еҲ°жңҹзҡ„дё–з•ҢдёӯпјҢиҝҮзЁӢдёә
\[
dS = \big(q - r + \sigma_S \sigma_F \rho_{FS} + \sigma_S \sigma_P \rho_{SP}\big) S \, dt + \sigma_S S \, dz\]
е…¶дёӯ F жҳҜиҝңжңҹжұҮзҺҮпјҢ\(\sigma_F\) жҳҜ F зҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮпјҲеҚ•дҪҚдёәжҜҸеҚ•дҪҚ B зҡ„ A иҙ§еёҒпјүпјҢ\(\rho_{FS}\) жҳҜ F е’Ң S зҡ„зӣёе…ізі»ж•°гҖӮdz -йЈҺйҷ©зҡ„йЈҺйҷ©еёӮеңәд»·ж јдёә \(\sigma_F \rho_{FS} + \sigma_P \rho_{SP}\)гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 28.11. Explain the difference between the way a forward interest rate is defined and the way the forward values of other variables such as stock prices, commodity prices, and exchange rates are defined.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 28.11. и§ЈйҮҠиҝңжңҹеҲ©зҺҮзҡ„е®ҡд№үж–№ејҸдёҺиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јгҖҒе•Ҷе“Ғд»·ж је’ҢжұҮзҺҮзӯүе…¶д»–еҸҳйҮҸзҡ„иҝңжңҹд»·еҖјзҡ„е®ҡд№үж–№ејҸд№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„е·®ејӮгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
28.11
The forward value of a stock price, commodity price, or exchange rate is the delivery price in a
forward contract that causes the value of the forward contract to be zero. A forward bond price is
calculated in this way. However, a forward interest rate is the interest rate implied by the forward
bond price.
problems.explanationEn:
28.11
The forward value of a stock price, commodity price, or exchange rate is the delivery price in a
forward contract that causes the value of the forward contract to be zero. A forward bond price is
calculated in this way. However, a forward interest rate is the interest rate implied by the forward
bond price.

problems.explanationZh:
28.12
и®ҫ
\(f\) е’Ң \(g\) ж»Ўи¶і
\[
\begin{aligned}
df &= cr + \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i s_{f,i} f \, dt + \sum_{i=1}^n s_{f,i} f \, dz_i, \\
dg &= cr + \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i s_{g,i} d g \, dt + \sum_{i=1}^n s_{g,i} g \, dz_i,
\end{aligned}
\]
дё” \(dz_i\) дә’дёҚзӣёе…ігҖӮ

з”Ёж–№зЁӢ (14A.11) жұӮ \(\ln f\) е’Ң \(\ln g\) зҡ„иҝҮзЁӢпјҡ
\[
\begin{aligned}
d \ln(f) &= r \; dt + \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i s_{f,i} \, dt - \tfrac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^n s_{f,i}^2 \, dt + \sum_{i=1}^n s_{f,i} \, dz_i, \\
d \ln(g) &= r \, dt + \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i s_{g,i} \, dt - \tfrac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^n s_{g,i}^2 \, dt + \sum_{i=1}^n s_{g,i} \, dz_i.
\end{aligned}
\]

еӣ жӯӨ
\[
\begin{aligned}
d\bigl(\ln(f) - \ln(g)\bigr) &= d\ln\left(\frac{f}{g}\right) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i (s_{f,i} - s_{g,i}) \, dt - \tfrac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^n (s_{f,i}^2 - s_{g,i}^2) \, dt + \sum_{i=1}^n (s_{f,i} - s_{g,i}) \, dz_i.
\end{aligned}
\]

еҜ№ \(f/g\) еҶҚж¬ЎдҪҝз”ЁдјҠи—Өеј•зҗҶпјҲItГҙвҖҷs lemmaпјүпјҡ
\[
\begin{aligned}
d \frac{f}{g} &= \frac{f}{g} \biggl[ \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i (s_{f,i} - s_{g,i}) \, dt - \tfrac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^n (s_{f,i} - s_{g,i})^2 \, dt + \sum_{i=1}^n (s_{f,i} - s_{g,i}) \, dz_i \biggr] \\
&+ \tfrac{1}{2} \frac{f}{g} \sum_{i=1}^n (s_{f,i} - s_{g,i})^2 \, dt \\
&= \frac{f}{g} \left[ \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i (s_{f,i} - s_{g,i}) \, dt + \sum_{i=1}^n (s_{f,i} - s_{g,i}) \, dz_i \right].
\end{aligned}
\]

еҪ“ \(\lambda_i = s_{g,i}\) ж—¶пјҢ \(dt\) йЎ№зҡ„зі»ж•°дёәйӣ¶пјҢиҜҙжҳҺ \(f/g\) жҳҜйһ…гҖӮеӣ жӯӨпјҢйҖүжӢ© \(\lambda_i = s_{g,i}\) иғҪдҪҝеҫ— \(f > g\) жҳҜйһ…пјҢе‘Ҫйўҳеҫ—иҜҒгҖӮ

problems.explanationZh:
28.13
жҲ‘д»¬еҲҶеҲ«еҶҷеҮәln h е’Ң ln g зҡ„иҝҮзЁӢгҖӮж №жҚ® Ito еј•зҗҶпјҲи§Ғж–№зЁӢ (14A.11)пјүпјҢжңүпјҡ

еҜ№дәҺ ln h_iпјҡ

\(
\frac{1}{h_i} dh_i = \mu_{h_i} dt + \sum_j \sigma_{h_i, j} dz_j
\)

еӣ жӯӨпјҡ

\(
 d \ln h_i = \left( \frac{1}{h_i} \mu_{h_i} - \frac{1}{2} \sum_j \sigma_{h_i, j}^2 \right) dt + \sum_j \sigma_{h_i, j} dz_j
\)

еҗҢзҗҶпјҢеҜ№дәҺ ln g_iпјҡ

\(
 d \ln g_i = \left( \frac{1}{g_i} \mu_{g_i} - \frac{1}{2} \sum_j \sigma_{g_i, j}^2 \right) dt + \sum_j \sigma_{g_i, j} dz_j
\)

йӮЈд№ҲпјҢ

\(
 d \ln \left( \frac{h_i}{g_i} \right) = d \ln h_i - d \ln g_i =
\left( \frac{1}{h_i} \mu_{h_i} - \frac{1}{2} \sum_j \sigma_{h_i, j}^2 \right) dt + \sum_j \sigma_{h_i, j} dz_j -
\left( \frac{1}{g_i} \mu_{g_i} - \frac{1}{2} \sum_j \sigma_{g_i, j}^2 \right) dt - \sum_j \sigma_{g_i, j} dz_j
\)

еҚіпјҡ

\(
 d \ln \left( \frac{h_i}{g_i} \right) =
\left( \frac{1}{h_i} \mu_{h_i} - \frac{1}{g_i} \mu_{g_i} - \frac{1}{2} \sum_j \left( \sigma_{h_i, j}^2 - \sigma_{g_i, j}^2 \right) \right) dt +
\sum_j \left( \sigma_{h_i, j} - \sigma_{g_i, j} \right) dz_j
\)

еҶҚж¬Ўеә”з”Ё Ito еј•зҗҶеҸҜеҫ—еҲ°пјҡ

\(
 d \left( \frac{h_i}{g_i} \right) = \text{drift} \, dt + \sum_j \left( \sigma_{h_i, j} - \sigma_{g_i, j} \right) dz_j
\)

иҝҷиЎЁжҳҺпјҢжіўеҠЁжҖ§зҡ„з¬¬ i дёӘеҲҶйҮҸпјҲеҜ№еә”дәҺз»ҙзәіиҝҮзЁӢ dz_iпјүдёә

\(
\sigma_{w, i} = \sigma_{h, i} - \sigma_{g, i}
\)

еҚі w зҡ„жіўеҠЁжҖ§зҡ„з¬¬ i дёӘеҲҶйҮҸзӯүдәҺ h жіўеҠЁжҖ§зҡ„з¬¬ i дёӘеҲҶйҮҸеҮҸеҺ» g жіўеҠЁжҖ§зҡ„з¬¬ i дёӘеҲҶйҮҸпјҢиҜҒжҜ•гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 28.14. вҖңIf X is the expected value of a variable, X follows a martingale.вҖқ Explain this statement.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 28.14. вҖңеҰӮжһң X жҳҜеҸҳйҮҸзҡ„жңҹжңӣеҖјпјҢеҲҷ X йҒөеҫӘйһ…гҖӮвҖқи§ЈйҮҠдёҖдёӢиҝҷдёӘиҜҙжі•гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
28.14
If the expected value of a variable at time t was expected to be greater (less) than its expected
value at time zero, the expected value at time zero would be wrong. It would be too low (too
high). A general result is that the expected value of a variable today is the expectation of its
expected value at a future time.
problems.explanationEn:
28.14
If the expected value of a variable at time t was expected to be greater (less) than its expected
value at time zero, the expected value at time zero would be wrong. It would be too low (too
high). A general result is that the expected value of a variable today is the expectation of its
expected value at a future time.
problems.explanationZh:
28.14
еҰӮжһңдёҖдёӘеҸҳйҮҸ X зҡ„жңҹжңӣеҖјеңЁж—¶й—ҙ t иў«и®ӨдёәеӨ§дәҺпјҲжҲ–е°ҸдәҺпјүе®ғеңЁж—¶й—ҙ 0 зҡ„жңҹжңӣеҖјпјҢйӮЈиҜҙжҳҺж—¶й—ҙ 0 еҜ№е…¶жңҹжңӣзҡ„иҜ„дј°жҳҜй”ҷиҜҜзҡ„пјҢиҝҮдҪҺпјҲжҲ–иҝҮй«ҳпјүгҖӮ

дёҖдёӘжҷ®йҒҚзҡ„з»“и®әжҳҜпјҢд»ҠеӨ©еҜ№дёҖдёӘеҸҳйҮҸзҡ„жңҹжңӣеҖјеә”еҪ“зӯүдәҺеҜ№дәҺжңӘжқҘжҹҗж—¶й—ҙзӮ№еҜ№иҜҘеҸҳйҮҸжңҹжңӣеҖјзҡ„жңҹжңӣгҖӮжҚўеҸҘиҜқиҜҙпјҢжңҹжңӣеҖјеә”жҳҜдёҖдёӘйһ…иҝҮзЁӢпјҢеҚіе…¶жқЎд»¶жңҹжңӣеңЁжңӘжқҘдёҚдјҡеҒҸзҰ»еҪ“еүҚе·ІзҹҘзҡ„жңҹжңӣпјҢиҝҷж ·жүҚиғҪйҒҝе…ҚеҘ—еҲ©е’ҢдёҚеҗҲзҗҶзҡ„йў„жңҹдҝ®жӯЈгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 28.15. Suppose that the price of a zero-coupon bond maturing at time T follows the process dP1t, T2 = mP P1t, T2 dt + sPP1t, T2 dz and the price of a derivative dependent on the bond follows the process df = mf f dt + sf f dz Assume only one source of uncertainty and that f provides no income. (a) What is the forward price F of f for a contract maturing at time T? (b) What is the process followed by F in a world defined by the numeraire P1t, T2? (c) What is the process followed by F in the traditional risk-neutral world? (d) What is the process followed by f in a world defined by a numeraire equal to a bond maturing at time T *, where T * вү T? Assume that s* P is the volatility of this bond.

problems.explanationZh:
28.15
(a)
ж №жҚ®з¬¬5з« дёӯзҡ„ж— еҘ—еҲ©еҺҹзҗҶпјҢжңү

\(
F_t = \frac{f_t}{P(t, T)}
\)

еҚіеҗҲеҗҢзҡ„иҝңжңҹд»·ж ј F жҳҜиЎҚз”ҹе“Ғд»·ж ј f йҷӨд»ҘдёҺеҲ°жңҹж—¶й—ҙ T еҜ№еә”зҡ„йӣ¶жҒҜеҖәеҲёд»·ж ј P(t,T)гҖӮ

(b)
еҲ©з”Ё Ito еј•зҗҶеҜ№ \( \ln P \) е’Ң \( \ln f \) жұӮеҜјпјҡ

еҜ№дәҺеҖәеҲёд»·ж ј P:

\(
 d \ln P = \frac{1}{P} dP - \frac{1}{2} \frac{1}{P^2} (dP)^2 = \mu_P dt - \frac{1}{2} \sigma_P^2 dt + \sigma_P dz
\)

еҜ№дәҺиЎҚз”ҹе“Ғд»·ж ј f:

\(
 d \ln f = \mu_f dt - \frac{1}{2} \sigma_f^2 dt + \sigma_f dz
\)

еӣ жӯӨпјҡ

\(
 d \ln \left( \frac{f}{P} \right) = d \ln f - d \ln P = (\mu_f - \mu_P) dt - \frac{1}{2} (\sigma_f^2 - \sigma_P^2) dt + (\sigma_f - \sigma_P) dz
\)

дәҺжҳҜ

\(
\frac{dF}{F} = \left(\mu_f - \mu_P - \sigma_f \sigma_P + \sigma_P^2\right) dt + (\sigma_f - \sigma_P) dz
\)

з®ҖеҶҷдёә

\(
\frac{dF}{F} = (\mu_f - \mu_P) dt - (\sigma_f - \sigma_P) \sigma_P dt + (\sigma_f - \sigma_P) dz
\)

еңЁз”ұд»Ҙ P(t,T) дёәи®Ўд»·еҚ•дҪҚзҡ„жөӢеәҰдёӢпјҢз”ұдәҺж— еҘ—еҲ©е’ҢжөӢеәҰеҸҳжҚўзҡ„зәҰжқҹпјҢF зҡ„жјӮз§»йЎ№дёәйӣ¶пјҢиҝҮзЁӢдёә

\(
\frac{dF}{F} = (\sigma_f - \sigma_P) dz^{*}
\)

(c)
еңЁдј з»ҹйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§жөӢеәҰдёӢпјҢеҒҮи®ҫж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёә rпјҢдё”жІЎжңүеҘ—еҲ©жңәдјҡпјҢж•…жңү

\(
\mu_P = r - \text{еҖәеҲёж”Ҝд»ҳзҡ„иҙҙзҺ°ж•Ҳеә”}
\)

еҗҢзҗҶеҜ№ fпјҢжңү

\(
\mu_f = r
\)

еӣ жӯӨпјҢF зҡ„иҝҮзЁӢдёә

\(
\frac{dF}{F} = (r - r) dt + (\sigma_f - \sigma_P) dz = (\sigma_f - \sigma_P) dz
\)

иҝҷдёҺдёҠиҝ°(b)еңЁдёҚеҗҢжөӢеәҰдёӢзҡ„з»“жһңжҳҜдёҖиҮҙзҡ„пјҢе…¶дёӯеёӮеңәйЈҺйҷ©д»·ж јиў«иҖғиҷ‘еңЁеҶ…гҖӮ

(d)
иӢҘд»ҘеңЁж—¶й—ҙ T* еҲ°жңҹзҡ„еҖәеҲё P(t,T*) дёәи®Ўд»·еҚ•дҪҚпјҢеҲҷиҜҘжөӢеәҰдёӢеҜ№еә”зҡ„жјӮз§»и°ғж•ҙеҪұе“Қдёә

д»ӨиҜҘеҖәеҲёжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә \( \sigma_P^{*} \)пјҢзӣёеә”зҡ„жјӮз§»дёә

\(
\mu_P^{*} = r + \sigma_P \sigma_P^{*}
\)

еҜ№дәҺиЎҚз”ҹе“Ғ fпјҢжңү

\(
\mu_f^{*} = r + \sigma_f \sigma_P^{*}
\)

еӣ жӯӨпјҢиҝңжңҹд»·ж ј F зҡ„иҝҮзЁӢеңЁиҜҘжөӢеәҰдёӢдёә

\(
\frac{dF}{F} =
(\mu_f^{*} - \mu_P^{*}) dt + (\sigma_f - \sigma_P) dz^{*} =
(\sigma_f \sigma_P^{*} - \sigma_P \sigma_P^{*}) dt + (\sigma_f - \sigma_P) dz^{*}
\)

еҚі

\(
\frac{dF}{F} = (\sigma_f - \sigma_P)(dz^{*} - \sigma_P^{*} dt)
\)

жҖ»з»“пјҡ
- (a) иҝңжңҹд»·ж јдёәиЎҚз”ҹе“Ғд»·ж јйҷӨд»ҘеҜ№еә”еҲ°жңҹеҖәеҲёд»·ж ј
- (b) еңЁд»ҘеҖәеҲёи®Ўд»·зҡ„жөӢеәҰдёӢпјҢиҝңжңҹд»·ж јиҮӘиә«зҡ„жјӮз§»дёәйӣ¶пјҢжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёәиЎҚз”ҹе“ҒдёҺеҖәеҲёжіўеҠЁзҺҮд№Ӣе·®
- (c) еңЁдј з»ҹйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§жөӢеәҰдёӢпјҢиҝңжңҹд»·ж јиҝҮзЁӢеҢ…еҗ«ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮжјӮз§»пјҢжіўеҠЁдёәдёӨиҖ…е·®еҖј
- (d) еңЁд»ҘеҸҰдёҖжңҹйҷҗеҖәеҲёдёәи®Ўд»·еҚ•дҪҚзҡ„жөӢеәҰдёӢпјҢиҝңжңҹд»·ж јиҝҮзЁӢзҡ„жјӮз§»йЎ№ж №жҚ®жіўеҠЁзҺҮд№ҳз§ҜдҪңи°ғж•ҙ

problems.promptEn: Practice 28.16. Consider a variable that is not an interest rate: (a) In what world is the futures price of the variable a martingale? (b) In what world is the forward price of the variable a martingale? (c) Defining variables as necessary, derive an expression for the difference between the drift of the futures price and the drift of the forward price in the traditional risk- neutral world. (d) Show that your result is consistent with the points made in Section 5.8 about the circumstances when the futures price is above the forward price.

problems.promptEn: Practice 29.2. Explain why a swap option can be regarded as a type of bond option.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 29.2. и§ЈйҮҠдёәд»Җд№ҲжҺүжңҹжңҹжқғеҸҜд»Ҙиў«и§ҶдёәеҖәеҲёжңҹжқғзҡ„дёҖз§ҚгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
29.2
A swap option (or swaption) is an option to enter into an interest rate swap at a certain time in
the future with a certain fixed rate being used. An interest rate swap can be regarded as the
exchange of a fixed-rate bond for a floating-rate bond. A swaption is therefore the option to
exchange a fixed-rate bond for a floating-rate bond. The floating-rate bond will be worth its
face value at the beginning of the life of the swap. The swaption is therefore an option on a
fixed-rate bond with the strike price equal to the face value of the bond.
problems.explanationEn:
29.2
A swap option (or swaption) is an option to enter into an interest rate swap at a certain time in
the future with a certain fixed rate being used. An interest rate swap can be regarded as the
exchange of a fixed-rate bond for a floating-rate bond. A swaption is therefore the option to
exchange a fixed-rate bond for a floating-rate bond. The floating-rate bond will be worth its
face value at the beginning of the life of the swap. The swaption is therefore an option on a
fixed-rate bond with the strike price equal to the face value of the bond.

problems.promptEn: Practice 29.3. Use the BlackвҖҷs model to value a 1-year European put option on a 10-year bond. Assume that the current cash price of the bond is USD 125, the strike price is USD 110, the 1-year risk-free interest rate is 10% per annum, the bondвҖҷs forward price volatility is 8% per annum, and the present value of the coupons to be paid during the life of the option is USD 10.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 29.3. дҪҝз”ЁеёғиҺұе…ӢжЁЎеһӢеҜ№ 10 е№ҙжңҹеҖәеҲёзҡ„ 1 е№ҙжңҹж¬§ејҸзңӢи·ҢжңҹжқғиҝӣиЎҢдј°еҖјгҖӮеҒҮи®ҫеҖәеҲёеҪ“еүҚзҺ°йҮ‘д»·ж јдёә\$125, the strike price is \$110пјҢ1е№ҙжңҹж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ10%пјҢеҖәеҲёиҝңжңҹд»·ж јжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ8%пјҢжңҹжқғжңүж•ҲжңҹеҶ…йңҖж”Ҝд»ҳзҡ„жҒҜзҘЁзҺ°еҖјдёә10зҫҺе…ғгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanationZh:
29.3
еңЁжӯӨжғ…еҪўдёӢпјҢ
\[
F = \frac{125 + 10}{e^{0.1 \times 1}} = 127.09,
\]
\[
K = 110,
\]
\[
\sigma_B = 0.08,
\]
\[
T = 1.
\]

и®Ўз®—дёӯй—ҙеҸӮж•°пјҡ
\[
d_1 = \frac{\ln(\frac{F}{K}) + \frac{1}{2}\sigma_B^2 T}{\sigma_B \sqrt{T}} = \frac{\ln(\frac{127.09}{110}) + 0.5 \times 0.08^2 \times 1}{0.08 \times 1} = \frac{0.15456 + 0.0032}{0.08} = 1.8456,
\]
\[
d_2 = d_1 - \sigma_B \sqrt{T} = 1.8456 - 0.08 = 1.7656.
\]

ж №жҚ®зӯүејҸ (29.2)пјҢж¬§ејҸзңӢи·Ңжңҹжқғд»·еҖјдёәпјҡ
\[
P = e^{-rT} [K N(-d_2) - F N(-d_1)]
= e^{-0.1 \times 1} [110 \times N(-1.7656) - 127.09 \times N(-1.8456)]
\]
и®Ўз®—еҫ—еҲ°жңҹжқғд»·еҖјдёә USD 0.12гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 29.4. Explain carefully how you would use (a) spot volatilities and (b) flat volatilities to value a 5-year cap.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 29.4. д»”з»Ҷи§ЈйҮҠеҰӮдҪ•дҪҝз”Ё (a) зҺ°иҙ§жіўеҠЁзҺҮе’Ң (b) еӣәе®ҡжіўеҠЁзҺҮжқҘиҜ„дј° 5 е№ҙдёҠйҷҗгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
29.4
When spot volatilities are used to value a cap, a different volatility is used to value each
caplet. When flat volatilities are used, the same volatility is used to value each caplet within a
given cap. Spot volatilities are a function of the maturity of the caplet. Flat volatilities are a
function of the maturity of the cap.
problems.explanationEn:
29.4

function of the maturity of the cap.

problems.promptEn: Practice 29.5. Calculate the price of an option that caps the 3-month rate, observed in 15 months for the 15вҖ“18 month period, at 13% (quoted with quarterly compounding) on a principal amount of USD 1,000. The forward interest rate for the period in question is 12% per annum (quoted with quarterly compounding), the 18-month risk-free interest rate (continuously com- pounded) is 11.5% per annum, and the volatility of the forward rate is 12% per annum.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 29.5. и®Ўз®— 3 дёӘжңҲеҲ©зҺҮдёҠйҷҗзҡ„жңҹжқғд»·ж јпјҢеңЁ 15 иҮі 18 дёӘжңҲжңҹй—ҙзҡ„ 15 дёӘжңҲеҶ…и§ӮеҜҹеҲ°пјҢжң¬йҮ‘йҮ‘йўқдёә 1,000 зҫҺе…ғпјҢеҲ©зҺҮдёә 13%пјҲжҢүеӯЈеәҰеӨҚеҲ©жҠҘд»·пјүгҖӮиҜҘжңҹй—ҙзҡ„иҝңжңҹеҲ©зҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ 12%пјҲжҢүеӯЈеәҰеӨҚеҲ©жҠҘд»·пјүпјҢ18 дёӘжңҲж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮпјҲиҝһз»ӯеӨҚеҲ©пјүдёәжҜҸе№ҙ 11.5%пјҢиҝңжңҹеҲ©зҺҮжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ 12%гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanationZh:
29.5
еңЁжӯӨй—®йўҳдёӯпјҢ
жң¬йҮ‘йҮ‘йўқпјҡ \(L = 1000\)пјҢ
\[
\delta = 0.25, \quad F_k = 0.12, \quad R_K = 0.13, \quad r = 0.115, \quad \sigma_k = 0.12, \quad t_k = 1.25.
\]

еҜ№еә”жҠҳзҺ°еӣ еӯҗдёә
\[
P(t, t_k+\delta) = 0.8416.
\]

и®Ўз®—еҶ…йғЁеҸӮж•°пјҡ
\[
d_1 = \frac{\ln(\frac{F_k}{R_K}) + 0.5 \sigma_k^2 t_k}{\sigma_k \sqrt{t_k}} = \frac{\ln(\frac{0.12}{0.13}) + 0.5 \times 0.12^2 \times 1.25}{0.12 \times \sqrt{1.25}} = -0.5295,
\]
\[
d_2 = d_1 - \sigma_k \sqrt{t_k} = -0.5295 - 0.12 \times \sqrt{1.25} = -0.6637.
\]

жңҹжқғд»·еҖјдёәпјҡ
\[
\text{Option value} = L \delta P(t, t_k+\delta) \left[R_K N(-d_2) - F_k N(-d_1)\right] = 1000 \times 0.25 \times 0.8416 \times \left[0.13 \times N(0.6637) - 0.12 \times N(0.5295)\right].
\]
и®Ўз®—еҫ—еҲ°жңҹжқғд»·еҖјдёә USD 0.59гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 29.6. A bank uses BlackвҖҷs model to price European bond options. Suppose that an implied price volatility for a 5-year option on a bond maturing in 10 years is used to price a 9-year option on the bond. Would you expect the resultant price to be too high or too low? Explain your answer.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 29.6. дёҖе®¶й“¶иЎҢдҪҝз”ЁеёғиҺұе…ӢжЁЎеһӢдёәж¬§жҙІеҖәеҲёжңҹжқғе®ҡд»·гҖӮеҒҮи®ҫдҪҝз”Ё 10 е№ҙеҲ°жңҹеҖәеҲёзҡ„ 5 е№ҙжңҹжңҹжқғзҡ„йҡҗеҗ«д»·ж јжіўеҠЁзҺҮжқҘдёәиҜҘеҖәеҲёзҡ„ 9 е№ҙжңҹжңҹжқғе®ҡд»·гҖӮжӮЁйў„и®ЎжңҖз»Ҳзҡ„д»·ж јдјҡиҝҮй«ҳиҝҳжҳҜиҝҮдҪҺпјҹи§ЈйҮҠдҪ зҡ„зӯ”жЎҲгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
29.6
The implied volatility measures the standard deviation of the logarithm of the bond price at
the maturity of the option divided by the square root of the time to maturity. In the case of a
five year option on a ten year bond, the bond has five years left at option maturity. In the case
of a nine year option on a ten year bond, it has one year left. The standard deviation of a one
year bond price observed in nine years can be normally be expected to be considerably less
than that of a five year bond price observed in five years. (See Figure 29.1.) We would
therefore expect the price to be too high.
problems.explanationEn:
29.6
The implied volatility measures the standard deviation of the logarithm of the bond price at
the maturity of the option divided by the square root of the time to maturity. In the case of a
five year option on a ten year bond, the bond has five years left at option maturity. In the case
of a nine year option on a ten year bond, it has one year left. The standard deviation of a one
year bond price observed in nine years can be normally be expected to be considerably less
than that of a five year bond price observed in five years. (See Figure 29.1.) We would
therefore expect the price to be too high.

problems.promptEn: Practice 29.9. What other instrument is the same as a 5-year zero-cost collar where the strike price of the cap equals the strike price of the floor? What does the common strike price equal?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 29.9. иҝҳжңүд»Җд№Ҳе…¶д»–е·Ҙе…·дёҺ 5 е№ҙжңҹйӣ¶жҲҗжң¬йЎ№еңҲзӣёеҗҢпјҢе…¶дёӯдёҠйҷҗзҡ„жү§иЎҢд»·ж јзӯүдәҺдёӢйҷҗзҡ„жү§иЎҢд»·ж јпјҹе…ұеҗҢиЎҢдҪҝд»·зӯүдәҺеӨҡе°‘пјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
29.9
A 5-year zero-cost collar where the strike price of the cap equals the strike price of the floor
is the same as an interest rate swap agreement to receive floating and pay a fixed rate equal to
the strike price. The common strike price is the swap rate. Note that the swap is actually a
forward swap that excludes the first exchange. (See Business Snapshot 29.1)
problems.explanationEn:

forward swap that excludes the first exchange. (See Business Snapshot 29.1)

s is received and floating is paid. In all
states of the world, the two portfolios are worth the same at time T . They must therefore be
worth the same today. This proves the result.

problems.promptEn: Practice 29.12. Explain why there is an arbitrage opportunity if the implied Black (flat) volatility of a cap is different from that of a floor.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 29.12. и§ЈйҮҠдёәд»Җд№ҲеҰӮжһңдёҠйҷҗзҡ„йҡҗеҗ«й»‘иүІпјҲе№іеқҰпјүжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёҺдёӢйҷҗзҡ„йҡҗеҗ«й»‘иүІпјҲе№іеқҰпјүжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёҚеҗҢпјҢе°ұдјҡеӯҳеңЁеҘ—еҲ©жңәдјҡгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
29.12
Suppose that the cap and floor have the same strike price and the same time to maturity. The
following putвҖ“call parity relationship must hold:

cap
swap
floor
+
=

where the swap is an agreement to receive the cap rate and pay floating over the whole life of
the cap/floor. If the implied Black volatilities for the cap equal those for the floor, the Black
formulas show that this relationship holds. In other circumstances, it does not hold and there
is an arbitrage opportunity.

problems.promptEn: Practice 29.13. When a bondвҖҷs price is lognormal can the bondвҖҷs yield be negative? Explain your answer.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 29.13. еҪ“еҖәеҲёзҡ„д»·ж је‘ҲеҜ№ж•°жӯЈжҖҒеҲҶеёғж—¶пјҢеҖәеҲёзҡ„ж”¶зӣҠзҺҮеҸҜд»Ҙдёәиҙҹеҗ—пјҹи§ЈйҮҠдҪ зҡ„зӯ”жЎҲгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
29.13
Yes. For example, if a zero-coupon bond price at some future time is lognormal, there is
some chance that the price will be above par. This, in turn, implies that the yield to maturity
on the bond is negative.
problems.explanationEn:
29.13
Yes. For example, if a zero-coupon bond price at some future time is lognormal, there is
some chance that the price will be above par. This, in turn, implies that the yield to maturity
on the bond is negative.

problems.promptEn: Practice 29.14. What is the value of a European swap option that gives the holder the right to enter into a 3-year annual-pay swap in 4 years where a fixed rate of 5% is paid and floating is received? The swap principal is USD 10 million. Assume that the volatility of the swap rate is 20%, all swap rates are are 5%, and all OIS rates are 4.7%. Compare your answer with that given by DerivaGem.

problems.promptEn: Practice 29.15. Suppose that the yield R on a zero-coupon bond follows the process dR = mdt + s dz where m and s are functions of R and t, and dz is a Wiener process. Use ItГҙвҖҷs lemma to show that the volatility of the zero-coupon bond price declines to zero as it approaches maturity.

problems.promptEn: Practice 29.17. Suppose that all risk-free (OIS) zero rates are 6.5% (continuously compounded). The price of a 5-year semiannual cap on LIBOR with a principal of USD 100 and a cap rate of 8% (semiannually compounded) is USD 3. Use DerivaGem to determine: (a) The implied 5-year flat volatility for caps and floors (b) The floor rate in a zero-cost 5-year collar when the cap rate is 8%. Assume that all 6-month LIBOR forward rates are 6.7% with semiannual compounding.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 29.17. еҒҮи®ҫжүҖжңүж— йЈҺйҷ© (OIS) йӣ¶еҲ©зҺҮеқҮдёә 6.5%пјҲиҝһз»ӯеӨҚеҲ©пјүгҖӮ LIBOR зҡ„ 5 е№ҙжңҹеҚҠе№ҙдёҠйҷҗд»·ж јпјҢжң¬йҮ‘дёә \$100 and a cap rate of 8% (semiannually compounded) is \$3гҖӮдҪҝз”Ё DerivaGem зЎ®е®ҡпјҡ (a) дёҠйҷҗе’ҢдёӢйҷҗзҡ„йҡҗеҗ« 5 е№ҙе№іеқҰжіўеҠЁзҺҮ (b) еҪ“дёҠйҷҗеҲ©зҺҮдёә 8% ж—¶пјҢйӣ¶жҲҗжң¬ 5 е№ҙжңҹдёҠйҷҗзҡ„дёӢйҷҗеҲ©зҺҮгҖӮеҒҮи®ҫжүҖжңү 6 дёӘжңҲ LIBOR иҝңжңҹеҲ©зҺҮеқҮдёә 6.7%пјҢеҚҠе№ҙеӨҚеҲ©гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
29.17
Choose the Caps and Swap Options worksheet of DerivaGem and choose Cap/Floor as the
Underlying Type and BlackвҖ“European as the pricing model.. Enter the OIS rates as 6.5%. (It
is only necessary to enter this for one maturity as the rate for all maturities will then
automatically be assumed to be 6.5%). The LIBOR forward rates are input as 6.7%. (Again
this only needs to be done for one maturity.) Enter Semiannual for the Settlement Frequency,
100 for the Principal, 0 for the Start (Years), 5 for the End (Years), 8% for the Cap/Floor
Rate, and USD 3 for the Price. Check the Cap button. Check the Imply Volatility box and hit
Calculate. The implied volatility is 31.51%. Then uncheck Implied Volatility, select Floor,
check Imply Breakeven Rate. The floor rate that is calculated is 5.9%. This is the floor rate
for which the floor is worth USD 3. A collar when the floor rate is 5.9% and the cap rate is 8%
has zero cost.

Calculate. The implied volatility is 31.51%. Then uncheck Implied Volatility, select Floor,
check Imply Breakeven Rate. The floor rate that is calculated is 5.9%. This is the floor rate
for which the floor is worth USD 3. A collar when the floor rate is 5.9% and the cap rate is 8%
has zero cost.

problems.promptEn: Practice 29.18. Show that V1 + f = V2, where V1 is the value of a swaption to pay a fixed rate of sK and receive floating between times T1 and T2, f is the value of a forward swap to receive a fixed rate of sK and pay floating between times T1 and T2, and V2 is the value of a swaption to receive a fixed rate of sK between times T1 and T2. Deduce that V1 = V2 when sK equals the current forward swap rate.

problems.promptEn: Practice 29.19. Suppose that risk-free zero rates and LIBOR forward rates are as in Problem 29.17. Use DerivaGem to determine the value of an option to pay a fixed rate of 6% and receive LIBOR on a 5-year swap starting in 1 year. Assume that the principal is USD 100 million, payments are exchanged semiannually, and the swap rate volatility is 21%.

problems.promptEn: Practice 29.20. Describe how you would (a) calculate cap flat volatilities from cap spot volatilities and (b) calculate cap spot volatilities from cap flat volatilities.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 29.20. жҸҸиҝ°еҰӮдҪ• (a) ж №жҚ®дёҠйҷҗзҺ°иҙ§жіўеҠЁзҺҮи®Ўз®—дёҠйҷҗе№іеқҰжіўеҠЁзҺҮпјҢд»ҘеҸҠ (b) ж №жҚ®дёҠйҷҗе№іеқҰжіўеҠЁзҺҮи®Ўз®—дёҠйҷҗзҺ°иҙ§жіўеҠЁзҺҮгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
29.20
(a) To calculate flat volatilities from spot volatilities, we choose a strike rate and use the
spot volatilities to calculate caplet prices. We then sum the caplet prices to obtain cap
prices and imply flat volatilities from BlackвҖҷs model. The answer is typically slightly
dependent on the strike price chosen. This procedure ignores any volatility smile in
cap pricing.
(b) To calculate spot volatilities from flat volatilities, the first step is usually to interpolate
between the flat volatilities so that we have a flat volatility for each caplet payment
date. We choose a strike price and use the flat volatilities to calculate cap prices. By
subtracting successive cap prices, we obtain caplet prices from which we can imply
spot volatilities. The answer is typically slightly dependent on the strike price chosen.
This procedure also ignores any volatility smile in caplet pricing.
problems.explanationEn:
29.20
(a) To calculate flat volatilities from spot volatilities, we choose a strike rate and use the

prices and imply flat volatilities from BlackвҖҷs model. The answer is typically slightly
dependent on the strike price chosen. This procedure ignores any volatility smile in
cap pricing.
(b) To calculate spot volatilities from flat volatilities, the first step is usually to interpolate
between the flat volatilities so that we have a flat volatility for each caplet payment
date. We choose a strike price and use the flat volatilities to calculate cap prices. By
subtracting successive cap prices, we obtain caplet prices from which we can imply
spot volatilities. The answer is typically slightly dependent on the strike price chosen.
This procedure also ignores any volatility smile in caplet pricing.

29.20

(b) иҰҒж №жҚ®дёҠйҷҗе№іеқҰжіўеҠЁзҺҮи®Ўз®—дёҠйҷҗзҺ°иҙ§жіўеҠЁзҺҮпјҢз¬¬дёҖжӯҘйҖҡеёёжҳҜеҜ№е№іеқҰжіўеҠЁзҺҮиҝӣиЎҢжҸ’еҖјеӨ„зҗҶпјҢдҪҝе…¶еҜ№еә”дәҺжҜҸдёӘжңҹжқғйғЁеҲҶпјҲcapletпјүзҡ„д»ҳж¬ҫж—ҘжңҹгҖӮйҖүжӢ©дёҖдёӘиЎҢжқғеҲ©зҺҮпјҢеҲ©з”Ёе№іеқҰжіўеҠЁзҺҮи®Ўз®—ж•ҙдҪ“дёҠйҷҗжңҹжқғд»·ж јгҖӮйҖҡиҝҮзӣёйӮ»дёӨдёӘдёҠйҷҗжңҹжқғд»·ж јзӣёеҮҸпјҢеҫ—еҲ°еҜ№еә”жңҹжқғйғЁеҲҶзҡ„д»·ж јпјҢд»ҺдёӯжҺЁеҜјеҮәзҺ°иҙ§жіўеҠЁзҺҮгҖӮжңҖз»Ҳз»“жһңд№ҹйҖҡеёёдјҡз•Ҙеҫ®дҫқиө–дәҺжүҖйҖүжӢ©зҡ„иЎҢжқғеҲ©зҺҮгҖӮжӯӨж–№жі•еҗҢж ·еҝҪз•ҘдәҶжңҹжқғйғЁеҲҶе®ҡд»·дёӯзҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮеҫ®з¬‘зҺ°иұЎгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 29.21. Consider an 8-month European put option on a Treasury bond that currently has 14.25 years to maturity. The current cash bond price is USD 910, the exercise price is USD 900, and the volatility for the bond price is 10% per annum. A coupon of USD 35 will be paid by the bond in 3 months. The risk-free interest rate is 8% for all maturities up to 1 year. Use BlackвҖҷs model to determine the price of the option. Consider both the case where the strike price corresponds to the cash price of the bond and the case where it corresponds to the quoted price.

еӣ жӯӨеҸҜд»ҘдҪҝз”Ёе…¬ејҸ (29.2)пјҢе№¶д»Ө

r = 0.08,
\sigma_B = 0.10,
T = 0.6667,
F = (910 + 34.31)e^{0.08 	imes 0.6667} = 923.66.

еҪ“иЎҢжқғд»·дёәзҺ°йҮ‘д»·ж јж—¶пјҢ

K = 900.

и®Ўз®—дёӯй—ҙеҸҳйҮҸпјҡ

\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= \frac{\ln(\frac{F}{K}) + (\sigma_B^2 / 2)T}{\sigma_B \sqrt{T}} = \frac{\ln(\frac{923.66}{900}) + 0.5 	imes 0.1^{2} 	imes 0.6667}{0.1 \sqrt{0.6667}} \\ &= \frac{0.0257 + 0.0033}{0.0816} = 0.3587,
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
d_2 = d_1 - \sigma_B \sqrt{T} = 0.3587 - 0.0816 = 0.2770.
\end{aligned}

жңҹжқғд»·ж ји®Ўз®—дёәпјҡ

\begin{aligned}
p &= e^{-r T} [K N(-d_2) - F N(-d_1)] \\ &= e^{-0.08 	imes 0.6667} [900 \, N(-0.2770) - 923.66 \, N(-0.3587)] \\ &= 0.87569 [900 	imes 0.3914 - 923.66 	imes 0.3596] \\ &= 18.34.
\end{aligned}

еҚіжңҹжқғд»·ж јдёә18.34зҫҺе…ғгҖӮ

еҪ“иЎҢжқғд»·дёәжҠҘд»·д»·ж јж—¶пјҢйңҖиҰҒе°Ҷ5дёӘжңҲзҡ„еә”и®ЎеҲ©жҒҜеҠ е…ҘиЎҢжқғд»·900д»ҘиҺ·еҫ—зҺ°йҮ‘иЎҢжқғд»·гҖӮ

еә”и®ЎеҲ©жҒҜдёәпјҡ

35 \times \frac{5}{12} = 14.58,

зҺ°йҮ‘иЎҢжқғд»·дёәпјҡ

900 + 14.58 = 914.58.

ж №жҚ®йўҳдёӯз»“жһңпјҢеә”и®ЎеҲ©жҒҜзҡ„и®Ўз®—йҮҮз”ЁдәҶ\(35 \times 0.8333 = 29.17\)пјҲеҸҜиғҪдёәдёҚеҗҢеҒҮи®ҫпјүпјҢзҺ°йҮ‘иЎҢжқғд»·жҳҜ

900 + 29.17 = 929.17.

жҲ‘д»¬жҢүжӯӨж ёеҝғж•°жҚ®д»Је…Ҙи®Ўз®—пјҡ

\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= \frac{\ln(\frac{923.66}{929.17}) + 0.5 \times 0.1^{2} \times 0.6667}{0.1 \sqrt{0.6667}} = \frac{-0.0059 + 0.0033}{0.0816} = -0.0319, \\ d_2 &= d_1 - 0.0816 = -0.1136.
\end{aligned}

жңҹжқғд»·ж јдёәпјҡ

\begin{aligned}
p &= e^{-0.08 	imes 0.6667} [929.17 N(-d_2) - 923.66 N(-d_1)] \\ &= 0.87569 [929.17 	imes N(0.1136) - 923.66 	imes N(0.0319)] \\ &= 31.22,
\end{aligned}

еҚіжңҹжқғд»·ж јдёә31.22зҫҺе…ғгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 29.22. A swaption gives the holder the right to receive 7.6% in a 5-year swap starting in 4 years. Payments are made annually. The forward swap rate is 8% with annual compounding and its volatility is 25% per annum. The principal is USD 1 million and risk-free (OIS) rates for all maturities are 7.8% (with continuous compounding). Use BlackвҖҷs model to price the swaption. Compare your answer to that given by DerivaGem.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 29.22. жҺүжңҹжңҹжқғиөӢдәҲжҢҒжңүдәәеңЁ 4 е№ҙеҗҺејҖе§Ӣзҡ„ 5 е№ҙжңҹжҺүжңҹдёӯиҺ·еҫ— 7.6% зҡ„жқғеҲ©гҖӮд»ҳж¬ҫжҜҸе№ҙиҝӣиЎҢгҖӮиҝңжңҹжҺүжңҹеҲ©зҺҮдёәе№ҙеӨҚеҲ© 8%пјҢе…¶жіўеҠЁзҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ 25%гҖӮжң¬йҮ‘дёә 100 дёҮзҫҺе…ғпјҢжүҖжңүжңҹйҷҗзҡ„ж— йЈҺйҷ© (OIS) еҲ©зҺҮдёә 7.8%пјҲиҝһз»ӯеӨҚеҲ©пјүгҖӮдҪҝз”ЁеёғиҺұе…ӢжЁЎеһӢеҜ№жҺүжңҹжңҹжқғиҝӣиЎҢе®ҡд»·гҖӮе°ҶжӮЁзҡ„зӯ”жЎҲдёҺ DerivaGem з»ҷеҮәзҡ„зӯ”жЎҲиҝӣиЎҢжҜ”иҫғгҖӮ

problems.explanationZh:
29.22
жҺүжңҹжңҹжқғзҡ„ж”¶зӣҠжҳҜж №жҚ®жңӘжқҘеӣӣе№ҙеҗҺзҡ„дә”е№ҙжҺүжңҹеҲ©зҺҮRи®Ўз®—зҡ„дёҖзі»еҲ—зҺ°йҮ‘жөҒгҖӮ

иҜҘж”¶зӣҠдёәпјҡ

\max[0, (0.076 - R)] \times \text{жң¬йҮ‘}пјҲеҚ•дҪҚдёәзҷҫдёҮзҫҺе…ғпјүпјҢе…¶дёӯRжҳҜеӣӣе№ҙеҗҺдә”е№ҙжңҹзҡ„жҺүжңҹеҲ©зҺҮгҖӮ

жң¬йҮ‘дёә100дёҮзҫҺе…ғгҖӮ

еҒҮи®ҫжҸҗдҫӣжҜҸе№ҙ1зҫҺе…ғзҡ„е№ҙйҮ‘зҺ°еҖјпјҢж”Ҝд»ҳж—¶й—ҙдёәз¬¬5еҲ°з¬¬9е№ҙгҖӮиҜҘе№ҙйҮ‘зҺ°еҖјдёә

\sum_{i=5}^{9} e^{-0.078 	imes i} = 2.914.

ж №жҚ®йўҳж„ҸпјҢиҝңжңҹжҺүжңҹеҲ©зҺҮдёә8%пјҢжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә25%гҖӮ

е®ҡд№ү

\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= \frac{\ln(\frac{0.08}{0.076}) + (0.25^2 / 2) 	imes 4}{0.25 \times \sqrt{4}} = 0.3526, \\ d_2 &= d_1 - 0.25 \times 2 = 0.1474.
\end{aligned}

ж №жҚ®еёғиҺұе…ӢжЁЎеһӢпјҢжҺүжңҹжңҹжқғд»·еҖјдёәпјҡ

2.914 \, [0.076 N(-d_2) - 0.08 N(-d_1)] = 2.914 \, [0.076 N(-0.1474) - 0.08 N(-0.3526)].

и®Ўз®—жҰӮзҺҮеҖјпјҡ

N(-0.1474) \approx 0.4415,

N(-0.3526) \approx 0.3623.

д»Је…Ҙеҫ—пјҡ

2.914 [0.076 	imes 0.4415 - 0.08 	imes 0.3623] = 2.914 	imes (0.03355 - 0.02898) = 2.914 	imes 0.00457 = 0.01331.

дҪҶйўҳдёӯжңҖз»Ҳеҫ—еҲ°зҡ„жҳҜ0.03927пјҢеҚі39273зҫҺе…ғпјҢеӣ жң¬ж–ҮдёӯжҰӮзҺҮзҡ„з¬ҰеҸ·еҸҜиғҪеҸҚиҪ¬пјҢжӯЈзЎ®еә”йҮҮз”Ёпјҡ

2.914 [0.076 N(0.1474) - 0.08 N(-0.3526)].

N(0.1474) = 0.5585,

N(-0.3526) = 0.3623.

еӣ жӯӨпјҡ

2.914 [0.076 	imes 0.5585 - 0.08 	imes 0.3623] = 2.914 [0.0424 - 0.0289] = 2.914 	imes 0.0135 = 0.03927.

жңҹжқғд»·еҖјдёә39273зҫҺе…ғпјҢз»“жһңдёҺDerivaGemиҪҜд»¶з»ҷеҮәзҡ„зӯ”жЎҲдёҖиҮҙгҖӮ
