problems.promptEn: Practice 29.23. Use the DerivaGem software to value a 5-year collar that guarantees that the maximum and minimum interest rates on a LIBOR-based loan (with quarterly resets) are 7% and 5%, respectively. All 3-month LIBOR forward rates are 6% per annum (with quarterly compounding). The flat volatility is 20%. Assume that the principal is USD 100 and the risk-free (OIS) term structure is flat at 5.8%.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 29.23. дҪҝз”Ё DerivaGem иҪҜд»¶еҜ№ 5 е№ҙжңҹжңҹйҷҗиҝӣиЎҢдј°еҖјпјҢиҜҘжңҹйҷҗдҝқиҜҒеҹәдәҺ LIBOR зҡ„иҙ·ж¬ҫзҡ„жңҖй«ҳе’ҢжңҖдҪҺеҲ©зҺҮпјҲжҢүеӯЈеәҰйҮҚзҪ®пјүеҲҶеҲ«дёә 7% е’Ң 5%гҖӮжүҖжңү 3 дёӘжңҲ LIBOR иҝңжңҹеҲ©зҺҮеқҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ 6%пјҲжҢүеӯЈеәҰеӨҚеҲ©пјүгҖӮе№іеқҰжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә 20%гҖӮеҒҮи®ҫжң¬йҮ‘дёә 100 зҫҺе…ғпјҢж— йЈҺйҷ© (OIS) жңҹйҷҗз»“жһ„жҢҒе№ідәҺ 5.8%гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
29.23
Use the Caps and Swap Options worksheet of DerivaGem. To set the OIS zero curve as flat at
5.8% with continuous compounding, you need only enter 5.8% for one maturity. Similarly,
you only need to enter the LIBOR forward rate as 6% for one maturity. To value the cap,
select Cap/Floor as the Underlying Type, select BlackвҖ“European as the pricing model, enter
Quarterly for the Settlement Frequency, 100 for the Principal, 0 for the Start (Years), 5 for
the End (Years), 7% for the Cap/Floor Rate, and 20% for the Volatility. Check the Cap
button. Do not check the Imply Breakeven Rate, and Imply Volatility boxes. Clicking on
Calculate gives the value of the cap as 1.514. To value the floor change the Cap/Floor Rate to
5% and check the Floor button rather than the Cap button. Clicking on Calculate gives the

value as 1.116. The collar is a long position in the cap and a short position in the floor. The
value of the collar is therefore,

1.514 вҖ“ 1.116 = 0.398

Use the Caps and Swap Options worksheet of DerivaGem. To set the OIS zero curve as flat at
5.8% with continuous compounding, you need only enter 5.8% for one maturity. Similarly,

select Cap/Floor as the Underlying Type, select BlackвҖ“European as the pricing model, enter
Quarterly for the Settlement Frequency, 100 for the Principal, 0 for the Start (Years), 5 for

button. Do not check the Imply Breakeven Rate, and Imply Volatility boxes. Clicking on
Calculate gives the value of the cap as 1.514. To value the floor change the Cap/Floor Rate to
5% and check the Floor button rather than the Cap button. Clicking on Calculate gives the

value as 1.116. The collar is a long position in the cap and a short position in the floor. The
value of the collar is therefore,

1.514 вҖ“ 1.116 = 0.398

и®ҫзҪ®OISйӣ¶жҒҜжӣІзәҝдёә5.8%пјҲиҝһз»ӯеӨҚеҲ©пјүеӣәе®ҡеҲ©зҺҮж—¶пјҢд»…йңҖдёәдёҖдёӘжңҹйҷҗиҫ“е…Ҙ5.8%гҖӮеҗҢж ·пјҢиҝңжңҹLIBORеҲ©зҺҮдёә6%пјҲеӯЈеәҰеӨҚеҲ©пјүпјҢеҚіеҸӘйңҖдёәдёҖдёӘжңҹйҷҗиҫ“е…Ҙ6%гҖӮ

дј°еҖје°ҒйЎ¶еҲ©зҺҮпјҲcapпјүпјҡ

- йҖүжӢ©"BlackвҖ“European"дҪңдёәе®ҡд»·жЁЎеһӢгҖӮ

- жң¬йҮ‘иҫ“е…Ҙ100зҫҺе…ғгҖӮ

- дёҠйҷҗеҲ©зҺҮпјҲCap/Floor Rateпјүдёә7%гҖӮ

- йҖүдёӯвҖңCapвҖқжҢүй’®гҖӮ

зӮ№еҮ»"Calculate"и®Ўз®—еҫ—е°ҒйЎ¶еҲ©зҺҮжңҹжқғд»·ж јдёә1.514гҖӮ

дј°еҖје°Ғеә•еҲ©зҺҮпјҲfloorпјүпјҡ

- е°ҶCap/Floor Rateж”№дёә5%гҖӮ
- йҖүдёӯвҖңFloorвҖқжҢүй’®пјҢиҖҢдёҚжҳҜвҖңCapвҖқжҢүй’®гҖӮ

зӮ№еҮ»"Calculate"и®Ўз®—еҫ—е°Ғеә•еҲ©зҺҮжңҹжқғд»·ж јдёә1.116гҖӮ

еҗҲжҲҗеҲ©зҺҮеҘ—жңҹдҝқеҖјз»„еҗҲпјҲcollarпјүдёәд№°е…Ҙе°ҒйЎ¶жңҹжқғпјҢеҚ–еҮәе°Ғеә•жңҹжқғгҖӮ

еӣ жӯӨеҘ—жңҹдҝқеҖјз»„еҗҲд»·еҖјдёәпјҡ

1.514 - 1.116 = 0.398гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 29.24. Use the DerivaGem software to value a European swaption that gives you the right in 2 years to enter into a 5-year swap in which you pay a fixed rate of 6% and receive floating. Cash flows are exchanged semiannually on the swap. The continuously compounded 1-year, 2-year, 5-year, and 10-year risk-free (OIS) zero rates are 5%, 6%, 6.5%, and 7%, respectively. Assume a principal of USD 100. The forward swap rate is 7% (compounded semiannually) and its volatility is 15% per annum. Give an example of how the swaption might be used by a corporation. What bond option is equivalent to the swaption?

problems.promptEn: Practice 30.1. The payoff from a forward contract is delayed by two years. Explain how the impact of the delay on value is affected by the correlation between the price of the underlying asset and interest rates.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 30.1. иҝңжңҹеҗҲзәҰзҡ„ж”Ҝд»ҳе»¶иҝҹдёӨе№ҙгҖӮи§ЈйҮҠж Үзҡ„иө„дә§д»·ж јдёҺеҲ©зҺҮд№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„зӣёе…іжҖ§еҰӮдҪ•еҪұе“Қе»¶иҝҹеҜ№д»·еҖјзҡ„еҪұе“ҚгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
30.1
Suppose first that the correlation between the underlying asset price and interest rates is
negative and we have a long forward contract. When interest rates increase, there will be
a tendency for the asset price to decrease. The increase in interest rates means that an
investor would like a positive payoff to be early and a negative one to be late. The
negative correlation means that a negative payoff is more likely.
When interest rates decrease, there will be a tendency for the asset price to increase. The
decrease in interest rates means that an investor would like a positive payoff to be late and
a negative one to be early. The negative correlation means that a positive payoff is more
likely.
This argument shows that a negative correlation works in the investorвҖҷs favor. Similarly,
a positive correlation works against the investorвҖҷs interests.

problems.promptEn: Practice 30.5. What difference does it make in Problem 30.4 if the swap rate is observed in 5 years but the exchange of payments takes place in (a) 6 years, and (b) 7 years? Assume that the volatilities of all forward rates are 20% and also that the forward swap rate for the period between years 5 and 7 has a correlation of 0.8 with the forward interest rate between years 5 and 6 and a correlation of 0.95 with the forward interest rate between years 5 and 7.

problems.promptEn: Practice 30.6. The price of a bond at time T, measured in terms of its yield, is G1yT2. Assume geometric Brownian motion for the forward bond yield y in a world that is defined by a numeraire equal to a bond maturing at time T. Suppose that the growth rate of the forward bond yield is a and its volatility is sy: (a) Use ItГҙвҖҷs lemma to calculate the process for the forward bond price in terms of a, sy, y, and G(y). (b) The forward bond price should follow a martingale in the world considered. Use this fact to calculate an expression for a. (c) Show that the expression for a is, to a first approximation, consistent with equation (30.1).

problems.explanationZh:
30.6
(a) y зҡ„иҝҮзЁӢдёә

\[
dy = \alpha y dt + \sigma_y y dz
\]

иҝңжңҹеҖәеҲёд»·ж јдёә \( G(y) \)гҖӮж №жҚ® ItГҙ еј•зҗҶпјҢе…¶иҝҮзЁӢдёә

\[
dG(y) = \left[ \alpha y G'(y) + \frac{1}{2} \sigma_y^2 y^2 G''(y) \right] dt + \sigma_y y G'(y) dz
\]

(b) з”ұдәҺеңЁжүҖиҖғиҷ‘зҡ„дё–з•ҢдёӯиҝңжңҹеҖәеҲёд»·ж јеә”жңҚд»Һйһ…иҝҮзЁӢпјҢе…¶жңҹжңӣеўһй•ҝзҺҮдёәйӣ¶пјҢеҚі

\[
\alpha y G'(y) + \frac{1}{2} \sigma_y^2 y^2 G''(y) = 0
\]

ж•ҙзҗҶеҫ—

\[
\alpha = -\frac{1}{2} \sigma_y^2 y \frac{G''(y)}{G'(y)}
\]

(c) еҒҮи®ҫдҪңдёәиҝ‘дјјпјҢy е§Ӣз»ҲзӯүдәҺе…¶еҲқе§ӢеҖј \( y_0 \)пјҢеҲҷиҝңжңҹж”¶зӣҠзҺҮзҡ„еўһй•ҝзҺҮдёә

\[
\alpha = \frac{1}{2} \sigma_y^2 y_0 \left(-\frac{G''(y_0)}{G'(y_0)}\right)
\]

еҸҳйҮҸ y д»ҺеҲқе§ӢеҖј \( y_0 \) ејҖе§ӢпјҢз»ҲеҖјдёә \( y_T \)гҖӮеңЁд»ҘдәҺж—¶й—ҙ T еҲ°жңҹзҡ„йӣ¶жҒҜеҖәеҲёдёәи®Ўд»·еҚ•дҪҚзҡ„дё–з•ҢдёӯпјҢи®Ўз®— \( y_T \) зҡ„жңҹжңӣеҖјж—¶пјҢ\( y_0 \) зҡ„еҮёжҖ§и°ғж•ҙзәҰдёә

\[
\frac{1}{2} \sigma_y^2 y_0 T \left(-\frac{G''(y_0)}{G'(y_0)}\right)
\]

иҝҷдёҺж–№зЁӢ (30.1) дёҖйҳ¶иҝ‘дјјжҳҜдёҖиҮҙзҡ„гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 30.7. The variable S is an investment asset providing income at rate q measured in currency A. It follows the process dS = mSS dt + sSS dz in the real world. Defining new variables as necessary, give the process followed by S, and the corresponding market price of risk, in: (a) A world that is the traditional risk-neutral world for currency A (b) A world that is the traditional risk-neutral world for currency B (c) A world that is defined by a numeraire equal to a zero-coupon currency A bond maturing at time T (d) A world that is defined by a numeraire equal to a zero-coupon currency B bond maturing at time T.

problems.explanationZh:
30.7
(a) еңЁдј з»ҹйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§дё–з•ҢдёӯпјҢиө„дә§ S йҒөеҫӘзҡ„иҝҮзЁӢдёә

\[
dS = (r - q) S dt + \sigma_S S dz
\]

е…¶дёӯ r жҳҜеҚіж—¶ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮгҖӮ
еңЁиҜҘдё–з•ҢдёӯпјҢ\( dz \) йЈҺйҷ©зҡ„еёӮеңәд»·ж јдёәйӣ¶гҖӮ

(b) еңЁиҙ§еёҒ B зҡ„дј з»ҹйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§дё–з•ҢдёӯпјҢS зҡ„иҝҮзЁӢдёә

\[
dS = (r - q) S dt + \rho \, \sigma_Q \sigma_S S dz + \sigma_S S dz
\]

иҝҷйҮҢ Q жҳҜжұҮзҺҮпјҲеҚ•дҪҚдёә A/еҚ•дҪҚ BпјүпјҢ\( \sigma_Q \) жҳҜ Q зҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮпјҢ\( \rho \) жҳҜ Q е’Ң S д№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„зӣёе…ізі»ж•°гҖӮ\( dz \) йЈҺйҷ©зҡ„еёӮеңәд»·ж јжҳҜ \( \rho \sigma_Q \)гҖӮ

(c) еңЁд»ҘдәҺж—¶й—ҙ T еҲ°жңҹзҡ„иҙ§еёҒ A йӣ¶жҒҜеҖәеҲёдёәи®Ўд»·еҚ•дҪҚзҡ„дё–з•ҢдёӯпјҢS йҒөеҫӘзҡ„иҝҮзЁӢдёә

\[
dS = (r - q) S dt + \sigma_P S dz + \sigma_S S dz
\]

е…¶дёӯ \( \sigma_P \) жҳҜеҖәеҲёд»·ж јзҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮгҖӮ\( dz \) йЈҺйҷ©зҡ„еёӮеңәд»·ж јдёә \( \sigma_P \)гҖӮ

(d) еңЁд»ҘдәҺж—¶й—ҙ T еҲ°жңҹзҡ„иҙ§еёҒ B йӣ¶жҒҜеҖәеҲёдёәи®Ўд»·еҚ•дҪҚзҡ„дё–з•ҢдёӯпјҢS йҒөеҫӘзҡ„иҝҮзЁӢжҳҜ

\[
dS = (r - q) S dt + \sigma_P S dz + \rho \sigma_F \sigma_S S dz + \sigma_S S dz
\]

е…¶дёӯ F жҳҜиҝңжңҹжұҮзҺҮпјҢ\( \sigma_F \) жҳҜ F зҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮпјҲеҚ•дҪҚдёә A/еҚ•дҪҚ BпјүпјҢ\( \rho \) жҳҜ F е’Ң S д№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„зӣёе…ізі»ж•°гҖӮ\( dz \) йЈҺйҷ©зҡ„еёӮеңәд»·ж јдёә

\[
\sigma_P + \rho \sigma_F
\]гҖӮ

30.8
е®ҡд№үпјҡ
\[
P(t,T)\]: ж—¶й—ҙ t з”Ёж—Ҙе…ғи®Ўд»·гҖҒж”Ҝд»ҳж—¶й—ҙ T ж—¶ 1 ж—Ҙе…ғзҡ„еҖәеҲёд»·ж ј
\[
E_T(\cdot)\]: д»Ҙд»Ҙ P(t,T) и®Ўд»·еҚ•дҪҚе®ҡд№үзҡ„дё–з•Ңдёӯзҡ„жңҹжңӣ
\[
F\]: й»„йҮ‘д»ҘзҫҺе…ғи®Ўзҡ„иҝңжңҹд»·ж јпјҢеҲ°жңҹж—¶й—ҙдёә T
\[
F_0\]: F еңЁж—¶й—ҙйӣ¶зҡ„еҖј
\[
\sigma_F\]: F зҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮ
\[
G\]: иҝңжңҹжұҮзҺҮпјҲзҫҺе…ғе…‘ж—Ҙе…ғпјү
\[
\sigma_G\]: G зҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮ

еҒҮи®ҫ \( S_T \) жңҚд»ҺеҜ№ж•°жӯЈжҖҒеҲҶеёғгҖӮжҲ‘д»¬еҸҜеңЁд»ҘйқһиҙҙзҺ°зҫҺе…ғиҝңжңҹеҖәеҲё \( P(t,T) \) и®Ўд»·зҡ„дё–з•Ңдёӯи®Ўз®—иҜҘзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјпјҡ

\[
\text{Call Value} = P(0,T) \left[ F_0 N(d_1) - K N(d_2) \right]
\]

е…¶дёӯ

\[
d_1 = \frac{\ln(F_0 / K) + \frac{1}{2} \sigma_F^2 T}{\sigma_F \sqrt{T}}, \quad d_2 = d_1 - \sigma_F \sqrt{T}
\]

з”ұдәҺдё–з•Ңд»Ҙйӣ¶жҒҜзҫҺе…ғеҖәеҲёдёәи®Ўд»·еҚ•дҪҚпјҢй»„йҮ‘зҡ„жңҹжңӣд»·ж јдёә \( F_0 \)гҖӮж №жҚ®е…¬ејҸ (30.6)пјҢеёҰе…ҘиҖғиҷ‘зҫҺе…ғе…‘ж—Ҙе…ғиҝңжңҹжұҮзҺҮзҡ„зӣёе…іжҖ§дҝ®жӯЈпјҢжңү

\[
E_T[S_T] = F_0 (1 + \rho \sigma_G \sigma_F T)
\]

еӣ жӯӨпјҢд»Ҙж—Ҙе…ғи®Ўд»·зҡ„жңҹжқғд»·ж јдёә

\[
\text{Call Value} = P(0,T) \left[ F_0 (1 + \rho \sigma_G \sigma_F T) N(d_1') - K N(d_2') \right]
\]

е…¶дёӯ

\[
d_1' = \frac{\ln \left( \frac{F_0 (1 + \rho \sigma_G \sigma_F T)}{K} \right) + \frac{1}{2} \sigma_F^2 T}{\sigma_F \sqrt{T}}, \quad d_2' = d_1' - \sigma_F \sqrt{T}
\]

иҝҷе°ұжҳҜиҖғиҷ‘иҝңжңҹжұҮзҺҮзӣёе…іжҖ§зҡ„еҗҲзәҰд»·еҖји®Ўз®—иЎЁиҫҫејҸгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 30.9. A Canadian equity index is 400. The Canadian dollar is currently worth 0.70 U.S. dollars. The risk-free interest rates in Canada and the U.S. are constant at 6% and 4%, respectively. The dividend yield on the index is 3%. Define Q as the number of Canadian dollars per U.S. dollar and S as the value of the index. The volatility of S is 20%, the volatility of Q is 6%, and the correlation between S and Q is 0.4. Use DerivaGem to determine the value of a 2-year American-style call option on the index if: (a) It pays off in Canadian dollars the amount by which the index exceeds 400. (b) It pays off in U.S. dollars the amount by which the index exceeds 400.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 30.9. еҠ жӢҝеӨ§иӮЎзҘЁжҢҮж•°дёә 400гҖӮеҠ е…ғзӣ®еүҚд»·еҖј 0.70 зҫҺе…ғгҖӮеҠ жӢҝеӨ§е’ҢзҫҺеӣҪзҡ„ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮеҲҶеҲ«жҒ’е®ҡеңЁ 6% е’Ң 4%гҖӮиҜҘжҢҮж•°зҡ„иӮЎжҒҜж”¶зӣҠзҺҮдёә3%гҖӮе°Ҷ Q е®ҡд№үдёәжҜҸзҫҺе…ғе…‘жҚўзҡ„еҠ е…ғж•°йҮҸпјҢе°Ҷ S е®ҡд№үдёәжҢҮж•°еҖјгҖӮ Sзҡ„жіўеҠЁжҖ§дёә20%пјҢQзҡ„жіўеҠЁжҖ§дёә6%пјҢSдёҺQзҡ„зӣёе…іжҖ§дёә0.4гҖӮдҪҝз”Ё DerivaGem зЎ®е®ҡиҜҘжҢҮж•°зҡ„ 2 е№ҙжңҹзҫҺејҸзңӢж¶Ёжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјпјҢеҰӮжһңпјҡ (a) еҪ“жҢҮж•°и¶…иҝҮ 400 ж—¶пјҢд»ҘеҠ е…ғж”Ҝд»ҳгҖӮ (b) еҪ“жҢҮж•°и¶…иҝҮ 400 ж—¶пјҢд»ҘзҫҺе…ғж”Ҝд»ҳгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 30.10. Consider an instrument that will pay off S dollars in 2 years, where S is the value of the Nikkei index. The index is currently 20,000. The yen/dollar exchange rate is 100 (yen per dollar). The correlation between the exchange rate and the index is 0.3 and the dividend yield on the index is 1% per annum. The volatility of the Nikkei index is 20% and the volatility of the yen/dollar exchange rate is 12%. The interest rates (assumed constant) in the U.S. and Japan are 4% and 2%, respectively. (a) What is the value of the instrument? (b) Suppose that the exchange rate at some point during the life of the instrument is Q and the level of the index is S. Show that a U.S. investor can create a portfolio that changes in value by approximately вҲҶS dollar when the index changes in value by вҲҶS yen by investing S dollars in the Nikkei and shorting SQ yen. (c) Confirm that this is correct by supposing that the index changes from 20,000 to 20,050 and the exchange rate changes from 100 to 99.7. (d) How would you delta hedge the instrument under consideration?

30.11
дёәи®Ўз®—дә”е№ҙеҲ©зҺҮзҡ„еҮёжҖ§и°ғж•ҙпјҢе®ҡд№үдә”е№ҙеҖәеҲёд»·ж јдҪңдёәе…¶ж”¶зӣҠзҺҮзҡ„еҮҪж•°

G_5(y) = e^{-5y}пјҢ

е…¶дёҖйҳ¶еҜјж•°дёә

G_5'(y) = -5 e^{-5y}пјҢ

дәҢйҳ¶еҜјж•°дёә

G_5''(y) = 25 e^{-5y}гҖӮ

еҮёжҖ§и°ғж•ҙдёә

\frac{1}{2} \times \sigma^2 \times T^2 \times \left( -\frac{G_5''(y)}{G_5(y)} \right) = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.25^2 \times 4^2 \times (5^2) = 0.004гҖӮ

зұ»дјјең°пјҢдәҢе№ҙеҲ©зҺҮзҡ„еҮёжҖ§и°ғж•ҙдёә

\frac{1}{2} \times 0.25^2 \times 4^2 \times (2^2) = 0.0016гҖӮ

еӣ жӯӨпјҢжҲ‘д»¬еҸҜд»ҘеҒҮи®ҫдә”е№ҙеҲ©зҺҮдёә8.4%пјҢдәҢе№ҙеҲ©зҺҮжҳҜ8.16%гҖӮиЎҚз”ҹе“Ғзҡ„д»·еҖјдёә

e^{-0.08 \times 4} \times (0.084 - 0.0816) = 0.174гҖӮ

еҰӮжһңеӣһжҠҘеҸ‘з”ҹеңЁдә”е№ҙиҖҢйқһеӣӣе№ҙпјҢеҲҷйңҖиҰҒи®Ўж—¶и°ғж•ҙгҖӮж №жҚ®зӯүејҸ(30.4)пјҢиҝҷж¶үеҸҠе°ҶиҝңжңҹеҲ©зҺҮд№ҳд»Ҙ

\exp\left(-0.25 \times 0.25 \times 0.08 \times 4 \times 1\right) = 0.98165гҖӮ

еӣ жӯӨпјҢиЎҚз”ҹе“Ғд»·еҖјдёә

0.24 \times 0.98165 \times e^{-0.08 \times 5} = 0.158гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 30.12. Suppose that the payoff from a derivative will occur in 10 years and will equal the 3-year U.S. dollar swap rate for a semiannual-pay swap observed at that time applied to a certain principal. Assume that the swap yield curve is flat at 8% (semiannually compounded) per annum in dollars and 3% (semiannually compounded) in yen. The forward swap rate volatility is 18%, the volatility of the 10-year вҖңyen per dollarвҖқ forward exchange rate is 12%, and the correlation between this exchange rate and U.S. dollar interest rates is 0.25. What is the value of the derivative if the swap rate is applied to a principal of (a) USD 100 million with a dollar payoff and (b) 100 million yen with a yen payoff? Assume that risk-free rates are 2% in yen and 6% in dollars (both semiannually compounded).
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 30.12. еҒҮи®ҫиЎҚз”ҹе“Ғзҡ„ж”¶зӣҠе°ҶеңЁ 10 е№ҙеҶ…еҸ‘з”ҹпјҢе№¶дё”зӯүдәҺеҪ“ж—¶и§ӮеҜҹеҲ°зҡ„еә”з”ЁдәҺзү№е®ҡжң¬йҮ‘зҡ„еҚҠе№ҙж”Ҝд»ҳдә’жҚўзҡ„ 3 е№ҙжңҹзҫҺе…ғдә’жҚўеҲ©зҺҮгҖӮеҒҮи®ҫжҺүжңҹж”¶зӣҠзҺҮжӣІзәҝе№іеқҰпјҢзҫҺе…ғе№ҙеҲ©зҺҮдёә 8%пјҲеҚҠе№ҙеӨҚеҲ©пјүпјҢж—Ҙе…ғе№ҙеҲ©зҺҮдёә 3%пјҲеҚҠе№ҙеӨҚеҲ©пјүгҖӮиҝңжңҹжҺүжңҹжұҮзҺҮжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә18%пјҢ10е№ҙжңҹвҖңж—Ҙе…ғе…‘зҫҺе…ғвҖқиҝңжңҹжұҮзҺҮжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә12%пјҢиҜҘжұҮзҺҮдёҺзҫҺе…ғеҲ©зҺҮзҡ„зӣёе…іжҖ§дёә0.25гҖӮеҰӮжһңжҺүжңҹеҲ©зҺҮйҖӮз”ЁдәҺ (a) 1 дәҝзҫҺе…ғдё”д»ҘзҫҺе…ғж”Ҝд»ҳзҡ„жң¬йҮ‘е’Ң (b) 1 дәҝж—Ҙе…ғд»Ҙж—Ҙе…ғж”Ҝд»ҳзҡ„жң¬йҮ‘пјҢеҲҷиЎҚз”ҹе“Ғзҡ„д»·еҖјжҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹеҒҮи®ҫж—Ҙе…ғж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёә 2%пјҢзҫҺе…ғж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёә 6%пјҲеқҮдёәеҚҠе№ҙеӨҚеҲ©пјүгҖӮ 718 з¬¬дёүеҚҒз«

problems.explanationZh:
30.12
(a) еңЁиҝҷз§Қжғ…еҶөдёӢпјҢжҲ‘д»¬еҝ…йЎ»еҜ№иҝңжңҹдә’жҚўеҲ©зҺҮеҒҡдёҖдёӘеҮёжҖ§и°ғж•ҙгҖӮ
е®ҡд№ү

\[
G(y) = \sum_{i=1}^6 \frac{1}{(1 + \frac{y}{2})^i} = 262.11
\]

йӮЈд№Ҳ

\[
G'(y) = \frac{d}{dy} G(y) = -\sum_{i=1}^6 \frac{i}{2} \frac{1}{(1 + \frac{y}{2})^{i+1}} = -853.29
\]

еҶҚиҝӣдёҖжӯҘпјҢ

\[
G''(y) = \frac{d^2}{dy^2} G(y) = \sum_{i=1}^6 \frac{i(i+1)}{4} \frac{1}{(1 + \frac{y}{2})^{i+2}} = 1050
\]

з»ҷе®ҡе№ҙеҲ©зҺҮдёә0.08пјҲеҚҠе№ҙеӨҚеҲ©пјүпјҢжҲ‘д»¬еҫ—еҮә

\[
G' = -262.11, \quad G'' = 853.29
\]

еҮёжҖ§и°ғж•ҙз”ұд»ҘдёӢиЎЁиҫҫејҸз»ҷеҮәпјҡ

\[
\text{convexity adjustment} = \frac{1}{2} \sigma_x^2 \frac{G''(y)}{G'(y)} \times T = \frac{1}{2} \times (0.18)^2 \times \frac{853.29}{262.11} \times 10 = 0.00338
\]

еӣ жӯӨи°ғж•ҙеҗҺзҡ„иҝңжңҹдә’жҚўеҲ©зҺҮдёәпјҡ

\[
0.08 + 0.00338 = 0.08338
\]

иЎҚз”ҹе“Ғд»ҘзҷҫдёҮзҫҺе…ғи®Ўзҡ„д»·еҖјдёәпјҡ

\[
100 \times 0.08338 / 1.03 = 4.617 \text{ зҷҫдёҮзҫҺе…ғ}
\]

(b)

еҪ“дә’жҚўеҲ©зҺҮеә”з”ЁдәҺд»Ҙж—Ҙе…ғи®Ўд»·зҡ„жң¬йҮ‘ж—¶пјҢйҷӨдәҶеҮёжҖ§и°ғж•ҙеӨ–пјҢиҝҳеҝ…йЎ»иҝӣиЎҢquantoи°ғж•ҙгҖӮж №жҚ®з¬¬30.3иҠӮпјҢquantoи°ғж•ҙдёәе°Ҷиҝңжңҹдә’жҚўеҲ©зҺҮд№ҳд»Ҙ

\[
\exp\left(-\rho \sigma_{FX} \sigma_{swap} T\right) = \exp\left(-0.25 \times 0.12 \times 0.18 \times 10\right) = 0.9474
\]

пјҲжіЁж„ҸиҝҷйҮҢзҡ„зӣёе…ізі»ж•°жҳҜзҫҺе…ғе…‘ж—Ҙе…ғжұҮзҺҮдёҺдә’жҚўеҲ©зҺҮд№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„зӣёе…іжҖ§пјҢеӣ жӯӨжҳҜ-0.25пјҢиҖҢйқһ+0.25гҖӮпјү

иЎҚз”ҹе“Ғд»ҘзҷҫдёҮж—Ҙе…ғи®Ўзҡ„д»·еҖјдёәпјҡ

\[
100 \times 0.08338 \times 0.9474 / 1.01 = 6.474 \text{ зҷҫдёҮж—Ҙе…ғ}
\]

problems.promptEn: Practice 31.1. Suppose that the short rate is currently 4% and its standard deviation in a short period of time вҲҶt is 0.012вҲҶt. What happens to this standard deviation when the short rate increases to 8% in (a) VasicekвҖҷs model, (b) Rendleman and BartterвҖҷs model, and (c) the Cox, Ingersoll, and Ross model?

tags: Equilibrium Models of the Short Rate
problems.promptEn: Practice 31.2. If a stock price were mean reverting or followed a path-dependent process there would be market inefficiency. Why is there not a market inefficiency when the short-term interest rate does so?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 31.2. еҰӮжһңиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јеқҮеҖјеӣһеҪ’жҲ–йҒөеҫӘи·Ҝеҫ„дҫқиө–иҝҮзЁӢпјҢе°ұдјҡеҮәзҺ°еёӮеңәж•ҲзҺҮдҪҺдёӢзҡ„жғ…еҶөгҖӮеҪ“зҹӯжңҹеҲ©зҺҮеҰӮжӯӨж—¶пјҢдёәд»Җд№ҲдёҚдјҡеҮәзҺ°еёӮеңәж— ж•ҲзҺҮе‘ўпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
31.2.
If the price of a traded security followed a mean-reverting or path-dependent process, there
would be market inefficiency. The short-term interest rate is not the price of a traded security.
In other words, we cannot trade something whose price is always the short-term interest rate.
There is therefore no market inefficiency when the short-term interest rate follows a mean-
reverting or path-dependent process. We can trade bonds and other instruments whose prices
do depend on the short rate. The prices of these instruments do not follow mean-reverting or
path-dependent processes.
problems.explanationEn:
31.2.
If the price of a traded security followed a mean-reverting or path-dependent process, there
would be market inefficiency. The short-term interest rate is not the price of a traded security.
In other words, we cannot trade something whose price is always the short-term interest rate.
There is therefore no market inefficiency when the short-term interest rate follows a mean-
reverting or path-dependent process. We can trade bonds and other instruments whose prices
do depend on the short rate. The prices of these instruments do not follow mean-reverting or
path-dependent processes.
problems.explanationZh:
31.2
еҰӮжһңдәӨжҳ“иҜҒеҲёзҡ„д»·ж јйҒөеҫӘеқҮеҖјеӣһеҪ’жҲ–и·Ҝеҫ„дҫқиө–иҝҮзЁӢпјҢйӮЈд№ҲеӯҳеңЁеёӮеңәж— ж•ҲзҺҮзҡ„еҸҜиғҪжҖ§гҖӮзҹӯжңҹеҲ©зҺҮдёҚжҳҜжҹҗз§ҚеҸҜдәӨжҳ“иҜҒеҲёзҡ„д»·ж јпјҢжҚўеҸҘиҜқиҜҙпјҢжҲ‘д»¬дёҚиғҪдәӨжҳ“дёҖдёӘд»·ж јжҒ’зӯүдәҺзҹӯжңҹеҲ©зҺҮзҡ„иө„дә§гҖӮеӣ жӯӨеҪ“зҹӯжңҹеҲ©зҺҮйҒөеҫӘеқҮеҖјеӣһеҪ’жҲ–и·Ҝеҫ„дҫқиө–иҝҮзЁӢж—¶пјҢдёҚдјҡеҮәзҺ°еёӮеңәж— ж•ҲзҺҮгҖӮжҲ‘д»¬еҸҜд»ҘдәӨжҳ“йӮЈдәӣд»·ж јдҫқиө–дәҺзҹӯжңҹеҲ©зҺҮзҡ„еҖәеҲёе’Ңе…¶д»–е·Ҙе…·пјҢиҖҢиҝҷдәӣе·Ҙе…·зҡ„д»·ж је№¶дёҚйҒөеҫӘеқҮеҖјеӣһеҪ’жҲ–и·Ҝеҫ„дҫқиө–иҝҮзЁӢгҖӮ

tags: Equilibrium Models of the Short Rate
problems.promptEn: Practice 31.3. Explain the difference between a one-factor and a two-factor model.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 31.3. и§ЈйҮҠеҚ•еӣ зҙ жЁЎеһӢе’ҢеҸҢеӣ зҙ жЁЎеһӢд№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„еҢәеҲ«гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
31.3
In a one-factor model, there is one source of uncertainty driving all rates. This usually means
that in any short period of time all rates move in the same direction (but not necessarily by the
same amount). In a two-factor model, there are two sources of uncertainty driving all rates. The
first source of uncertainty usually gives rise to a roughly parallel shift in rates. The second
gives rise to a twist where long and short rates moves in opposite directions.
problems.explanationEn:

first source of uncertainty usually gives rise to a roughly parallel shift in rates. The second
gives rise to a twist where long and short rates moves in opposite directions.

problems.promptEn: Practice 31.4. Suppose that in a risk-neutral world the Vasicek parameters are a = 0.1, b = 0.03, and s = 0.01. What is the price of a 5-year zero-coupon bond with a principal of USD 1 when the short rate is 2%?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 31.4. еҒҮи®ҫеңЁйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§дё–з•ҢдёӯпјҢVasicek еҸӮж•°дёә a = 0.1гҖҒb = 0.03 е’Ң s = 0.01гҖӮеҪ“зҹӯжңҹеҲ©зҺҮдёә 2% ж—¶пјҢжң¬йҮ‘дёә 1 зҫҺе…ғзҡ„ 5 е№ҙжңҹйӣ¶жҒҜеҖәеҲёзҡ„д»·ж јжҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanationZh:
31.4

еңЁйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§дё–з•ҢдёӯпјҢVasicekжЁЎеһӢзҡ„еҸӮж•°дёә a=0.1пјҢb=0.03пјҢПғ=0.01гҖӮеҪ“еүҚзҹӯжңҹеҲ©зҺҮ r=2%гҖӮ

йӣ¶жҒҜеҖәеҲёд»·ж је…¬ејҸдёә

\[ P(0,T) = A(0,T) \cdot e^{-B(0,T) r(0)} \]

е…¶дёӯ

\[ B(0,T) = \frac{1 - e^{-aT}}{a} \]

\[ A(0,T) = \exp \left( \left( b - \frac{\sigma^2}{2a^2} \right) (B(0,T) - T) - \frac{\sigma^2}{4a} B(0,T)^2 \right) \]

и®Ўз®—пјҡ
\[ B(0,5) = \frac{1 - e^{-0.1 \times 5}}{0.1} = 4.3295 \]
\[ A(0,5) = \exp \left( (0.03 - \frac{0.01^2}{2 \times 0.1^2})(4.3295 - 5) - \frac{0.01^2}{4 \times 0.1} (4.3295)^2 \right) = 0.9347 \]

еӣ жӯӨпјҢ5е№ҙжңҹйӣ¶жҒҜеҖәеҲёзҡ„д»·ж јдёә

\[ P(0,5) = 0.9347 \times e^{-4.3295 \times 0.02} = 0.8966 \]

еҚіжң¬йҮ‘дёә1зҫҺе…ғзҡ„5е№ҙжңҹйӣ¶жҒҜеҖәеҲёзҡ„д»·ж јжҳҜ0.8966зҫҺе…ғгҖӮ

problems.explanationZh:
31.5

з»ҷе®ҡCIRжЁЎеһӢеҸӮж•°дёә a=0.1пјҢb=0.03пјҢПғ=0.07пјҢеҲ©зҺҮйЈҺйҷ©еёӮд»·дёә -1 д№ҳд»ҘзҹӯжңҹеҲ©зҺҮзҡ„е№іж–№ж №гҖӮ

(a) зҹӯжңҹеҲ©зҺҮзҡ„йЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§иҝҮзЁӢдёәпјҡ

\[ dr = a(b - r) dt + \sigma \sqrt{r} dz = 0.1(0.03 - r) dt + 0.07 \sqrt{r} dz \]

зңҹе®һдё–з•ҢиҝҮзЁӢдёәпјҡ

еёӮеңәеҲ©зҺҮйЈҺйҷ©д»·ж јдёә \( \lambda = -1 \times \sqrt{r} \)пјҢеҲҷжјӮз§»и°ғж•ҙдёәпјҡ

\[ dr = a(b - r) dt + \sigma \lambda dt + \sigma \sqrt{r} dz = [0.1(0.03 - r) + 0.07 \times (-1) \times \sqrt{r} ] dt + 0.07 \sqrt{r} dz \]

еҸҜж•ҙзҗҶдёәпјҡ

\[ dr = [0.1(0.03 - r) - 0.07 \sqrt{r}] dt + 0.07 \sqrt{r} dz \]

(b) еҪ“еүҚеү©дҪҷжңҹйҷҗдёә4е№ҙзҡ„йӣ¶жҒҜеҖәеҲёзҡ„йЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§иҝҮзЁӢдёәпјҡ

\[ dP(t,4) = r P(t,4) dt + \sigma B(t,4) P(t,4) dz \]

е…¶дёӯ \( B(t,4) \) ж»Ўи¶іпјҡ

\[ \frac{\partial B(t,T)}{\partial t} = 1 - a B(t,T) + \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 B(t,T)^2 \quad \text{дё”} \quad B(T,T) = 0 \]

е…·дҪ“еҪўејҸдёәпјҡ

\[ B(t,4) = \frac{2(e^{\gamma (4 - t)} - 1)}{(\gamma + a)(e^{\gamma (4 - t)} - 1) + 2 \gamma} \]

\[ \gamma = \sqrt{a^2 + 2 \sigma^2} = \sqrt{(0.1)^2 + 2 (0.07)^2} = 0.1407 \]

еңЁзңҹе®һдё–з•ҢдёӯпјҢз”ұдәҺеҲ©зҺҮйЈҺйҷ©д»·ж јпјҢйӣ¶жҒҜеҖәеҲёиҝҮзЁӢзҡ„жјӮз§»и°ғж•ҙдёәпјҡ

\[ dP(t,4) = [r + \lambda \sigma B(t,4)] P(t,4) dt + \sigma B(t,4) P(t,4) dz \]

иҝҷйҮҢ \( \lambda = -1 \times \sqrt{r} \)пјҢеӣ жӯӨзңҹе®һдё–з•Ңзҡ„йӣ¶жҒҜеҖәеҲёиҝҮзЁӢзҡ„жјӮз§»дёәйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§иҝҮзЁӢеҠ дёҠ \( \lambda \sigma B(t,4) \) йЎ№зҡ„и°ғж•ҙгҖӮ

problems.explanationZh:
31.6
зҹӯжңҹеҲ©зҺҮзҡ„йЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§иҝҮзЁӢдёә
\[
dr = a(b - r)dt + \sigma dz
\]
зҺ°е®һдё–з•ҢиҝҮзЁӢдёә
\[
dr = [a(b - r) + \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 r] dt + \sigma dz
\]
е…¶дёӯпјҢ\( \lambda = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 r \)пјҢдё”\( \lambda_1 \)е’Ң\( \lambda_2 \)дёәиҙҹгҖӮ

е°ҶйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§жөӢеәҰдёӢзҡ„жјӮз§»йЎ№иЎЁиҫҫдёә
\[
dr = (a(b - r) - \lambda \sigma) dt + \sigma dz = (a(b - r) - (\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 r) \sigma) dt + \sigma dz
\]

ж•ҙзҗҶеҗҺеҫ—
\[
dr = [a b - a r - \lambda_1 \sigma - \lambda_2 r \sigma] dt + \sigma dz = [a b - \lambda_1 \sigma - (a + \lambda_2 \sigma) r] dt + \sigma dz
\]

дәҺжҳҜйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§иҝҮзЁӢд»Қз„¶дҝқжҢҒVasicekеҪўејҸпјҡ
- еӣһеҪ’йҖҹеәҰдёә \( a^* = a + \lambda_2 \sigma \)
- еӣһеҪ’ж°ҙе№ідёә \( b^* = \frac{a b - \lambda_1 \sigma}{a^*} \)

з»јдёҠпјҡ
(a) йЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§еӣһеҪ’зҺҮ \( a^* = a - \lambda_2 \sigma \) пјҲеӣ  \( \lambda_2 < 0 \), жіЁж„Ҹз¬ҰеҸ·пјү

(b) йЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§еӣһеҪ’ж°ҙе№і \( b^* = \frac{a b + \lambda_1 \sigma}{a^*} \)

иҝҷиЎЁжҳҺйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§иҝҮзЁӢдёҺзҺ°е®һдё–з•ҢиҝҮзЁӢе…·жңүзӣёеҗҢзҡ„еҮҪж•°еҪўејҸпјҢд»…еҸӮж•°еҸ‘з”ҹи°ғж•ҙгҖӮ

problems.explanationZh:
31.7
и®ҫеҜ№зҹӯжңҹеҲ©зҺҮзҡ„з¬¬ i ж¬Ўи§ӮжөӢдёә \(r_i\)пјҢж—¶й—ҙй—ҙйҡ”дёә \(\Delta t\)гҖӮеңЁ Vasicek жЁЎеһӢдёӢпјҢеўһйҮҸ \(r_i - r_{i-1}\) жңҚд»ҺеқҮеҖјдёә \(a(b^* - r_{i-1}) \Delta t\)пјҢж–№е·®дёә \( \sigma^2 \Delta t \) зҡ„жӯЈжҖҒеҲҶеёғгҖӮ

иҜҘи§ӮжөӢзҡ„жҰӮзҺҮеҜҶеәҰеҮҪж•°дёәпјҡ
\[
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma^2 \Delta t}} \exp\left[-\frac{(r_i - r_{i-1} - a(b^* - r_{i-1}) \Delta t)^2}{2 \sigma^2 \Delta t}\right]
\]

еҜ№жүҖжңүи§ӮжөӢеҖјеҸ–иҒ”еҗҲжҰӮзҺҮеҜҶеәҰеҮҪж•°д№ҳз§ҜпјҢе№¶еҸ–еҜ№ж•°пјҢеҫ—еҲ°еҜ№ж•°дјјз„¶еҮҪж•°пјҡ
\[
\sum_{i=1}^m \left[ -\frac{1}{2} \ln (2 \pi \sigma^2 \Delta t) - \frac{(r_i - r_{i-1} - a(b^* - r_{i-1}) \Delta t)^2}{2 \sigma^2 \Delta t} \right]
\]

жңҖеӨ§дјјз„¶дј°и®ЎеҚідёәдҪҝиҜҘеҜ№ж•°дјјз„¶еҮҪж•°жңҖеӨ§еҢ–зҡ„еҸӮж•° \(a\)гҖҒ\(b^*\)гҖҒ\(\sigma\)гҖӮ

еӣ жӯӨпјҢжңҖеӨ§дјјз„¶дј°и®Ўй—®йўҳиҪ¬еҢ–дёәжңҖеӨ§еҢ–иЎЁиҫҫејҸпјҡ
\[
\sum_{i=1}^m \left[ -\ln(\sigma \sqrt{\Delta t}) - \frac{(r_i - r_{i-1} - a(b^* - r_{i-1}) \Delta t)^2}{2 \sigma^2 \Delta t} \right]
\]

иҝҷдёҺйўҳзӣ®дёӯз»ҷеҮәзҡ„жұӮи§ЈиЎЁиҫҫејҸзӣёз¬ҰпјҢиҜҙжҳҺдәҶжұӮеҸӮж•°зҡ„жңҖеӨ§дјјз„¶и§ЈеҚіеҜ№еә”жңҖеӨ§еҢ–иҜҘиЎЁиҫҫејҸгҖӮ

B(t, t+10) = \frac{1 - e^{-a( T - t )}}{a} = \frac{1 - e^{-0.1 \times 10}}{0.1} = 6.32121гҖӮ

е№¶дё”

A(t, t+10)= \exp\left( (b - \frac{\sigma^2}{2a^2}) (B(t, t+10) - (T-t)) - \frac{\sigma^2}{4a} B^2(t, t+10) \right)
= \exp\left( 0.1 \times (6.32121 -10) - 0.0004 \times 6.32121^2 \right)
= 0.71587гҖӮ

еӣ жӯӨпјҢеҖәеҲёд»·ж јдёә
\exp\left(-B(t, t+10) \times r_t \right) \times A(t, t+10) = e^{-6.32121 \times 0.1} \times 0.71587 = 0.38046гҖӮ

еңЁ Cox, Ingersoll е’Ң Ross (CIR) жЁЎеһӢдёӯпјҢ
0.1 = aпјҢ
0.1 = bпјҢ
sigma = 0.02 \sqrt{r}гҖӮ
йҰ–е…ҲпјҢи®Ўз®—
\gamma = \sqrt{a^2 + 2 \sigma^2} = \sqrt{0.1^2 + 2 \times 0.02^2} = 0.13416гҖӮ

е®ҡд№ү

\beta = \frac{a + \gamma}{\sigma^2} = \frac{0.1 + 0.13416}{0.02^2} = 583.75пјҢ

и®Ўз®—

B(t, t+10) = \frac{2 (e^{\gamma (T-t)} - 1)}{(\gamma + a)(e^{\gamma (T-t)} -1) + 2 \gamma} = 6.07650пјҢ

и®Ўз®—

A(t, t+10) = \left( \frac{2 \gamma e^{0.5(\gamma + a)(T-t)}}{(\gamma + a)(e^{\gamma(T-t)} -1) + 2 \gamma} \right)^{2ab/\sigma^2} = 0.69746гҖӮ

еӣ жӯӨпјҢеҖәеҲёд»·ж јдёә
0.69746 \times e^{-6.07650 \times 0.1} = 0.37986гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 31.10. Suppose the short rate r is 4% and its real-world process is dr = 0.110.05 - r2 dt + 0.01 dz, while the risk-neutral process is dr = 0.110.11 - r2 dt + 0.01 dz: (a) What is the market price of interest rate risk? (b) What is the expected return and volatility for a 5-year zero-coupon bond in the risk- neutral world? (c) What is the expected return and volatility for a 5-year zero-coupon bond in the real world?

problems.explanationZh:
31.10
(a) зҹӯжңҹеҲ©зҺҮ r зҡ„йЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§иҝҮзЁӢзӣёжҜ”зңҹе®һиҝҮзЁӢзҡ„жјӮз§»зҺҮй«ҳеҮә 0.006/rпјҢжіўеҠЁзҺҮжҳҜ 0.01/rгҖӮиҝҷж„Ҹе‘ізқҖеҲ©зҺҮйЈҺйҷ©зҡ„еёӮеңәд»·ж јдёә
-0.006/0.01 = -0.6гҖӮ

(b) еңЁйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§дё–з•ҢдёӯпјҢеҖәеҲёзҡ„йў„жңҹеӣһжҠҘзҺҮзӯүдәҺж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮ 4%гҖӮжіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә 0.01Г—B(0,5)пјҢе…¶дёӯ

B(0,5) = \frac{1 - e^{-5 \times 0.1}}{0.1} = 3.935гҖӮ

еӣ жӯӨпјҢжіўеҠЁзҺҮжҳҜ 3.935%гҖӮ

(c) еңЁйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§дё–з•ҢпјҢеҖәеҲёд»·ж јиҝҮзЁӢдёә
\frac{dP}{P} = 0.03935 dz - 0.04 dtгҖӮ

иҝҷйҮҢ dz йЎ№зі»ж•°дёәиҙҹиЎЁжҳҺеҖәеҲёд»·ж јдёҺеҲ©зҺҮе‘Ҳиҙҹзӣёе…ігҖӮеҪ“иҪ¬еҲ°зңҹе®һдё–з•Ңж—¶пјҢеӣһжҠҘзҺҮйҖҡиҝҮеёӮеңәд»·ж јд№ҳд»Ҙ dz йЈҺйҷ©зҡ„д№ҳз§ҜеўһеҠ пјҢеҚі

\frac{dP}{P} = 0.03935 dz + [0.04 - (-0.6) \times 0.03935] dt = 0.03935 dz + 0.06361 dtгҖӮ

еӣ жӯӨпјҢеҖәеҲёзҡ„йў„жңҹеӣһжҠҘзҺҮд»ҺйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§дё–з•Ңзҡ„ 4% дёҠеҚҮиҮізҺ°е®һдё–з•Ңзҡ„ 6.361%гҖӮ

31.11
(a) еҜ№дәҺ Vasicek е’Ң CIR жЁЎеһӢпјҢP(t,T) еҜ№ r зҡ„дәҢйҳ¶еҒҸеҜјж•°жҳҜ
\[
\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial r^2} = P(t,T) \cdot B(t,T)^2,
\]
е…¶дёӯ B(t,T) жҳҜжЁЎеһӢдёӯзҡ„иЎ°еҮҸеҮҪж•°гҖӮ

(b) зұ»дјјең°пјҢеҜ№дәҺеҮёжҖ§еәҰйҮҸ CпјҢжҲ‘д»¬е®ҡд№үе…¶жӣҝд»Јж–№жЎҲ\(\hat{C}\)дёә
\[
\hat{C} = \frac{1}{P} \frac{\partial^2 Q}{\partial r^2}.
\]

(c) еҪ“ Q жҳҜйӣ¶жҒҜеҖәеҲёд»·ж ј P(t,T) ж—¶пјҢ\(\hat{C}\) дёә
\[
\hat{C} = B(t,T)^2.
\]
еҜ№дәҺйҷ„жҒҜеҖәеҲёпјҢ\(\hat{C}\) жҳҜз»„жҲҗзҡ„еҗ„йӣ¶жҒҜеҖәеҲёзҡ„ \(\hat{C}\) зҡ„еҠ жқғе№іеқҮпјҢжқғйҮҚжҢүеҖәеҲёд»·ж јжҜ”дҫӢеҲҶй…ҚгҖӮ

(d) Vasicek е’Ң CIR жЁЎеһӢдёӯпјҢе…ідәҺ О”r е’Ң О”r^2 зҡ„жі°еӢ’еұ•ејҖдёә
\[
\Delta P = \frac{\partial P}{\partial r} \Delta r + \frac{1}{2} \frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial r^2} (\Delta r)^2.
\]
иҝҷиЎЁзӨәеҖәеҲёд»·ж јзҡ„еҸҳеҢ–еҸҜд»Ҙз”ЁдёҖйҳ¶е’ҢдәҢйҳ¶еҒҸеҜјж•°д№ҳд»Ҙзӣёеә”зҡ„еҲ©зҺҮеҸҳеҢ–йҮҸжқҘиҝ‘дјјгҖӮ

зҹӯжңҹеҲ©зҺҮзҡ„зҺ°е®һдё–з•ҢиҝҮзЁӢдёә
\[
dr = [a(b - r) - \lambda s] dt + s dz = [0.15(0.025 - r) - (-0.2)(0.012)] dt + 0.012 dz
\]
жҲ–иҖ…
\[
dr = 0.15(0.009 - r) dt + 0.012 dz
\]
3е№ҙжңҹйӣ¶жҒҜеҖәеҲёзҡ„йЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§иҝҮзЁӢдёә
\[
dP(t,3) = [r P(t,3) - B(t,3)] dt + s B(t,3) dz = -0.012 B(t,3) dt + B(t,3) dz
\]
е…¶дёӯ
\[
B(t,T) = \frac{1 - e^{-a(T-t)}}{a} = \frac{1 - e^{-0.15 (3 - t)}}{0.15}
\]
еңЁзҺ°е®һдё–з•ҢдёӯпјҢиҝҷдёӘиҝҮзЁӢеҸҳдёә
\[
dP(t,3) = [(r P(t,3) + \lambda s B(t,3)) - s B(t,3)] dt + s B(t,3) dz
\]
жҲ–иҖ…
\[
dP(t,3) = [0.0024 P(t,3)] dt + 0.012 B(t,3) dz
\]

problems.explanationZh:
31.13
зҹӯжңҹеҲ©зҺҮзҡ„йЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§иҝҮзЁӢдёә
\(
dr = a(b - r) \, dt + \sigma \sqrt{r} \, dz
\)

зҺ°е®һдё–з•ҢиҝҮзЁӢдёә
\(
dr = a(b - r) \, dt + \sigma \sqrt{r} \, dz^{\mathbb{P}}\)

е…¶дёӯпјҢ\(dz^{\mathbb{P}}\) жҳҜеңЁзҺ°е®һдё–з•ҢжҰӮзҺҮ \(\mathbb{P}\) дёӢзҡ„еёғжң—иҝҗеҠЁпјҢиҖҢйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§иҝҮзЁӢдёӯзҡ„еёғжң—иҝҗеҠЁжҳҜ \(dz\)гҖӮ

иҖғиҷ‘еёӮеңәйЈҺйҷ©д»·ж јдёә \(\lambda > 0\)пјҢж»Ўи¶і
\(dz^{\mathbb{P}} = dz + \lambda \, dt\)

д»Је…ҘзҺ°е®һдё–з•ҢиҝҮзЁӢдёӯпјҢеҫ—еҲ°йЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§иҝҮзЁӢпјҡ
\[
\begin{aligned}
dr &= a(b - r) \, dt + \sigma \sqrt{r} (dz + \lambda \, dt) \\
&= \left[a(b - r) + \lambda \sigma \sqrt{r} \right] dt + \sigma \sqrt{r} \, dz
\end{aligned}
\]

д»ӨйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§иҝҮзЁӢд»Қдёә CIR еҪўејҸпјҢиЎЁзӨәдёә
\[
dr = a^{*} (b^{*} - r) \, dt + \sigma \sqrt{r} \, dz
\]

жҜ”иҫғдёӨдёӘиЎЁиҫҫејҸзҡ„жјӮз§»йЎ№пјҡ
\[
a^{*}(b^{*} - r) = a(b - r) + \lambda \sigma \sqrt{r}\]

жіЁж„Ҹ \(\sqrt{r}\) йЎ№дёҚиғҪзӣҙжҺҘиЎЁиҫҫдёә \(b^{*} - r\) зҡ„зәҝжҖ§з»„еҗҲпјҢйңҖз”ЁйҮҚеҸӮж•°еҢ–еӨ„зҗҶгҖӮ

д»Һе®һеҠЎдёӯе·ІзҹҘпјҢйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§зҡ„еӣһеҪ’йҖҹзҺҮе’ҢеӣһеҪ’ж°ҙе№ідёҺзҺ°е®һдё–з•Ңзҡ„е…ізі»дёәпјҡ

(a) йЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§еӣһеҪ’зҺҮ
\[
a^{*} = a + \lambda \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{r}} \approx a - \lambda \sigma\]

еңЁ CIR жЁЎеһӢеҸӮж•°еҢ–дёӯпјҢйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§еӣһеҪ’зҺҮиЎЁзӨәдёә
\[
a^{*} = a - \lambda \sigma\]

(b) йЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§еӣһеҪ’ж°ҙе№і
\[
b^{*} = \frac{a b}{a - \lambda \sigma} \]

еҚійЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§ж°ҙе№іи°ғж•ҙдәҶеёӮеңәйЈҺйҷ©д»·ж јеҜ№еқҮеҖјеӣһеҪ’ж°ҙе№ізҡ„еҪұе“ҚгҖӮ

жҖ»з»“пјҡ
йЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§иҝҮзЁӢд»Қз„¶дҝқжҢҒ CIR зҡ„еҪўејҸпјҢе…ій”®еңЁдәҺеёӮеңәйЈҺйҷ©д»·ж ј \(\lambda\) и°ғж•ҙдәҶеқҮеҖјеӣһеҪ’йҖҹзҺҮе’Ңж°ҙе№ігҖӮе…·дҪ“иЎЁзҺ°дёә
\[
a^{*} = a - \lambda \sigma, \quad b^{*} = \frac{a b}{a - \lambda \sigma}
\]
иҝҷиЎЁжҳҺйЈҺйҷ©жәўд»·дҪҝеҫ—йЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§еҸӮж•°дёҺзҺ°е®һдё–з•ҢеҸӮж•°е‘ҲзәҝжҖ§еҒҸз§»е…ізі»гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 31.14. In the two-factor extension of Vasicek given in Section 31.5, derive the differential equation which must be satisfied by a bond price, P1t, T2. Use this to derive differential equations that must be satisfied by A1t, T2, B1t, T2, and C1t, T2 in P1t, T2 = A1t, T2e -B1t, T2r-C1t, T2u. Show that the expressions given for B1t, T2 in equation (31.7) and C1t, T2 in equation (31.14) satisfy these equations. [Hint: Use equation (14A.10) to obtain the drift of P1t, T2 and set this drift equal to rP1t, T2.]

problems.explanationZh:
31.14
еҖәеҲёд»·ж ј P(t,T) ж»Ўи¶ізҡ„еҫ®еҲҶж–№зЁӢдёә
\[
\frac{\partial P}{\partial t} + (a - b r) \frac{\partial P}{\partial r} + (u - v u) \frac{\partial P}{\partial u} + \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma_r^2 \frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial r^2} + \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma_u^2 \frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial u^2} + \rho \sigma_r \sigma_u \frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial r \partial u} = r P
\]
е…¶дёӯпјҢиҙҙзҺ°еҲ©зҺҮж–№зЁӢе®ҡд№үдёә
\[
dr = (a - b r) dt + \sigma_r dW_r, \quad du = (u - v u) dt + \sigma_u dW_u
\]
дҪҝз”ЁжҸҗзӨәдёӯз»ҷеҮәзҡ„е…¬ејҸ (14A.10)пјҢжҲ‘д»¬и®Ўз®—еҖәеҲёд»·ж јзҡ„жјӮз§»пјҢд»ӨиҜҘжјӮз§»зӯүдәҺ \( r P(t,T) \)пјҢз”ұжӯӨеҫ—еҲ° P(t,T)пјҲи®°дёә P_{1,t,T_2}пјү зҡ„еҒҸеҫ®еҲҶж–№зЁӢгҖӮ

з»ҷе®ҡеҪўејҸзҡ„еҖәеҲёд»·ж јдёә
\[
P_{1,t,T_2} = A_{1,t,T_2} e^{-B_{1,t,T_2} r - C_{1,t,T_2} u}
\]
еҜ№иҜҘиЎЁиҫҫејҸеҲҶеҲ«еҜ№ tгҖҒrгҖҒu жұӮеҒҸеҜјпјҢд»Је…ҘеҺҹеҫ®еҲҶж–№зЁӢпјҢе°Ҷеҫ—еҲ°дёүдёӘеҮҪж•° A_{1,t,T_2}гҖҒB_{1,t,T_2} е’Ң C_{1,t,T_2} ж»Ўи¶ізҡ„еҫ®еҲҶж–№зЁӢгҖӮ

з»ҸиҝҮж•ҙзҗҶеҗҺпјҢB_{1,t,T_2} е’Ң C_{1,t,T_2} ж»Ўи¶ідёӢеҲ—еёёеҫ®еҲҶж–№зЁӢпјҡ
\[
\frac{\partial B_{1,t,T_2}}{\partial t} = 1 - b B_{1,t,T_2}, \quad B_{1,T_2,T_2} = 0
\]
\[
\frac{\partial C_{1,t,T_2}}{\partial t} = 1 - v C_{1,t,T_2}, \quad C_{1,T_2,T_2} = 0
\]
иҝҷдәӣж–№зЁӢеҜ№еә”дәҺйўҳдёӯе…¬ејҸ (31.7) е’Ң (31.14) дёӯз»ҷеҮәзҡ„иЎЁиҫҫејҸпјҢи§Јдёәпјҡ
\[
B_{1,t,T_2} = \frac{1 - e^{-b (T_2 - t)}}{b}
\]
\[
C_{1,t,T_2} = \frac{1 - e^{-v (T_2 - t)}}{v}
\]

еҮҪж•° A_{1,t,T_2} ж»Ўи¶іпјҡ
\[
\frac{1}{A_{1,t,T_2}} \frac{\partial A_{1,t,T_2}}{\partial t} = b B_{1,t,T_2} + v C_{1,t,T_2} - \frac{1}{2} \sigma_r^2 B_{1,t,T_2}^2 - \frac{1}{2} \sigma_u^2 C_{1,t,T_2}^2 - \rho \sigma_r \sigma_u B_{1,t,T_2} C_{1,t,T_2}
\]

з»јдёҠпјҢйўҳдёӯжүҖз»ҷзҡ„ B_{1,t,T_2} е’Ң C_{1,t,T_2} иЎЁиҫҫејҸзЎ®е®һж»Ўи¶іеҖәеҲёд»·ж ј P_{1,t,T_2} зҡ„еҫ®еҲҶж–№зЁӢиҰҒжұӮпјҢиҝҷдҝқиҜҒдәҶжЁЎеһӢзҡ„дёҖиҮҙжҖ§гҖӮ

tags: No-Arbitrage Models of the Short Rate
problems.promptEn: Practice 32.1. What is the difference between an equilibrium model and a no-arbitrage model?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 32.1. еқҮиЎЎжЁЎеһӢе’Ңж— еҘ—еҲ©жЁЎеһӢжңүд»Җд№ҲеҢәеҲ«пјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
32.1
Equilibrium models usually start with assumptions about economic variables and derive the
behavior of interest rates. The initial term structure is an output from the model. In a no-
arbitrage model, the initial term structure is an input. The behavior of interest rates in a no-
arbitrage model is designed to be consistent with the initial term structure.
problems.explanationEn:
32.1
Equilibrium models usually start with assumptions about economic variables and derive the
behavior of interest rates. The initial term structure is an output from the model. In a no-
arbitrage model, the initial term structure is an input. The behavior of interest rates in a no-
arbitrage model is designed to be consistent with the initial term structure.

problems.promptEn: Practice 32.2. Can the approach described in Section 32.2 for decomposing an option on a coupon- bearing bond into a portfolio of options on zero-coupon bonds be used in conjunction with a two-factor model? Explain your answer.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 32.2. з¬¬ 32.2 иҠӮдёӯжҸҸиҝ°зҡ„е°Ҷйҷ„жҒҜеҖәеҲёжңҹжқғеҲҶи§Јдёәйӣ¶жҒҜеҖәеҲёжңҹжқғз»„еҗҲзҡ„ж–№жі•иғҪеҗҰдёҺдәҢеӣ зҙ жЁЎеһӢз»“еҗҲдҪҝз”Ёпјҹи§ЈйҮҠдҪ зҡ„зӯ”жЎҲгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
32.2
No. The approach in Section 32.2 relies on the argument that, at any given time, all bond prices
are moving in the same direction. This is not true when there is more than one factor.
problems.explanationEn:

problems.promptEn: Practice 32.5. Suppose that a = 0.05, b = 0.08, and s = 0.015 in VasicekвҖҷs model with the initial short- term interest rate being 6%. Calculate the price of a 2.1-year European call option on a bond that will mature in 3 years. Suppose that the bond pays a coupon of 5% semiannually. The principal of the bond is 100 and the strike price of the option is 99. The strike price is the cash price (not the quoted price) that will be paid for the bond.

Calculate the price of a 2.1-year European call option on a bond that will mature in 3 years. Suppose that the bond pays a coupon of 5% per annum semiannually. The principal of the bond is 100 and the strike price of the option is 99. The strike price is the cash price (not the quoted price) that will be paid for the bond.

the call option on the coupon-bearing bond can be decomposed into a call option with a strike
price of 2.434614 on a bond that pays off 2.5 at time 2.5 years and a call option with a strike
price of 96.56539 on a bond that pays off 102.5 at time 3.0 years.
The options are valued using equation (32.10).
For the first option, L = 2.5, K = 2.434614, T = 2.1, and s = 2.5. Also,
P(0,T) = exp(вҲ’ 0.059118 Г— 2.1) = 0.88325 and P(0,s) = exp(вҲ’ 0.059118 Г— 2.5) = 0.862609.
Furthermore, sigmaP = 0.008176 and h = 0.353374. so that the option price is 0.010523.
For the second option, L = 102.5, K = 96.56539, T = 2.1, and s = 3.0. Also,
P(0,T) = exp(вҲ’ 0.059118 Г— 2.1) = 0.88325 and P(0,s) = exp(вҲ’ 0.059118 Г— 3.0) = 0.837484.
Furthermore, sigmaP = 0.018168 and h = 0.363366. so that the option price is 0.934074.

The total value of the option is therefore 0.010523 + 0.934074 = 0.944596.

problems.explanation:
32.13
The calibration of a one-factor interest rate model involves determining its volatility parameters
so that it matches the market prices of actively traded interest rate options as closely as
possible.

problems.promptEn: Practice 32.15. A trader wishes to compute the price of a 1-year American call option on a 5-year bond with a face value of 100. The bond pays a coupon of 6% semiannually and the (quoted) strike price of the option is USD 100. The continuously compounded zero rates for maturities of 6 months, 1 year, 2 years, 3 years, 4 years, and 5 years are 4.5%, 5%, 5.5%, 5.8%, 6.1%, and 6.3%. The best-fit reversion rate for either the normal or the lognormal model has been estimated as 5%. A 1-year European call option with a (quoted) strike price of 100 on the bond is actively traded. Its market price is USD 0.50. The trader decides to use this option for calibration. Use the DerivaGem software with 10 time steps to answer the following questions: (a) Assuming a normal model, imply the s parameter from the price of the European option. (b) Use the s parameter to calculate the price of the option when it is American. (c) Repeat (a) and (b) for the lognormal model. Show that the model used does not significantly affect the price obtained providing it is calibrated to the known European price. (d) Display the tree for the normal model and calculate the probability of a negative interest rate occurring. (e) Display the tree for the lognormal model and verify that the option price is correctly calculated at the node where, with the notation of Section 32.4, i = 9 and j = -1.

problems.promptEn: Practice 32.16. Verify that the DerivaGem software gives Figure 32.9 for the example considered. Use the software to calculate the price of the American bond option for the lognormal and normal models when the strike price is 95, 100, and 105. In the case of the normal model, assume that a = 5% and s = 1%. Discuss the results in the context of the heavy-tails arguments of Chapter 20.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 32.16. йӘҢиҜҒ DerivaGem иҪҜд»¶жҳҜеҗҰз»ҷеҮәдәҶжүҖиҖғиҷ‘зӨәдҫӢзҡ„еӣҫ 32.9гҖӮдҪҝз”ЁиҜҘиҪҜд»¶и®Ўз®—жү§иЎҢд»·ж јдёә 95гҖҒ100 е’Ң 105 ж—¶зҡ„еҜ№ж•°жӯЈжҖҒе’ҢжӯЈжҖҒжЁЎеһӢзҡ„зҫҺеӣҪеҖәеҲёжңҹжқғд»·ж јгҖӮеңЁжӯЈжҖҒжЁЎеһӢзҡ„жғ…еҶөдёӢпјҢеҒҮи®ҫ a = 5% е’Ң s = 1%гҖӮеңЁз¬¬ 20 з« зҡ„йҮҚе°ҫи®әиҜҒзҡ„иғҢжҷҜдёӢи®Ёи®әз»“жһңгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
32.16
With 100 time steps, the lognormal model gives prices of 5.585, 2.443, and 0.703 for strike
prices of 95, 100, and 105. With 100 time steps, the normal model gives prices of 5.508, 2.522,
and 0.895 for the three strike prices, respectively. The normal model gives a heavier left tail
and a less heavy right tail than the lognormal model for interest rates. This translates into a less
heavy left tail and a heavier right tail for bond prices. The arguments in Chapter 20 show that
we expect the normal model to give higher option prices for high strike prices and lower option
prices for low strike. This is indeed what we find.

problems.promptEn: Practice 32.17. Modify Sample Application G in the DerivaGem Application Builder software to test the convergence of the price of the trinomial tree when it is used to price a 2-year call option on a 5-year bond with a face value of 100. Suppose that the strike price (quoted) is 100, the coupon rate is 7% with coupons being paid twice a year. Assume that the zero curve is as in Table 32.2. Compare results for the following cases: (a) Option is European; normal model with s = 0.01 and a = 0.05 (b) Option is European; lognormal model with s = 0.15 and a = 0.05 (c) Option is American; normal model with s = 0.01 and a = 0.05 (d) Option is American; lognormal model with s = 0.15 and a = 0.05. 755 Modeling Forward Rates The interest rate models discussed in Chapter 32 are widely used for pricing instruments when the simpler models in Chapter 29 are inappropriate. They are easy to implement and, if used carefully, can ensure that most nonstandard interest rate derivatives are priced consistently with actively traded instruments such as interest rate caps, European swap options, and European bond options. Two limitations of the models are: 1. Most involve only one factor (i.e., one source of uncertainty). 2. They do not give the user complete freedom in choosing the volatility structure. By making the parameters a and s functions of time, an analyst can use the models so that they fit the volatilities observed in the market today, but as mentioned in Section 32.6 the volatility term structure is then nonstationary. The volatility structure in the future is liable to be quite different from that observed in the market today. This chapter discusses some general approaches to building term structure models that give the user more flexibility in specifying the volatility environment and allow several factors to be used. This chapter also covers the agency mortgage-backed security market in the United States and describes how some of the ideas presented in the chapter can be used to price instruments in that market. 33 C H A P T E R

problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
33.1
In a Markov model, the expected change and volatility of the short rate at time t depend only
on the value of the short rate at time t . In a non-Markov model, they depend on the history of
the short rate prior to time t .

33.2
ж–№зЁӢ (33.1) еҸҳдёә

\(
\frac{dP(t,T)}{P(t,T)} = r(t) dt + v(t,T) d z_t^\Omega,
\)

еӣ жӯӨжңү

\(
\ln [P(t,T)] = -\int_0^t r(s) ds + \sum_k \int_0^t v_k(s,T) d z_s^{\Omega,k} - \frac{1}{2} \sum_k \int_0^t v_k^2(s,T) ds
\)

ж №жҚ®ж–№зЁӢ (33.2)

\(
df(t,T,T) = r(t) dt + \sum_k v_k(t,T) d z_t^{\Omega,k} - \frac{1}{2} \sum_k v_k^2(t,T) dt
\)

д»Ө \(T_1 = T\), \(T_2 = T + \Delta\)пјҢеҪ“ \(\Delta \to 0\) ж—¶пјҢдёҠејҸеҸҳдёә

\(
\frac{\partial f(t,T)}{\partial T} dT = \alpha(t,T) dt + \sum_k \sigma_k(t,T) d z_t^{\Omega,k}
\)

е…¶дёӯ

\(
\alpha(t,T) = \sum_k \sigma_k(t,T) \int_t^T \frac{\partial \sigma_k(t,s)}{\partial T} ds
\)

йҖҡиҝҮд»ЈжҚў

\(
v_k(t,T) = \frac{\partial s_k(t,T)}{\partial T}
\)

еҫ—еҲ°ж–№зЁӢ (33.6) дёӯжјӮз§»дёҺжіўеҠЁд№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„е…ізі»гҖӮ

33.4
дҪҝз”Ёз¬¬ 33.1 иҠӮдёӯзҡ„з¬ҰеҸ·пјҢеҪ“

(\(s(t,T) = \sigma e^{-a(T - t)}\))

еҲҷ

(\(v(t,T) = - \frac{\partial}{\partial T} s(t,T) = a \sigma e^{-a(T - t)}\))

з§ҜеҲҶеҫ—

(\(\int_t^T v(t,u) du = \sigma (1 - e^{-a(T - t)})\))

еӣ жӯӨ

(\(\alpha(t,T) = \frac{\sigma}{a}(1 - e^{-a(T - t)})\))

еҲ©з”Ё \(v(T,T) = 0\) зҡ„дәӢе®һпјҢеҝ…йЎ»жңү

(\(v(t,T) = - \sigma e^{-a(T - t)} = -s(t,T)\))

жҢүз…§з¬¬ 32 з« зҡ„з¬ҰеҸ·пјҢHullвҖ“White жЁЎеһӢдёӯ

(\(r_t = A(t,T)e^{-r_t} + B(t,T)\))

зҹӯжңҹеҲ©зҺҮзҡ„ж ҮеҮҶе·®жҳҜеёёж•° \(\sigma\)гҖӮз”ұ ItГҙ еј•зҗҶзҹҘпјҢеҖәеҲёд»·ж јж ҮеҮҶе·®дёә

(\(\sigma P(t,T) B(t,T)\))

еӣ жӯӨеҖәеҲёд»·ж јзҡ„жіўеҠЁзҺҮдёә

(\(\sigma B(t,T)\))

иҝҷиЎЁжҳҺд»·зі»ж•°еҪўеҰӮ

(\(a(T-t) s(t,T) = \sigma e^{-a(T-t)}\))

зҡ„ HJM жЁЎеһӢдёҺ HullвҖ“White жЁЎеһӢжҳҜдёҖиҮҙзҡ„гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 33.5. What is the advantage of BGM over HJM?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 33.5. BGMзӣёеҜ№дәҺHJMжңүд»Җд№ҲдјҳеҠҝпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
33.5
BGM is a similar model to HJM. It has the advantage over HJM that it involves forward rates
that are readily observable. HJM involves instantaneous forward rates.

problems.promptEn: Practice 33.6. Provide an intuitive explanation of why a ratchet cap increases in value as the number of factors increase.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 33.6. зӣҙи§Ӯең°и§ЈйҮҠдёәд»Җд№ҲжЈҳиҪ®еёҪзҡ„д»·еҖјйҡҸзқҖеӣ зҙ ж•°йҮҸзҡ„еўһеҠ иҖҢеўһеҠ гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
33.6
A ratchet cap tends to provide relatively low payoffs if a high (low) interest rate at one reset
date is followed by a high (low) interest rate at the next reset date. High payoffs occur when a
low interest rate is followed by a high interest rate. As the number of factors increase, the
correlation between successive forward rates declines and there is a greater chance that a low
interest rate will be followed by a high interest rate.
problems.explanationEn:
33.6
A ratchet cap tends to provide relatively low payoffs if a high (low) interest rate at one reset
date is followed by a high (low) interest rate at the next reset date. High payoffs occur when a
low interest rate is followed by a high interest rate. As the number of factors increase, the
correlation between successive forward rates declines and there is a greater chance that a low
interest rate will be followed by a high interest rate.

\(
\frac{dF_i(t)}{F_i(t)} = \sum_{k=1}^m \delta_k \zeta_k(t) + \zeta_i(t) dz_i(t)
\)

еҪ“ \(i \delta\) и¶ӢдәҺйӣ¶ж—¶пјҢ\(\zeta_i(t)\) жҲҗдёәж—¶еҲ» \(t\) еҚіж—¶еҲ°жңҹиҝңжңҹеҲ©зҺҮзҡ„ж ҮеҮҶе·®гҖӮдҪҝз”Ёз¬¬33.1иҠӮзҡ„з¬ҰеҸ·пјҢиҝҷе°ұжҳҜ \(s_i(t, t + \delta, \Omega)\)гҖӮеҪ“ \(i\delta\) и¶ӢдәҺйӣ¶ж—¶пјҢ

\(
\sum_{k=1}^m \delta_k \zeta_k(t) \rightarrow \int_t^{t+\delta} s_k(t, \tau, \Omega) d\tau
\)

еӣ жӯӨпјҢж–№зЁӢ(33.10)еҸҳжҲҗ

\(
\frac{dF_k(t)}{F_k(t)} = \left[\int_t^{t+\delta} s_k(t, \tau, \Omega) d\tau \right] dt + s_k(t, t+\delta, \Omega) dz(t, \tau)
\)

problems.promptEn: Practice 33.9. Explain why IOs and POs have opposite sensitivities to the rate of prepayments.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 33.9. и§ЈйҮҠдёәд»Җд№Ҳ IO е’Ң PO еҜ№йў„д»ҳж¬ҫзҺҮе…·жңүзӣёеҸҚзҡ„ж•Ҹж„ҹжҖ§гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
33.9
When prepayments increase, the principal is received sooner. This increases the value of a
PO. When prepayments increase, less interest is received. This decreases the value of an IO.
problems.explanationEn:
33.9
When prepayments increase, the principal is received sooner. This increases the value of a
PO. When prepayments increase, less interest is received. This decreases the value of an IO.

problems.promptEn: Practice 33.10. вҖңAn option adjusted spread is analogous to the yield on a bond.вҖқ Explain this statement.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 33.10. вҖңжңҹжқғи°ғж•ҙеҲ©е·®зұ»дјјдәҺеҖәеҲёзҡ„ж”¶зӣҠзҺҮгҖӮвҖқи§ЈйҮҠдёҖдёӢиҝҷдёӘиҜҙжі•гҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
33.10
A bond yield is the discount rate that causes the bondвҖҷs price to equal the market price. The
same discount rate is used for all maturities. An OAS is the parallel shift to the Treasury zero
curve that causes the price of an instrument such as a mortgage-backed security to equal its
market price.
problems.explanationEn:
33.10
A bond yield is the discount rate that causes the bondвҖҷs price to equal the market price. The
same discount rate is used for all maturities. An OAS is the parallel shift to the Treasury zero
curve that causes the price of an instrument such as a mortgage-backed security to equal its
market price.

problems.promptEn: Practice 33.14. In the flexi cap considered in Section 33.2 the holder is obligated to exercise the first N in-the-money caplets. After that no further caplets can be exercised. (In the example, N = 5.) Two other ways that flexi caps are sometimes defined are: (a) The holder can choose whether any caplet is exercised, but there is a limit of N on the total number of caplets that can be exercised. (b) Once the holder chooses to exercise a caplet all subsequent in-the-money caplets must be exercised up to a maximum of N. Discuss the problems in valuing these types of flexi caps. Of the three types of flexi caps, which would you expect to be most expensive? Which would you expect to be least expensive? Swaps Revisited
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 33.14. еңЁз¬¬ 33.2 иҠӮиҖғиҷ‘зҡ„зҒөжҙ»дёҠйҷҗдёӯпјҢжҢҒжңүдәәжңүд№үеҠЎиЎҢдҪҝеүҚ N дёӘд»·еҶ…еӣҠзүҮгҖӮжӯӨеҗҺдёҚиғҪеҶҚиЎҢдҪҝжӣҙеӨҡзҡ„иғ¶еӣҠгҖӮ пјҲеңЁзӨәдҫӢдёӯпјҢN = 5гҖӮпјүзҒөжҙ»дёҠйҷҗжңүж—¶е®ҡд№үзҡ„е…¶д»–дёӨз§Қж–№ејҸжҳҜпјҡ (a) жҢҒжңүдәәеҸҜд»ҘйҖүжӢ©жҳҜеҗҰиЎҢдҪҝд»»дҪ•дёҠйҷҗпјҢдҪҶеҸҜд»ҘиЎҢдҪҝзҡ„дёҠйҷҗзҡ„жҖ»ж•°жңү N зҡ„йҷҗеҲ¶гҖӮ (b) дёҖж—ҰжҢҒжңүдәәйҖүжӢ©иЎҢдҪҝдёҖдёӘ CapletпјҢжүҖжңүеҗҺз»ӯзҡ„д»·еҶ… Caplet йғҪеҝ…йЎ»иў«иЎҢдҪҝпјҢжңҖеӨҡдёә NгҖӮи®Ёи®әеҜ№иҝҷдәӣзұ»еһӢзҡ„зҒөжҙ» Caplet иҝӣиЎҢдј°еҖјзҡ„й—®йўҳгҖӮеңЁдёүз§Қзұ»еһӢзҡ„еј№жҖ§зӣ–дёӯпјҢжӮЁи®Өдёәе“ӘдёҖз§ҚжңҖиҙөпјҹжӮЁи®Өдёәе“ӘдёӘжңҖдҫҝе®ңпјҹ йҮҚжё©жҺүжңҹз« иҠӮ жҺүжңҹеҜ№дәҺеңәеӨ–иЎҚз”ҹе“ҒеёӮеңәзҡ„жҲҗеҠҹиҮіе…ійҮҚиҰҒгҖӮдәӢе®һиҜҒжҳҺпјҢе®ғд»¬жҳҜз®ЎзҗҶйЈҺйҷ©зҡ„йқһеёёзҒөжҙ»зҡ„е·Ҙе…·гҖӮж №жҚ®зӣ®еүҚдәӨжҳ“зҡ„дёҚеҗҢеҗҲзәҰзҡ„иҢғеӣҙд»ҘеҸҠжҜҸе№ҙдәӨжҳ“зҡ„дёҡеҠЎжҖ»йҮҸпјҢдә’жҚўеҸҜд»ҘиҜҙжҳҜйҮ‘иһҚеёӮеңәжңүеҸІд»ҘжқҘжңҖжҲҗеҠҹзҡ„еҲӣж–°д№ӢдёҖгҖӮз¬¬ 7 з« и®Ёи®әдәҶеҰӮдҪ•еҜ№жҷ®йҖҡеҲ©зҺҮжҺүжңҹиҝӣиЎҢдј°еҖјгҖӮж ҮеҮҶж–№жі•еҸҜд»ҘжҰӮжӢ¬дёәпјҡвҖңеҒҮи®ҫиҝңжңҹеҲ©зҺҮе°Ҷдјҡе®һзҺ°гҖӮвҖқжӯҘйӘӨеҰӮдёӢпјҡ 1. еҒҮи®ҫжңӘжқҘеҲ©зҺҮзӯүдәҺж №жҚ®еҪ“еүҚеёӮеңәдәӨжҳ“е·Ҙе…·и®Ўз®—еҮәзҡ„иҝңжңҹеҲ©зҺҮпјҢи®Ўз®—жҺүжңҹзҡ„еҮҖзҺ°йҮ‘жөҒйҮҸгҖӮ 2. дҪҝз”Ёж— йЈҺйҷ© (OIS) еҲ©зҺҮе°ҶжҺүжңҹд»·еҖји®ҫзҪ®дёәзӯүдәҺеҮҖзҺ°йҮ‘жөҒйҮҸзҡ„зҺ°еҖјгҖӮжң¬з« жҸҸиҝ°дәҶдёҖдәӣйқһж ҮеҮҶдәӨжҚўгҖӮжңүдәӣеҸҜд»ҘдҪҝз”ЁвҖңеҒҮи®ҫиҝңжңҹеҲ©зҺҮе°Ҷдјҡе®һзҺ°вҖқзҡ„ж–№жі•иҝӣиЎҢдј°еҖјпјӣжңүдәӣйңҖиҰҒеә”з”ЁжҲ‘д»¬еңЁз¬¬ 30 з« дёӯйҒҮеҲ°зҡ„еҮёжҖ§гҖҒж—¶еәҸе’ҢеҸҢеёҒз§Қи°ғж•ҙпјӣжңүдәӣеҢ…еҗ«еөҢе…ҘжңҹжқғпјҢеҝ…йЎ»дҪҝз”Ёз¬¬ 29 з« гҖҒз¬¬ 32 з« е’Ңз¬¬ 33 з« дёӯжҸҸиҝ°зҡ„зЁӢеәҸиҝӣиЎҢдј°еҖјгҖӮ 34.1 жҷ®йҖҡдәӨжҳ“зҡ„еҸҳеҢ– и®ёеӨҡеҲ©зҺҮдә’жҚўж¶үеҸҠз¬¬ 7 з« дёӯи®Ёи®әзҡ„жҷ®йҖҡз»“жһ„зҡ„зӣёеҜ№иҫғе°Ҹзҡ„еҸҳеҢ–гҖӮеңЁжҹҗдәӣдә’жҚўдёӯпјҢеҗҚд№үжң¬йҮ‘д»Ҙйў„е®ҡж–№ејҸйҡҸж—¶й—ҙеҸҳеҢ–гҖӮеҗҚд№үжң¬йҮ‘жҳҜж—¶й—ҙйҖ’еўһеҮҪж•°зҡ„жҺүжңҹиў«з§°дёәеҚҮеҺӢжҺүжңҹгҖӮеҗҚд№үжң¬йҮ‘жҳҜж—¶й—ҙйҖ’еҮҸеҮҪж•°зҡ„жҺүжңҹз§°дёәж‘Ҡй”ҖжҺүжңҹгҖӮеҜ№дәҺжү“з®—д»Ҙжө®еҠЁеҲ©зҺҮеҖҹе…ҘжӣҙеӨҡиө„йҮ‘жқҘдёәзү№е®ҡйЎ№зӣ®иһҚиө„е№¶еёҢжңӣиҪ¬жҚўдёәеӣәе®ҡеҲ©зҺҮиһҚиө„зҡ„е»әзӯ‘е…¬еҸёжқҘиҜҙпјҢйҳ¶жўҜејҸдә’жҚўеҸҜиғҪеҫҲжңүз”ЁгҖӮжӢҘжңүеӣәе®ҡеҲ©зҺҮеҖҹж¬ҫе№¶жңүдёҖе®ҡжҸҗеүҚиҝҳж¬ҫи®ЎеҲ’е№¶еёҢжңӣд»Ҙжө®еҠЁеҲ©зҺҮеҖҹж¬ҫзҡ„е…¬еҸёеҸҜд»ҘдҪҝз”Ёж‘Ҡй”ҖжҺүжңҹгҖӮ жҺүжңҹдёӨдҫ§зҡ„жң¬йҮ‘еҸҜд»ҘдёҚеҗҢгҖӮд»ҳж¬ҫйў‘зҺҮд№ҹеҸҜиғҪдёҚеҗҢгҖӮ Business Snapshot 34.1 йҖҡиҝҮеұ•зӨәеҫ®иҪҜе’Ңй«ҳзӣӣд№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„еҒҮи®ҫдәӨжҚўжқҘиҜҙжҳҺиҝҷдёҖзӮ№пјҢе…¶дёӯеӣәе®ҡж–№еҗҚд№үжң¬йҮ‘дёә \$120 million on the floating side and \$1 дәҝгҖӮжө®еҠЁйғЁеҲҶжҜҸдёүдёӘжңҲд»ҳж¬ҫдёҖж¬ЎпјҢеӣәе®ҡйғЁеҲҶжҜҸе…ӯдёӘжңҲд»ҳж¬ҫдёҖж¬ЎгҖӮиҝҷдәӣеҜ№еҹәжң¬жҷ®йҖҡз»“жһ„зҡ„еҸҳеҢ–дёҚдјҡеҪұе“Қдј°еҖјж–№жі•гҖӮ вҖңеҒҮи®ҫиҝңжңҹеҲ©зҺҮе®һзҺ°вҖқзҡ„ж–№жі•д»Қз„¶еҸҜд»ҘдҪҝз”ЁгҖӮдј з»ҹдёҠпјҢLIBOR жҳҜдә’жҚўдёӯжңҖеёёи§Ғзҡ„жө®еҠЁеҸӮиҖғеҲ©зҺҮгҖӮйҡҸзқҖдјҰж•Ұй“¶иЎҢеҗҢдёҡжӢҶеҖҹеҲ©зҺҮ (LIBOR) зҡ„йҖҗжӯҘж·ҳжұ°пјҢеҹәдәҺйҡ”еӨңеҲ©зҺҮзҡ„дә’жҚўеҸҜиғҪдјҡеҸҳеҫ—жӣҙеҠ жҷ®йҒҚгҖӮ пјҲеҰӮз¬¬ 7 з« жүҖиҝ°пјҢе…ёеһӢзҡ„ OIS problems.promptEn: Practice 34.3. What is the value of a 2-year fixed-for-floating compounding swap where the principal is USD 100 million and payments are made semiannually? Fixed interest is received and floating is paid. The fixed rate is 3% per annum and fixed cash flows are compounded every six months at a rate of 3.3% per annum. (Both the 3% and 3.3% are expressed with semiannual compounding.) The floating rate is a risk-free rate plus 10 basis points and it is compounded at the risk-free rate plus 20 basis points. The risk-free zero curve is flat at 3% with semiannual compounding.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 34.3. жң¬йҮ‘дёә 1 дәҝзҫҺе…ғдё”жҜҸеҚҠе№ҙд»ҳж¬ҫдёҖж¬Ўзҡ„ 2 е№ҙжңҹеӣәе®ҡжө®еҠЁеӨҚеҲ©дә’жҚўзҡ„д»·еҖјжҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹж”¶еҸ–еӣәе®ҡеҲ©жҒҜе№¶ж”Ҝд»ҳжө®еҠЁеҲ©жҒҜгҖӮеӣәе®ҡеҲ©зҺҮдёәжҜҸе№ҙ 3%пјҢеӣәе®ҡзҺ°йҮ‘жөҒжҜҸе…ӯдёӘжңҲд»ҘжҜҸе№ҙ 3.3% зҡ„еҲ©зҺҮеӨҚеҲ©гҖӮ пјҲ3%е’Ң3.3%еқҮд»ҘеҚҠе№ҙеӨҚеҲ©иЎЁзӨәгҖӮпјүжө®еҠЁеҲ©зҺҮдёәж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮеҠ 10дёӘеҹәзӮ№пјҢеӨҚеҲ©дёәж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮеҠ 20дёӘеҹәзӮ№гҖӮж— йЈҺйҷ©йӣ¶жӣІзәҝе№іеқҰпјҢеҲ©зҺҮдёә 3%пјҢеҚҠе№ҙеӨҚеҲ©дёҖж¬ЎгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
34.3
The final fixed payment is in millions of dollars:

[(1.5 Г— 1.0165 + 1.5) Г— 1.0165 + 1.5] Г— 1.0165 + 1.5 = 6.1501
The final floating payment assuming forward rates are realized is

[(1.55Г— 1.016 + 1.55)Г— 1.016 + 1.55] Г— 1.016 + 1.55= 6.3504
The value of the swap is therefore the present value of вҖ“ 0.2003 or вҖ“ 0.2003/(1.0154)
= вҖ“ 0.1887. This makes the small approximation discussed in footnote 1 of Chapter 34.
problems.explanationEn:
34.3
The final fixed payment is in millions of dollars:

[(1.5 Г— 1.0165 + 1.5) Г— 1.0165 + 1.5] Г— 1.0165 + 1.5 = 6.1501
The final floating payment assuming forward rates are realized is

[(1.55Г— 1.016 + 1.55)Г— 1.016 + 1.55] Г— 1.016 + 1.55= 6.3504
The value of the swap is therefore the present value of вҖ“ 0.2003 or вҖ“ 0.2003/(1.0154)

34.3
жңҖз»Ҳзҡ„еӣәе®ҡж”Ҝд»ҳйҮ‘йўқпјҲеҚ•дҪҚпјҡзҷҫдёҮзҫҺе…ғпјүдёәпјҡ

\[
[(1.5 \times 1.0165 + 1.5) \times 1.0165 + 1.5] \times 1.0165 + 1.5 = 6.1501
\]

еҒҮи®ҫе®һйҷ…еҲ©зҺҮзӯүдәҺиҝңжңҹеҲ©зҺҮпјҢжңҖз»Ҳзҡ„жө®еҠЁж”Ҝд»ҳйҮ‘йўқдёәпјҡ

\[
[(1.55 \times 1.016 + 1.55) \times 1.016 + 1.55] \times 1.016 + 1.55 = 6.3504
\]

еӣ жӯӨпјҢдә’жҚўзҡ„д»·еҖјжҳҜиҝҷдәӣзҺ°йҮ‘жөҒзҺ°еҖјд№Ӣе·®пјҢеҚі -0.2003пјҢжҠҳзҺ°еҲ°зҺ°еҖјдёә -0.2003/(1.0154) = -0.1887гҖӮиҝҷз¬ҰеҗҲз¬¬34з« и„ҡжіЁ1дёӯи®Ёи®әзҡ„е°Ҹиҝ‘дјјгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 34.4. What is the value of a 5-year swap where a risk-free floating rate is paid in the usual way and in return the risk-free rate compounded at the risk-free rate is received on the other side? The principal on both sides is USD 100 million. Payment dates on the pay side and compounding dates on the receive side are every 3 months. The risk-free zero curve is flat at 2% with quarterly compounding.

problems.promptEn: Practice 34.5. In the accrual swap discussed in the text, the fixed side accrues only when the floating reference rate lies below a certain level. Discuss how the analysis can be extended to cope with a situation where the fixed side accrues only when the floating reference rate is between two levels.

problems.promptEn: Practice 34.6. Risk-free zero rates are flat at 2% in the United States and flat at 5% in Australia (both annually compounded). In a 4-year diff swap the Australian floating risk-free rate is received and 4% is paid, with both being applied to a USD principal of USD 10 million. Payments are exchanged annually. The volatility of all 1-year forward rates in Australia is estimated to be 25%, the volatility of the forward USD/AUD exchange rate (AUD per USD) is 15% for all maturities, and the correlation between the two is 0.3. What is the value of the swap?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 34.6. зҫҺеӣҪзҡ„ж— йЈҺйҷ©йӣ¶еҲ©зҺҮжҢҒе№ідәҺ 2%пјҢжҫіеӨ§еҲ©дәҡжҢҒе№ідәҺ 5%пјҲеқҮжҢүе№ҙеӨҚеҲ©и®Ўз®—пјүгҖӮеңЁ 4 е№ҙжңҹе·®д»·жҺүжңҹдёӯпјҢж”¶еҲ°жҫіеӨ§еҲ©дәҡжө®еҠЁж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮе№¶ж”Ҝд»ҳ 4%пјҢдёӨиҖ…еқҮйҖӮз”ЁдәҺ 1000 дёҮзҫҺе…ғзҡ„зҫҺе…ғжң¬йҮ‘гҖӮд»ҳж¬ҫжҜҸе№ҙдәӨжҚўдёҖж¬ЎгҖӮжҫіеӨ§еҲ©дәҡжүҖжңү 1 е№ҙжңҹиҝңжңҹжұҮзҺҮзҡ„жіўеҠЁжҖ§дј°и®Ўдёә 25%пјҢжүҖжңүжңҹйҷҗзҡ„иҝңжңҹзҫҺе…ғ/жҫіе…ғжұҮзҺҮпјҲжҫіе…ғе…‘зҫҺе…ғпјүзҡ„жіўеҠЁжҖ§дёә 15%пјҢдёӨиҖ…д№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„зӣёе…іжҖ§дёә 0.3гҖӮдә’жҚўзҡ„д»·еҖјжҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
34.6
There are four payments of USD 0.4 million. The present value in millions of dollars is

0.4/1.02+0.4/1.022+0.4/1.023+0.4/1.024=1.5231

The forward Australian floating rate is 5% with annual compounding. The quanto adjustment
to the floating payment at time
1
it + is

0.3 Г— 0.15 Г— 0.25ti = 0.01125ti

The value of the floating payments received is therefore

0.5/1.02 + 0.5 Г— 1.01125/1.022 + 0.5 Г— 1.0225/1.023 + 0.5 Г— 1.03375/1.024 =1.9355

The value of the swap is 1.9355 вҖ“ 1.5231 = 0.4124 million.

problems.promptEn: Practice 34.7. Estimate the interest rate paid by P&G on the 5>30 swap in Section 34.6 if (a) the CP rate is 6.5% and the Treasury yield curve is flat at 6% and (b) the CP rate is 7.5% and the Treasury yield curve is flat at 7% with semiannual compounding.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 34.7. дј°и®Ўз¬¬ 34.6 иҠӮдёӯ P&G еҜ№ 5>30 жҺүжңҹж”Ҝд»ҳзҡ„еҲ©зҺҮпјҢеҰӮжһң (a) CP еҲ©зҺҮдёә 6.5%пјҢеӣҪеҖәж”¶зӣҠзҺҮжӣІзәҝе№іеқҰдәҺ 6%пјҢе№¶дё” (b) CP еҲ©зҺҮдёә иЎҚз”ҹе“Ғзҡ„еҹәзЎҖеҸҳйҮҸжңүж—¶з®Җз§°дёәеҹәзЎҖеҸҳйҮҸгҖӮжң¬д№ҰеүҚйқўзҡ„йғЁеҲҶйҮҚзӮ№и®Ёи®әдәҶж Үзҡ„зү©жҳҜиӮЎзҘЁд»·ж јгҖҒиӮЎзҘЁжҢҮж•°гҖҒжұҮзҺҮгҖҒеҖәеҲёд»·ж јгҖҒеҲ©зҺҮжҲ–дҝЎз”ЁдәӢд»¶жҚҹеӨұзҡ„жғ…еҶөгҖӮеңЁжң¬з« дёӯпјҢжҲ‘д»¬иҖғиҷ‘еҗ„з§Қе…¶д»–еҹәзЎҖгҖӮжң¬з« зҡ„з¬¬дёҖйғЁеҲҶж¶үеҸҠж Үзҡ„зү©жҳҜе•Ҷе“Ғзҡ„жғ…еҶөгҖӮз¬¬2з« и®Ёи®әдәҶе•Ҷе“Ғжңҹиҙ§еҗҲзәҰпјҢз¬¬18з« и®Ёи®әдәҶж¬§жҙІе’ҢзҫҺеӣҪе•Ҷе“Ғжңҹиҙ§еҗҲзәҰзҡ„жңҹжқғеҰӮдҪ•е®ҡд»·гҖӮз”ұдәҺеҪ“жңҹиҙ§еҗҲзәҰдёҺжңҹжқғеҗҢж—¶еҲ°жңҹж—¶пјҢж¬§жҙІжңҹиҙ§жңҹжқғдёҺж¬§жҙІзҺ°иҙ§жңҹжқғе…·жңүзӣёеҗҢзҡ„ж”¶зӣҠпјҢеӣ жӯӨз”ЁдәҺиҜ„дј°ж¬§жҙІжңҹиҙ§жңҹжқғзҡ„жЁЎеһӢпјҲеёғиҺұе…ӢжЁЎеһӢпјүд№ҹеҸҜд»Ҙз”ЁдәҺиҜ„дј°ж¬§жҙІзҺ°иҙ§жңҹжқғгҖӮз„¶иҖҢпјҢзҫҺеӣҪзҺ°иҙ§жңҹжқғе’Ңе…¶д»–дҫқиө–дәҺе•Ҷе“ҒзҺ°иҙ§д»·ж јзҡ„жӣҙеӨҚжқӮзҡ„иЎҚз”ҹе“ҒйңҖиҰҒжӣҙеӨҚжқӮзҡ„жЁЎеһӢгҖӮеӨ§е®—е•Ҷе“Ғд»·ж јзҡ„дёҖдёӘзү№зӮ№жҳҜе®ғд»¬з»ҸеёёиЎЁзҺ°еҮәеқҮеҖјеӣһеҪ’пјҲзұ»дјјдәҺеҲ©зҺҮпјүпјҢе№¶дё”жңүж—¶д№ҹдјҡеҮәзҺ°и·іи·ғгҖӮдёәеҲ©зҺҮејҖеҸ‘зҡ„дёҖдәӣжЁЎеһӢеҸҜд»ҘйҖӮз”ЁдәҺе•Ҷе“ҒгҖӮжң¬з« зҡ„з¬¬дәҢйғЁеҲҶи®Ёи®әеӨ©ж°”е’Ңдҝқйҷ©иЎҚз”ҹе“ҒгҖӮиҝҷдәӣиЎҚз”ҹе“Ғзҡ„дёҖдёӘжҳҫзқҖзү№еҫҒжҳҜе®ғд»¬дҫқиө–дәҺжІЎжңүзі»з»ҹйЈҺйҷ©зҡ„еҸҳйҮҸгҖӮдҫӢеҰӮпјҢеҸҜд»ҘеҗҲзҗҶең°еҒҮи®ҫжҹҗдёӘдҪҚзҪ®зҡ„жё©еәҰйў„жңҹеҖјжҲ–йЈ“йЈҺйҖ жҲҗзҡ„жҚҹеӨұеңЁйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§дё–з•Ңе’ҢзҺ°е®һдё–з•ҢдёӯзӣёеҗҢгҖӮиҝҷж„Ҹе‘ізқҖеҺҶеҸІж•°жҚ®еҜ№дәҺиҜ„дј°иҝҷдәӣзұ»еһӢзҡ„иЎҚз”ҹе“ҒеҸҜиғҪжҜ”е…¶д»–дёҖдәӣиЎҚз”ҹе“Ғжӣҙжңүз”ЁгҖӮеҶңдә§е“ҒеҢ…жӢ¬з§ҚжӨҚзҡ„дә§е“ҒпјҲжҲ–з”ұз§ҚжӨҚзҡ„дә§е“Ғз”ҹдә§зҡ„дә§е“ҒпјүпјҢдҫӢеҰӮзҺүзұігҖҒе°ҸйәҰгҖҒеӨ§иұҶгҖҒеҸҜеҸҜгҖҒе’–е•ЎгҖҒзі–гҖҒжЈүиҠұе’ҢеҶ·еҶ»ж©ҷжұҒгҖӮе®ғд»¬иҝҳеҢ…жӢ¬дёҺзүІз•ңжңүе…ізҡ„дә§е“ҒпјҢеҰӮзүӣгҖҒз”ҹзҢӘе’Ңдә”иҠұиӮүгҖӮдёҺжүҖжңүе•Ҷе“ҒдёҖж ·пјҢеҶңдә§е“Ғзҡ„д»·ж јз”ұдҫӣжұӮе…ізі»еҶіе®ҡгҖӮзҫҺеӣҪеҶңдёҡйғЁеҸ‘еёғеә“еӯҳе’Ңз”ҹдә§жҠҘе‘ҠгҖӮдёҖйЎ№й’ҲеҜ№зҺүзұізӯүеӨ§е®—е•Ҷе“Ғзҡ„з»ҹи®Ўж•°жҚ® 35.1 еҶңдә§е“Ғ е°ҸйәҰжҳҜеә“еӯҳдёҺз”ЁйҮҸзҡ„жҜ”зҺҮгҖӮиҝҷжҳҜе№ҙжң«еә“еӯҳдёҺеҪ“е№ҙдҪҝз”ЁйҮҸзҡ„жҜ”зҺҮгҖӮйҖҡеёёеңЁ 20% еҲ° 40% д№Ӣй—ҙгҖӮе®ғеҜ№д»·ж јжіўеҠЁжңүеҪұе“ҚгҖӮйҡҸзқҖе•Ҷе“Ғзҡ„жҜ”зҺҮеҸҳдҪҺпјҢе•Ҷе“Ғзҡ„д»·ж јеҜ№дҫӣеә”еҸҳеҢ–еҸҳеҫ—жӣҙеҠ ж•Ҹж„ҹпјҢд»ҺиҖҢжіўеҠЁжҖ§еўһеҠ гҖӮеҒҮи®ҫеҶңдә§е“Ғд»·ж јеҮәзҺ°жҹҗз§ҚзЁӢеәҰзҡ„еқҮеҖјеӣһеҪ’жҳҜжңүзҗҶз”ұзҡ„гҖӮйҡҸзқҖд»·ж јдёӢи·ҢпјҢеҶңж°‘еҸ‘зҺ°з”ҹдә§е•Ҷе“Ғзҡ„еҗёеј•еҠӣйҷҚдҪҺпјҢдҫӣеә”еҮҸе°‘пјҢйҖ жҲҗд»·ж јдёҠиЎҢеҺӢеҠӣгҖӮеҗҢж ·пјҢйҡҸзқҖеҶңдә§е“Ғд»·ж јдёҠж¶ЁпјҢеҶңж°‘жӣҙжңүеҸҜиғҪжҠ•е…Ҙиө„жәҗз”ҹдә§иҜҘе•Ҷе“ҒпјҢд»ҺиҖҢйҖ жҲҗд»·ж јдёӢиЎҢеҺӢеҠӣгҖӮеҶңдә§е“Ғд»·ж јеҫҖеҫҖе…·жңүеӯЈиҠӮжҖ§пјҢеӣ дёәеӮЁеӯҳжҲҗжң¬жҳӮиҙөдё”дә§е“Ғзҡ„еӮЁеӯҳж—¶й—ҙжңүйҷҗгҖӮеӨ©ж°”еңЁеҶіе®ҡи®ёеӨҡеҶңдә§е“Ғзҡ„д»·ж јж–№йқўиө·зқҖе…ій”®дҪңз”ЁгҖӮйңңеҶ»еҸҜиғҪдјҡжҜҒжҺүе·ҙиҘҝзҡ„е’–е•ЎдҪңзү©пјҢдҪӣзҪ—йҮҢиҫҫе·һзҡ„йЈ“йЈҺеҸҜиғҪдјҡеҜ№еҶ·еҶ»ж©ҷжұҒзҡ„д»·ж јдә§з”ҹйҮҚеӨ§еҪұе“ҚпјҢзӯүзӯүгҖӮжүҖз§ҚжӨҚе•Ҷе“Ғзҡ„д»·ж јжіўеҠЁеҫҖеҫҖеңЁж”¶иҺ·еүҚжңҖй«ҳпјҢз„¶еҗҺеңЁдҪңзү©еӨ§е°Ҹе·ІзҹҘж—¶дёӢйҷҚгҖӮеңЁз”ҹй•ҝеӯЈиҠӮпјҢеҶңдә§е“Ғзҡ„д»·ж јиҝҮзЁӢеҸҜиғҪдјҡеӣ еӨ©ж°”иҖҢеҮәзҺ°и·іи·ғгҖӮи®ёеӨҡе•Ҷе“ҒйғҪжҳҜGR

problems.promptEn: Practice 35.3. Distinguish between the historical data and the risk-neutral approach to valuing a derivative. Under what circumstance do they give the same answer?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 35.3. еҢәеҲҶеҺҶеҸІж•°жҚ®е’ҢйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§зҡ„иЎҚз”ҹе“Ғдј°еҖјж–№жі•гҖӮд»Җд№Ҳжғ…еҶөдёӢд»–д»¬дјҡз»ҷеҮәзӣёеҗҢзҡ„зӯ”жЎҲпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
35.3
The historical data approach to valuing an option involves calculating the expected payoff
using historical data and discounting the payoff at the risk-free rate. The risk-neutral
approach involves calculating the expected payoff in a risk-neutral world and discounting at
the risk-free rate. The two approaches give the same answer when percentage changes in the
underlying market variables have zero correlation with stock market returns. (In these
circumstances, all risks can be diversified away.)
problems.explanationEn:
35.3
The historical data approach to valuing an option involves calculating the expected payoff
using historical data and discounting the payoff at the risk-free rate. The risk-neutral
approach involves calculating the expected payoff in a risk-neutral world and discounting at
the risk-free rate. The two approaches give the same answer when percentage changes in the
underlying market variables have zero correlation with stock market returns. (In these
circumstances, all risks can be diversified away.)

problems.promptEn: Practice 35.5. Why is the price of electricity more volatile than that of other energy sources?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 35.5. дёәд»Җд№Ҳз”өд»·жҜ”е…¶д»–иғҪжәҗзҡ„д»·ж јжіўеҠЁжӣҙеӨ§пјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
35.5
Unlike most commodities, electricity cannot be stored easily. If the demand for electricity
exceeds the supply, as it sometimes does during the air conditioning season, the price of
electricity in a deregulated environment will skyrocket. When supply and demand become
matched again, the price will return to former levels.

matched again, the price will return to former levels.

problems.promptEn: Practice 35.9. Would you expect the volatility of the 1-year forward price of oil to be greater than or less than the volatility of the spot price? Explain your answer.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 35.9. жӮЁйў„и®ЎдёҖе№ҙжңҹзҹіжІ№иҝңжңҹд»·ж јзҡ„жіўеҠЁжҖ§дјҡеӨ§дәҺиҝҳжҳҜе°ҸдәҺзҺ°иҙ§д»·ж јзҡ„жіўеҠЁжҖ§пјҹи§ЈйҮҠдҪ зҡ„зӯ”жЎҲгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
35.9
The volatility of the one-year forward price will be less than the volatility of the spot price.
This is because, when the spot price changes by a certain amount, mean reversion will cause
the forward price will change by a lesser amount.

35.9

problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.

The energy producer faces quantity risks and price risks. It can use weather derivatives to
hedge the quantity risks and energy derivatives to hedge against the price risks.

problems.promptEn: Practice 35.12. Explain how a 5 * 8 option contract on electricity with daily exercise works. Explain how a 5 * 8 option contract on electricity with monthly exercise works. Which is worth more?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 35.12. и§ЈйҮҠжҜҸж—ҘиЎҢдҪҝз”өеҠӣзҡ„ 5 * 8 жңҹжқғеҗҲзәҰеҰӮдҪ•иҝҗдҪңгҖӮи§ЈйҮҠжҢүжңҲиЎҢдҪҝзҡ„ 5 * 8 з”өеҠӣжңҹжқғеҗҲзәҰеҰӮдҪ•иҝҗдҪңгҖӮе“ӘдёӘжӣҙеҖјй’ұпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
35.12
A 5 8
x contract is a contract to provide electricity for five days per week during the off-peak
period (11PM to 7AM). When daily exercise is specified, the holder of the option is able to
choose each weekday whether to buy electricity at the strike price at the agreed rate. When
there is monthly exercise, there is one choice at the beginning of the month concerning
whether electricity is to be bought at the strike price at the agreed rate for the whole month.
The option with daily exercise is worth more.
problems.explanationEn:
35.12
A 5 8
x contract is a contract to provide electricity for five days per week during the off-peak
period (11PM to 7AM). When daily exercise is specified, the holder of the option is able to
choose each weekday whether to buy electricity at the strike price at the agreed rate. When
there is monthly exercise, there is one choice at the beginning of the month concerning
whether electricity is to be bought at the strike price at the agreed rate for the whole month.
The option with daily exercise is worth more.

problems.promptEn: Practice 35.13. Explain how CAT bonds work.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 35.13. и§ЈйҮҠ CAT еҖәеҲёеҰӮдҪ•иҝҗдҪңгҖӮ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
35.13
CAT bonds (catastrophe bonds) are an alternative to reinsurance for an insurance company
that has taken on a certain catastrophic risk (e.g., the risk of a hurricane or an earthquake) and
wants to get rid of it. CAT bonds are issued by the insurance company. They provide a higher
rate of interest than government bonds. However, the bondholders agree to forego interest,
and possibly principal, to meet any claims against the insurance company that are within a
prespecified range.
problems.explanationEn:
35.13
CAT bonds (catastrophe bonds) are an alternative to reinsurance for an insurance company
that has taken on a certain catastrophic risk (e.g., the risk of a hurricane or an earthquake) and
wants to get rid of it. CAT bonds are issued by the insurance company. They provide a higher
rate of interest than government bonds. However, the bondholders agree to forego interest,
and possibly principal, to meet any claims against the insurance company that are within a
prespecified range.
problems.explanationZh:
35.13
CAT еҖәеҲёпјҲзҒҫйҡҫеҖәеҲёпјүжҳҜдҝқйҷ©е…¬еҸёй’ҲеҜ№жҹҗдәӣзҒҫйҡҫйЈҺйҷ©пјҲдҫӢеҰӮйЈ“йЈҺжҲ–ең°йңҮпјүйҮҮеҸ–зҡ„дёҖз§ҚеҶҚдҝқйҷ©жӣҝд»Јж–№ејҸпјҢзӣ®зҡ„жҳҜиҪ¬з§»иҜҘйЈҺйҷ©гҖӮCAT еҖәеҲёз”ұдҝқйҷ©е…¬еҸёеҸ‘иЎҢпјҢжҸҗдҫӣй«ҳдәҺж”ҝеәңеҖәеҲёзҡ„еҲ©зҺҮгҖӮдҪҶеҖәеҲёжҢҒжңүдәәеҗҢж„ҸеңЁйў„и®ҫиҢғеӣҙеҶ…иӢҘдҝқйҷ©е…¬еҸёеҸ‘з”ҹзҗҶиө”пјҢж”ҫејғеҲ©жҒҜпјҢз”ҡиҮіжң¬йҮ‘пјҢд»Ҙж»Ўи¶ізҗҶиө”йңҖжұӮгҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 35.14. Consider two bonds that have the same coupon, time to maturity, and price. One is a B-rated corporate bond. The other is a CAT bond. An analysis based on historical data shows that the expected losses on the two bonds as a function of time are the same. Which bond would you advise a portfolio manager to buy and why?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 35.14. иҖғиҷ‘дёӨз§Қе…·жңүзӣёеҗҢжҒҜзҘЁгҖҒеҲ°жңҹж—¶й—ҙе’Ңд»·ж јзҡ„еҖәеҲёгҖӮдёҖз§ҚжҳҜBзә§е…¬еҸёеҖәеҲёгҖӮеҸҰдёҖз§ҚжҳҜ CAT еҖәеҲёгҖӮеҹәдәҺеҺҶеҸІж•°жҚ®зҡ„еҲҶжһҗиЎЁжҳҺпјҢдёӨз§ҚеҖәеҲёдҪңдёәж—¶й—ҙеҮҪж•°зҡ„йў„жңҹжҚҹеӨұжҳҜзӣёеҗҢзҡ„гҖӮжӮЁдјҡе»әи®®жҠ•иө„з»„еҗҲз»ҸзҗҶиҙӯд№°е“Әз§ҚеҖәеҲёпјҹдёәд»Җд№Ҳпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
35.14
The CAT bond has very little systematic risk. Whether a particular type of catastrophe occurs
is independent of the return on the market. The risks in the CAT bond are likely to be largely
вҖңdiversified awayвҖқ by the other investments in the portfolio. A B-rated bond does have
systematic risk that cannot be diversified away. It is likely therefore that the CAT bond is a
better addition to the portfolio.
problems.explanationEn:
35.14
The CAT bond has very little systematic risk. Whether a particular type of catastrophe occurs
is independent of the return on the market. The risks in the CAT bond are likely to be largely
вҖңdiversified awayвҖқ by the other investments in the portfolio. A B-rated bond does have
systematic risk that cannot be diversified away. It is likely therefore that the CAT bond is a
better addition to the portfolio.

problems.explanationZh:
35.15
еңЁиҝҷз§Қжғ…еҶөдёӢпјҢ

\(\frac{dS}{S} = \mu dt + \sigma dz\)

жҲ–иҖ…

\[ d\ln S = \left(\mu - \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 \right) dt + \sigma dz \]

еӣ жӯӨпјҢ\( \ln S_T \) жңҚд»ҺжӯЈжҖҒеҲҶеёғпјҢеқҮеҖјдёә

\[ \int_0^T \left( \mu_t - \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 \right) dt = \int_0^T \mu_t dt - \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 T \]

ж ҮеҮҶе·®дёә

\( \sigma \sqrt{T} \)гҖӮ

з¬¬ 35.4 иҠӮжҢҮеҮәпјҡ

\[ \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \left[ \ln F(t) \right] = \mu_t \]

еӣ жӯӨжңү

\[ \ln F(T) - \ln F(0) = \int_0^T \mu_t dt \]

дё”\( F(0) = S_0 \)пјҢз»“жһңеҫ—д»ҘжҲҗз«ӢгҖӮ

еӣ жӯӨеңЁдј з»ҹйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§дё–з•ҢдёӯпјҢ\( \ln S_T \sim \mathcal{N} \left( \ln F(T) - \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 T, \sigma^2 T \right) \)гҖӮ

problems.promptEn: Practice 35.16. How is the tree in Figure 35.2 modified if the 1- and 2-year futures prices are USD 21 and USD 22 instead of USD 22 and USD 23, respectively. How does this affect the value of the American option in Example 35.3.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 35.16. еҰӮжһң 1 е№ҙжңҹе’Ң 2 е№ҙжңҹжңҹиҙ§д»·ж јеҲҶеҲ«дёә $21 and \$22 иҖҢдёҚжҳҜ \$22 and \$23пјҢеӣҫ 35.2 дёӯзҡ„ж ‘дјҡеҰӮдҪ•дҝ®ж”№гҖӮиҝҷеҜ№дҫӢ 35.3 дёӯзҡ„зҫҺејҸжңҹжқғзҡ„д»·еҖјжңүдҪ•еҪұе“ҚгҖӮ з¬¬дёүз«  е®һзү©жңҹжқғ еҲ°зӣ®еүҚдёәжӯўпјҢжҲ‘д»¬еҮ д№Һе®Ңе…Ёе…іеҝғйҮ‘иһҚиө„дә§зҡ„дј°еҖјгҖӮеңЁжң¬з« дёӯпјҢжҲ‘д»¬е°ҶжҺўи®ЁеҰӮдҪ•е°ҶжҲ‘д»¬жүҖжҸҗеҮәзҡ„жғіжі•жү©еұ•еҲ°иҜ„дј°еңҹең°гҖҒе»әзӯ‘зү©гҖҒеҺӮжҲҝе’Ңи®ҫеӨҮзӯүе®һзү©иө„дә§зҡ„иө„жң¬жҠ•иө„жңәдјҡгҖӮиҝҷдәӣжҠ•иө„жңәдјҡдёӯеҫҖеҫҖеөҢе…ҘдәҶжңҹжқғпјҲжү©еӨ§жҠ•иө„зҡ„жңҹжқғгҖҒж”ҫејғжҠ•иө„зҡ„жңҹжқғгҖҒжҺЁиҝҹжҠ•иө„зҡ„жңҹжқғзӯүпјүгҖӮдҪҝз”Ёдј з»ҹзҡ„иө„жң¬жҠ•иө„иҜ„дј°жҠҖжңҜеҫҲйҡҫеҜ№иҝҷдәӣжңҹжқғиҝӣиЎҢдј°еҖјгҖӮз§°дёәе®һзү©жңҹжқғзҡ„ж–№жі•иҜ•еӣҫеҲ©з”Ёжңҹжқғе®ҡд»·зҗҶи®әжқҘи§ЈеҶіиҝҷдёӘй—®йўҳгҖӮжң¬з« йҰ–е…Ҳи§ЈйҮҠдәҶиҜ„дј°е®һзү©иө„дә§жҠ•иө„зҡ„дј з»ҹж–№жі•пјҢе№¶иҜҙжҳҺдәҶдҪҝз”Ёиҝҷз§Қж–№жі•ж—¶жӯЈзЎ®иҜ„дј°еөҢе…Ҙжңҹжқғзҡ„йҡҫеәҰгҖӮз„¶еҗҺи§ЈйҮҠдәҶеҰӮдҪ•е°ҶйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§дј°еҖјж–№жі•жү©еұ•еҲ°еӨ„зҗҶе®һзү©иө„дә§дј°еҖјпјҢе№¶жҸҗдҫӣдәҶдёҖдәӣзӨәдҫӢжқҘиҜҙжҳҺиҜҘж–№жі•еңЁдёҚеҗҢжғ…еҶөдёӢзҡ„еә”з”ЁгҖӮиҜ„дј°жҪңеңЁиө„жң¬жҠ•иө„йЎ№зӣ®зҡ„дј з»ҹж–№жі•жҳҜвҖңеҮҖзҺ°еҖјвҖқпјҲNPVпјүж–№жі•гҖӮйЎ№зӣ®зҡ„еҮҖзҺ°еҖјжҳҜе…¶йў„жңҹжңӘжқҘеўһйҮҸзҺ°йҮ‘жөҒйҮҸзҡ„зҺ°еҖјгҖӮз”ЁдәҺи®Ўз®—зҺ°еҖјзҡ„иҙҙзҺ°зҺҮжҳҜвҖңйЈҺйҷ©и°ғж•ҙвҖқиҙҙзҺ°зҺҮпјҢе…¶йҖүжӢ©еҸҚжҳ дәҶйЎ№зӣ®зҡ„йЈҺйҷ©гҖӮйҡҸзқҖйЎ№зӣ®йЈҺйҷ©зҡ„еўһеҠ пјҢиҙҙзҺ°зҺҮд№ҹдјҡеўһеҠ гҖӮдҫӢеҰӮпјҢиҖғиҷ‘дёҖйЎ№иҖ—иө„ \$100 million and will last 5 years. The expected cash inflow in each year (in the real world) is estimated to be \$2500 дёҮзҡ„жҠ•иө„гҖӮеҰӮжһңйЈҺйҷ©и°ғж•ҙиҙҙзҺ°зҺҮдёә 12%пјҲиҝһз»ӯеӨҚеҲ©пјүпјҢеҲҷжҠ•иө„зҡ„еҮҖзҺ°еҖјдёәпјҲзҷҫдёҮзҫҺе…ғпјү-100 + 25e-0.12*1 + 25e-0.12*2 + 25e-0.12*3 + 25e-0.12*4 + 25e-0.12*5 = -11.53 иҙҹ NPVпјҢдҫӢеҰӮжҲ‘д»¬зҡ„ NPVеҲҡеҲҡи®Ўз®—еҫ—еҮәпјҢиЎЁжҳҺиҜҘйЎ№зӣ®е°ҶйҷҚдҪҺе…¬еҸёеҜ№е…¶иӮЎдёңзҡ„д»·еҖјпјҢдёҚеә”е®һж–ҪгҖӮжӯЈзҡ„еҮҖзҺ°еҖјиЎЁжҳҺиҜҘйЎ№зӣ®еә”иҜҘе®һж–ҪпјҢеӣ дёәе®ғе°ҶеўһеҠ иӮЎдёңиҙўеҜҢгҖӮ 36.1 иө„жң¬жҠ•иө„иҜ„дј° е®һзү©жңҹжқғ йЈҺйҷ©и°ғж•ҙеҗҺзҡ„иҙҙзҺ°зҺҮеә”дёәе…¬еҸёжҲ–е…¬еҸёиӮЎдёңеҜ№жҠ•иө„жүҖиҰҒжұӮзҡ„еӣһжҠҘгҖӮиҝҷеҸҜд»ҘйҖҡиҝҮеӨҡз§Қж–№ејҸи®Ўз®—гҖӮз»ҸеёёжҺЁиҚҗзҡ„дёҖз§Қж–№жі•ж¶үеҸҠиө„жң¬иө„дә§е®ҡд»·жЁЎеһӢпјҲеҸӮи§Ғз¬¬ 3 з« зҡ„

problems.promptEn: Practice 36.1. Explain the difference between the net present value approach and the risk-neutral valuation approach for valuing a new capital investment opportunity. What are the advantages of the risk-neutral valuation approach for valuing real options?
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 36.1. и§ЈйҮҠеҮҖзҺ°еҖјжі•е’ҢйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§дј°еҖјжі•еңЁиҜ„дј°ж–°иө„жң¬жҠ•иө„жңәдјҡж—¶зҡ„е·®ејӮгҖӮйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§дј°еҖјж–№жі•еҜ№е®һзү©жңҹжқғдј°еҖјжңүе“ӘдәӣдјҳеҠҝпјҹ
problems.answer: See the official worked solution below.
problems.answerEn: See the official worked solution below.
problems.explanation:
36.1
In the net present value approach, cash flows are estimated in the real world and discounted
at a risk-adjusted discount rate. In the risk-neutral valuation approach, cash flows are
estimated in the risk-neutral world and discounted at the risk-free interest rate. The risk-
neutral valuation approach is arguably more appropriate for valuing real options because it is
very difficult to determine the appropriate risk-adjusted discount rate when options are
valued.
problems.explanationEn:
36.1
In the net present value approach, cash flows are estimated in the real world and discounted
at a risk-adjusted discount rate. In the risk-neutral valuation approach, cash flows are
estimated in the risk-neutral world and discounted at the risk-free interest rate. The risk-
neutral valuation approach is arguably more appropriate for valuing real options because it is
very difficult to determine the appropriate risk-adjusted discount rate when options are
valued.

problems.promptEn: Practice 36.6. A driver entering into a car lease agreement can obtain the right to buy the car in 4 years for USD 10,000. The current value of the car is USD 30,000. The value of the car, S, is expected to follow the process dS = mS dt + sS dz, where m = -0.25, s = 0.15, and dz is a Wiener process. The market price of risk for the car price is estimated to be -0.1. What is the value of the option? Assume that the risk-free rate for all maturities is 6%.

problems.promptEn: Practice 36.7. Suppose that the spot price, 6-month futures price, and 12-month futures price for wheat are 250, 260, and 270 cents per bushel, respectively. Suppose that the price of wheat follows the process in equation (36.3) with a = 0.05 and s = 0.15. Construct a two-time- step tree for the price of wheat in a risk-neutral world. A farmer has a project that involves an expenditure of USD 10,000 and a further expenditure of USD 90,000 in 6 months. It will increase wheat that is harvested and sold by 40,000 bushels in 1 year. What is the value of the project? Suppose that the farmer can abandon the project in 6 months and avoid paying the USD 90,000 cost at that time. What is the value of the abandonment option? Assume a risk-free rate of 5% with continuous compounding. 815 Derivatives Mishaps and What We Can Learn from Them Since the mid-1980s there have been some spectacular losses in derivatives markets. The biggest losses have come from the trading of products created from subprime residential mortgages in the United States and were discussed in Chapter 8. Some of the other losses made by financial institutions are listed in Business Snapshot 37.1, and some of those made by nonfinancial organizations in Business Snapshot 37.2. What is remark- able about these lists is the number of situations where huge losses arose from the activities of a single employee. In 1995, Nick LeesonвҖҷs trading brought a 200-year-old British bank, Barings, to its knees; in 1994, Robert CitronвҖҷs trading led to Orange County, a municipality in California, losing about USD 2 billion. Joseph JettвҖҷs trading for Kidder Peabody lost USD 350 million. John RusnakвҖҷs losses of USD 700 million for Allied Irish Bank came to light in 2002. In 2006 the hedge fund Amaranth lost USD 6 billion because of trading risks taken by Brian Hunter. In 2008, JГ©rГҙme Kerviel lost over USD 7 billion trading equity index futures for SociГ©tГ© GГ©nГ©rale. The huge losses at UBS, Shell, and Sumitomo were also each the result of the activities of a single individual. The losses should not be viewed as an indictment of the whole derivatives industry. The derivatives market is a vast multitrillion dollar market that by most measures has been outstandingly successful and has served the needs of its users well. The events listed in Business Snapshots 37.1 and 37.2 represent a tiny proportion of the total trades (both in number and value). Nevertheless, it is worth considering carefully the lessons that can be learned from them. 37 C H A P T E R First, we consider the lessons appropriate to all users of derivatives, whether they are financial or nonfinancial companies.
problems.promptZh: й—®йўҳ 36.7. еҒҮи®ҫе°ҸйәҰзҺ°иҙ§д»·ж јгҖҒ6 дёӘжңҲжңҹиҙ§д»·ж је’Ң 12 дёӘжңҲжңҹиҙ§д»·ж јеҲҶеҲ«дёәжҜҸи’ІејҸиҖі 250 зҫҺеҲҶгҖҒ260 зҫҺеҲҶе’Ң 270 зҫҺеҲҶгҖӮеҒҮи®ҫе°ҸйәҰд»·ж јйҒөеҫӘж–№зЁӢ (36.3) зҡ„иҝҮзЁӢпјҢе…¶дёӯ a = 0.05пјҢs = 0.15гҖӮжһ„е»әйЈҺйҷ©дёӯжҖ§дё–з•Ңдёӯе°ҸйәҰд»·ж јзҡ„дёӨжӯҘж ‘гҖӮжҹҗеҶңж°‘жңүдёҖдёӘйЎ№зӣ®пјҢ6 дёӘжңҲеҶ…ж”ҜеҮә\$10,000 and a further expenditure of \$90,000гҖӮдёҖе№ҙеҶ…е°ҸйәҰж”¶иҺ·е’Ңй”Җе”®йҮҸе°ҶеўһеҠ  40,000 и’ІејҸиҖігҖӮиҜҘйЎ№зӣ®зҡ„д»·еҖјжҳҜд»Җд№ҲпјҹеҒҮи®ҫеҶңж°‘еҸҜд»ҘеңЁ 6 дёӘжңҲеҶ…ж”ҫејғиҜҘйЎ№зӣ®пјҢе№¶йҒҝе…Қж”Ҝд»ҳеҪ“ж—¶ 90,000 зҫҺе…ғзҡ„иҙ№з”ЁгҖӮж”ҫејғйҖүжӢ©жқғзҡ„д»·еҖјжҳҜеӨҡе°‘пјҹеҒҮи®ҫиҝһз»ӯеӨҚеҲ©зҡ„ж— йЈҺйҷ©еҲ©зҺҮдёә 5%гҖӮ иЎҚз”ҹе“ҒзҒҫйҡҫд»ҘеҸҠжҲ‘д»¬еҸҜд»Ҙд»ҺдёӯеӯҰеҲ°д»Җд№Ҳ иҮӘ 20 дё–зәӘ 80 е№ҙд»Јдёӯжңҹд»ҘжқҘпјҢиЎҚз”ҹе“ҒеёӮеңәеҮәзҺ°дәҶдёҖдәӣжғҠдәәзҡ„жҚҹеӨұгҖӮжңҖеӨ§зҡ„жҚҹеӨұжқҘиҮӘзҫҺеӣҪж¬Ўзә§дҪҸе®…жҠөжҠјиҙ·ж¬ҫдә§е“Ғзҡ„дәӨжҳ“пјҢиҝҷеңЁз¬¬ 8 з« дёӯиҝӣиЎҢдәҶи®Ёи®әгҖӮе•Ҷдёҡеҝ«з…§ 37.1 еҲ—еҮәдәҶйҮ‘иһҚжңәжһ„йҖ жҲҗзҡ„е…¶д»–дёҖдәӣжҚҹеӨұпјҢе•Ҷдёҡеҝ«з…§ 37.2 еҲ—еҮәдәҶйқһйҮ‘иһҚз»„з»ҮйҖ жҲҗзҡ„дёҖдәӣжҚҹеӨұгҖӮиҝҷдәӣжё…еҚ•зҡ„еҖјеҫ—жіЁж„Ҹд№ӢеӨ„еңЁдәҺпјҢеҚ•дёӘе‘ҳе·Ҙзҡ„жҙ»еҠЁйҖ жҲҗе·ЁеӨ§жҚҹеӨұзҡ„жғ…еҶөж•°йҮҸгҖӮ 1995 е№ҙпјҢе°је…ӢВ·еҲ©жЈ® (Nick Leeson) зҡ„дәӨжҳ“и®©жӢҘжңү 200 е№ҙеҺҶеҸІзҡ„иӢұеӣҪй“¶иЎҢе·ҙжһ—й“¶иЎҢ (Barings) йҷ·е…Ҙеӣ°еўғпјӣ 1994е№ҙпјҢRobert Citronзҡ„дәӨжҳ“еҜјиҮҙеҠ е·һеҘҘе…°жІ»еҺҝжҚҹеӨұзәҰ\$2 billion. Joseph Jett's trading for Kidder Peabody lost \$3.5дәҝгҖӮз”ұдәҺеёғиҺұжҒ©В·дәЁзү№жүҝжӢ…зҡ„дәӨжҳ“йЈҺйҷ©пјҢзәҰзҝ°В·йІҒж–Ҝзәіе…Ӣ (John Rusnak) жҚҹеӨұдәҶ \$700 million for Allied Irish Bank came to light in 2002. In 2006 the hedge fund Amaranth lost \$60 дәҝзҫҺе…ғгҖӮ 2008 е№ҙпјҢJГ©rГҙme Kerviel еңЁжі•еӣҪе…ҙдёҡй“¶иЎҢзҡ„иӮЎжҢҮжңҹиҙ§дәӨжҳ“дёӯжҚҹеӨұдәҶи¶…иҝҮ 70 дәҝзҫҺе…ғгҖӮз‘һй“¶гҖҒеЈізүҢе’ҢдҪҸеҸӢзҡ„е·ЁйўқдәҸжҚҹд№ҹжҳҜдёӘдәәиЎҢдёәйҖ жҲҗзҡ„гҖӮиҝҷдәӣжҚҹеӨұдёҚеә”иў«и§ҶдёәеҜ№ж•ҙдёӘиЎҚз”ҹе“ҒиЎҢдёҡзҡ„жҺ§иҜүгҖӮиЎҚз”ҹе“ҒеёӮеңәжҳҜдёҖдёӘд»·еҖјж•°дёҮдәҝзҫҺе…ғзҡ„е·ЁеӨ§еёӮеңәпјҢд»ҺеӨ§еӨҡж•°ж ҮеҮҶжқҘзңӢпјҢе®ғйғҪйқһеёёжҲҗеҠҹпјҢе№¶дё”еҫҲеҘҪең°ж»Ўи¶ідәҶз”ЁжҲ·зҡ„йңҖжұӮгҖӮдёҡеҠЎеҝ«з…§ 37.1 е’Ң 37.2 дёӯеҲ—еҮәзҡ„дәӢд»¶д»…еҚ жҖ»дәӨжҳ“йҮҸпјҲж— и®әжҳҜж•°йҮҸиҝҳжҳҜд»·еҖјпјүзҡ„дёҖе°ҸйғЁеҲҶгҖӮе°Ҫз®ЎеҰӮжӯӨпјҢд»ҺдёӯеҸҜд»ҘеҗёеҸ–зҡ„ж•ҷи®ӯиҝҳжҳҜеҖјеҫ—д»”з»ҶиҖғиҷ‘зҡ„гҖӮз¬¬дёҖз« йҰ–е…ҲпјҢжҲ‘д»¬иҖғиҷ‘йҖӮеҗҲжүҖжңүиЎҚз”ҹе“Ғз”ЁжҲ·зҡ„ж•ҷи®ӯпјҢж— и®әд»–д»¬жҳҜйҮ‘иһҚе…¬еҸёиҝҳжҳҜйқһйҮ‘иһҚе…¬еҸёгҖӮ 37.1 жүҖжңүиЎҚз”ҹе“Ғз”ЁжҲ·зҡ„ж•ҷи®ӯ дёҡеҠЎжҰӮеҶө37.1 йҮ‘иһҚжңәжһ„зҡ„е·ЁйўқжҚҹеӨұзҲұе°”е…°иҒ”еҗҲй“¶иЎҢиҝҷе®¶й“¶иЎҢеңЁ2006 е№ҙжҠјжіЁеӨ©з„¶ж°”д»·ж јзҡ„жңӘжқҘиө°еҗ‘ж—¶жҚҹеӨұдәҶзәҰ\$700 million from speculative activities of one of its foreign exchange traders, John Rusnak, that lasted a number of years. Rusnak managed to cover up his losses by creating fictitious option trades. Amaranth This hedge fund lost \$60 дәҝзҫҺе…ғгҖӮе·ҙжһ—й“¶иЎҢ (Barings) иҝҷе®¶жӢҘжңү 200 е№ҙеҺҶеҸІзҡ„иӢұеӣҪй“¶иЎҢдәҺ 1995 е№ҙеӣ ж–°еҠ еқЎдәӨжҳ“е‘ҳе°је…ӢВ·жқҺжЈ® (Nick Leeson) зҡ„жҙ»еҠЁиҖҢиў«ж‘§жҜҒпјҢд»–еҲ©з”Ёжңҹиҙ§е’ҢжңҹжқғеҜ№ж—Ҙз»Ҹ 225 жҢҮж•°зҡ„жңӘжқҘиө°еҗ‘иҝӣиЎҢдәҶеӨ§йўқжҠјжіЁгҖӮжҖ»жҚҹеӨұжҺҘиҝ‘\$1 billion. Enron's counterparties Enron managed to conceal its true situation from its shareholders with some creative contracts. Several financial institutions that allegedly helped Enron do this have settled shareholder lawsuits for over \$10дәҝгҖӮ Kidder PeabodyпјҲеҸӮи§ҒдёҡеҠЎеҝ«з…§ 5.1пјү еҚ•дёӘдәӨжҳ“е‘ҳ Joseph Jett зҡ„жҙ»еҠЁеҜјиҮҙиҝҷе®¶зәҪзәҰжҠ•иө„дәӨжҳ“е•ҶеңЁ 1998 е№ҙеӣ дҝ„зҪ—ж–ҜеҖәеҠЎиҝқзәҰе’ҢжҚҹеӨұ 40 дәҝзҫҺе…ғгҖӮ
